topics.title: Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

Welcome to the foundational module of **Data Structures**. Before we can analyze algorithms or write a single line of code, we must build a rock-solid vocabulary. The KTU Board examiner expects you to define every underlined term in the syllabus with surgical precision. This section builds that vocabulary brick by brick.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - Module 1):** The "Definitions" topic is the most frequently tested opening question in KTU ESE Part A. Master these terms verbatim — they form the base of nearly every sub-question in Part B.

---

## 1.1 The Term "Data" — The Raw Material

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Data** is a collection of raw, unprocessed facts, figures, symbols, or values that by themselves carry no specific meaning to the recipient. It is the smallest unit of information that can be input, stored, and processed by a computer system.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of data as the **ingredients on a kitchen counter** — flour, eggs, sugar, milk. By themselves, they are just raw materials. Until a chef (the processor) combines them using a recipe (an algorithm), they don't become a *cake* (meaningful information). Data is the "before-cooking" state of knowledge.

**Key Properties of Data:**
- It is **unprocessed** and **unorganized**.
- It exists in multiple forms: **numerical** (45, 3.14), **alphabetical** ("KTU"), **alphanumeric** ("Roll23"), and **symbolic** (#, @).
- On its own, it conveys **no decision-making value**.

---

## 1.2 Information — The Processed Outcome

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Information** is the processed, organized, and structured form of data that carries logical meaning, context, and utility, enabling a recipient to make decisions or derive conclusions.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Returning to the kitchen — once the chef bakes the ingredients, you get a **cake**. That cake, with its flavor, shape, and purpose, is *information*. The transformation is:

$$\text{Data} \xrightarrow{\text{Processing (Algorithm)}} \text{Information}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Data-Information Duality:** KTU examiners often ask: *"Differentiate between Data and Information."* Memorize the contrast — Data is raw, Information is meaningful; Data is unorganized, Information is structured; Data is input to a process, Information is its output.

---

## 1.3 Entity, Attribute, and Domain — The Modeling Trinity

To model the real world inside a computer, we use three intertwined concepts:

| Term | Formal Definition | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Entity** | A real-world object or concept about which data is collected (also called a *record object*). | A specific **Student** in your classroom. |
| **Attribute** | A property, characteristic, or feature that describes an entity (also called a *field*, *data item*, or *column*). | The **roll number, name, age, or CGPA** of that student. |
| **Domain** | The set of all permissible values that an attribute can legally take. | For `Age`, the domain is `[0, 150]` (whole years). |

**Mathematical Representation of an Entity:**
An entity $E$ with attributes $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$ is the **Cartesian product** of their domains:

$$E = D_1 \times D_2 \times \dots \times D_n$$

where $D_i$ is the domain of the $i^{th}$ attribute. Each ordered tuple $(v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n)$ where $v_i \in D_i$ represents a single instance of the entity.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** When asked to define a "Record" — answer: *"A record is a collection of related data items (fields/attributes) describing a single entity."* This is the most awarded definition for a 2-mark question.

---

## 1.4 Data Item (Field) — The Atomic Unit

> **Data Item** is the smallest named unit of information that cannot be meaningfully subdivided further in the context of a given application.

**Two Sub-Categories:**
1. **Group Item:** A data item composed of smaller sub-items (e.g., *Date* = Day + Month + Year). Also called a *composite* or *aggregate* item.
2. **Elementary Item (Atomic Item):** A data item that cannot be decomposed further (e.g., *Roll Number*, *Salary*). Also called a *scalar* item.

**Analogy:** Think of your full postal address. The full address is the **Group Item**, while House Number, Street, City, and PIN are the **Elementary Items** inside it.

---

## 1.5 Data Object — The Container

> **Data Object** is a variable, register, or memory location that holds a data item at a given point in time, taking values from a specific domain.

Every data object has:
- A **Name** (identifier) — e.g., `student_age`
- A **Type** — e.g., `int`
- A **Value** — e.g., `20`
- A **Scope** (lifetime and visibility) — e.g., local vs. global
- A **Domain** — the set of values it can legally hold

---

## 1.6 Data Type — The Contract of Behavior

> **Data Type** is a classification that specifies (a) the set of values a variable can hold, and (b) the set of operations that can be performed on those values.

**Classification (KTU 2024 Mandated Diagram):**

| Category | Sub-Category | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Primitive (Basic) Data Types** | Built into the language | `int`, `float`, `char`, `double`, `void` |
| **Non-Primitive (User-Defined) Data Types** | Derived from primitive types | Arrays, Structures, Unions, Classes, Pointers |

**Expanded Mathematical View:** A data type $T$ is formally a 2-tuple:

$$T = (D, O)$$

where:
- $D$ = the **Domain** (set of all valid values)
- $O$ = the **Set of Operations** defined on $D$

For example, the integer type $T_{int}$ in C has:
- $D = \{-2^{31}, -2^{31}+1, \dots, 2^{31}-1\}$
- $O = \{+, -, \times, \div, \%, <, >, ==, ++, --, \dots\}$

---

## 1.7 Abstract Data Type (ADT) — The Logical Blueprint

> **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its **behavior (semantics)** from the point of view of a user, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations.

**The "Abstract" Principle:**
An ADT specifies **WHAT** operations do, never **HOW** they are implemented. Implementation details are hidden — this is called **information hiding** or **encapsulation**.

**The Famous Three Components of an ADT:**

1. **Data Declaration (Data Part):** Specifies the structure and organization of the data.
2. **Operation Declaration (Function/Method Part):** Lists all operations with their signatures (name, parameters, return type).
3. **Encapsulation:** Bundles data and operations into a single unit; internal representation is hidden from the user.

**Examples of ADTs (from your syllabus):**
- **Stack ADT** — supports `push()`, `pop()`, `peek()`, `isEmpty()`.
- **Queue ADT** — supports `enqueue()`, `dequeue()`, `front()`, `isEmpty()`.
- **List ADT** — supports `insert()`, `delete()`, `find()`, `traverse()`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The ADT-vs-Data-Structure distinction is the single most-tested definition in Part A. A **Data Structure** is the *concrete implementation* of an **ADT** in a specific programming language. The ADT is the *theory*; the Data Structure is the *practice*.

---

## 1.8 Data Structure — The Concrete Implementation

> **Data Structure** is a specialized format for organizing, processing, retrieving, and storing data in memory such that it can be accessed and worked with efficiently according to the operations required by the algorithm.

**Mathematical Definition:**
A data structure is the realization of an ADT using a concrete programming construct. Formally, it is the pairing of:

$$\text{Data Structure} = (\text{Storage Structure}, \text{Set of Algorithms})$$

The **storage structure** determines *how* data is laid out in memory, and the **algorithms** determine *how* the operations are executed on that layout.

**Why Study Data Structures?**
- **Efficiency:** Right structure turns a 10-hour job into 10 seconds.
- **Scalability:** Algorithms on good structures scale to billions of records.
- **Abstraction:** Allows problem-solving at a logical level, divorced from hardware.
- **Reusability:** Well-designed structures form reusable libraries.

---

## 1.9 The Core Operations on Data Structures

Regardless of type, almost every data structure supports the following fundamental operations (often abbreviated as **CRUD**):

| Operation | Meaning | Common Synonyms |
|---|---|---|
| **Create / Initialize** | Allocate memory and set up an empty structure. | `init()`, `create()` |
| **Insert** | Add a new data element at a given position. | `add()`, `push()`, `enqueue()` |
| **Delete** | Remove an existing element. | `remove()`, `pop()`, `dequeue()` |
| **Search / Traverse** | Visit elements to find a target or to print/inspect all. | `find()`, `scan()` |
| **Update / Modify** | Change the value of an existing element. | `set()`, `assign()` |
| **Sort** | Rearrange elements in a specific order. | `sort()` |
| **Merge** | Combine two structures into one. | `concat()`, `union()` |
| **Destroy** | Deallocate all memory. | `free()`, `delete()` |

---

## 1.10 The Master Picture — Putting It All Together

The hierarchy of concepts, from the most abstract to the most concrete, is:

$$\text{Problem} \rightarrow \text{ADT (Logical Model)} \rightarrow \text{Data Structure (Physical Implementation)}$$

For example:
- **Problem:** Manage function calls in a compiler.
- **ADT:** Stack (LIFO behavior).
- **Data Structure:** Array-based stack *or* Linked-list-based stack.

Both are valid data structures implementing the **same ADT**, but they have different performance trade-offs in time and space.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This section moves from *naming* things to *understanding* how the definitions interconnect, how data structures are classified, and what mathematical notations you must know for KTU 2024.

---

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Model of Data Representation

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly distinguishes between three layers, and examiners test this layering:

1. **Abstract Level (Mathematical/Logical):** Defines the ADT — *what* operations exist and *what* they mean, with no implementation detail. Example: A "Set" is a collection of unique elements supporting union, intersection, difference.
2. **Logical Level (Data Structure):** Chooses a specific organization — e.g., **Array**, **Linked List**, **Tree**, **Graph**. The same ADT can be realized by different data structures.
3. **Physical Level (Storage):** Decides how the structure is laid out in actual memory — contiguous (arrays) vs. non-contiguous (linked lists), static (compile-time) vs. dynamic (run-time) allocation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in the exam:** A common 7-mark question is *"Discuss the three levels of data structure representation with an example."* You must explicitly name all three levels and show how an example (e.g., a Stack) flows through them.

---

## 2.2 Primary vs. Secondary Data Structures

A subtle but high-yield distinction:

| Aspect | Primary Data Structures | Secondary Data Structures |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Built-in; directly operated upon by machine instructions. | Built using primary structures to overcome their limitations. |
| **Location in Memory** | Typically reside in main memory (RAM). | Often reside in secondary storage (disk, SSD) for large datasets. |
| **Speed** | Faster access (nanoseconds). | Slower access (milliseconds). |
| **Examples** | Integer, Float, Character, Boolean, Arrays, Pointers. | B-Trees, B+ Trees, Indexed Sequential Files, Hash Tables used on disk. |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners sometimes ask: *"Are arrays a primary or secondary data structure?"* — Answer: **Primary** (they are basic, memory-resident constructs).

---

## 2.3 Classification of Data Structures (KTU Mandatory)

The KTU 2024 syllabus divides all data structures into two master categories:

### A. Primitive Data Structures
- **Integer, Float, Character, Boolean, Pointer.**
- Directly supported by hardware / language.
- Can hold only a single value at a time.

### B. Non-Primitive Data Structures
These are derived from primitives and are further divided into:

#### B.1 Linear Data Structures
Data is organized in a **sequential / linear** order. Each element has exactly one predecessor and one successor (except the first and last).

- **Static Linear:** Arrays (size fixed at compile-time).
- **Dynamic Linear:** Linked Lists (Singly, Doubly, Circular), Stacks, Queues, Deques.

#### B.2 Non-Linear Data Structures
Data is organized in a **hierarchical or networked** manner. Each element can have multiple predecessors and/or successors.

- **Trees:** Binary Tree, Binary Search Tree (BST), AVL Tree, Heap, B-Tree, Trie.
- **Graphs:** Directed, Undirected, Weighted, Bipartite, DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph).

#### B.3 File Structures (Specialized Non-Primitive)
Used for storage and retrieval of bulk data on secondary memory.

- Sequential, Indexed, Hashed, B-Tree-based files.

**Tree-Style Mental Map:**

```
                DATA STRUCTURES
                       |
        +--------------+--------------+
        |                             |
   PRIMITIVE                   NON-PRIMITIVE
        |                             |
   (int,float,...)        +-----------+-----------+
                          |                       |
                       LINEAR                NON-LINEAR
                          |                       |
                  +-------+-------+        +------+------+
                  |               |        |             |
               STATIC         DYNAMIC    TREES        GRAPHS
            (Arrays)        (Lists,    (BST,Heap)  (Directed,
                          Stacks,                  Undirected)
                          Queues)
```

---

## 2.4 Static vs. Dynamic Data Structures

| Property | Static | Dynamic |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Allocation** | Compile-time (fixed size) | Run-time (grow/shrink) |
| **Resizing** | Not possible without re-creation | Possible in-place |
| **Memory Efficiency** | May waste memory if under-utilized | More efficient; pays only for what it uses |
| **Implementation Cost** | Simple, fast access via indexing | Complex, uses pointers, slightly slower access |
| **Example** | Array | Linked List, Dynamic Array (vector) |

---

## 2.5 In-place vs. Out-of-place Operations

A definition KTU frequently tests:

- **In-place Operation:** An algorithm that transforms the input data structure *using only a constant amount of extra memory* (i.e., $O(1)$ auxiliary space). Example: Bubble Sort, Selection Sort.
- **Out-of-place Operation:** An algorithm that requires *additional memory proportional to the input size* (i.e., $O(n)$ or more). Example: Merge Sort (needs an auxiliary array of size $n$).

---

## 2.6 The KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula & Notation Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table.** It contains every symbolic notation, formula, and boundary condition you will see in Module 1 of PCITT303.

| Symbol / Term | Formal Meaning | Where It Appears |
|---|---|---|
| $D$ | Set of all valid values (Domain) | Data Type definition |
| $E$ | Entity (a real-world object) | Database / Modeling |
| $A_i$ | The $i^{th}$ attribute of an entity | Tuple representation |
| $T = (D, O)$ | A data type = (Domain, Operations) | ADT definition |
| $\mid D \mid$ | Cardinality (number of elements) of a domain | Size calculation |
| $n$ | Number of elements in the data structure | Time/space complexity |
| $O(f(n))$ | Big-Oh — asymptotic upper bound | Worst-case analysis |
| $\Omega(f(n))$ | Big-Omega — asymptotic lower bound | Best-case analysis |
| $\Theta(f(n))$ | Big-Theta — tight bound (same order) | Average-case analysis |
| $\text{arr}[i]$ | The $i^{th}$ element of an array (0-indexed) | Array operations |
| $\text{next}(x)$ | Pointer/successor of node $x$ | Linked list traversal |
| $LIFO$ | Last In, First Out | Stack ADT |
| $FIFO$ | First In, First Out | Queue ADT |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1)$ | Recurrence for linear traversal | Recurrence analysis |
| $\text{sizeof}(T)$ | Memory bytes required to store one element of type $T$ | Space calculation |

> **Critical Reminder:** When using $\vert$ inside a math expression, always wrap it as $\lvert D \rvert$ or $\mid D \mid$ in LaTeX — never as a bare pipe character — to avoid breaking markdown table rendering.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why These Definitions Matter

Every modern computing system relies on the abstraction layers defined above:

- **Compiler Design:** Uses a **Stack** for function calls, a **Symbol Table** (often a Hash Table) for variable lookup, and a **Syntax Tree** (non-linear) for parsing.
- **Operating Systems:** Process scheduling uses **Queues** (Ready Queue, Wait Queue); memory management uses **Linked Lists** (Free List).
- **Databases:** Indexing relies on **B-Trees** and **B+ Trees**; query optimization uses **Graph** algorithms.
- **Computer Networks:** Routing uses **Graph algorithms** (Dijkstra's, Bellman-Ford); packet buffering uses **Queues**.
- **Artificial Intelligence:** Search uses **Trees** (DFS, BFS) and **Graphs**; priority handling uses **Heaps**.
- **Computer Graphics:** Scene graphs are **Trees**; collision detection uses spatial **Hash Tables**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** The "right" data structure can change an algorithm's runtime from **years to milliseconds**. Google's original search ranking relied on a clever choice of data structures (inverted index) to handle billions of web pages in under a second.

---

## 2.8 The Time-Space Tradeoff — A Universal Principle

For nearly every data structure problem, you face a **tradeoff**:

$$\text{Time} \longleftrightarrow \text{Space}$$

- Faster access (e.g., hash table $O(1)$) typically costs more memory.
- Compact storage (e.g., run-length encoding) typically costs more processing time.
- KTU questions on "discuss the time-space tradeoff" appear in almost every Module 1 exam.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives the **size of a data structure**, the **memory footprint**, and the **fundamental recurrence for traversal** — all foundational calculations that KTU Part B problems expect you to perform.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Footprint of a Linear Data Structure

**Problem Statement:**
Given an array of $n$ elements, where each element is of data type $T$ occupying $\text{sizeof}(T)$ bytes, derive the total memory required to store the entire structure.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let:
- $n$ = number of elements in the structure
- $s$ = size of one element in bytes, i.e., $s = \text{sizeof}(T)$
- $M$ = total memory in bytes

**Step 1 — Memory for the data elements alone:**

$$M_{\text{data}} = n \times s$$

**Logical Explanation:** Each of the $n$ elements occupies exactly $s$ contiguous bytes. Multiplying gives the total data footprint.

**Step 2 — Memory for structural overhead (bookkeeping):**

In static arrays, the only overhead is storing $n$ and the base address. In dynamic structures like linked lists, each node also stores a pointer to the next node. For a singly linked list:

$$M_{\text{node}} = s + p$$

where $p = \text{sizeof}(\text{pointer})$ (typically **8 bytes** on a 64-bit system).

**Step 3 — Total memory for a singly linked list of $n$ nodes:**

$$M_{\text{linked}} = n \times (s + p) = n \times s + n \times p$$

**Step 4 — Comparison: Array vs. Linked List for the same $n$ elements:**

$$\Delta M = M_{\text{linked}} - M_{\text{array}} = n \times p$$

This means a linked list always uses $n \times p$ extra bytes compared to an array, **regardless of element size**.

**Numerical Example:**
Let $n = 1000$, $s = 4$ bytes (an `int`), $p = 8$ bytes.

$$M_{\text{array}} = 1000 \times 4 = 4000 \text{ bytes}$$

$$M_{\text{linked}} = 1000 \times (4 + 8) = 12000 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\Delta M = 12000 - 4000 = 8000 \text{ bytes}$$

Conclusion: The linked list uses **3× the memory** of the array for the same data, purely due to pointer overhead.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Domain Cardinality and Attribute Combinations

**Problem Statement:**
An entity `Student` has three attributes — `Roll No` (domain: $\{1, 2, \dots, 100\}$), `Branch` (domain: $\{\text{CS}, \text{EC}, \text{ME}, \text{EE}\}$, 4 values), and `Year` (domain: $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$). Derive the total number of distinct student records that can be represented.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Identify the domains:**

$$D_1 = \text{Domain of Roll No} = \{1, 2, \dots, 100\}$$

$$D_2 = \text{Domain of Branch} = \{\text{CS}, \text{EC}, \text{ME}, \text{EE}\}$$

$$D_3 = \text{Domain of Year} = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Cartesian Product Definition:**

The set of all possible student records is the Cartesian product:

$$S = D_1 \times D_2 \times D_3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Multiplication Rule of Cardinalities:**

For finite sets, the cardinality of a Cartesian product equals the product of the individual cardinalities:

$$\mid S \mid = \mid D_1 \mid \times \mid D_2 \mid \times \mid D_3 \mid$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\lvert D_1 \rvert = 100, \quad \lvert D_2 \rvert = 4, \quad \lvert D_3 \rvert = 4$$

$$\mid S \mid = 100 \times 4 \times 4 = 1600$$

**Conclusion:** The data type `Student` can legally represent up to **1,600 unique combinations**. This is the upper bound on the number of distinct student records.

**Generalization:**
For an entity with $k$ attributes and domain cardinalities $\lvert D_1 \rvert, \lvert D_2 \rvert, \dots, \lvert D_k \rvert$:

$$\lvert S \rvert = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \lvert D_i \rvert = \lvert D_1 \rvert \times \lvert D_2 \rvert \times \dots \times \lvert D_k \rvert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** This formula is a guaranteed sub-question in any "Introduction" or "Definitions" paper. The student is expected to *show step 3* — the rule of product for set cardinalities — and *not just* state the final number.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Recurrence Relation for Sequential Traversal

**Problem Statement:**
Derive the recurrence relation $T(n)$ for the time taken to traverse a linear data structure (e.g., an array or linked list) of $n$ elements using a simple iterative visit, and solve it.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Define the base case:**

When there is **only 1 element** to visit, the traversal cost is a constant — the time to access the element, read it, and check the loop condition. Denote this constant as $c_1$.

$$T(1) = c_1$$

**Step 2 — Define the recursive (general) case:**

To traverse a structure of $n$ elements, the algorithm:
1. Visits the first element — cost $c_1$ (constant time).
2. Recursively/iteratively traverses the **remaining $n-1$ elements** — cost $T(n-1)$.

Therefore, the recurrence is:

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + c_1$$

**Step 3 — Solve the recurrence by unrolling (substitution method):**

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + c_1$$

$$T(n) = T(n-2) + 2c_1$$

$$T(n) = T(n-3) + 3c_1$$

After $k$ unrollings:

$$T(n) = T(n-k) + k \cdot c_1$$

**Step 4 — Apply the base case:**

Set $n - k = 1$, which gives $k = n - 1$.

$$T(n) = T(1) + (n-1) \cdot c_1$$

$$T(n) = c_1 + (n-1) \cdot c_1 = c_1 + c_1 n - c_1 = c_1 n$$

**Step 5 — Final asymptotic form:**

$$T(n) = c_1 \cdot n = O(n)$$

**Conclusion:** Sequential traversal of a linear structure with $n$ elements takes time **linear in $n$**. This is the seed result from which all later complexity analyses (search, sort, tree traversal) will be derived.

**Numerical Verification:**
Let $c_1 = 1$ microsecond, $n = 1000$.

$$T(1000) = 1 \times 1000 = 1000 \, \mu s = 1 \text{ millisecond}$$

Sanity check: Reading 1,000 integers in a loop is consistent with taking about 1 ms in modern hardware.

---

## 3.4 The Analytical Matrix — Symbol-to-Meaning Map

| KTU Symbol | Domain | Operational Meaning | Step-by-Step Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D$ | Data Type | The set of legal values | Defined by language or user; bounded by hardware |
| $O$ | Data Type | The set of legal operations | Closed under the type's semantics |
| $T = (D,O)$ | ADT | Mathematical type definition | A 2-tuple of domain and operations |
| $n$ | Complexity | Number of elements | Input size parameter |
| $f(n)$ | Complexity | Rate-of-growth function | $f: \mathbb{N} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^+$ |
| $O(f(n))$ | Complexity | Upper asymptotic bound | $\exists c, n_0: \forall n \geq n_0, \, g(n) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ |
| $\Omega(f(n))$ | Complexity | Lower asymptotic bound | $\exists c, n_0: \forall n \geq n_0, \, g(n) \geq c \cdot f(n)$ |
| $\Theta(f(n))$ | Complexity | Tight bound | Both $O$ and $\Omega$ hold simultaneously |
| $\lvert S \rvert$ | Set Theory | Cardinality of $S$ | Number of distinct elements |
| $\prod$ | Set Theory | Product of domain sizes | Used in attribute-combination derivations |
| $M(n)$ | Space | Memory footprint | Linear or quadratic in $n$ depending on structure |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Putting Theory Together

**Problem:**
A class has the following attributes — `ID` (domain: integers 1 to 60), `Name` (any of 500 strings), `Grade` (A, B, C, D, E — 5 values).

**(a)** How many distinct student records can be represented?
**(b)** If we store these in a singly linked list, with each pointer being 8 bytes and each integer ID being 4 bytes, derive the memory footprint.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Domain cardinalities: $\lvert D_{\text{ID}} \rvert = 60$, $\lvert D_{\text{Name}} \rvert = 500$, $\lvert D_{\text{Grade}} \rvert = 5$.

$$\mid S \mid = 60 \times 500 \times 5 = 150{,}000 \text{ records}$$

**(b)** For a linked list of 60 students (we store the actual count, not the maximum), each node has:
- `ID` = 4 bytes
- `Name` = 50 bytes (assume 50 characters × 1 byte)
- `Grade` = 1 byte
- `next` pointer = 8 bytes
- **Total per node** = $4 + 50 + 1 + 8 = 63$ bytes

$$M_{\text{linked}} = 60 \times 63 = 3780 \text{ bytes}$$

**Memory for an array (no pointers, no dynamic overhead):**

$$M_{\text{array}} = 60 \times 55 = 3300 \text{ bytes}$$

**Overhead of using a linked list:**

$$\Delta M = 3780 - 3300 = 480 \text{ bytes} = 60 \times 8 \text{ bytes}$$

This perfectly matches the theoretical formula $\Delta M = n \times p = 60 \times 8 = 480$ bytes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The diagrams below follow the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards**: every node ID is purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; node labels contain no markdown formatting; reserved keywords are avoided.

---

## 4.1 Master Classification Diagram of Data Structures

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRoot["DATA STRUCTURES"]
    nodePrim["PRIMITIVE DS"]
    nodeNonPrim["NON PRIMITIVE DS"]
    nodeInt["Integer"]
    nodeFloat["Float"]
    nodeChar["Character"]

    nodeNonLinear["NON LINEAR DS"]

    nodeDynamic["DYNAMIC LINEAR"]
    nodeTree["TREES"]
    nodeGraph["GRAPHS"]
    nodeArray["Arrays"]
    nodeList["Linked Lists"]
    nodeStack["Stacks"]
    nodeQueue["Queues"]
    nodeDeque["Deques"]
    nodeBST["Binary Search Tree"]
    nodeHeap["Heaps"]
    nodeAVL["AVL Trees"]

    nodeUndir["Undirected Graphs"]

    nodeRoot --> nodePrim
    nodeRoot --> nodeNonPrim
    nodePrim --> nodeInt
    nodePrim --> nodeFloat
    nodePrim --> nodeChar
    nodePrim --> nodeBool
    nodePrim --> nodePointer
    nodeNonPrim --> nodeLinear
    nodeNonPrim --> nodeNonLinear
    nodeLinear --> nodeStatic
    nodeLinear --> nodeDynamic
    nodeStatic --> nodeArray
    nodeDynamic --> nodeList
    nodeDynamic --> nodeStack
    nodeDynamic --> nodeQueue
    nodeDynamic --> nodeDeque
    nodeNonLinear --> nodeTree
    nodeNonLinear --> nodeGraph
    nodeTree --> nodeBST
    nodeTree --> nodeHeap
    nodeTree --> nodeAVL
    nodeGraph --> nodeDir
    nodeGraph --> nodeUndir
    nodeGraph --> nodeWeighted
```

---

## 4.2 The ADT-to-Data-Structure Mapping (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeProblem["REAL WORLD PROBLEM"]
    nodeADT["ABSTRACT DATA TYPE LOGICAL MODEL"]
    nodeDS1["DATA STRUCTURE OPTION A ARRAY BASED"]
    nodeDS2["DATA STRUCTURE OPTION B LINKED LIST BASED"]
    nodeOps1["O 1 access fixed size"]
    nodeOps2["O n access dynamic size"]

    nodeProblem -->|identify operations| nodeADT
    nodeADT -->|implement choice 1| nodeDS1
    nodeADT -->|implement choice 2| nodeDS2
    nodeDS1 -->|tradeoff| nodeOps1
    nodeDS2 -->|tradeoff| nodeOps2
```

---

## 4.3 The Three-Level Data Representation Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeAbs["LEVEL 1 ABSTRACT MATHEMATICAL MODEL"]
    nodeLog["LEVEL 2 LOGICAL DATA STRUCTURE"]
    nodePhy["LEVEL 3 PHYSICAL STORAGE IN MEMORY"]
    nodeADT2["ADT Specification WHAT operations exist"]
    nodeDS2a["Array OR Linked List OR Tree choice"]
    nodePhy2a["Contiguous bytes OR Nodes with pointers"]

    nodeAbs --> nodeADT2
    nodeLog --> nodeDS2a
    nodePhy --> nodePhy2a
    nodeADT2 -.realized by.-> nodeDS2a
    nodeDS2a -.stored as.-> nodePhy2a
```

---

## 4.4 Data Item Decomposition — A Reference Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeDI["DATA ITEM"]
    nodeGI["GROUP ITEM COMPOSITE"]
    nodeEI["ELEMENTARY ITEM ATOMIC"]
    nodeEx1["Example DATE equals Day plus Month plus Year"]
    nodeEx2["Example Roll Number Salary"]

    nodeDI --> nodeGI
    nodeDI --> nodeEI
    nodeGI --> nodeEx1
    nodeEI --> nodeEx2
```

---

## 4.5 Operation Categories on a Data Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeOp["OPERATIONS ON DATA STRUCTURE"]
    nodeMut["MUTATORS modify the structure"]
    nodeAcc["ACCESSORS read the structure"]
    nodeCons["CONSTRUCTORS initialize or destroy"]
    nodeIns["Insert Delete Update"]
    nodeSrch["Search Traverse Retrieve"]
    nodeInit["Create Destroy"]

    nodeOp --> nodeMut
    nodeOp --> nodeAcc
    nodeOp --> nodeCons
    nodeMut --> nodeIns
    nodeAcc --> nodeSrch
    nodeCons --> nodeInit
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter:** KTU 2024 Part B questions frequently ask for a *neat labeled diagram* of the classification. The diagrams above give you an examiner-ready visual that fits in a single page and uses no protected syntax.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section mirrors the **exact KTU ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks, no choice) and Part B (14 marks, internal choice between two full questions). Each question carries a simulated past-year tag and a complete model answer.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Data Item, (ii) Data Object, (iii) Abstract Data Type.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(i) Data Item (1 mark):** A data item is the smallest named unit of information in a data structure. It can be either an *elementary item* (e.g., `Roll Number = 45`, atomic and indivisible) or a *group item* (e.g., `Date = (Day, Month, Year)`, composed of sub-items).

**(ii) Data Object (1 mark):** A data object is a named memory location (variable/register) that holds a data item at a given moment, with a defined type and a permissible value domain. Example: `int age = 20;` — here `age` is the data object, of type `int`, holding the value 20, with domain $[-2^{31}, 2^{31}-1]$.

**(iii) Abstract Data Type (1 mark):** An ADT is a mathematical model of a data type defined by its behavior — the set of values it can hold and the set of operations supported — without specifying implementation. Example: **Stack ADT** with operations `push()`, `pop()`, `peek()`, `isEmpty()`. Implementation can be array-based or linked-list-based.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Differentiate between Primitive and Non-Primitive data structures. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Primitive DS | Non-Primitive DS |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Built into the programming language. | Derived from primitive types by the programmer. |
| **Complexity** | Simple, atomic, hardware-supported. | Complex, composed of primitives. |
| **Examples** | `int`, `float`, `char`, `double`, `pointer`. | Arrays, Structures, Linked Lists, Stacks, Trees, Graphs. |

Primitive DS hold a single value and are directly operated upon by machine instructions. Non-primitive DS organize collections of primitive or other non-primitive values, enabling complex relationships like linear ordering, hierarchies, and networks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B Module 1 questions award **7 marks for part (a)** and **7 marks for part (b)**. Both sub-questions together carry 14 marks. Examiners award 1 mark for a clear diagram, 1–2 marks for definitions, and the rest for explanation, examples, and correct classification.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 1**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**(a)** Explain the concept of an Abstract Data Type (ADT) in detail. Discuss its key characteristics and explain with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain the basic operations performed on any data structure with examples. Compare static and dynamic data structures. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Formal Definition (2 marks):**
An Abstract Data Type (ADT) is a theoretical model for data types in which a data type is defined by its **behavior (semantics)** from a user's perspective. Formally, an ADT is a 2-tuple:

$$T_{ADT} = (D, O)$$

where $D$ is the domain (set of valid values) and $O$ is the set of operations defined on $D$.

**Step 2 — Key Characteristics (3 marks):**
1. **Abstraction:** The user knows *what* operations do, never *how* they are implemented.
2. **Encapsulation:** Data and operations are bundled together; internal representation is hidden.
3. **Modularity:** ADTs can be designed, implemented, and tested independently.
4. **Reusability:** A well-defined ADT can be used across multiple programs.
5. **Information Hiding:** Implementation details are private to the developer of the ADT.

**Step 3 — Example: Stack ADT (2 marks):**
- **Data:** A collection of elements with a *top* pointer.
- **Operations:**
  - `push(x)` — add element $x$ to the top.
  - `pop()` — remove and return the top element.
  - `peek()` — return the top element without removing it.
  - `isEmpty()` — return `true` if the stack has no elements.

The Stack ADT can be implemented using either an **array** (fixed capacity, contiguous memory) or a **linked list** (dynamic capacity, scattered memory) — both fulfill the same logical contract.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Basic Operations (3 marks):**
1. **Insertion** — Add a new element. Example: `push(x)` in a stack.
2. **Deletion** — Remove an existing element. Example: `pop()` in a stack.
3. **Traversal** — Visit all elements. Example: printing all nodes of a linked list.
4. **Search** — Locate a target element. Example: linear search in an array.
5. **Sorting** — Rearrange elements. Example: ascending order.
6. **Merging** — Combine two structures. Example: merging two sorted lists.
7. **Update** — Change an existing element's value.

**Step 2 — Static vs. Dynamic Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | Static DS | Dynamic DS |
|---|---|---|
| **Size** | Fixed at compile time | Grows/shrinks at run time |
| **Memory** | Contiguous allocation | Contiguous or non-contiguous |
| **Resizing** | Not possible without recreation | Possible |
| **Examples** | Array, fixed-size stack | Linked list, dynamic stack, vector |
| **Access Time** | $O(1)$ random access | $O(n)$ sequential access (linked list) |
| **Memory Wastage** | Possible (if under-utilized) | Minimal (pay-as-you-use) |

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
Static structures offer **speed and simplicity**; dynamic structures offer **flexibility and memory efficiency**. The choice depends on whether the maximum size is known in advance and whether frequent insertions/deletions are expected.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 2 (ALTERNATIVE)**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**(a)** Define a data structure. Explain the classification of data structures in detail with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between an Abstract Data Type (ADT) and a Data Structure (DS). Explain the three levels of data structure representation with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
A data structure is a specialized way of organizing, storing, and managing data in memory so that it can be accessed and used efficiently. Formally:

$$\text{Data Structure} = (\text{Storage Layout}, \text{Set of Algorithms})$$

**Step 2 — Classification (4 marks):**
Data structures are classified as:

- **A. Primitive DS:** Integer, Float, Character, Boolean, Pointer. These are basic, built-in types directly supported by hardware.
- **B. Non-Primitive DS:** Derived from primitives. Subdivided into:
  - **B.1 Linear DS:** Arrays, Linked Lists, Stacks, Queues, Deques. Data is arranged sequentially.
  - **B.2 Non-Linear DS:** Trees (BST, Heap, AVL) and Graphs (Directed, Undirected, Weighted). Data is arranged hierarchically or as a network.

**Step 3 — Neat Diagram (1 mark):** *(Reproduce the Master Classification Diagram from SECTION 4.1.)*

```
DATA STRUCTURES
    ├── PRIMITIVE (int, float, char, pointer)
    └── NON-PRIMITIVE
         ├── LINEAR
         │    ├── STATIC → Array
         │    └── DYNAMIC → List, Stack, Queue, Deque
         └── NON-LINEAR
              ├── TREES → BST, AVL, Heap
              └── GRAPHS → Directed, Undirected, Weighted
```

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — ADT vs. Data Structure (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ADT | Data Structure |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Logical / mathematical model | Physical / concrete implementation |
| **Focus** | *What* operations are done | *How* operations are implemented |
| **Language Dependency** | Independent of any language | Tied to a specific language |
| **Example** | Stack ADT (push, pop, peek) | An array-based stack in C using a fixed array |
| **Hiding** | Hides implementation details | Exposes the chosen storage method |

**Step 2 — Three Levels of Data Representation (3 marks):**
1. **Abstract Level:** Defines the ADT logically. Example: *A Stack is a LIFO collection supporting push and pop.* No memory is allocated.
2. **Logical Level:** Chooses a data structure. Example: *Use an array of size 100 with an integer `top` initialized to $-1$.*
3. **Physical Level:** Actual memory layout. Example: *In RAM, the array occupies bytes 1000 to 1099, `top` is stored at address 2000, and the CPU reads/writes using base address + offset.*

**Step 3 — Example Flow (1 mark):** A *Compiler's Symbol Table* — at the abstract level, it is a Map ADT (key-value lookup); at the logical level, it is implemented as a Hash Table; at the physical level, it is a contiguous array of buckets with chained linked lists for collision resolution.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Definitions" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating ADT and Data Structure (–2 marks):** Many students write *"ADT and Data Structure are the same."* They are NOT. ADT is the *blueprint*; Data Structure is the *realized building*. The examiner will deduct at least 2 marks.
>
> 2. **Omitting Examples (–1 mark per question):** A definition without an example is considered *incomplete* in KTU valuation. Always pair definitions with at least one concrete example.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Neat Diagram (–1 mark):** For any "classify/explain" question, a labeled diagram is **mandatory** for full marks. Draw boxes with clear arrows — not paragraphs.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Mathematical Form (–1 mark):** When defining a data type or entity, examiners appreciate the $T = (D, O)$ or $E = D_1 \times D_2 \times \dots \times D_n$ notation. Omitting math costs you a mark.
>
> 5. **Wrong Units or Cardinalities in Memory Calculations (–1 mark):** Always state units (bytes, KB) and verify your domain cardinalities before multiplying.
>
> 6. **Writing Essays Instead of Crisp Definitions (–1 mark):** A 3-mark question expects ~5–6 lines, not a 2-page essay. Be precise.
>
> 7. **Forgetting LIFO/FIFO Acronyms (–1 mark):** For Stack and Queue, you **must** state the full form of the acronym first. *"LIFO = Last In, First Out"* is non-negotiable.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This and Read It the Night Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Data** = raw, unprocessed facts. **Information** = processed, meaningful data. The transformation is `Data → Algorithm → Information`.
- [ ] **Entity** = real-world object. **Attribute** = property of an entity. **Domain** = legal values of an attribute.
- [ ] **Group Item** is decomposable (e.g., Date). **Elementary Item** is atomic (e.g., Roll Number).
- [ ] **Data Object** = a named memory location holding a data item with a defined type, value, and domain.
- [ ] **Data Type** is a 2-tuple: $T = (D, O)$ where $D$ is the domain and $O$ is the set of operations.
- [ ] **ADT** is the *logical specification*; **Data Structure** is the *physical implementation*. They are **not** the same.
- [ ] **Classification Tree:** Primitive (int, float, char, pointer) → Non-Primitive → Linear (Array, List, Stack, Queue) or Non-Linear (Tree, Graph).
- [ ] **LIFO** = Last In, First Out (Stack). **FIFO** = First In, First Out (Queue).
- [ ] **Static DS** = fixed size, compile-time (Array). **Dynamic DS** = variable size, run-time (Linked List, Vector).
- [ ] **In-place algorithm** uses $O(1)$ extra memory. **Out-of-place** uses $O(n)$ or more.
- [ ] **Cartesian product formula:** For $k$ attributes with domain sizes $\lvert D_1 \rvert, \lvert D_2 \rvert, \dots, \lvert D_k \rvert$, the total combinations = $\prod_{i=1}^{k} \lvert D_i \rvert$.
- [ ] **Memory of a singly linked list** with $n$ nodes of size $s$ and pointer $p$ = $n \times (s + p)$ bytes.
- [ ] **Sequential traversal** of $n$ elements has recurrence $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$, solving to $T(n) = O(n)$.
- [ ] **Time-Space Tradeoff:** Faster access ↔ more memory. Compact storage ↔ more compute.
- [ ] **Three Levels of Representation:** Abstract (ADT) → Logical (Data Structure) → Physical (RAM layout).
- [ ] **CRUD Operations:** Create, Read, Update, Delete — the universal vocabulary of data manipulation.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Pro Tip:** Before submitting your answer script, re-read your definitions aloud. If any term can be replaced by a synonym without losing meaning, you have not yet mastered the formal definition. KTU examiners want the *exact* textbook phrasing, not your own paraphrase.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classifications of Data Structures - ADT and CDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classifications of Data Structures - ADT and CDT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Data Structure — Formal Definition
A **Data Structure** is a systematic way of organizing, storing, managing, and retrieving data in computer memory so that it can be accessed and manipulated efficiently according to the requirements of an algorithm. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, a data structure is defined as a mathematical/ logical model used to store, organize, and process data, along with the set of operations permitted on that data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize this verbatim):**
> *"A data structure is a particular way of storing and organizing data in a computer so that it can be used efficiently. It defines the relationship between data, the operations that can be performed on data, and the storage pattern."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Library Bookshelf
Imagine a **library**. If books are dumped in a pile on the floor, finding a specific book takes a long time. But if books are organized on **shelves by subject, then by author, then by serial number** (an *indexed* structure), retrieval becomes instant.
- The **books** = the **data**.
- The **shelves + numbering system** = the **data structure**.
- The librarian's actions (*issuing, returning, searching*) = the **operations**.

Just as the librarian chooses the right shelving strategy depending on the library's size and needs, a programmer chooses the right data structure depending on the problem's requirements.

### 1.3 Abstract Data Type (ADT) — Formal Definition
An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a theoretical mathematical model for data types in which a data type is defined by its **behavior (semantics)** from the point of view of a *user*, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations. Crucially, an ADT **does not specify how the data is stored in memory or how the operations are implemented** — it only specifies *what* operations exist and *what* they do.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** ADT = **What** the data type does, NOT **how** it does it. Think of it as a *black box* with a contract.

**Examples of ADTs:** Stack, Queue, List, Tree, Graph, Dictionary, Set.

### 1.4 Concrete Data Type (CDT) — Formal Definition
A **Concrete Data Type (CDT)** is the actual **implementation** of an Abstract Data Type. It specifies the *how* — how the data is laid out in memory (representation), and how each operation of the ADT is implemented algorithmically (in a specific programming language, with specific storage like arrays or linked nodes, and with a defined complexity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** CDT = **How** the data type is actually built. It is the *realization* of the ADT in a specific programming environment.

**Examples of CDTs:**
- ADT "List" can be implemented as: **Array-based List** (CDT) or **Linked List** (CDT).
- ADT "Stack" can be implemented as: **Array-based Stack** (CDT) or **Linked-List-based Stack** (CDT).

### 1.5 Why ADT vs CDT Matters in KTU
The KTU syllabus expects students to clearly distinguish the *logical* description (ADT) from the *physical* implementation (CDT). This separation is the foundation of **software engineering principles** like *encapsulation*, *modularity*, and *information hiding*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Data Structures — The KTU Taxonomy

Data structures are classified along **two principal dimensions** in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

#### Dimension A: By Data Type / Nature
- **Primitive Data Structures**
- **Non-Primitive Data Structures**

#### Dimension B: By Organization / Memory Layout
- **Linear Data Structures**
- **Non-Linear Data Structures**

#### Dimension C: By Size Behavior (Memory Allocation)
- **Static Data Structures**
- **Dynamic Data Structures**

#### Dimension D: By Access Pattern
- **Homogeneous** vs **Heterogeneous**
- **Direct Access**, **Sequential Access**, **Indexed Access**

---

### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Each Class

#### A. Primitive Data Structures
These are the **basic, built-in data types** directly supported by the programming language. They cannot be broken down into simpler types.
- **Examples:** `int`, `float`, `char`, `double`, `pointer`, `boolean`.
- **Storage:** Fixed size, known at compile-time.
- **Operations:** Arithmetic (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`), relational (`<`, `>`, `==`), logical (`&&`, `||`).

#### B. Non-Primitive Data Structures
These are **derived** from primitive types and can hold multiple values (often called *derived* or *user-defined*).
- **Sub-categories:**
  - **Linear:** Array, String, Stack, Queue, Linked List.
  - **Non-Linear:** Tree, Graph, Heap, Trie.
- **Operations:** Traversal, Insertion, Deletion, Searching, Sorting, Merging.

#### C. Linear vs Non-Linear

| Property | Linear | Non-Linear |
|---|---|---|
| Element arrangement | Sequential (one after another) | Hierarchical / networked |
| Traversal | Single level (one pass) | Multiple levels (recursive/iterative) |
| Memory | Contiguous (mostly) | Non-contiguous (linked) |
| Examples | Array, Stack, Queue | Tree, Graph, Heap |
| Time complexity of search (avg) | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ to $O(n)$ |

#### D. Static vs Dynamic

| Property | Static | Dynamic |
|---|---|---|
| Size at compile-time | Fixed | Variable |
| Memory allocation | Stack (compile-time) | Heap (run-time, e.g., `malloc`, `new`) |
| Resizing | Not possible without reallocation | Possible (insert/delete nodes) |
| Examples | Static array, struct | Linked list, dynamic array, tree |
| Speed | Faster (no allocation overhead) | Slower (allocation cost) |

### 2.3 Operations Common to Data Structures (KTU High-Yield)
Every non-primitive data structure supports a **canonical set of operations**:
1. **Traversal** — visiting every element exactly once.
2. **Search** — locating an element (linear search, binary search).
3. **Insertion** — adding a new element.
4. **Deletion** — removing an existing element.
5. **Sorting** — arranging elements in a defined order.
6. **Merging** — combining two structures into one.
7. **Update/Modify** — changing the value of an existing element.

### 2.4 The ADT Specification — The "Mathematical Contract"

An ADT is formally specified using **three components**:

$$\text{ADT} = \langle D, O, S \rangle$$

Where:
- $D$ = the set (domain) of data values the type can hold.
- $O$ = the set of operations defined on $D$.
- $S$ = the set of axioms (semantic rules / pre-conditions / post-conditions) that govern how operations behave.

**Example — ADT Specification of a Stack (KTU expected form):**

> **ADT Stack**
> - **Data:** A finite ordered sequence of elements $D = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$, $n \ge 0$.
> - **Operations:**
>   - `push(x, S)` — inserts $x$ at the top.
>   - `pop(S)` — removes and returns the top element.
>   - `peek(S)` / `top(S)` — returns the top element without removing.
>   - `isEmpty(S)` — returns `true` if $S$ is empty.
>   - `isFull(S)` — returns `true` if $S$ is at capacity (CDT-specific).
> - **Axioms (LIFO behavior):**
>   - If `push(x, S)` is followed by `pop(S)`, the result is $x$.
>   - `pop(S)` on an empty stack is an **underflow error**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the ADT specification **does not mention** whether the stack is implemented using an array or a linked list. That choice is the **CDT's** responsibility.

### 2.5 CDT — The Implementation Realization
Once the ADT is defined, the CDT picks a **representation strategy** and writes the actual code. For the ADT `Stack`:
- **CDT Option 1:** Array-based stack
  - Representation: a fixed-size array + an integer `top` index.
  - `push` at index `top+1`, `pop` returns `arr[top]` and decrements `top`.
  - Limitation: `isFull` may return `true` (overflow).
- **CDT Option 2:** Linked-list-based stack
  - Representation: a singly linked list with pointer to the head (top).
  - `push` inserts a new node at the head, `pop` removes the head.
  - Limitation: no `isFull`; limited only by heap memory.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Data Structure Metrics

| Metric | Notation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Size of structure | $n$ | Number of elements currently stored | All complexity formulas |
| Memory footprint | $M$ | Total bytes occupied (data + pointers + overhead) | Space complexity |
| Time complexity | $T(n)$ | Number of primitive operations in the worst case | Big-O analysis |
| Address of i-th element | $\text{addr}(A[i]) = \text{Base} + (i \times w)$ | Random access in array (CDT detail) | Array CDT |
| Node memory in linked list | $M_{\text{node}} = M_{\text{data}} + M_{\text{pointer}}$ | Per-node overhead | Linked CDT |
| Load factor (hash) | $\alpha = \frac{n}{m}$ | Ratio of elements to slots | Hash tables |
| Height of balanced tree | $h = O(\log n)$ | Logarithmic height | Trees |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The ADT/CDT separation is the **backbone of OOP** (Object-Oriented Programming). Classes in C++/Java/Python are *concrete implementations* of *abstract interfaces* — the same idea as ADT $\rightarrow$ CDT. This is why Java's `List` is an *interface* (ADT) and `ArrayList`/`LinkedList` are *classes* (CDTs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — ADT vs CDT (Full Comparison)

The KTU board **frequently** asks 14-mark questions comparing ADT and CDT. The following analytical derivation shows the precise logical relationship between the two.

Let $A$ be an Abstract Data Type and $C$ be its corresponding Concrete Data Type.

$$\text{ADT} \; A \xRightarrow{\text{Implementation}} \text{CDT} \; C$$

**Step 1: Define the abstraction layer.**
An ADT specifies the *signature* of each operation (name, parameters, return type) and the *semantic contract* (axioms).
- Example: Operation `push(x, S)` has signature `(element, Stack) $\rightarrow$ void` and semantic "After push, `peek(S) == x`".

**Step 2: Define the representation layer.**
The CDT chooses a *concrete storage* and binds each operation signature to a *concrete algorithm*.

$$\text{Storage}(C) \in \{\text{Array},\; \text{Linked Node},\; \text{Hybrid}\}$$

**Step 3: Derive the time complexity gap.**
For the same ADT, different CDTs yield different complexities. For the ADT "List" with operation `insertAtPosition(i, x)`:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ArrayList}}(n, i) &= O(n) \quad &\text{(shift elements right of } i \text{)} \\
T_{\text{LinkedList}}(n, i) &= O(i) \quad &\text{(traverse to } i \text{-th node, } i \le n) \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Logical conversion explained:**
- In the **array-based CDT**, all elements are stored contiguously. Inserting at position $i$ requires shifting $n - i$ elements to make a slot — this is a linear operation.
- In the **linked-list-based CDT**, we must walk through $i$ nodes sequentially to reach the insertion point — also linear, but with a *different* constant factor and *no shifting*.

**Step 4: Derive the space complexity gap.**
For $n$ elements of size $s$ bytes each, with pointer size $p$ bytes:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Array CDT}} &= n \times s + C_1 \quad &\text{(just the data, } C_1 = \text{header overhead)} \\
S_{\text{Linked CDT}} &= n \times (s + p) + C_2 \quad &\text{(data + per-node pointer)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** Each linked-list node stores *both* the user's data and one (or two) pointer(s) to the next/previous node. Therefore the linked-list CDT always uses **more memory per element** than the array CDT.

**Step 5: Quantify the trade-off.**
The ratio of linked-CDT space to array-CDT space:

$$R = \frac{S_{\text{Linked CDT}}}{S_{\text{Array CDT}}} = \frac{n(s + p) + C_2}{n \cdot s + C_1} \approx 1 + \frac{p}{s} \quad \text{as } n \to \infty$$

For typical values $s = 4$ bytes (int) and $p = 8$ bytes (64-bit pointer):

$$R \approx 1 + \frac{8}{4} = 3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of derivation:** A linked-list CDT uses **3× the memory** of an array CDT for `int` data on a 64-bit system. This is the engineering reason for choosing an *array CDT* when memory is tight, and a *linked CDT* when frequent insertions/deletions dominate.

### 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — Height of a Balanced Tree CDT

Suppose an ADT "Balanced Binary Search Tree" is implemented as a CDT (e.g., AVL tree). For $n$ nodes:

$$h = O(\log_2 n)$$

**Derivation:**
Let $N(h)$ = minimum number of nodes in a balanced binary tree of height $h$.

$$\begin{aligned}
N(0) &= 1 \quad &\text{(empty or single-node tree)} \\
N(1) &= 2 \quad &\text{(root + one child)} \\
N(h) &= 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2) \quad &\text{(root + both subtrees)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Solving this recurrence yields $N(h) \ge 2^{h/2}$, which gives:

$$h \le 2 \log_2 N \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = O(\log n)$$

This is the canonical reason balanced trees offer $O(\log n)$ search — a direct property of the *CDT implementation* that the *ADT* simply promises.

### 3.3 Summary Analytical Table

| Question | ADT Answer | CDT Answer |
|---|---|---|
| *What is it?* | Logical/mathematical model | Actual implementation in code |
| *What does it specify?* | Behavior & operations (the "what") | Storage layout & algorithms (the "how") |
| *Is it language-specific?* | No (language-independent) | Yes (e.g., C/C++/Java) |
| *Memory layout defined?* | No | Yes |
| *Complexity defined?* | Specifies operation *behaviors* | Specifies *exact* time/space complexity |
| *Example* | "Stack" (LIFO behavior) | "ArrayStack with size 100" |
| *User sees?* | Only the interface | Interface + internals (if exposed) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Tree of Data Structures (KTU Taxonomy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Data Structures"]:::main
    prim["Primitive Data Structures"]:::node1
    nonprim["Non-Primitive Data Structures"]:::node1

    intT["int float char pointer"]:::leaf
    lin["Linear DS"]:::node2
    nlin["Non-Linear DS"]:::node2

    arr["Array"]:::leaf
    stk["Stack"]:::leaf
    que["Queue"]:::leaf
    lst["Linked List"]:::leaf
    str["String"]:::leaf

    tree["Tree"]:::leaf
    graph["Graph"]:::leaf
    heap["Heap"]:::leaf
    trie["Trie"]:::leaf

    rootA --> prim
    rootA --> nonprim
    prim --> intT

    nonprim --> lin
    nonprim --> nlin

    lin --> arr
    lin --> stk
    lin --> que
    lin --> lst
    lin --> str

    nlin --> tree
    nlin --> graph
    nlin --> heap
    nlin --> trie

    classDef main fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef node1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#ffffff
    classDef node2 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The root `Data Structures` branches into `Primitive` and `Non-Primitive`. The non-primitive branch is further split by **organization** into `Linear` (sequential) and `Non-Linear` (hierarchical).

---

### 4.2 ADT vs CDT — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    userA["USER / CLIENT"]:::actor
    adtLayer["ADT LAYER Logical Contract"]:::layer1
    cdtLayer["CDT LAYER Physical Implementation"]:::layer2
    memA["Array Storage"]:::mem
    memL["Linked Node Storage"]:::mem
    memT["Tree Node Storage"]:::mem

    op1["push x"]:::op
    op2["pop"]:::op
    op3["peek"]:::op

    userA -- "calls operation" --> adtLayer
    adtLayer -- "delegates to" --> cdtLayer
    cdtLayer -- "uses" --> memA
    cdtLayer -- "uses" --> memL
    cdtLayer -- "uses" --> memT

    adtLayer --- op1
    adtLayer --- op2
    adtLayer --- op3

    classDef actor fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    classDef layer1 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#a04411,color:#000000
    classDef layer2 fill:#ffc000,stroke:#a07700,color:#000000
    classDef mem fill:#70ad47,stroke:#385723,color:#ffffff
    classDef op fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#385723,color:#000000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The user interacts only with the **ADT layer** (the contract: *what* the operations do). The **CDT layer** sits *below* the ADT, hidden from the user, and chooses *how* to store the data. Multiple CDTs (array, linked, tree) can implement the *same* ADT without the user noticing.

---

### 4.3 Memory Layout of Static Array CDT vs Linked List CDT

```mermaid
graph TD
    arrTitle["ARRAY CDT Contiguous Memory"]:::title
    a0["A0 100"]:::cell
    a1["A1 200"]:::cell
    a2["A2 300"]:::cell
    a3["A3 400"]:::cell
    arrTitle --> a0
    a0 --> a1
    a1 --> a2
    a2 --> a3

    listTitle["LINKED LIST CDT Scattered Memory"]:::title
    n1["Node 1 Data 100 Next 0x4A"]:::cellL
    n2["Node 2 Data 200 Next 0x9C"]:::cellL
    n3["Node 3 Data 300 Next 0x12"]:::cellL
    n4["Node 4 Data 400 Next NULL"]:::cellL
    listTitle --> n1
    n1 -- "pointer 0x4A" --> n2
    n2 -- "pointer 0x9C" --> n3
    n3 -- "pointer 0x12" --> n4

    classDef title fill:#7030a0,stroke:#3a1058,color:#ffffff
    classDef cell fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#000000
    classDef cellL fill:#f4b183,stroke:#7f3a00,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In the **array CDT**, all elements are stored side-by-side in contiguous memory, allowing $O(1)$ random access via `Base + i × w`. In the **linked list CDT**, each node is scattered in memory, and traversal follows pointer links — yielding $O(n)$ access but $O(1)$ insertion at a known position.

---

### 4.4 ADT-to-CDT Mapping Matrix (Block View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    adtList["ADT LIST"]:::adt
    adtStack["ADT STACK"]:::adt
    adtQueue["ADT QUEUE"]:::adt
    adtTree["ADT TREE"]:::adt

    cdtAL["CDT ArrayList"]:::cdt
    cdtLL["CDT LinkedList"]:::cdt
    cdtAS["CDT ArrayStack"]:::cdt
    cdtLS["CDT LinkedStack"]:::cdt
    cdtAQ["CDT ArrayQueue"]:::cdt
    cdtLQ["CDT LinkedQueue"]:::cdt
    cdtBT["CDT BinaryTree"]:::cdt
    cdtAVL["CDT AVLTree"]:::cdt

    adtList --> cdtAL
    adtList --> cdtLL
    adtStack --> cdtAS
    adtStack --> cdtLS
    adtQueue --> cdtAQ
    adtQueue --> cdtLQ
    adtTree --> cdtBT
    adtTree --> cdtAVL

    classDef adt fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#7f3a00,color:#000000
    classDef cdt fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#385f8a,color:#000000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** The above diagram is the *exact mental model* KTU examiners expect when they ask "Explain how the same ADT can be implemented using different CDTs." One ADT $\rightarrow$ Many CDTs is the cornerstone of *polymorphism* in OOP.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Abstract Data Type (ADT). List any two advantages of using ADTs in software design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its *behavior* (semantics) in terms of possible values, possible operations, and the behavior of those operations — *without specifying the underlying implementation* (storage or algorithm details).

**Two advantages:**
1. **Modularity / Information Hiding:** The user is shielded from internal implementation details, so the implementation can be changed (e.g., from array to linked list) without affecting the client code.
2. **Reusability:** The same ADT specification can be implemented in multiple ways (CDTs) and reused across different programs and languages.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between primitive and non-primitive data structures with two examples each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Primitive | Non-Primitive |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Basic, built-in types directly supported by the language | Derived types that aggregate primitive or other non-primitive types |
| Size | Fixed at compile-time | Can be variable (static or dynamic) |
| Examples | `int`, `float`, `char`, `pointer` | Array, Stack, Tree, Linked List |
| Operations | Arithmetic, relational | Traversal, insertion, deletion, search |

Examples: `int` and `char` (primitive); Array and Linked List (non-primitive).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Define an Abstract Data Type (ADT). Explain with a suitable example how an ADT differs from a Concrete Data Type (CDT). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of ADT (2 marks):**
An Abstract Data Type (ADT) is a high-level, implementation-independent description of a data type that specifies:
- The set of data values it can hold (the *domain*).
- The set of operations that can be performed on the data.
- The *semantic behavior* (axioms / pre and post conditions) of each operation.

The ADT **does NOT specify** how the data is stored or how operations are implemented algorithmically.

**Definition of CDT (2 marks):**
A Concrete Data Type (CDT) is the *actual implementation* of an ADT in a specific programming language, using a specific storage representation (array, linked nodes, etc.) and well-defined algorithms for each operation. The CDT specifies the *how* — the physical storage layout and the time/space complexity.

**Example — Stack (3 marks):**
- **ADT Stack:** A linear structure with LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) behavior. Operations: `push(x, S)`, `pop(S)`, `peek(S)`, `isEmpty(S)`. Axiom: the element returned by `pop` is the most recently `push`-ed element.
- **CDT Option 1 (Array-based):** Uses a fixed-size array `arr[N]` and an integer `top` initialized to $-1$. `push` does `arr[++top] = x`. Complexity: $O(1)$.
- **CDT Option 2 (Linked-list-based):** Uses nodes with `data` and `next` pointers, with `head` as the top. `push` inserts at head. Complexity: $O(1)$ time but $O(n)$ extra memory for pointers.

**Conclusion:** The ADT tells the *user* that a Stack supports LIFO operations; the CDT tells the *implementer* exactly which storage and algorithm to use.

---

**(b)** Classify data structures into various categories with a neat diagram. Explain any two classification schemes in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification Diagram (3 marks):**
*(Refer to the Mermaid tree diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes — Data Structures $\rightarrow$ Primitive / Non-Primitive $\rightarrow$ Linear / Non-Linear, etc.)*

**Two Classification Schemes Explained (4 marks — 2 each):**

**Scheme 1: Linear vs Non-Linear**
- **Linear Data Structures:** Elements are arranged in a *sequential* order. Each element has a unique predecessor and successor (except first and last). Traversal is single-level.
  - Examples: Array, Stack, Queue, Linked List.
  - Operations are typically $O(n)$ in time for search/traversal.
- **Non-Linear Data Structures:** Elements are arranged in a *hierarchical* or *networked* manner. An element may have multiple predecessors and successors.
  - Examples: Tree, Graph, Heap.
  - Search in balanced trees is $O(\log n)$; graph traversal is $O(V + E)$.

**Scheme 2: Static vs Dynamic**
- **Static Data Structures:** Size is fixed at compile-time; memory is allocated on the stack or in the data segment.
  - Examples: Static array, struct.
  - **Pros:** Faster access, no allocation overhead, predictable memory.
  - **Cons:** Cannot grow/shrink; potential memory wastage.
- **Dynamic Data Structures:** Size varies at run-time; memory is allocated on the heap.
  - Examples: Linked list, dynamic array (vector), tree, graph.
  - **Pros:** Flexible sizing, efficient insert/delete.
  - **Cons:** Allocation overhead, potential fragmentation, slightly slower access.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** What is meant by a Concrete Data Type (CDT)? Explain with the example of a Queue ADT implemented in two different ways. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of CDT (2 marks):**
A **Concrete Data Type (CDT)** is the specific implementation of an ADT. It defines:
- The physical *storage representation* (array, linked nodes, hybrid).
- The *algorithm* for each ADT operation, including its time and space complexity.
- Any implementation-specific constraints (e.g., capacity limits for arrays).

**ADT Queue — Specification (1 mark):**
A Queue is a linear ADT with **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) behavior.
- **Operations:** `enqueue(x, Q)`, `dequeue(Q)`, `front(Q)`, `isEmpty(Q)`, `isFull(Q)`.
- **Axiom:** The element returned by `dequeue` is the one that was `enqueue`-ed earliest.

**CDT 1 — Array-based Circular Queue (2 marks):**
- **Storage:** A fixed-size array `Q[N]` and two integer indices `front` and `rear`.
- **enqueue:** Place element at `Q[rear]` and advance `rear = (rear + 1) \mod N`.
- **dequeue:** Return `Q[front]` and advance `front = (front + 1) \mod N`.
- **Time complexity:** Both operations are $O(1)$.
- **Limitation:** Capacity is fixed; `isFull` triggers overflow.

**CDT 2 — Linked-list-based Queue (2 marks):**
- **Storage:** A singly/doubly linked list with pointers to `head` (front) and `tail` (rear).
- **enqueue:** Append a new node at the `tail` pointer — $O(1)$.
- **dequeue:** Remove the `head` node — $O(1)$.
- **Time complexity:** $O(1)$ for both, but with extra memory per node (pointer overhead).
- **Limitation:** No overflow until heap is exhausted.

---

**(b)** Discuss the importance of separating the ADT from its implementation (CDT) in software engineering. Illustrate with a real-world example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Importance of ADT/CDT Separation (4 marks):**

1. **Encapsulation and Information Hiding:** The user knows only the *interface* (the ADT's operations and their contracts). Internal implementation details are hidden. This reduces coupling between modules.
2. **Flexibility in Implementation:** The same ADT can have multiple CDTs (e.g., ArrayList vs LinkedList for ADT List). The client code does not change when the implementation switches — only the CDT does.
3. **Maintainability:** Bugs or performance bottlenecks in one CDT can be fixed without rewriting the entire application.
4. **Reusability:** A well-defined ADT can be reused across many projects and many programming languages.
5. **Team Productivity:** Different team members can work on the ADT specification and the CDT implementation in parallel.

**Real-World Example (3 marks):**
Consider a **navigation application** (like Google Maps) that needs a structure to store frequently searched routes. The *ADT* is a **Priority Queue** (operations: `insert(route, priority)`, `extractMin()`, `peek()`). 
- For a small in-memory map, the CDT could be a **binary heap (array-based)** — $O(\log n)$ operations, low memory.
- For a server handling millions of concurrent requests, the CDT could be a **Fibonacci heap (linked)** — amortized $O(1)$ insert.

The application's *high-level code* (the part that uses `insert` and `extractMin`) remains *unchanged* in both cases. The choice of CDT is an internal optimization, demonstrating the power of separating the *abstract* specification from the *concrete* implementation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write only "ADT is a model, CDT is implementation" without an *example*. Always pair definitions with a *concrete example* (Stack, Queue, List).
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse **data type** with **data structure**. A *data type* is a classification of data; a *data structure* is the actual storage layout and operations. KTU expects this distinction.
> 3. **Do NOT** write code when the question asks for *classification* or *comparison*. Code is asked only when the question explicitly says "implement" or "write an algorithm."
> 4. **Always draw a diagram** when a question carries 7+ marks. A neat classification tree or memory layout diagram fetches at least 1–2 extra marks.
> 5. In 14-mark questions, **state the time/space complexity** of any CDT example — this is a high-yield KTU differentiator.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Data Structure = Storage scheme + Operations on data.**
- [ ] **Classification Axes:** Primitive/Non-Primitive, Linear/Non-Linear, Static/Dynamic, Homogeneous/Heterogeneous.
- [ ] **ADT = Abstract Data Type** = the *logical* / *behavioral* model (the "what"). It is **language-independent** and specifies only the operations, signatures, and semantic axioms.
- [ ] **CDT = Concrete Data Type** = the *physical* / *implementation* model (the "how"). It is **language-specific** and specifies the storage layout and the actual algorithm with time/space complexity.
- [ ] **One ADT can have many CDTs.** Example: ADT List $\rightarrow$ ArrayList CDT, LinkedList CDT, DoublyLinkedList CDT.
- [ ] **Canonical operations on any non-primitive DS:** Traversal, Search, Insertion, Deletion, Sorting, Merging, Update.
- [ ] **ADT specification uses the triple** $\langle D, O, S \rangle$ where $D$ = data, $O$ = operations, $S$ = semantics/axioms.
- [ ] **Static DS = fixed size, compile-time allocation (stack/data segment).** Examples: static array, struct.
- [ ] **Dynamic DS = variable size, run-time allocation (heap).** Examples: linked list, tree, dynamic array.
- [ ] **Linear DS = sequential layout; Non-Linear DS = hierarchical/networked layout.**
- [ ] **Engineering principle:** ADT/CDT separation = foundation of OOP, encapsulation, information hiding, and polymorphism.
- [ ] **Trade-off rule of thumb:** Array CDT is faster for *access*; Linked CDT is faster for *insertion/deletion at known positions*; Tree CDT is best for *hierarchical/search* operations.
- [ ] **KTU board expects:** definition + 2 examples + a diagram in every 7-mark question on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear and Non-Linear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Linear and Non-Linear ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Data Structure** is a specialized format for organizing, processing, retrieving, and storing data in memory so that it can be accessed and worked with efficiently. Data structures are broadly classified into two fundamental categories based on the **arrangement of data elements** and the **relationship (linear vs. hierarchical/network) between them**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Box** > > **Linear Data Structure:** A data structure in which data elements are arranged in a **sequential order**, and each element is connected to its **previous** and **next** element in a single level. Traversal occurs in **one single run** (single level). > > **Non-Linear Data Structure:** A data structure in which data elements are arranged in a **hierarchical** or **non-sequential** manner, where one element can be connected to **multiple** (many) elements at different levels. Traversal does **not** occur in a single run. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **train** 🚌 vs. an **airline route network map** ✈️: | Analogy | Maps To | |---|---| | A **train** with coaches connected in a line (Engine → Coach A → Coach B → ... → Last Coach). You can only go forward or backward, one coach at a time. | **Linear Data Structure** (Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue) | | An **airline route map** where one city (e.g., Delhi) connects to multiple cities (Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata), and each of those connects to even more cities. You can have multiple paths and levels. | **Non-Linear Data Structure** (Trees, Graphs, Heaps, Tries) | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick for Exams:** If you can draw the structure as a **straight line** → it is **Linear**. If you need to draw **branches, levels, or cycles** → it is **Non-Linear**. ### 1.3 Classification Chart of Data Structures Data Structures ├── **Primitive** (int, float, char, boolean) └── **Non-Primitive** ├── **Linear** │ ├── Static: Array │ └── Dynamic: Linked List, Stack, Queue └── **Non-Linear** ├── Trees (Binary Tree, BST, AVL, Heap, B-Tree, Trie) └── Graphs (Directed, Undirected, Weighted) ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics In data structures, the key performance metrics (all in **bold** as per KTU convention) are: - **Time Complexity** — measured in Big-O notation (e.g., $O(1)$, $O(\log n)$, $O(n)$) - **Space Complexity** — auxiliary memory consumed, measured in bytes/words - **n** — number of elements stored in the structure ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Characteristics of Linear Data Structures 1. **Sequential Organization:** Elements form a sequence / linear order. 2. **Single-Level Traversal:** We can traverse all elements in a **single run** from the first element to the last. 3. **Single Successor / Predecessor:** Each element (except the first and last) has exactly **one predecessor** and **one successor**. 4. **Predictable Memory Layout:** Either contiguous (Array) or pointer-based (Linked List). 5. **Easy Implementation:** Simpler algorithms for insertion, deletion, and searching. **Examples:** Arrays, Linked Lists (Singly, Doubly, Circular), Stacks, Queues, Deques. ### 2.2 Characteristics of Non-Linear Data Structures 1. **Hierarchical / Network Organization:** Elements are arranged in **levels** (trees) or as an arbitrary **network** (graphs). 2. **Multi-Level Traversal:** Traversal requires **multiple runs / levels** (e.g., Level Order, DFS, BFS). 3. **Multiple Successors:** One element (parent / node) can be connected to **two or more** elements (children / neighbors). 4. **No Single Starting Point (in graphs):** A graph may have multiple starting points; trees have exactly one root. 5. **Complex Implementation:** Requires recursion, pointers, or adjacency representations. **Examples:** Binary Tree, Binary Search Tree (BST), AVL Tree, Heap (Min/Max), B-Tree, Trie, Graph (Directed, Undirected, Weighted). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet | Property | Linear Data Structure | Non-Linear Data Structure | |---|---|---| | **Arrangement** | Sequential (one level) | Hierarchical / Network (multi-level) | | **Traversal** | Single level run | Multi-level run | | **Relationships** | One-to-one (1:1) | One-to-many (1:N) or Many-to-many (M:N) | | **Implementation Difficulty** | Easy to Moderate | Moderate to Complex | | **Memory Utilization** | Sometimes wastes memory (arrays) or uses extra pointers (linked list) | Efficient for hierarchical data | | **Examples** | Array, Stack, Queue, Linked List | Tree, Graph, Heap, Trie | | **Best Used For** | Simple, ordered data | Complex, relational, hierarchical data | | **Search Efficiency** | Linear Search: $O(n)$; Binary Search: $O(\log n)$ | BST/AVL Search: $O(\log n)$; Graph Search: $O(V+E)$ | | **Real-World Use** | Buffers, CPU scheduling, undo operations | File systems, databases, social networks, maps | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention **"single level run"** for linear and **"multi-level run"** for non-linear — this is the exact phrasing used in KTU answer keys for Module 1. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Linear Structure Used | Non-Linear Structure Used | |---|---|---| | **Operating Systems** | Stack (function calls, recursion), Queue (CPU scheduling) | B-Tree (file systems), Graph (resource allocation) | | **Databases** | — | B-Tree / B+ Tree indexing | | **Compilers** | Stack (parsing, expression evaluation) | Syntax Tree (AST), DAG | | **Networking** | Queue (packet buffering) | Graph (routing algorithms like Dijkstra) | | **AI / ML** | Queue (BFS) | Decision Tree, Graph (neural networks) | | **Social Media** | — | Graph (friends network, recommendations) | | **Web Browsers** | Stack (back button) | Tree (DOM), Trie (URL autocomplete) | ### 2.5 Operations Supported by Data Structures The four fundamental operations that any data structure must support (and KTU frequently asks about) are: 1. **Traversal** — visiting every element exactly once 2. **Insertion** — adding a new element 3. **Deletion** — removing an existing element 4. **Searching** — finding the location of an element For **Stack** specifically: push, pop, peek, isEmpty. For **Queue** specifically: enqueue, dequeue, front, rear, isEmpty. ## 3.
