Image processing (pixels stored in row-major 2-D arrays), scientific computing (matrices for FEM/FDM solvers), and database systems (columnar storage in analytics) all depend on the *order* in which elements are stored in memory — which is precisely row-major vs column-major. A wrong formula leads to **cache misses, wrong output, or segmentation faults** in production code.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Row-Major Address of 1-D Array

**Problem:** Consider a 1-D array `A[10]` of `int` elements. `int` is **4 bytes**. Base address `B = 2000`. Find the address of `A[5]`.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\text{Address}(A[i]) = B + i \times w
$$

Substitute `i = 5`, `B = 2000`, `w = 4`:

$$
\text{Address}(A[5]) = 2000 + 5 \times 4
$$

$$
= 2000 + 20 = 2020
$$

**Verification:** `A[0]` is at 2000, `A[1]` is at 2004, …, `A[5]` is at `2000 + 5*4 = 2020`. ✓

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: 2-D Array, Row-Major Order

**Problem:** A 2-D array `A[3][4]` of `int` (4 bytes each) is stored in **Row-Major Order** starting at base address `B = 1000`. Find the address of `A[2][3]`.

Here `m = 3` (rows), `n = 4` (columns), `w = 4`, `B = 1000`, `i = 2`, `j = 3`.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$
\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + (i \times n + j) \times w
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
= 1000 + (2 \times 4 + 3) \times 4
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate inside the brackets first (PEMDAS/BODMAS):**

$$
= 1000 + (8 + 3) \times 4
$$

$$
= 1000 + 11 \times 4
$$

**Step 4 — Final multiplication and addition:**

$$
= 1000 + 44 = 1044
$$

**Answer:** `Address(A[2][3]) = 1044`.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: 2-D Array, Column-Major Order

**Problem:** Same array `A[3][4]` of `int` (4 bytes), same `B = 1000`. Now it is stored in **Column-Major Order**. Find the address of `A[2][3]`.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$
\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + (j \times m + i) \times w
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
= 1000 + (3 \times 3 + 2) \times 4
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$
= 1000 + (9 + 2) \times 4
$$

$$
= 1000 + 11 \times 4 = 1000 + 44 = 1044
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interesting observation:** In this *particular* square-style problem, the address came out the same (1044) because `A[2][3]` happens to be the last element in both row-major and column-major for a 3×4 array. **This is a coincidence — in general, RMO and CMO give different addresses.** Students often lose marks by not carefully reading whether the array is row-major or column-major.

---

### 3.4 Worked Derivation #4: Generalized Lower Bound Case

**Problem:** A 2-D array `X[5..8][3..7]` of `float` (size = 4 bytes) is stored in **Row-Major Order** with `B = 3000`. Find the address of `X[7][6]`.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- `lb1 = 5`, `ub1 = 8` (rows)
- `lb2 = 3`, `ub2 = 7` (columns)
- `m = ub1 - lb1 + 1 = 8 - 5 + 1 = 4` rows
- `n = ub2 - lb2 + 1 = 7 - 3 + 1 = 5` columns
- `i = 7`, `j = 6`, `w = 4`, `B = 3000`

**Step 2 — Apply the generalized formula:**

$$
\text{Address}(X[i][j]) = B + \left[(i - lb_1) \times n + (j - lb_2)\right] \times w
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
= 3000 + \left[(7 - 5) \times 5 + (6 - 3)\right] \times 4
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate inner terms:**

$$
= 3000 + \left[2 \times 5 + 3\right] \times 4
$$

$$
= 3000 + \left[10 + 3\right] \times 4
$$

$$
= 3000 + 13 \times 4
$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**

$$
= 3000 + 52 = 3052
$$

**Answer:** `Address(X[7][6]) = 3052`.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

> [!TIP]
> **The following table compares the *same* logical element under both storage orders. Use it as a quick "what changes" reference for last-minute revision.**

| Logical Element | Row-Major Address (RMO) | Column-Major Address (CMO) | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| `A[0][0]` | $B$ | $B$ | $0$ |
| `A[0][1]` | $B + 1 \cdot w$ | $B + 1 \cdot m \cdot w$ | $(m-1) \cdot w$ |
| `A[1][0]` | $B + 1 \cdot n \cdot w$ | $B + 1 \cdot w$ | $(n-1) \cdot w$ |
| `A[i][j]` | $B + (in + j)w$ | $B + (jm + i)w$ | $w \cdot [in + j - jm - i] = w(i(n-1) - j(m-1))$ |

> **Key Insight from the Difference Row:** The address gap between RMO and CMO grows as the array gets larger. For very large matrices (e.g., in deep learning), this choice *directly affects CPU cache performance* — RMO is cache-friendlier for row-wise traversal, CMO is better for column-wise.

---

### 3.6 Proof of n-Dimensional Array Formula (Row-Major)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This proof appears in many KTU 14-mark questions. Practice writing it.**

**Claim:** For an n-D array `A[d_1][d_2] \dots [d_n]` in row-major order, the address of `A[i_1][i_2]\dots[i_n]` is:

$$
\text{Address} = B + w \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left( i_k \cdot \prod_{l=k+1}^{n} d_l \right)
$$

**Proof Sketch (Inductive on number of dimensions):**

**Base Case (n = 1):** The formula reduces to `B + i_1 · w`. This is just the 1-D formula — ✓

**Inductive Hypothesis:** Assume the formula holds for n − 1 dimensions.

**Inductive Step:** Treat the n-D array as a 1-D array of `(n-1)`-D sub-arrays. There are `d_1` such sub-arrays, and each has size equal to the number of elements in an `(n-1)`-D array of dimensions `d_2, d_3, …, d_n`.

**Number of elements per sub-array:**

$$
\prod_{l=2}^{n} d_l
$$

**Offset of the `i_1`-th sub-array:**

$$
i_1 \cdot \prod_{l=2}^{n} d_l
$$

**Within the chosen sub-array** (apply inductive hypothesis, with `B` replaced by the sub-array's base address):

$$
\sum_{k=2}^{n} \left( i_k \cdot \prod_{l=k+1}^{n} d_l \right)
$$

**Total offset** (combine both parts):

$$
i_1 \cdot \prod_{l=2}^{n} d_l + \sum_{k=2}^{n} \left( i_k \cdot \prod_{l=k+1}^{n} d_l \right) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left( i_k \cdot \prod_{l=k+1}^{n} d_l \right)
$$

Multiplying by `w` and adding `B` proves the claim. ∎
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Logical vs Physical Representation of a 2-D Array

This diagram illustrates the conceptual gap between the *matrix* a programmer sees and the *linear memory* the hardware actually uses.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LP["LOGICAL VIEW MATRIX 3 ROWS BY 4 COLUMNS"]
        direction TB
        A00["A 0 0"] --- A01["A 0 1"] --- A02["A 0 2"] --- A03["A 0 3"]
        A10["A 1 0"] --- A11["A 1 1"] --- A12["A 1 2"] --- A13["A 1 3"]
        A20["A 2 0"] --- A21["A 2 1"] --- A22["A 2 2"] --- A23["A 2 3"]
    end

    subgraph RMO["PHYSICAL RAM ROW MAJOR ORDER"]
        direction LR
        R0["A00"] --> R1["A01"] --> R2["A02"] --> R3["A03"]
        R3 --> R4["A10"] --> R5["A11"] --> R6["A12"] --> R7["A13"]
        R7 --> R8["A20"] --> R9["A21"] --> R10["A22"] --> R11["A23"]
    end

    subgraph CMO["PHYSICAL RAM COLUMN MAJOR ORDER"]
        direction LR
        C0["A00"] --> C1["A10"] --> C2["A20"] --> C3["A01"]
        C3 --> C4["A11"] --> C5["A21"] --> C6["A02"] --> C7["A12"]
        C7 --> C8["A22"] --> C9["A03"] --> C10["A13"] --> C11["A23"]
    end

    LP -.maps to.-> RMO
    LP -.maps to.-> CMO
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Address Calculation Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

This block diagram explains *how* the CPU computes the address of an array element at runtime.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN["Input Index Values i and j"] --> M1["Step 1: Compute Effective Index"]
    M1 --> M2["Step 2: Subtract Lower Bound if any"]
    M2 --> M3["Step 3: Multiply by Stride n in RMO or m in CMO"]
    M3 --> M4["Step 4: Add Inner Index j in RMO or i in CMO"]
    M4 --> M5["Step 5: Multiply Total Offset by Word Size w"]
    M5 --> M6["Step 6: Add Base Address B"]
    M6 --> OUT["Output Physical Memory Address of A i j"]

    style IN fill:#fef3c7
    style OUT fill:#dcfce7
    style M1 fill:#dbeafe
    style M2 fill:#dbeafe
    style M3 fill:#dbeafe
    style M4 fill:#dbeafe
    style M5 fill:#dbeafe
    style M6 fill:#dbeafe
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Memory Layout of a 1-D Array

This diagram shows how a 1-D array of 5 integers (each 4 bytes) occupies contiguous memory starting at base address 1000.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAM["MAIN MEMORY BYTE ADDRESSES"]
        direction LR
        MEM0["1000 1001 1002 1003  STORE A0"] --> MEM1["1004 1005 1006 1007  STORE A1"]
        MEM1 --> MEM2["1008 1009 1010 1011  STORE A2"]
        MEM2 --> MEM3["1012 1013 1014 1015  STORE A3"]
        MEM3 --> MEM4["1016 1017 1018 1019  STORE A4"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is **4 bytes wide** (the size of an `int`). The arrows show that memory is *one long street* — there is no "row" or "column" in physical RAM. The grid structure of 2-D arrays is purely a *programmer's abstraction* maintained by the compiler.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Decision Tree — Which Formula to Use?

This flowchart helps students pick the correct formula at a glance during exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the array 1D or 2D"} --> Q1A["Use B plus i times w"]
    Q1 --> Q2["Go to 2D branch"]

    Q2{"Which storage order"} --> Q2A["ROW MAJOR: B plus i times n plus j times w"]
    Q2 --> Q2B["COLUMN MAJOR: B plus j times m plus i times w"]

    Q2A --> Q3{"Is lower bound zero or non zero"}
    Q2B --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q3A["Use raw i and j directly"]
    Q3 --> Q3B["Subtract lower bounds first: i minus lb1 and j minus lb2"]

    style Q1A fill:#dcfce7
    style Q2A fill:#dbeafe
    style Q2B fill:#fee2e2
    style Q3A fill:#dcfce7
    style Q3B fill:#fef3c7
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an array. List any four characteristics of an array data structure.**

**Model Answer:**

An array is a **linear, homogeneous, contiguous, fixed-size** data structure that stores a collection of elements of the same data type in sequential memory locations, accessible using indices.

**Four Characteristics:**

1. **Homogeneity:** All elements must be of the same data type (e.g., all `int` or all `float`).
2. **Contiguous Memory Allocation:** Elements are stored in adjacent memory locations.
3. **Fixed Size:** The size of the array is determined at compile time and cannot be changed at runtime in C.
4. **Random Access / Direct Access:** Any element `A[i]` can be accessed in **O(1)** time using its index, regardless of its position.
5. *(Bonus)* **Static Memory:** Memory is allocated on the stack (for local arrays) or in the data segment (for global arrays) at compile time.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Row-Major Order and Column-Major Order storage of a 2-D array.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Row-Major Order (RMO) | Column-Major Order (CMO) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Elements are stored **row by row**. The entire first row is placed first, then the second row, and so on. | Elements are stored **column by column**. The entire first column is placed first, then the second column, and so on. |
| 2 | Address formula: $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (i \cdot n + j) \cdot w$ | Address formula: $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (j \cdot m + i) \cdot w$ |
| 3 | Used by **C, C++, Java, Python (NumPy)**. | Used by **Fortran, MATLAB, R**. |
| 4 | Cache-friendly for **row-wise traversal** of matrices. | Cache-friendly for **column-wise traversal** of matrices. |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Choice A]**

**(a)** *A 2-D array `A[10][15]` is stored in **Row-Major Order** in the main memory. Each element occupies **4 bytes**. If the base address of the array is `2000`, find the address of `A[7][9]`. Also, what would the address be if the array were stored in **Column-Major Order**? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** *Derive the general address calculation formula for a 2-D array stored in Row-Major Order with non-zero lower bounds. Apply it to find the address of `A[4][3]` in an array declared as `int A[-3..4][-2..5]` stored at base address `4000`, with `w = 4` bytes. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Given:** `m = 10` rows, `n = 15` columns, `w = 4` bytes, `B = 2000`, `i = 7`, `j = 9`.

**Case 1: Row-Major Order**

$$
\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + (i \times n + j) \times w
$$

$$
= 2000 + (7 \times 15 + 9) \times 4
$$

$$
= 2000 + (105 + 9) \times 4
$$

$$
= 2000 + 114 \times 4 = 2000 + 456 = 2456
$$

**Answer (RMO):** `Address(A[7][9]) = 2456`.

**Case 2: Column-Major Order**

$$
\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + (j \times m + i) \times w
$$

$$
= 2000 + (9 \times 10 + 7) \times 4
$$

$$
= 2000 + (90 + 7) \times 4
$$

$$
= 2000 + 97 \times 4 = 2000 + 388 = 2388
$$

**Answer (CMO):** `Address(A[7][9]) = 2388`.

> The two addresses differ by `2456 − 2388 = 68` bytes, confirming that storage order *matters*.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Generalized Row-Major Formula:**

Let the array `A` be declared as `A[lb1..ub1][lb2..ub2]`. Define:

- Number of rows: $m = ub_1 - lb_1 + 1$
- Number of columns: $n = ub_2 - lb_2 + 1$

To reach element `A[i][j]`, we skip `(i − lb1)` full rows of `n` elements each, then `(j − lb2)` more elements inside row `i`.

$$
\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + \left[(i - lb_1) \times n + (j - lb_2)\right] \times w
$$

**Step 2 — Apply to the given problem:**

**Given:** `A[-3..4][-2..5]`, so `lb1 = -3`, `ub1 = 4`, `lb2 = -2`, `ub2 = 5`.

$$
m = 4 - (-3) + 1 = 8
$$

$$
n = 5 - (-2) + 1 = 8
$$

`i = 4`, `j = 3`, `B = 4000`, `w = 4`.

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
= 4000 + \left[(4 - (-3)) \times 8 + (3 - (-2))\right] \times 4
$$

$$
= 4000 + \left[7 \times 8 + 5\right] \times 4
$$

$$
= 4000 + \left[56 + 5\right] \times 4
$$

$$
= 4000 + 61 \times 4 = 4000 + 244 = 4244
$$

**Answer:** `Address(A[4][3]) = 4244`.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Choice B]**

**(a)** *Explain the storage of a 1-D array in memory with a suitable diagram. A 1-D array `MARK[50]` of `float` is stored at base address `5000`. Each `float` occupies **8 bytes**. Find the address of `MARK[35]`. Also, calculate the total memory consumed by the array. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** *A 3-D array `SCORE[3][4][2]` of `int` (4 bytes each) is stored in Row-Major Order at base address `6000`. Using the n-dimensional address formula, find the address of `SCORE[2][3][1]`. Show all intermediate steps clearly. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Explanation with Diagram:**

A 1-D array `MARK[50]` is a linear collection of 50 floating-point numbers stored contiguously in memory. The **base address** `B = 5000` holds `MARK[0]`, and each subsequent element is placed exactly `w = 8` bytes after the previous one.

```
Address  5000   5008   5016   5024  ...   5392
         |------|------|------|------|------|
          M[0]   M[1]   M[2]   M[3]  ...   M[49]
```

**Step 2 — Address of `MARK[35]`:**

$$
\text{Address}(MARK[i]) = B + i \times w
$$

$$
= 5000 + 35 \times 8
$$

$$
= 5000 + 280 = 5280
$$

**Answer:** `Address(MARK[35]) = 5280`.

**Step 3 — Total Memory Consumed:**

$$
\text{Total Memory} = n \times w = 50 \times 8 = 400 \text{ bytes}
$$

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- `d1 = 3`, `d2 = 4`, `d3 = 2`
- `i1 = 2`, `i2 = 3`, `i3 = 1`
- `w = 4`, `B = 6000`

**Step 2 — Apply n-D Row-Major Formula:**

$$
\text{Address}(A[i_1][i_2][i_3]) = B + w \cdot \left[ i_1 \cdot d_2 \cdot d_3 + i_2 \cdot d_3 + i_3 \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
= 6000 + 4 \cdot \left[ 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 2 + 1 \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate inner brackets:**

$$
= 6000 + 4 \cdot \left[ 16 + 6 + 1 \right]
$$

$$
= 6000 + 4 \cdot 23
$$

$$
= 6000 + 92 = 6092
$$

**Answer:** `Address(SCORE[2][3][1]) = 6092`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully to Avoid Marks Loss:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RMO with CMO:** Always circle/highlight the phrase *"row-major"* or *"column-major"* in the question. The single most common error is using `i × n + j` when CMO is required (or vice versa).
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by `w`:** Students often compute the offset in *element units* but forget to multiply by word size to get *bytes*. The final address must be in bytes.
> 3. **Wrong row/column count in lower-bound problems:** When the array is `A[lb1..ub1][lb2..ub2]`, the number of rows is `ub1 − lb1 + 1` (NOT `ub1 − lb1`). The "+1" is critical.
> 4. **Index order swap in CMO:** In Column-Major, the formula uses `j × m + i` — students often accidentally use `i × m + j` (RMO pattern). This swaps the roles of rows and columns.
> 5. **Units in `float` arrays:** `float` is **4 bytes** in C (not 8). The 8-byte type is `double`. Many students incorrectly assume `float = 8 bytes`. Read the question carefully.
> 6. **No working shown:** KTU examiners award *step-marks*. Always show the formula, substitution, and step-by-step simplification, even if you can compute mentally.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- **Array** = linear, homogeneous, contiguous, fixed-size, random-access data structure.
- **Base address (B)** is the address of the *first* element (index 0).
- **Word size (w)** = number of bytes per element (`int` → 4, `char` → 1, `float` → 4, `double` → 8).
- **1-D Array Address:** $\text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + i \times w$ (zero-based).
- **2-D RMO Address:** $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (i \times n + j) \times w$.
- **2-D CMO Address:** $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (j \times m + i) \times w$.
- **Generalized 2-D RMO with lower bounds:** $B + [(i - lb_1) \times n + (j - lb_2)] \times w$.
- **Generalized 2-D CMO with lower bounds:** $B + [(j - lb_2) \times m + (i - lb_1)] \times w$.
- **RMO is default in C / C++ / Java / Python**; **CMO is default in Fortran / MATLAB / R**.
- **Number of elements** between bounds `lb` and `ub` = $ub - lb + 1$.
- **Total memory** consumed by an array of `N` elements of size `w` = $N \times w$ bytes.
- **n-D array (RMO) general formula:** $B + w \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left( i_k \cdot \prod_{l=k+1}^{n} d_l \right)$.
- **Random access time complexity** = O(1) (this is what makes arrays *fast*).
- **Insertion/Deletion in middle** of array = O(n) (this is what makes arrays *slow* for dynamic ops).
- **In a `float` array, w = 4 bytes** (this is the most commonly mistaken constant in KTU papers).
- **Always re-state the formula before plugging in numbers** — it earns easy step-marks.
- **RMO and CMO give the *same* address ONLY for first element `A[0][0]` and last element `A[m-1][n-1]`.** All middle elements differ.

> **Final Exam Tip from a Senior KTU Examiner:** *"In 14-mark problems, 4 marks go to stating the correct formula, 4 marks to correct substitution, 4 marks to simplification, and 2 marks to the final boxed answer with units (bytes). If you skip any stage, you lose proportional marks. Show your work."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sparse Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Sparse Matrix ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **Sparse Matrix** is a two-dimensional array (matrix) in which the number of zero elements is significantly larger than the number of non-zero elements. Formally, for a matrix $A$ of order $m \times n$ containing $t$ non-zero entries, the matrix is classified as **sparse** when: $$t \ll m \times n$$ In standard engineering practice (and as followed in the KTU 2024 syllabus), a matrix is treated as sparse when: $$t < \frac{m \times n}{2}$$ The corresponding quantitative metrics are: $$\text{Density} = \frac{t}{m \times n} \qquad \text{Sparsity} = 1 - \frac{t}{m \times n}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PCITT303 - Module 1):** > The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly expects students to (a) identify a sparse matrix, (b) choose an efficient memory representation, and (c) implement *Transpose* using both **Simple Transpose** and **Fast Transpose** algorithms with asymptotic time-complexity analysis. ### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Cinema Hall Seating Chart:** > Imagine a 1000-seat cinema hall. Out of 1000 seats, only **120** are actually booked for a weekday morning show. Storing the entire $32 \times 32$ seating chart wastes memory — most cells simply record *empty*. A far smarter approach is to keep a small list: `[(Row 3, Seat 14, Booked), (Row 8, Seat 02, Booked), ...]` — **only the meaningful data**. > > This compact list is exactly the **Triplet (3-tuple) Representation** of a sparse matrix. The original 2D grid is your *mathematical matrix*; the compact list is your *engineering storage structure*. ### 1.3 Why Sparse Matrices Matter in Engineering - **Network Analysis (Electrical Engineering):** Circuit netlists contain thousands of nodes but few active connections. - **Graph Algorithms (Computer Science):** Adjacency matrices of sparse graphs (social networks, road maps) are mostly zero. - **Machine Learning:** TF-IDF, Bag-of-Words, and One-Hot encoded text corpora are extremely sparse. - **Finite Element Methods (Mechanical/Civil):** Stiffness matrices in structural analysis contain mostly zeros. Efficient storage is therefore not optional — it is **computationally mandatory**. > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Non-zero Element ($t$):** > In this module, $t$ denotes the **count of non-zero elements** in the matrix. The triplet form stores exactly $t$ data rows plus 1 header row, giving $t + 1$ total rows in the compact representation. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Memory Representations of a Sparse Matrix A sparse matrix of order $m \times n$ with $t$ non-zero elements can be stored using **three principal methods**: #### A. Conventional (2D Array) Representation - Uses a simple $m \times n$ two-dimensional array. - **Memory Required:** $m \times n$ cells. - **Drawback:** Wastes memory storing zeros — defeats the purpose when $t$ is small. #### B. Triplet (3-Tuple / Coordinate List) Representation - Stores only the non-zero elements as a 1D array of triples. - **Header Row:** $[m, \; n, \; t]$ — total rows, total columns, count of non-zeros. - **Data Rows:** Each subsequent row is $[i, \; j, \; \text{value}]$ where $i$ is the row index, $j$ is the column index, and value is the non-zero entry at position $(i, j)$. - **Memory Required:** $3 \times (t + 1)$ cells. - This is the **most commonly tested representation** in KTU examinations. #### C. Linked List Representation - Each non-zero element is a node with fields: `row`, `col`, `value`, `next` (and optionally a `down` pointer for orthogonal lists). - **Memory:** Dynamic; no size pre-allocation required. - **Drawback:** Pointer overhead per node. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (Triplet Form) 1. Scan the input matrix $A$ element-by-element. 2. Whenever a non-zero $A[i][j]$ is encountered, record a triple $(i, j, A[i][j])$ in the storage array $S$. 3. Increment the non-zero counter. 4. After full scan, store the header $[m, n, t]$ in $S[0]$. 5. All remaining operations (transpose, addition, multiplication) operate on this compact form. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Type | Engineering Use | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | Matrix Order | $m \times n$ | rows × cols | Defines base array size | | Non-zero Count | $t$ | integer | Drives compact storage size | | Density | $t / (m \cdot n)$ | dimensionless ratio $\in [0, 1]$ | Higher = denser matrix | | Sparsity | $1 - t / (m \cdot n)$ | dimensionless ratio $\in [0, 1]$ | Higher = sparser matrix | | 2D Storage | $m \times n$ | cells | Conventional representation | | Triplet Storage | $3 \times (t + 1)$ | cells | Compact representation | | Linked-List Storage | $3 \times t + p \times t$ | cells ($p$ = pointer fields) | Dynamic representation | | Simple Transpose Time | $O(n \cdot t)$ | asymptotic | Column-by-column scan | | Fast Transpose Time | $O(n + t)$ | asymptotic | Single-pass with index map | | Memory Saved | $mn - 3(t+1)$ | cells | Quantifies storage efficiency | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Board Tip:** When using the **density** or **sparsity** values in your answer, write the formula symbolically *first*, then substitute the numbers. Examiners in KTU 2024 ESE award step-marks for the explicit substitution step. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Triplet Form - **Google's PageRank:** Operates on adjacency matrices of the web graph — which is *extremely* sparse. - **Simplex Method (Operations Research):** Sparse tableau representation accelerates solving linear programs. - **Image Processing (Compressed Sensing):** Sparse signal recovery in MRI reconstruction. - **Compiler Design:** Sparse representations optimize symbol tables and sparse DAG traversals. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — Identifying a Sparse Matrix Consider: $$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 6 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ Here $m = 4$, $n = 4$, total cells $= 16$, and the non-zero count is $t = 4$. - **Density:** $4 / 16 = 0.25$ - **Sparsity:** $1 - 0.25 = 0.75 = 75\%$ Since $t = 4 < 16/2 = 8$, the matrix is **sparse**. ### 3.2 Triplet Form Construction (Derivation) For matrix $A$ above, the triplet form $S$ is built as: $$S = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 5 \\ 1 & 3 & 8 \\ 2 & 2 & 3 \\ 3 & 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step-by-step derivation:** - $S[0] = [4, 4, 4]$ — header: 4 rows, 4 columns, 4 non-zeros. - $S[1] = [0, 1, 5]$ — the non-zero at $A[0][1] = 5$. - $S[2] = [1, 3, 8]$ — the non-zero at $A[1][3] = 8$. - $S[3] = [2, 2, 3]$ — the non-zero at $A[2][2] = 3$. - $S[4] = [3, 0, 6]$ — the non-zero at $A[3][0] = 6$. ### 3.3 Simple Transpose — Full Derivation **Goal:** Given triplet $A$ representing matrix $A$, produce triplet $T$ representing $A^T$ (the transpose). **Transpose Property:** Element $A[i][j]$ becomes $A^T[j][i]$. **Algorithm (Simple Transpose):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step 1: Initialize the header of } T. \\ &T[0][0] = A[0][1] \quad \text{(rows of } T = \text{columns of } A) \\ &T[0][1] = A[0][0] \quad \text{(columns of } T = \text{rows of } A) \\ &T[0][2] = A[0][2] \quad \text{(non-zero count is unchanged)} \\ &\text{Step 2: For each column } j \text{ of } A \text{, scan } A \text{ and pick matching rows.} \\ &\text{For } j = 0 \text{ to } A[0][1] - 1: \\ &\quad \text{For } i = 1 \text{ to } A[0][2]: \\ &\quad\quad \text{If } A[i][1] == j: \\ &\quad\quad\quad T[k][0] = A[i][1] \\ &\quad\quad\quad T[k][1] = A[i][0] \\ &\quad\quad\quad T[k][2] = A[i][2] \\ &\quad\quad\quad k = k + 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Worked example (continuing from §3.2):** Applying Simple Transpose to $A$, we iterate over columns $j = 0, 1, 2, 3$: - For $j = 0$: $A[4][1] = 0$ matches $\Rightarrow$ place $(0, 3, 6)$ at $T[1]$. - For $j = 1$: $A[1][1] = 1$ matches $\Rightarrow$ place $(1, 0, 5)$ at $T[2]$. - For $j = 2$: $A[3][1] = 2$ matches $\Rightarrow$ place $(2, 2, 3)$ at $T[3]$. - For $j = 3$: $A[2][1] = 3$ matches $\Rightarrow$ place $(3, 1, 8)$ at $T[4]$. Resulting transposed triplet: $$T = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 & 4 \\ 0 & 3 & 6 \\ 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 2 & 2 & 3 \\ 3 & 1 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Time Complexity Derivation:** The outer loop runs $n$ times. The inner loop runs $t$ times for each outer iteration. Total operations: $$T(n, t) = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} t = n \cdot t$$ $$\boxed{\text{Simple Transpose Time} = O(n \cdot t)}$$ ### 3.4 Fast Transpose — Full Derivation **Goal:** Reduce the time complexity from $O(n \cdot t)$ to $O(n + t)$ by eliminating repeated full scans. **Key Idea:** Pre-compute, for each column $j$ of $A$, two arrays: - $\text{row\_terms}[j]$ = number of non-zero elements in column $j$ of $A$. - $\text{starting\_pos}[j]$ = starting index in $T$ where elements from column $j$ should be placed. **Mathematical relationship (recursive position formula):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{starting\_pos}[0] &= 1 \\ \text{starting\_pos}[j] &= \text{starting\_pos}[j-1] + \text{row\_terms}[j-1] \quad \text{for } j \geq 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Worked example (continuing from §3.2):** Step 1 — Count non-zeros per column of $A$: - Column 0 of $A$: contains $6$ at row 3 $\Rightarrow$ $\text{row\_terms}[0] = 1$ - Column 1 of $A$: contains $5$ at row 0 $\Rightarrow$ $\text{row\_terms}[1] = 1$ - Column 2 of $A$: contains $3$ at row 2 $\Rightarrow$ $\text{row\_terms}[2] = 1$ - Column 3 of $A$: contains $8$ at row 1 $\Rightarrow$ $\text{row\_terms}[3] = 1$ Step 2 — Compute starting positions: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{starting\_pos}[0] &= 1 \\ \text{starting\_pos}[1] &= 1 + 1 = 2 \\ \text{starting\_pos}[2] &= 2 + 1 = 3 \\ \text{starting\_pos}[3] &= 3 + 1 = 4 \end{aligned} $$ Step 3 — Single pass through $A$ to populate $T$ directly: - $A[1] = [0, 1, 5]$: column $1$ $\Rightarrow$ $T[\text{starting\_pos}[1]=2] = [1, 0, 5]$; then $\text{starting\_pos}[1]++ \Rightarrow 3$. - $A[2] = [1, 3, 8]$: column $3$ $\Rightarrow$ $T[\text{starting\_pos}[3]=4] = [3, 1, 8]$; then $\text{starting\_pos}[3]++ \Rightarrow 5$. - $A[3] = [2, 2, 3]$: column $2$ $\Rightarrow$ $T[\text{starting\_pos}[2]=3] = [2, 2, 3]$; then $\text{starting\_pos}[2]++ \Rightarrow 4$. - $A[4] = [3, 0, 6]$: column $0$ $\Rightarrow$ $T[\text{starting\_pos}[0]=1] = [0, 3, 6]$; then $\text{starting\_pos}[0]++ \Rightarrow 2$. Result: $$T = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 & 4 \\ 0 & 3 & 6 \\ 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 2 & 2 & 3 \\ 3 & 1 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Time Complexity Derivation:** - Computing `row_terms`: requires one pass over $A$ $\Rightarrow O(t)$. - Computing `starting_pos`: requires one pass over $n$ columns $\Rightarrow O(n)$. - Single pass to place elements: $O(t)$. Total: $$T(n, t) = O(t) + O(n) + O(t)$$ $$\boxed{\text{Fast Transpose Time} = O(n + t)}$$ ### 3.5 Comparative Analysis Table | Criterion | Simple Transpose | Fast Transpose | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | Time Complexity | $O(n \cdot t)$ | $O(n + t)$ | | Auxiliary Memory | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ (for row\_terms and starting\_pos) | | Number of Passes over $A$ | $n$ passes | 1 pass | | Optimal When | $t \ll n$ | $t$ comparable to $n$ | | Implementation Difficulty | Trivial | Moderate | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Speedup Ratio:** Fast Transpose is asymptotically faster by a factor of $\frac{n \cdot t}{n + t}$, which approaches $t$ when $n$ is small. For very sparse matrices, Fast Transpose is dramatically more efficient. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Sparse Matrix Storage Strategies ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef repNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef detailNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 A["SPARSE MATRIX A of order m x n with t non zeros"]:::startNode B["CHOOSE STORAGE STRATEGY"]:::startNode C["2D ARRAY REPRESENTATION"]:::repNode D["TRIPLET 3 TUPLE FORM"]:::repNode E["LINKED LIST NODES"]:::repNode F["Memory used = m x n cells"]:::detailNode G["Memory used = 3 x t plus 1 cells"]:::detailNode H["Memory used = 3 t plus p t cells"]:::detailNode I["Access time O of 1"]:::detailNode J["Access time O of t traversal"]:::detailNode K["Access time O of t traversal"]:::detailNode L["Suitable for dense matrices"]:::detailNode M["Suitable for static sparse matrices"]:::detailNode N["Suitable for dynamic sparse matrices"]:::detailNode A --> B B --> C B --> D B --> E C --> F C --> I C --> L D --> G D --> J D --> M E --> H E --> K E --> N ``` ### 4.2 Memory Layout — Triplet Form of Matrix $A$ (from §3.2) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef header fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef data fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef index fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFF H0["4
m"]:::header H1["4
n"]:::header H2["4
t"]:::header D0["0
row"]:::data D1["1
col"]:::data D2["5
val"]:::data E0["1
row"]:::data E1["3
col"]:::data E2["8
val"]:::data F0["2
row"]:::data F1["2
col"]:::data F2["3
val"]:::data G0["3
row"]:::data G1["0
col"]:::data G2["6
val"]:::data Idx1["S index 0"]:::index Idx2["S index 1"]:::index Idx3["S index 2"]:::index Idx4["S index 3"]:::index Idx5["S index 4"]:::index Idx1 --- H0 Idx2 --- D0 Idx3 --- E0 Idx4 --- F0 Idx5 --- G0 H0 --- H1 H1 --- H2 D0 --- D1 D1 --- D2 E0 --- E1 E1 --- E2 F0 --- F1 F1 --- F2 G0 --- G1 G1 --- G2 ``` ### 4.3 Flow Chart — Fast Transpose Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef procNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef condNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef ioNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef endNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 S0["START Fast Transpose"]:::ioNode S1["Read triplet A of order m x n with t elements"]:::ioNode S2["Allocate T of size t plus 1 by 3"]:::procNode S3["Set T 0 0 to n, T 0 1 to m, T 0 2 to t"]:::procNode S4["Initialize row terms array of size n to zero"]:::procNode S5["FOR i from 1 to t, increment row terms A i 1"]:::procNode S6["Set starting pos 0 to 1"]:::procNode S7["FOR j from 1 to n minus 1, set starting pos j to starting pos j minus 1 plus row terms j minus 1"]:::procNode S8["FOR i from 1 to t, let col equal A i 1"]:::procNode S9["Set pos to starting pos col"]:::procNode S10["Set T pos 0 to A i 1, T pos 1 to A i 0, T pos 2 to A i 2"]:::procNode S11["Increment starting pos col"]:::procNode S12["OUTPUT T and END"]:::endNode S0 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7 S7 --> S8 S8 --> S9 S9 --> S10 S10 --> S11 S11 --> S12 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]** #### Q1. Define a sparse matrix. How do you determine whether a given matrix is sparse or not? *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A sparse matrix is a matrix in which the number of zero elements is considerably larger than the number of non-zero elements. For a matrix $A$ of order $m \times n$ with $t$ non-zero elements, it is considered sparse if $t < (m \times n)/2$. The decision is made by computing the **density** $t / (m \cdot n)$ and verifying that it is less than 0.5 (i.e., sparsity > 0.5). --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper Style]** #### Q2. List any three advantages of representing a sparse matrix in triplet form over conventional 2D array representation. *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** 1. **Memory Efficiency:** Triplet form uses only $3(t + 1)$ cells instead of $m \times n$ cells — a huge saving when $t \ll mn$. 2. **Faster Traversal:** Operations like transpose can be performed in $O(n + t)$ time using the Fast Transpose algorithm. 3. **Scalability:** For very large matrices (e.g., $10^6 \times 10^6$) with few non-zeros, the triplet form is the *only* feasible storage; a 2D array would exhaust memory. --- ### Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Full 14-Mark Question Style]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the **triplet representation** of a sparse matrix. Construct the triplet form for the following matrix and state the memory saved. *(7 Marks)* $$M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 4 & 0 \\ 5 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 7 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Model Solution:** **Triplet Representation Definition:** In the triplet (3-tuple) form, a sparse matrix $A$ of order $m \times n$ with $t$ non-zero elements is stored as a 2D array $S$ of size $(t+1) \times 3$ where: - $S[0][0] = m$, $S[0][1] = n$, $S[0][2] = t$. - For $1 \leq k \leq t$: $S[k] = [i_k, j_k, v_k]$ where $v_k = A[i_k][j_k] \neq 0$. **Construction for $M$:** Here $m = 4$, $n = 4$, and non-zero elements are at: $(0,2)=4$, $(1,0)=5$, $(2,3)=7$, $(3,1)=2$. So $t = 4$. $$S = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 & 4 \\ 0 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 2 & 3 & 7 \\ 3 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Memory Comparison:** - 2D Array memory: $4 \times 4 = 16$ cells. - Triplet memory: $3 \times (4 + 1) = 15$ cells. - **Memory Saved:** $16 - 15 = 1$ cell. *(Note: For a true sparse matrix, savings are typically massive. Here the matrix is only marginally sparse — the question illustrates the construction, not maximum efficiency.)* --- **(b)** Write the **Simple Transpose algorithm** for a sparse matrix stored in triplet form. Analyze its **time complexity**. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Algorithm (Pseudocode):** ``` SimpleTranspose(A, T, m, n, t): T[0][0] = A[0][1] // rows of T = columns of A T[0][1] = A[0][0] // columns of T = rows of A T[0][2] = A[0][2] // count unchanged if t > 0: k = 1 // index in T for j = 0 to n - 1: // for each column of A for i = 1 to t: // scan all of A if A[i][1] == j: // element in column j found T[k][0] = A[i][1] // swap indices T[k][1] = A[i][0] T[k][2] = A[i][2] k = k + 1 ``` **Time Complexity Analysis:** - The outer `for` loop runs $n$ times. - The inner `for` loop runs $t$ times. - Total comparisons: $n \cdot t$. - Hence, **Time Complexity = $O(n \cdot t)$**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Alternative **(a)** Explain the **Fast Transpose algorithm** for a sparse matrix in triplet form with an example. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Motivation:** Simple transpose takes $O(n \cdot t)$ time. Fast Transpose improves this to $O(n + t)$ by using two auxiliary arrays. **Auxiliary Arrays:** - `row_terms[j]` = number of non-zeros in column $j$ of $A$. - `starting_pos[j]` = index in $T$ where column $j$ of $A$ begins to be placed. **Algorithm:** ``` FastTranspose(A, T, m, n, t): T[0][0] = A[0][1] T[0][1] = A[0][0] T[0][2] = A[0][2] if t > 0: // Step 1: Count column frequencies for i = 0 to n - 1: row_terms[i] = 0 for i = 1 to t: row_terms[A[i][1]] = row_terms[A[i][1]] + 1 // Step 2: Compute starting positions starting_pos[0] = 1 for i = 1 to n - 1: starting_pos[i] = starting_pos[i-1] + row_terms[i-1] // Step 3: Place elements for i = 1 to t: j = A[i][1] // column of current element k = starting_pos[j] // position in T T[k][0] = A[i][1] T[k][1] = A[i][0] T[k][2] = A[i][2] starting_pos[j] = starting_pos[j] + 1 ``` **Worked Example (using matrix $M$ from Q.A):** Given triplet $A$: $$A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 & 4 \\ 0 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 2 & 3 & 7 \\ 3 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$ Step 1 — `row_terms`: Scanning $A[1..4]$ column values $\{2, 0, 3, 1\}$: - `row_terms[0] = 1` (from $A[2] = [1,0,5]$) - `row_terms[1] = 1` (from $A[4] = [3,1,2]$) - `row_terms[2] = 1` (from $A[1] = [0,2,4]$) - `row_terms[3] = 1` (from $A[3] = [2,3,7]$) Step 2 — `starting_pos`: - `starting_pos[0] = 1` - `starting_pos[1] = 1 + 1 = 2` - `starting_pos[2] = 2 + 1 = 3` - `starting_pos[3] = 3 + 1 = 4` Step 3 — Placement: - $A[1]=[0,2,4]$: col $2$, pos $3$ $\Rightarrow T[3]=[2,0,4]$ - $A[2]=[1,0,5]$: col $0$, pos $1$ $\Rightarrow T[1]=[0,1,5]$ - $A[3]=[2,3,7]$: col $3$, pos $4$ $\Rightarrow T[4]=[3,2,7]$ - $A[4]=[3,1,2]$: col $1$, pos $2$ $\Rightarrow T[2]=[1,3,2]$ Final Transpose: $$T = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 5 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 & 4 \\ 3 & 2 & 7 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Verification:** $M^T$ at position $(0,1)$ should equal $M[1][0] = 5$ ✓. --- **(b)** Compare the **time complexities** of Simple Transpose and Fast Transpose. State one situation where Simple Transpose might still be preferred. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Simple Transpose | Fast Transpose | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | Time Complexity | $O(n \cdot t)$ | $O(n + t)$ | | Auxiliary Space | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ (for two arrays) | | Implementation | Simple | Requires `row_terms` and `starting_pos` | | Best Case | $t \ll n$ (fast anyway) | Always preferred for large $t$ | | Worst Case | $t \approx mn$ degrades to $O(n^2 m)$ | Always linear | **Derivation of Complexity Difference:** For Simple Transpose, the inner loop scans all $t$ elements for *each* of the $n$ columns: $$T_{simple} = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} t = n \cdot t$$ For Fast Transpose, the algorithm uses three sequential linear passes: $$T_{fast} = O(t) + O(n) + O(t) = O(n + t)$$ **Speedup Factor:** $$\frac{T_{simple}}{T_{fast}} = \frac{n \cdot t}{n + t} \approx t \quad \text{when } t \gg n$$ **When is Simple Transpose Preferred?** Simple Transpose is preferred when **auxiliary memory is severely constrained** (e.g., embedded systems with $O(1)$ extra space budget) and the matrix is very sparse ($t \ll n$). In such cases, the extra $O(n)$ auxiliary space of Fast Transpose is not justified, and $O(n \cdot t)$ is already near-optimal. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Index Swapping Mistake:** When writing the transposed element, students often write `T[k][0] = A[i][0]` instead of `T[k][0] = A[i][1]`. The indices **must swap**: column becomes row, row becomes column. *This single error costs 2–3 marks.* > 2. **Header Row Oversight:** Many students forget to update $T[0][0]$, $T[0][1]$, $T[0][2]$ correctly. The header is worth 1 mark. > 3. **Time Complexity Expression:** Writing just $O(nt)$ without explaining that it comes from nested loops loses the 1 mark allotted for derivation. *Always state which loops contribute which factors.* > 4. **Forgetting the "If $t > 0$" Guard:** The transpose algorithms must check $t > 0$ before scanning; omitting this costs partial credit in KTU 2024 ESE. > 5. **Confusing `row_terms` and `starting_pos`:** These are *two separate arrays*. Students often mix them up. Remember: `row_terms` counts, `starting_pos` accumulates. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A matrix is **sparse** when $t < (m \cdot n)/2$, i.e., **density** $< 0.5$. - ✅ The **triplet (3-tuple) form** is the standard compact representation: header row $[m, n, t]$ followed by $t$ rows of $[i, j, \text{value}]$. - ✅ Memory required: **2D array** uses $m \cdot n$ cells; **triplet** uses $3(t+1)$ cells — a *huge* saving for sparse matrices. - ✅ **Simple Transpose** has time complexity $O(n \cdot t)$ — uses two nested loops. - ✅ **Fast Transpose** has time complexity $O(n + t)$ — uses `row_terms` and `starting_pos` auxiliary arrays. - ✅ The position formula is: $\text{starting\_pos}[j] = \text{starting\_pos}[j-1] + \text{row\_terms}[j-1]$. - ✅ Element index swapping: $A[i][j] \to T[j][i]$ (column becomes row index, row becomes column index). - ✅ Header update rule: $T[0][0] = n$, $T[0][1] = m$, $T[0][2] = t$. - ✅ **Linked list** representation offers dynamic memory but has pointer overhead — not commonly tested in KTU 2024. - ✅ Real-world applications: graph algorithms (PageRank), FEM, ML sparse features, circuit analysis. - ✅ **Always write the time complexity as a derivation** — KTU examiners award step-marks.

topics.title: Polynomial representation and manipulation using Arrays (Addition only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polynomial representation and manipulation using Arrays (Addition only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **polynomial** $P(x)$ in a single variable $x$ of degree $n$ is a finite sum of terms of the form $a_i x^i$, where each $a_i$ is a real (or complex) coefficient and $i$ is a non-negative integer exponent. Formally:

$$P(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \dots + a_n x^n = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i x^i$$

The **degree** of the polynomial is the largest exponent $n$ for which the coefficient $a_n \neq 0$. Each component $a_i x^i$ is called a **term**, and $a_i$ is the **coefficient** of $x^i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** A polynomial with $m$ non-zero terms out of a possible $n+1$ terms is called a **sparse polynomial** when $m \ll n+1$, and a **dense polynomial** when most coefficients are non-zero. This distinction directly drives the choice of array representation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Staircase of Importance"

Imagine a polynomial as a **ladder** where each rung corresponds to a specific power of $x$.

- The **bottom rung** ($i = 0$) is the *constant term* — it exists even if $x = 0$.
- The **top rung** ($i = n$) is the *leading term* — it dominates when $x$ is large.
- Some rungs may be **missing** (coefficient = 0) — like broken ladder steps you simply skip.

A computer does not understand "$x$" symbolically. It can only store **numbers**. Therefore, we must *encode* the polynomial as an array — the staircase gets translated into a numbered list of coefficient cells.

### 1.3 The Two Standard Array Representations

**Representation 1: Coefficient-Only Array (Dense Form)**

If the maximum degree is known, we store coefficients in an array indexed by the exponent.

| Aspect | Description |
|---|---|
| Index | $i$ corresponds to exponent of $x$ |
| Value at index $i$ | Coefficient $a_i$ |
| Array size | $n+1$, where $n$ is the maximum degree |
| Best for | **Dense** polynomials (most coefficients $\neq 0$) |

**Representation 2: Coefficient–Exponent Pair Array (Sparse Form)**

Each non-zero term is stored as a 2-tuple $(coefficient, exponent)$.

| Aspect | Description |
|---|---|
| Structure | Parallel arrays or struct array |
| Number of elements | $m$ (count of non-zero terms) |
| Best for | **Sparse** polynomials (many zeros) |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU Module 1, the **coefficient-only array** is the default and most-tested representation. The sparse form is generally introduced in Module 2/3 (Linked List module).

### 1.4 Why Polynomials Matter in Engineering

Polynomials are the **backbone of numerical computing**:

- **Signal processing:** FIR/IIR filters are polynomial evaluations.
- **Computer graphics:** Bezier curves and splines are polynomial parametric forms.
- **Cryptography:** Polynomial-based secret sharing (Shamir's scheme).
- **Machine learning:** Polynomial regression fits data to curves.
- **Control systems:** Transfer functions are rational functions of polynomials.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Memory Model of the Coefficient-Only Array

Given $P(x) = 5x^4 + 3x^2 + 7$, we represent it in memory as:

- The leading degree $n = 4$ is stored separately (often in a struct or variable).
- An array $A[0 \dots n]$ is allocated.
- $A[i] = a_i$, so $A[0] = 7$, $A[2] = 3$, $A[4] = 5$, and $A[1] = A[3] = 0$.

The polynomial is thus captured entirely by the pair $(A, n)$. Two polynomials $P_1$ and $P_2$ are stored as $(A_1, n_1)$ and $(A_2, n_2)$.

### 2.2 The Addition Problem — Setting Up the Logic

To compute $R(x) = P_1(x) + P_2(x)$, observe:

$$R(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{\max(n_1, n_2)} \left( a^{(1)}_i + a^{(2)}_i \right) x^i$$

where $a^{(1)}_i = 0$ if $i > n_1$, and $a^{(2)}_i = 0$ if $i > n_2$. Therefore, the result array $C$ has size $\max(n_1, n_2) + 1$, and for every index $i$:

$$C[i] = A_1[i] + A_2[i]$$

where out-of-range indices are treated as zero.

### 2.3 Algorithmic Steps (Plain-English Walkthrough)

1. **Identify the larger degree.** Let $d_{\max} = \max(n_1, n_2)$. The result will have degree at most $d_{\max}$.
2. **Allocate the result array** $C[0 \dots d_{\max}]$.
3. **Iterate** $i$ from $0$ to $d_{\max}$:
   - Let $a = A_1[i]$ if $i \leq n_1$, else $0$.
   - Let $b = A_2[i]$ if $i \leq n_2$, else $0$.
   - Set $C[i] = a + b$.
4. **Trim leading zeros** (optional but good practice): reduce the effective degree by skipping trailing zero coefficients.
5. **Return** the result $(C, d_{\text{effective}})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works:** Polynomial addition is **term-by-term alignment by exponent**. The coefficient-only array already aligns terms by index = exponent, so addition reduces to a single linear pass — a major efficiency advantage.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Unit / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Polynomial definition | $P(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i x^i$ | $n$ = degree, $a_i$ = coefficient |
| Resulting degree of sum | $\deg(R) = \max(n_1, n_2)$ | Reduced if leading coeff cancels |
| Coefficient relation | $C[i] = A_1[i] + A_2[i]$ | Index aligned with exponent |
| Dense array size | $n + 1$ | Memory in coefficient slots |
| Sparse array size | $2m$ (or $m$ pairs) | $m$ = non-zero term count |
| Addition time complexity | $O(\max(n_1, n_2))$ | Single linear pass |
| Dense space complexity | $O(n + 1)$ | Allocates $n+1$ slots |
| Sparse space complexity | $O(m)$ | Allocates $m$ pairs |
| Out-of-range term | $A_k[i] = 0$ for $i > n_k$ | Treated as implicit zero |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation for board exams:** If $P_1$ has degree 100 but only 3 non-zero terms, the dense array wastes **97 memory slots**. The sparse representation would use just **3 pairs**. This trade-off is a classic KTU question.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Polynomial Use Case |
|---|---|
| Embedded systems | Fixed-degree filter design in DSP chips |
| CAD/CAM | Curve fitting in CNC tool paths |
| Compiler design | Horner's method for expression evaluation |
| Numerical methods | Newton's divided differences, Taylor series |

The array representation is favored when the degree is **bounded and small** (e.g., a 10th-order filter) — common in real-time control loops.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example

**Problem:** Given two polynomials stored as coefficient arrays, compute their sum.

$$P_1(x) = 5x^4 + 3x^2 + 7, \quad n_1 = 4$$
$$P_2(x) = 4x^3 + 2x^2 + 6x + 1, \quad n_2 = 3$$

**Step 1 — Determine the larger degree.**

$$d_{\max} = \max(n_1, n_2) = \max(4, 3) = 4$$

**Step 2 — Write the input arrays indexed from 0 to $d_{\max}$.**

For $P_1$:

$$A_1 = [\,a_0,\, a_1,\, a_2,\, a_3,\, a_4\,] = [\,7,\, 0,\, 3,\, 0,\, 5\,]$$

For $P_2$, we pad up to index 4 (implicit zeros):

$$A_2 = [\,1,\, 6,\, 2,\, 4,\, 0\,]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the elementwise addition formula $C[i] = A_1[i] + A_2[i]$ for each index.**

| Index $i$ | Exponent | $A_1[i]$ | $A_2[i]$ | $C[i] = A_1[i] + A_2[i]$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $x^0$ | 7 | 1 | **8** |
| 1 | $x^1$ | 0 | 6 | **6** |
| 2 | $x^2$ | 3 | 2 | **5** |
| 3 | $x^3$ | 0 | 4 | **4** |
| 4 | $x^4$ | 5 | 0 | **5** |

**Step 4 — Write the result array and reconstruct the polynomial.**

$$C = [\,8,\, 6,\, 5,\, 4,\, 5\,]$$

$$R(x) = 5x^4 + 4x^3 + 5x^2 + 6x + 8$$

The result has degree $4$, and there are no trailing zero coefficients, so no trimming is needed.

### 3.2 Algebraic Verification

We verify term-by-term by collecting like exponents:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1(x) + P_2(x) &= (5x^4 + 3x^2 + 7) + (4x^3 + 2x^2 + 6x + 1) \\
&= 5x^4 + 4x^3 + (3+2)x^2 + 6x + (7+1) \\
&= 5x^4 + 4x^3 + 5x^2 + 6x + 8
\end{aligned}$$

This matches $R(x)$ exactly. **Algebraic confirmation is a key step expected in KTU 14-mark answers.**

### 3.3 Edge Case — Different Lengths

**Problem:** $P_1(x) = 2x^2 + 1$ (degree 2), $P_2(x) = 3x^5 + x$ (degree 5).

**Step 1:** $d_{\max} = \max(2, 5) = 5$.

**Step 2:** Pad arrays to size 6.

$$A_1 = [\,1,\, 0,\, 2,\, 0,\, 0,\, 0\,]$$
$$A_2 = [\,0,\, 1,\, 0,\, 0,\, 0,\, 3\,]$$

**Step 3:** Elementwise sum.

$$C = [\,1,\, 1,\, 2,\, 0,\, 0,\, 3\,]$$

**Step 4:**

$$R(x) = 3x^5 + 2x^2 + x + 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> The algorithm handles unequal lengths **natively** through implicit-zero padding. No special branching is required inside the loop — a hallmark of clean, efficient code.

### 3.4 Edge Case — Leading Coefficient Cancellation

**Problem:** $P_1(x) = 4x^2 + 1$, $P_2(x) = -4x^2 + 5x$.

$$A_1 = [\,1,\, 0,\, 4\,], \quad A_2 = [\,0,\, 5,\, -4\,]$$

$$C = [\,1,\, 5,\, 0\,]$$

Raw degree = 2, but $C[2] = 0$, so the **effective degree is 1**. We trim to obtain:

$$R(x) = 5x + 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always trim trailing zeros in the result to maintain the invariant that the highest index holds a non-zero coefficient. Failing to trim is a common KTU board mistake.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table

| Scenario | Result Degree | Trim Required? | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n_1 > n_2$, $A_1[n_1] + A_2[n_1] \neq 0$ | $n_1$ | No | Leading term survives |
| $n_1 > n_2$, $A_1[n_1] + A_2[n_1] = 0$ | $< n_1$ | **Yes** | Leading coefficient cancelled |
| $n_1 = n_2$, leading sum $\neq 0$ | $n_1$ | No | Symmetric case |
| $n_1 = n_2$, leading sum $= 0$ | $< n_1$ | **Yes** | Full cancellation possible |
| $n_1 < n_2$ | $n_2$ | Depends on $A_2[n_2]$ | Symmetric to case 1 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Layout of Two Polynomials

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph poly1["POLYNOMIAL P1 : degree 4"]
        d1["n1 = 4"]
        a1_0["A1[0] = 7"]
        a1_1["A1[1] = 0"]
        a1_2["A1[2] = 3"]
        a1_3["A1[3] = 0"]
        a1_4["A1[4] = 5"]
    end
    subgraph poly2["POLYNOMIAL P2 : degree 3"]
        d2["n2 = 3"]
        a2_0["A2[0] = 1"]
        a2_1["A2[1] = 6"]
        a2_2["A2[2] = 2"]
        a2_3["A2[3] = 4"]
        a2_4["A2[4] = 0 (implicit)"]
    end
    subgraph result["RESULT R : degree 4"]
        rd["nr = 4"]
        rc_0["C[0] = 8"]
        rc_1["C[1] = 6"]
        rc_2["C[2] = 5"]
        rc_3["C[3] = 4"]
        rc_4["C[4] = 5"]
    end
    a1_0 --> rc_0
    a2_0 --> rc_0
    a1_1 --> rc_1
    a2_1 --> rc_1
    a1_2 --> rc_2
    a2_2 --> rc_2
    a1_3 --> rc_3
    a2_3 --> rc_3
    a1_4 --> rc_4
    a2_4 --> rc_4
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Flowchart for Polynomial Addition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START])
    inputA[/Read P1: A1, n1/]
    inputB[/Read P2: A2, n2/]
    comp{"dmax = n1 >= n2 ?"}
    setD[/dmax = n1/]
    setE[/dmax = n2/]
    alloc[/Allocate C of size dmax + 1/]
    init[/Set i = 0/]
    cond{"i <= dmax ?"}
    getA1["term1 = A1[i] if i <= n1 else 0"]
    getA2["term2 = A2[i] if i <= n2 else 0"]
    sumOp["C[i] = term1 + term2"]
    inc[/i = i + 1/]
    trim{"Trim leading zeros?"}
    trimYes["Reduce nr to last non-zero index"]
    trimNo["Keep nr = dmax"]
    output[/Display C and nr/]
    endA([END])

    startA --> inputA --> inputB --> comp
    comp -- YES --> setD --> alloc
    comp -- NO  --> setE --> alloc
    alloc --> init --> cond
    cond -- YES --> getA1 --> getA2 --> sumOp --> inc --> cond
    cond -- NO  --> trim
    trim -- YES --> trimYes --> output
    trim -- NO  --> trimNo --> output
    output --> endA
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    user([User / Caller Program])
    iface["Input Interface : Read coefficients and degree"]
    degComp["Degree Comparator Module : Computes dmax"]
    allocMod["Memory Allocator : Creates result array C"]
    addEngine["Addition Engine : Single pass elementwise sum"]
    trimMod["Trim Module : Removes leading zeros"]
    outFormat["Output Formatter : Builds polynomial string"]
    display([Display Result Polynomial])

    user --> iface --> degComp --> allocMod --> addEngine --> trimMod --> outFormat --> display
```

### 4.4 Coefficient Indexing Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    idx0["Index 0 : x to power 0 : constant term"]
    idx1["Index 1 : x to power 1 : linear term"]
    idx2["Index 2 : x to power 2 : quadratic term"]
    idx3["Index 3 : x to power 3 : cubic term"]
    idx4["Index 4 : x to power 4 : quartic term"]
    idx0 --> idx1 --> idx2 --> idx3 --> idx4
```

> [!NOTE]
> The indexing convention is **critical** in KTU board answers. Always state: *"Array index $i$ stores the coefficient of $x^i$."* Mis-indexing is the #1 reason students lose marks in polynomial addition problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a polynomial. Explain the dense (coefficient-only) array representation with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**

A polynomial $P(x)$ in a single variable $x$ of degree $n$ is an expression of the form:

$$P(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \dots + a_n x^n$$

where $a_i$ are coefficients and $n$ is the largest exponent with $a_n \neq 0$.

In the **dense (coefficient-only) array representation**, we allocate an array of size $n+1$ and store the coefficient $a_i$ at index $i$. The degree $n$ is stored separately.

**Example:** $P(x) = 6x^3 + 2x + 9$ has $n = 3$ and is stored as:

$$A = [\,9,\, 2,\, 0,\, 6\,], \quad n = 3$$

The array is indexed from 0 to 3, with $A[0] = 9$, $A[1] = 2$, $A[2] = 0$ (implicit zero for missing $x^2$ term), and $A[3] = 6$.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Explanation of representation (1) + Example (1).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three advantages and two disadvantages of representing a polynomial using an array.*

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**

1. **Random access in $O(1)$:** Any coefficient $a_i$ can be retrieved instantly using index $i$.
2. **Cache-friendly storage:** Contiguous memory layout yields excellent CPU cache performance.
3. **Simple addition algorithm:** Polynomial addition reduces to a single linear pass, $O(n)$ time.

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Wastes memory for sparse polynomials:** A polynomial of degree 1000 with only 5 non-zero terms still allocates 1001 slots.
2. **Fixed maximum degree:** The array size must be known at compile time or allocated dynamically; resizing is costly.

**Marks Distribution:** 3 advantages (1.5 marks) + 2 disadvantages (1 mark) + Examples (0.5 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure to add two polynomials using arrays. Illustrate with an example where the polynomials have different degrees. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two polynomials are given as:
- $P_1(x) = 7x^4 + 2x^3 + 5x + 1$ (degree 4)
- $P_2(x) = 3x^4 + 6x^2 + 2x$ (degree 4)

Write the coefficient arrays and compute $P_1(x) + P_2(x)$. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Procedure and Illustration**

**Procedure:**

1. Read the two polynomials as $(A_1, n_1)$ and $(A_2, n_2)$ where $A_k$ is the coefficient array and $n_k$ is the degree.
2. Compute $d_{\max} = \max(n_1, n_2)$.
3. Allocate result array $C$ of size $d_{\max} + 1$.
4. For $i = 0$ to $d_{\max}$:
   - $a = A_1[i]$ if $i \leq n_1$, else $0$.
   - $b = A_2[i]$ if $i \leq n_2$, else $0$.
   - $C[i] = a + b$.
5. Trim trailing zeros to find the effective degree $n_r$.
6. Return $(C, n_r)$.

**Illustration with different degrees:**

Let $P_1(x) = 4x^2 + 3$ (degree 2) and $P_2(x) = 5x^4 + x^2 + 2x$ (degree 4).

- $A_1 = [\,3,\, 0,\, 4\,]$, $n_1 = 2$.
- $A_2 = [\,0,\, 2,\, 1,\, 0,\, 5\,]$, $n_2 = 4$.
- $d_{\max} = 4$, allocate $C[0 \dots 4]$.
- Elementwise sum:

$$\begin{aligned}
C[0] &= 3 + 0 = 3 \\
C[1] &= 0 + 2 = 2 \\
C[2] &= 4 + 1 = 5 \\
C[3] &= 0 + 0 = 0 \\
C[4] &= 0 + 5 = 5
\end{aligned}$$

- $C = [\,3,\, 2,\, 5,\, 0,\, 5\,]$, effective degree = 4.
- Result: $R(x) = 5x^4 + 5x^2 + 2x + 3$.

**Time complexity:** $O(\max(n_1, n_2))$.

**(b) Solution for the Given Polynomials**

- $P_1(x) = 7x^4 + 2x^3 + 5x + 1$, so $A_1 = [\,1,\, 5,\, 0,\, 2,\, 7\,]$, $n_1 = 4$.
- $P_2(x) = 3x^4 + 6x^2 + 2x$, so $A_2 = [\,0,\, 2,\, 6,\, 0,\, 3\,]$, $n_2 = 4$.

Since $n_1 = n_2 = 4$, $d_{\max} = 4$. Compute elementwise sum:

| Index $i$ | $A_1[i]$ | $A_2[i]$ | $C[i]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 0 | **1** |
| 1 | 5 | 2 | **7** |
| 2 | 0 | 6 | **6** |
| 3 | 2 | 0 | **2** |
| 4 | 7 | 3 | **10** |

Result array: $C = [\,1,\, 7,\, 6,\, 2,\, 10\,]$, $n_r = 4$.

$$P_1(x) + P_2(x) = 10x^4 + 2x^3 + 6x^2 + 7x + 1$$

**Verification (algebraic):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 + P_2 &= (7x^4 + 2x^3 + 5x + 1) + (3x^4 + 6x^2 + 2x) \\
&= (7+3)x^4 + 2x^3 + 6x^2 + (5+2)x + 1 \\
&= 10x^4 + 2x^3 + 6x^2 + 7x + 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Marks Split (7 + 7):**
- (a) Procedure steps (3) + Example with different degrees (4).
- (b) Writing arrays (2) + Tabular/computed sum (3) + Final polynomial and verification (2).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare the dense array representation and sparse (coefficient-exponent pair) representation of polynomials. Mention the time and space complexity of polynomial addition in both forms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polynomial $P_1(x) = x^{100} + 1$ is to be added to $P_2(x) = 2x^{50} + 3$. Discuss which representation is more memory-efficient, and compute the result using that representation. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison of Representations**

| Parameter | Dense Array | Sparse (Pair) Array |
|---|---|---|
| Storage unit | Single coefficient per slot | (coefficient, exponent) pair per slot |
| Array size | $n+1$ | $m$ (number of non-zero terms) |
| Best for | Dense polynomials | Sparse polynomials |
| Access to $a_i$ | Direct via index $i$ in $O(1)$ | Linear search in $O(m)$ |
| Addition time | $O(\max(n_1, n_2))$ | $O(m_1 + m_2)$ |
| Space complexity | $O(n+1)$ | $O(m)$ |
| Memory waste | High for sparse | Negligible |
| Implementation | Simple | Needs search/merge logic |

**Trade-off Summary:** Dense form is faster for random access; sparse form saves memory when most coefficients are zero.

**(b) Memory-Efficient Choice and Computation**

For $P_1(x) = x^{100} + 1$ and $P_2(x) = 2x^{50} + 3$:

- **Dense representation** would require arrays of size 101 each — wasting 99 zero slots for $P_1$ and 48 for $P_2$.
- **Sparse representation** is vastly more efficient:
  - $P_1$ has 2 non-zero terms: $\{(1, 100), (1, 0)\}$.
  - $P_2$ has 2 non-zero terms: $\{(2, 50), (3, 0)\}$.
  - Total storage: 4 pairs vs. 202 coefficient slots in dense form.

**Sparse-form addition algorithm:** Since exponents are sorted in descending order, we use a **merge procedure** (like merge-sort):

1. Compare the highest exponents: $100$ vs. $50$ → $100$ goes first with coefficient $1$.
2. Next: $50$ vs. $0$ → $50$ goes with coefficient $2$.
3. Next: $0$ (from $P_1$) vs. $0$ (from $P_2$) → **exponents match** → sum coefficients: $1 + 3 = 4$.

**Result (sparse form):**

$$R(x) = x^{100} + 2x^{50} + 4$$

**Memory saved:** Dense form uses $101 + 51 = 152$ slots (assuming max-degree arrays); sparse form uses just $4$ pairs. **Savings: ~97%.**

**Marks Split (7 + 7):**
- (a) Table comparison (4) + Complexity analysis (2) + Conclusion (1).
- (b) Choice justification with memory math (3) + Step-by-step merge (3) + Final answer (1).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mis-indexing the array:** Storing $a_i$ at index $i+1$ or reversing the order. Always start at index 0 for $x^0$.
> 2. **Forgetting to handle unequal lengths:** Treating $A_2[i]$ as undefined when $i > n_2$ causes crashes or wrong answers. Use the conditional `if i <= n2` pattern.
> 3. **Skipping the algebraic verification step:** Examiners award 1–2 marks for cross-checking the array result against the manual polynomial sum. Always include this in 14-mark answers.
> 4. **Not trimming leading zeros:** If the highest coefficient sums to 0, students often report the wrong degree. Always trim.
> 5. **Confusing sparse vs. dense storage costs:** A common trick question asks which is better for a specific polynomial — answer based on the **ratio of non-zero to total terms**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Polynomial:** $\sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i x^i$, degree $n$ is highest non-zero exponent.
- **Coefficient-only array:** Index = exponent; size = $n+1$.
- **Pair array:** Stores $(coeff, exp)$ tuples; size = number of non-zero terms.
- **Addition formula:** $C[i] = A_1[i] + A_2[i]$, treating out-of-range as 0.
- **Result degree:** $\max(n_1, n_2)$, reduced if leading coefficient cancels.
- **Time complexity:** $O(\max(n_1, n_2))$ for dense, $O(m_1 + m_2)$ for sparse.
- **Space complexity:** $O(n+1)$ for dense, $O(m)$ for sparse.
- **Dense preferred when:** Polynomial is dense, degree is small, random access needed.
- **Sparse preferred when:** Polynomial has high degree but few non-zero terms.
- **Always trim trailing zeros** in the result to maintain degree integrity.
- **Always verify** the array result by reconstructing the polynomial and confirming algebraically.
- **Edge cases to remember:** Unequal lengths, leading coefficient cancellation, all-zero result (degree 0 or special "zero polynomial").
- **Board mantra:** *"Index is exponent, sum is coefficient-aligned, trim is mandatory."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stack: Operations on Stack using Array
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stack: Operations on Stack using Array

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — STACK: OPERATIONS ON STACK USING ARRAY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STACK (ADT Definition)**
> A **Stack** is a linear data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** discipline, in which insertion and deletion of elements are permitted at only one end, called the **TOP** of the stack. The two fundamental operations — insertion (**PUSH**) and deletion (**POP**) — are restricted to this single endpoint.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a Stack is treated as an **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** characterized by:
- A finite ordered sequence of homogeneous elements.
- A pointer/index `TOP` that marks the position of the most recently inserted element.
- An underlying **contiguous memory** model when implemented using an **array** (Static Implementation).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Plate Dispenser 🍽️

Imagine a **cylindrical plate dispenser** in a college canteen:
- You can only **add a plate on top** of the existing pile.
- You can only **remove the plate that is on top**.
- The plate that was placed **last** comes out **first**.
- If the dispenser is full, you cannot push more plates in (**Overflow**).
- If the dispenser is empty, you cannot pull a plate out (**Underflow**).

This real-world object behaves **exactly** like a computational stack. The **spring-loaded mechanism** at the bottom simulates how a stack's `TOP` pointer always points to the last occupied cell of the underlying array.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus-Mandated Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | Symbol / Default |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **Stack** | LIFO-ordered linear structure | $S = \{ s_0, s_1, \ldots, s_{n-1} \}$ |
| **Top Pointer** | Index of the last inserted element | `top` |
| **Push** | Insert an element at the top | $S \leftarrow S \cup \{x\}$ |
| **Pop** | Remove and return the top element | $x \leftarrow \text{top}(S)$ |
| **Peek / Top** | Inspect the top element without removal | returns $S[\text{top}]$ |
| **Overflow** | Cannot push — stack is full | `top == MAX - 1` |
| **Underflow** | Cannot pop — stack is empty | `top == -1` |
| **MAX** | Maximum capacity of array-based stack | Constant |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** In the 2024 Scheme, the empty stack is initialized with **`top = -1`**. The full stack condition is **`top = MAX - 1`**. Examiners strictly enforce this convention — a student writing `top = 0` for empty may lose marks.

### 1.4 Visual Snapshot — Stack States

$$
\text{Empty Stack: } \quad \text{top} = -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \big[\, \;\; \;\; \;\; \;\; \;\; \,\big]
$$

$$
\text{Stack after Push(10), Push(20), Push(30):} \quad \text{top} = 2
$$

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|}
\hline
\text{Index} & \text{Value} \\
\hline
0 & 10 \\
\hline
1 & 20 \\
\hline
2 & 30 \;\; \leftarrow \text{top} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Core Operations of a Stack (ADT)

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **five canonical operations**. Each must be mastered with both the *algorithm* and the *boundary check*.

#### Operation 1 — `createStack()` (Initialization)
Allocates an array of size `MAX` and sets the `top` pointer to `-1`.
- **Pre-condition:** Memory available.
- **Post-condition:** Empty stack with `top = -1`.

#### Operation 2 — `push(S, x)` (Insertion)
Inserts element `x` at position `top + 1`.
- **Pre-condition:** `top < MAX - 1` (else Overflow).
- **Post-condition:** `top` incremented by 1; `S[top] = x`.
- **Time Complexity:** $\Theta(1)$
- **Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ auxiliary

#### Operation 3 — `pop(S)` (Deletion)
Removes and returns the element at `S[top]`.
- **Pre-condition:** `top \geq 0` (else Underflow).
- **Post-condition:** `top` decremented by 1; previous top is logically discarded.
- **Time Complexity:** $\Theta(1)$

#### Operation 4 — `peek(S)` / `top(S)` (Inspection)
Returns the value at `S[top]` **without** modifying the stack.
- **Pre-condition:** `top \geq 0`.
- **Time Complexity:** $\Theta(1)$

#### Operation 5 — `isEmpty(S)` / `isFull(S)` (Status Checks)
- `isEmpty(S)` returns `true` if `top == -1`.
- `isFull(S)` returns `true` if `top == MAX - 1`.
- Both run in $\Theta(1)$ time.

### 2.2 Boundary Conditions — The Two Critical Failure Modes

$$
\text{Overflow Condition (Push Failure):} \quad \text{top} = \text{MAX} - 1
$$

$$
\text{Underflow Condition (Pop Failure):} \quad \text{top} = -1
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Hot-Spot:** Questions worth 3–7 marks frequently test whether a student correctly identifies *which* condition triggers in a given scenario. Memorize the formulas; do not derive them during the exam.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit / Type |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Position of $k^{\text{th}}$ pushed element | $\text{index} = k - 1$ | If pushed in sequence 1..$k$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Number of elements in stack | $n = \text{top} - (-1) = \text{top} + 1$ | Valid when `top` $\geq 0$ | count |
| 3 | Overflow trigger | $\text{top} = \text{MAX} - 1$ | Push must be rejected | boolean |
| 4 | Underflow trigger | $\text{top} = -1$ | Pop must be rejected | boolean |
| 5 | Memory footprint | $M = \text{MAX} \times \text{sizeof}(\text{element})$ | Static array allocation | bytes |
| 6 | Time per push/pop | $T = \Theta(1)$ | No loop, no recursion | operations |
| 7 | Total pushes possible | $N_{\max} = \text{MAX}$ | Equal to declared size | count |
| 8 | Top value after $p$ pushes and $q$ pops | $\text{top} = p - q - 1$ | Only if $p - q \leq \text{MAX}$ | index |
| 9 | Worst-case space used | $S_{\max} = \text{MAX} \times \text{sizeof}(\text{element})$ | Static bound | bytes |
| 10 | Average utilisation ratio | $U = \dfrac{n}{\text{MAX}} = \dfrac{\text{top} + 1}{\text{MAX}}$ | $0 \leq U \leq 1$ | ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas above are **memoryless** — i.e., they depend only on the *current* state (`top` and `MAX`), never on the history of operations. This is the defining property of an array-based stack and a frequent Part-A question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Array-based stacks are foundational in:

- **Compiler Design:** Function call stack, recursion unwinding, expression evaluation (infix → postfix), parenthesis matching.
- **Operating Systems:** Interrupt handling, kernel-mode stacks, process context switching.
- **Embedded Systems:** Memory-constrained microcontrollers use static stacks (no `malloc` overhead).
- **Text Editors:** Undo/Redo functionality (each action is pushed onto a stack).
- **Browser Engines:** Back-button navigation history.
- **Numerical Computing:** Evaluation of Reverse Polish Notation (RPN) on calculators.

### 2.5 C Language Implementation (KTU Reference Code)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#define MAX 100

typedef struct {
    int data[MAX];
    int top;
} Stack;

void createStack(Stack *s) {
    s->top = -1;
}

int isEmpty(Stack *s) {
    return s->top == -1;
}

int isFull(Stack *s) {
    return s->top == MAX - 1;
}

void push(Stack *s, int x) {
    if (isFull(s)) {
        printf("Stack Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    s->top = s->top + 1;
    s->data[s->top] = x;
    printf("Pushed %d\n", x);
}

int pop(Stack *s) {
    if (isEmpty(s)) {
        printf("Stack Underflow\n");
        return -9999;   // sentinel
    }
    int x = s->data[s->top];
    s->top = s->top - 1;
    return x;
}

int peek(Stack *s) {
    if (isEmpty(s)) {
        printf("Stack is empty\n");
        return -9999;
    }
    return s->data[s->top];
}
```

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Empty-Stack Initial Condition

The choice of `top = -1` is **not arbitrary** — it is the unique value that simultaneously satisfies *all three* invariants of an array-based stack. We derive it formally.

**Invariant 1 — Element Count Invariant:**
The number of valid elements $n$ in the stack must equal `top + 1` (since valid indices are $0, 1, \ldots, \text{top}$).

$$
n = \text{top} + 1
$$

**Invariant 2 — Empty Stack Invariant:**
When the stack is empty, the element count must be zero.

$$
n = 0
$$

**Invariant 3 — Combining (1) and (2):**

$$
0 = \text{top} + 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{top} = -1
$$

This result is **deterministic and unique** — no other integer satisfies both invariants. The KTU 2024 evaluator will deduct marks for choosing `top = 0` (which would imply $n = 1$ on initialization, i.e., the stack "magically" contains a phantom element).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Overflow Condition

**Goal:** Determine the maximum value `top` can attain before the next push would exceed the array bounds.

**Step 1:** Array indices in C are bounded by $[0,\;\text{MAX}-1]$.

$$
\text{valid index range} = [0,\;\text{MAX}-1]
$$

**Step 2:** The largest legal index is therefore `MAX - 1`.

$$
\text{top}_{\max} = \text{MAX} - 1
$$

**Step 3:** If `top` is already at `MAX - 1` and we attempt `push`, the system would write to `data[MAX]`, which is **out of bounds** — undefined behaviour, segmentation fault, or silent corruption.

**Step 4:** Therefore, the overflow trigger is:

$$
\text{Overflow} \iff \text{top} \geq \text{MAX} - 1
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Underflow Condition

**Step 1:** Reading `data[top]` requires `top` to be a legal index.

**Step 2:** For an empty stack, the set of valid indices is **empty**.

$$
\text{valid indices} = \emptyset
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore `top` must lie strictly below `0`.

$$
\text{Underflow} \iff \text{top} < 0 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{top} = -1
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — State Transition Table for `MAX = 5`

This table traces the **complete lifecycle** of a stack of capacity 5 through 11 operations. Use it to compute the final state for any sequence.

| Step | Operation | Pre-`top` | Condition Check | Action | Post-`top` | Stack Contents (bottom → top) |
|------|-----------|-----------|------------------|--------|-------------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | `createStack` | undefined | — | `top = -1` | $-1$ | $[ \;\;]$ |
| 2 | `push(10)` | $-1$ | not full | insert at 0 | $0$ | $[10]$ |
| 3 | `push(20)` | $0$ | not full | insert at 1 | $1$ | $[10, 20]$ |
| 4 | `push(30)` | $1$ | not full | insert at 2 | $2$ | $[10, 20, 30]$ |
| 5 | `peek()` | $2$ | not empty | return 30 | $2$ | $[10, 20, 30]$ |
| 6 | `pop()` | $2$ | not empty | return 30, top-- | $1$ | $[10, 20]$ |
| 7 | `push(40)` | $1$ | not full | insert at 2 | $2$ | $[10, 20, 40]$ |
| 8 | `push(50)` | $2$ | not full | insert at 3 | $3$ | $[10, 20, 40, 50]$ |
| 9 | `push(60)` | $3$ | not full | insert at 4 | $4$ | $[10, 20, 40, 50, 60]$ |
| 10 | `push(70)` | $4$ | **full!** | **OVERFLOW** | $4$ | $[10, 20, 40, 50, 60]$ (unchanged) |
| 11 | `pop()` | $4$ | not empty | return 60, top-- | $3$ | $[10, 20, 40, 50]$ |

### 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example — Evaluating `top` after a Sequence

**Problem:** A stack of `MAX = 8` is initially empty. We perform: `push(5), push(15), push(25), pop(), pop(), push(35), push(45), push(55), pop()`. Find the **final value of `top`**, the **top element**, and whether **any overflow/underflow** occurred.

**Step 1 — Initialize:**
`top = -1`, stack is empty.

**Step 2 — push(5):**
Check `top < MAX - 1` → $-1 < 7$ ✓
`top = 0`, `data[0] = 5`.

**Step 3 — push(15):**
Check $-1 < 7$ ✓
`top = 1`, `data[1] = 15`.

**Step 4 — push(25):**
`top = 2`, `data[2] = 25`.

**Step 5 — pop():**
Check `top >= 0` → $2 \geq 0$ ✓
Return 25, `top = 1`.

**Step 6 — pop():**
Return 15, `top = 0`.

**Step 7 — push(35):**
`top = 1`, `data[1] = 35`.

**Step 8 — push(45):**
`top = 2`, `data[2] = 45`.

**Step 9 — push(55):**
`top = 3`, `data[3] = 55`.

**Step 10 — pop():**
Return 55, `top = 2`.

**Step 11 — Aggregate Results:**

$$
\text{Final top} = 2
$$

$$
\text{Top element} = 45
$$

$$
\text{Overflows} = 0,\quad \text{Underflows} = 0
$$

$$
\text{Number of valid elements} = \text{top} + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

$$
\text{Stack contents (bottom to top)} = [5, 35, 45]
$$

### 3.6 Complexity Analysis — Formal Derivation

Let $T(n)$ denote the time taken by a sequence of $n$ push/pop operations on an array-based stack.

**Lower Bound:**
Each operation touches exactly one cell of the array and updates one integer (`top`). At least one memory write and one comparison are mandatory.

$$
T(n) \geq 1 \cdot n = \Omega(n)
$$

**Upper Bound:**
The implementation contains a single conditional check and a constant number of assignments. No loop, no recursion.

$$
T(n) \leq c \cdot n = O(n)
$$

**Amortised per-Operation Cost:**

$$
T_{\text{per op}} = \dfrac{T(n)}{n} = \Theta(1)
$$

**Space Complexity:**

$$
S(n) = \text{MAX} \cdot \text{sizeof}(\text{element}) = \Theta(\text{MAX})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The amortised $O(1)$ per operation is the **single most important complexity fact** about array-based stacks. The KTU 2024 paper (Module 1, Part B) often includes a sub-question asking students to "justify why each push/pop is $O(1)$". Your answer must mention: *no shifting of elements, no traversal, no dynamic resizing*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Array-Based Stack in Memory

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CodeLayer["USER PROGRAM LAYER"]
        A["main() function"]
        B["createStack()"]
        C["push(S, x)"]
        D["pop(S)"]
        E["peek(S)"]
        F["isEmpty(S) / isFull(S)"]
    end

    subgraph ADTLayer["STACK ADT LAYER"]
        G["Stack Structure: data[0..MAX-1], top"]
    end

    subgraph MemoryLayer["PHYSICAL MEMORY LAYER (Contiguous Block)"]
        H["data[0]"]
        I["data[1]"]
        J["data[2]"]
        K["data[3]"]
        L["data[MAX-1]"]
    end

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F

    B --> G
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H
    G --> I
    G --> J
    G --> K
    G --> L
```

### 4.2 State Machine — Push Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["START: push(S, x) called"] --> P1{"isFull(S) ?"}
    P1 -- "YES (top == MAX-1)" --> P2["Print STACK OVERFLOW"]
    P2 --> P3["Return without modification"]
    P1 -- "NO" --> P4["top = top + 1"]
    P4 --> P5["data[top] = x"]
    P5 --> P6["Print Pushed x"]
    P6 --> P7["END: Stack updated"]
```

### 4.3 State Machine — Pop Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0["START: pop(S) called"] --> Q1{"isEmpty(S) ?"}
    Q1 -- "YES (top == -1)" --> Q2["Print STACK UNDERFLOW"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Return error sentinel"]
    Q1 -- "NO" --> Q4["x = data[top]"]
    Q4 --> Q5["top = top - 1"]
    Q5 --> Q6["Return x"]
    Q6 --> Q7["END: Element removed"]
```

### 4.4 Lifecycle Snapshot — Three Distinct Stack States

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
