```mermaid
    subgraph StateA["STATE A: Empty Stack (top = -1)"]
        A0["data[0]: --"]
        A1["data[1]: --"]
        A2["data[2]: --"]
        A3["data[3]: --"]
        A4["top = -1"]
    end

    subgraph StateB["STATE B: Partially Filled (top = 1)"]
        B0["data[0]: 10"]
        B1["data[1]: 20"]
        B2["data[2]: --"]
        B3["data[3]: --"]
        B4["top = 1"]
    end

    subgraph StateC["STATE C: Full Stack (top = 3)"]
        C0["data[0]: 10"]
        C1["data[1]: 20"]
        C2["data[2]: 30"]
        C3["data[3]: 40"]
        C4["top = 3  MAX-1"]
    end

    StateA -- "push x 2 times" --> StateB
    StateB -- "push x 2 more times" --> StateC
    StateC -- "pop x 1 time" --> StateB
    StateB -- "pop x 2 times" --> StateA
```

### 4.5 Pointer Movement Diagram — Push & Pop in Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Initial: top arrow at -1 (below array)"] --> R2["push 5: top moves to 0, data[0] holds 5"]
    R2 --> R3["push 7: top moves to 1, data[1] holds 7"]
    R3 --> R4["push 9: top moves to 2, data[2] holds 9"]
    R4 --> R5["pop: returns 9, top moves back to 1"]
    R5 --> R6["push 11: top moves to 2, data[2] overwritten with 11"]
    R6 --> R7["Final state: data = 5, 7, 11 with top at 2"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `P0`, `Q1`, `StateA`) and **never** use reserved words (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) as standalone IDs — this guarantees clean rendering on the KTU-LMS viewer and GitHub.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]`
**Define a Stack. List any four operations performed on a stack and state the conditions under which push and pop operations fail.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A Stack is a linear data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** principle, where insertion and deletion occur at one end called the **TOP**.

> **Four Operations (1 mark):** `push()`, `pop()`, `peek()` (or `top()`), `isEmpty()` / `isFull()`.

> **Failure Conditions (1 mark):**
> - `push` fails when the stack is **full** — *Overflow*: $\text{top} = \text{MAX} - 1$.
> - `pop` fails when the stack is **empty** — *Underflow*: $\text{top} = -1$.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]`
**What is the time complexity of push and pop operations in an array-based stack? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Time Complexity (1 mark):** Both `push` and `pop` execute in $\Theta(1)$ time (constant time).

> **Justification (2 marks):**
> 1. The `top` pointer is directly updated by ±1 — a single integer operation.
> 2. A direct array access `data[top]` is performed — array indexing in C is $O(1)$.
> 3. There is **no loop, no traversal, and no shifting** of elements, hence the operation count is bounded by a constant $c$ independent of the stack size $n$.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Module-1 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **two sub-parts: (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **array implementation of a stack**. Show the state of the stack (and the value of `top`) after each step of the following sequence of operations on an empty stack of `MAX = 5`:
`push(11), push(22), push(33), pop(), pop(), push(44), push(55), push(66), pop(), push(77)`

**(b)** Write the **complete C program** to implement push, pop, peek, isEmpty, and isFull operations on a stack using an array. Explain the **overflow** and **underflow** conditions with formulas.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Initialise:** `top = -1`, empty stack.

$$
\text{Stack: } [\,],\;\;\text{top} = -1
$$

**Step 2 — push(11):** `top` becomes 0.

$$
\text{Stack: } [11],\;\;\text{top} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — push(22):** `top` becomes 1.

$$
\text{Stack: } [11,\,22],\;\;\text{top} = 1
$$

**Step 4 — push(33):** `top` becomes 2.

$$
\text{Stack: } [11,\,22,\,33],\;\;\text{top} = 2
$$

**Step 5 — pop():** Return 33, `top` becomes 1.

$$
\text{Stack: } [11,\,22],\;\;\text{top} = 1
$$

**Step 6 — pop():** Return 22, `top` becomes 0.

$$
\text{Stack: } [11],\;\;\text{top} = 0
$$

**Step 7 — push(44):** `top` becomes 1.

$$
\text{Stack: } [11,\,44],\;\;\text{top} = 1
$$

**Step 8 — push(55):** `top` becomes 2.

$$
\text{Stack: } [11,\,44,\,55],\;\;\text{top} = 2
$$

**Step 9 — push(66):** `top` becomes 3.

$$
\text{Stack: } [11,\,44,\,55,\,66],\;\;\text{top} = 3
$$

**Step 10 — pop():** Return 66, `top` becomes 2.

$$
\text{Stack: } [11,\,44,\,55],\;\;\text{top} = 2
$$

**Step 11 — push(77):** `top` becomes 3.

$$
\text{Final Stack: } [11,\,44,\,55,\,77],\;\;\text{top} = 3
$$

**Diagram (Neat Sketch Required — 2 marks):**

```
Index:    0      1      2      3      4
        +------+------+------+------+------+
Value:  |  11  |  44  |  55  |  77  |      |
        +------+------+------+------+------+
                                 ^
                                top = 3
```

**Final Summary (1 mark):**
- Final value of `top` = **3**
- Top element = **77**
- Overflows = 0; Underflows = 0
- Final stack size = 4

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Complete C Program (5 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#define MAX 100

int stack[MAX];
int top = -1;

int isEmpty() {
    return top == -1;
}

int isFull() {
    return top == MAX - 1;
}

void push(int x) {
    if (isFull()) {
        printf("Stack Overflow! Cannot push %d\n", x);
        return;
    }
    top = top + 1;
    stack[top] = x;
    printf("%d pushed onto stack\n", x);
}

int pop() {
    if (isEmpty()) {
        printf("Stack Underflow! Stack is empty\n");
        return -1;
    }
    int x = stack[top];
    top = top - 1;
    return x;
}

int peek() {
    if (isEmpty()) {
        printf("Stack is empty\n");
        return -1;
    }
    return stack[top];
}

int main() {
    push(10);
    push(20);
    push(30);
    printf("Top element is %d\n", peek());
    printf("Popped: %d\n", pop());
    printf("Popped: %d\n", pop());
    push(40);
    printf("Top element is %d\n", peek());
    return 0;
}
```

**Overflow Condition Explanation (1 mark):**
Overflow occurs when the stack is full and a new element cannot be inserted.

$$
\text{Overflow} \iff \text{top} = \text{MAX} - 1
$$

**Underflow Condition Explanation (1 mark):**
Underflow occurs when the stack is empty and a deletion is attempted.

$$
\text{Underflow} \iff \text{top} = -1
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative]`

**(a)** What is a **stack**? Explain the **PUSH** and **POP** operations with algorithms. State the time and space complexity of each.

**(b)** Consider an array-based stack of capacity `MAX = 6`. Starting from an empty state, perform the following operations and show the contents of the stack and the value of `top` after every operation:
`push(2), push(4), push(6), push(8), pop(), push(10), push(12), push(14), pop(), pop(), pop(), pop(), pop()`

Identify any **overflow** or **underflow** conditions encountered. Justify each one using the relevant formula.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A stack is a non-primitive, linear data structure that stores elements in **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** order. All insertions and deletions take place at one end called the **top**.

**PUSH Algorithm (2.5 marks):**

```
Algorithm: PUSH(STACK, TOP, MAX, ITEM)
1. [Check for Overflow]
   IF TOP = MAX - 1 THEN
       PRINT "Stack Overflow"
       RETURN
   [End of IF]
2. SET TOP = TOP + 1
3. SET STACK[TOP] = ITEM
4. EXIT
```

**POP Algorithm (2.5 marks):**

```
Algorithm: POP(STACK, TOP)
1. [Check for Underflow]
   IF TOP = -1 THEN
       PRINT "Stack Underflow"
       RETURN
   [End of IF]
2. SET ITEM = STACK[TOP]
3. SET TOP = TOP - 1
4. RETURN ITEM
```

**Complexity Analysis (1 mark):**
- **Time:** $T(n) = \Theta(1)$ for both push and pop.
- **Auxiliary Space:** $S(n) = O(1)$.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks**

**MAX = 6, initially `top = -1`.**

| Step | Operation | Pre-`top` | Check | Action | Post-`top` | Stack (bottom→top) |
|------|-----------|-----------|-------|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | `push(2)` | $-1$ | $-1 < 5$ ✓ | insert at 0 | $0$ | $[2]$ |
| 2 | `push(4)` | $0$ | $0 < 5$ ✓ | insert at 1 | $1$ | $[2,4]$ |
| 3 | `push(6)` | $1$ | $1 < 5$ ✓ | insert at 2 | $2$ | $[2,4,6]$ |
| 4 | `push(8)` | $2$ | $2 < 5$ ✓ | insert at 3 | $3$ | $[2,4,6,8]$ |
| 5 | `pop()` | $3$ | $3 \geq 0$ ✓ | return 8, top-- | $2$ | $[2,4,6]$ |
| 6 | `push(10)` | $2$ | $2 < 5$ ✓ | insert at 3 | $3$ | $[2,4,6,10]$ |
| 7 | `push(12)` | $3$ | $3 < 5$ ✓ | insert at 4 | $4$ | $[2,4,6,10,12]$ |
| 8 | `push(14)` | $4$ | $4 < 5$ ✓ | insert at 5 | $5$ | $[2,4,6,10,12,14]$ |
| 9 | `pop()` | $5$ | $5 \geq 0$ ✓ | return 14 | $4$ | $[2,4,6,10,12]$ |
| 10 | `pop()` | $4$ | $4 \geq 0$ ✓ | return 12 | $3$ | $[2,4,6,10]$ |
| 11 | `pop()` | $3$ | $3 \geq 0$ ✓ | return 10 | $2$ | $[2,4,6]$ |
| 12 | `pop()` | $2$ | $2 \geq 0$ ✓ | return 6 | $1$ | $[2,4]$ |
| 13 | `pop()` | $1$ | $1 \geq 0$ ✓ | return 4 | $0$ | $[2]$ |

**Justifications (1 mark):**
- **No Overflow:** The stack never exceeded $\text{top} = \text{MAX} - 1 = 5$ during any push.
- **No Underflow:** The stack never dropped to $\text{top} = -1$ during any pop; the smallest value of `top` was 0 (after step 13).

**Final State (1 mark):**
- `top = 0`, top element = 2, one element remaining.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS**
>
> 1. **Wrong initial `top` value:** Many students write `top = 0` for an empty stack. This is **incorrect** under KTU convention — you lose 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting to check overflow/underflow:** In Part-B, a C program *without* the `isFull()` / `isEmpty()` guard loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing `peek()` with `pop()`:** `peek` does **not** decrement `top`. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 4. **No time-complexity justification:** Simply writing "$O(1)$" without explaining *why* (no loop, no traversal) is considered incomplete — loses up to 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up stack growth direction:** Some textbooks grow the stack from index `0` upward (as in KTU), others grow downward. Stick to the **upward** convention of KTU unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 6. **Returning a value from pop without checking underflow first:** Always check `isEmpty` *before* reading `data[top]`.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Stack = LIFO discipline**, single-ended access, abstract data type.
- ✅ **Empty Stack Convention:** `top = -1` (not `0`).
- ✅ **Overflow:** `top = MAX - 1` (push fails).
- ✅ **Underflow:** `top = -1` (pop fails).
- ✅ **Five canonical operations:** `createStack`, `push`, `pop`, `peek`, `isEmpty/isFull`.
- ✅ **Time complexity:** $\Theta(1)$ for push, pop, and peek — *no* loop or shift.
- ✅ **Space complexity:** $\Theta(\text{MAX})$ static allocation.
- ✅ **Memory layout:** Contiguous block; no fragmentation, no pointer overhead.
- ✅ **Disadvantage:** Fixed size — cannot grow dynamically (use **linked-list-based stack** for variable size, covered in Module 2).
- ✅ **KTU exam favourites:** Trace tables, C code, complexity justification, overflow/underflow identification.
- ✅ **Real-world uses:** Function call stack, undo/redo, parenthesis matching, expression evaluation, browser back-button.

> **End of Module 1 — Stack (Array Implementation) Notes** | *Refer to Module 1 linked-list stack and Module 2 applications (Infix-to-Postfix, Recursion, Balanced Parentheses) for continuation.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Queues: Definition, Operations on Queue using Array
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Queues: Definition, Operations on Queue using Array

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **QUEUE (Linear Data Structure)**
> A **Queue** is an ordered, linear data structure that follows the **FIFO (First In, First Out)** principle — also called **FCFS (First Come, First Served)** discipline. Insertions are permitted at one end called the **Rear** (or **Tail** / **Back**), and deletions are permitted at the other end called the **Front** (or **Head**). The element that is inserted first is the one that is removed first.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 1 – Linear Data Structures), the queue is formally classified as a **homogeneous, non-primitive, linear dynamic data structure** with restricted access — only the two extreme ends are accessible for the two primitive operations.

The two fundamental primitive operations are:
- **Enqueue (Insert / Add / Push-rear):** Adds an element at the rear end.
- **Dequeue (Delete / Remove / Pop-front):** Removes an element from the front end.

Auxiliary operations include: `peek()/front()`, `rear()`, `isEmpty()`, `isFull()`, `size()`.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Movie Ticket Counter Line**
> Imagine a crowd waiting at a cinema ticket counter:
> - People join the line from the **rear (tail)** end → this is **Enqueue**.
> - The person standing at the **front (head)** of the line buys the ticket and leaves → this is **Dequeue**.
> - You cannot jump in the middle of the line; you cannot serve someone from the middle.
> - Whoever comes **first** is served **first** → hence **FIFO**.

**Other Real-World Examples:**
- **CPU Scheduling (Round-Robin Queue)** — processes are arranged in a ready queue.
- **Print Spooler** — print jobs wait in a queue.
- **Call Center Phone System** — callers wait in a hold queue.
- **Keyboard Buffer** — characters typed are stored in a buffer queue before the OS processes them.
- **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** traversal of graphs uses a queue.
- **Disk Scheduling (FCFS)** — requests served in arrival order.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> The standard asymptotic metrics for all primitive queue operations using an **array-based implementation** are:
> - **Time Complexity = O(1)** (constant time) for `enqueue`, `dequeue`, `peek`, `isEmpty`, `isFull`.
> - **Space Complexity = O(n)**, where **n** is the maximum capacity of the array.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown (Structured Logic)

### A. Linear Queue using Array — Structural Setup

A **linear (non-circular) queue** is implemented using a **one-dimensional array** of fixed maximum size, say `SIZE`, with two integer pointers:

- **`front`** → Index of the element at the front (the next element to be dequeued). Initial value: **`-1`**.
- **`rear`** → Index of the last inserted element (the most recent enqueue position). Initial value: **`-1`**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Initial State Convention (KTU Standard):**
> $$\text{front} = -1, \quad \text{rear} = -1$$
> This denotes an **empty queue** — no valid element exists at the front end.

### B. Logical Steps of Each Operation

**1. ENQUEUE (Insertion at Rear)**
- Step 1: Check **overflow** condition: `rear == SIZE - 1` ⇒ queue is full, reject insertion.
- Step 2: If queue is **empty** (`front == -1`), set `front = 0`.
- Step 3: Increment `rear` by 1: `rear = rear + 1`.
- Step 4: Place the new element at `arr[rear]`.

**2. DEQUEUE (Deletion from Front)**
- Step 1: Check **underflow** condition: `front == -1` **OR** `front > rear` ⇒ queue is empty, reject deletion.
- Step 2: Retrieve the element at `arr[front]`.
- Step 3: Increment `front` by 1: `front = front + 1` (effectively "removing" the element).
- Step 4 (optional optimization): Reset both pointers to `-1` when the queue becomes empty (`front > rear`) to reclaim space logically.

**3. PEEK / FRONT**
- Step 1: If queue is not empty, return `arr[front]`.
- Step 2: Else, signal "queue empty" error.

**4. ISEMPTY**
- Return `true` if `front == -1` **OR** `front > rear`; else `false`.

**5. ISFULL**
- Return `true` if `rear == SIZE - 1`; else `false`.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — LINEAR QUEUE (ARRAY)**
> All conditions use single-line notation. Absolute value bars are written as `\vert` to prevent table parsing errors.

| # | Operation | Condition / Formula | Time Complexity | Space Used |
|---|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|------------|
| 1 | **Empty Check** | $\text{front} = -1 \;\;\text{OR}\;\; \text{front} > \text{rear}$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |
| 2 | **Full Check (Overflow)** | $\text{rear} = \text{SIZE} - 1$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |
| 3 | **Enqueue** | $\text{rear} \leftarrow \text{rear} + 1;\; \text{arr[rear]} \leftarrow \text{item}$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | 1 unit |
| 4 | **Dequeue** | $\text{item} \leftarrow \text{arr[front]};\; \text{front} \leftarrow \text{front} + 1$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Frees 1 unit logically |
| 5 | **Peek / Front** | $\text{return arr[front]}$ (if not empty) | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |
| 6 | **Number of Elements** | $n = \text{rear} - \text{front} + 1$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |
| 7 | **Initial State** | $\text{front} = -1,\; \text{rear} = -1$ | — | — |
| 8 | **Rear Element Access** | $\text{return arr[rear]}$ (if not empty) | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Usage of Queue |
|-------------------|----------------|
| **Operating Systems** | Process scheduling (FCFS, Round-Robin), I/O buffering, semaphore wait queues. |
| **Networking** | Packet routing buffers (router queues), congestion control. |
| **Compiler Design** | Symbol table management, parsing intermediate tokens. |
| **Disk & Memory Management** | Disk arm scheduling, page replacement buffers. |
| **Real-Time Systems** | Interrupt handling queues, sensor data buffering. |
| **Graph Algorithms** | BFS traversal, topological sorting, shortest path in unweighted graphs. |

## 2.4 Limitation of Linear Queue (Sets up Circular Queue in Module 2)

> [!WARNING]
> **The Wasted Space Problem (Critical KTU Concept):**
> In a linear array queue, `rear` only moves **forward** and `front` only moves **forward**. Once `rear` reaches `SIZE - 1`, the queue is declared "full" — even if there is empty space at the **lower indices** (between index 0 and `front - 1`) vacated by dequeued elements. This phenomenon is called **False Overflow** or **Memory Wastage**. The solution is a **Circular Queue** (covered in later modules).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete C Implementation (Step-by-Step, KTU Board Style)

> Since **PCITT303 – Data Structures** is a Computer Science programming course, the **C language source code** is part of the syllabus and is required in KTU lab and theory exams. The implementation is shown **line-by-line with explanatory comments**.

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#define SIZE 5

int queue[SIZE];
int front = -1, rear = -1;

/* ----- isEmpty() ----- */
int isEmpty() {
    return (front == -1 || front > rear);
}

/* ----- isFull() ----- */
int isFull() {
    return (rear == SIZE - 1);
}

/* ----- ENQUEUE Operation ----- */
void enqueue(int item) {
    if (isFull()) {
        printf("\n[OVERFLOW] Queue is full. Cannot insert %d\n", item);
        return;
    }
    if (front == -1)          // First insertion
        front = 0;
    rear = rear + 1;
    queue[rear] = item;
    printf("[ENQUEUE] Inserted %d at index %d\n", item, rear);
}

/* ----- DEQUEUE Operation ----- */
int dequeue() {
    if (isEmpty()) {
        printf("\n[UNDERFLOW] Queue is empty. Nothing to delete.\n");
        return -999;          // Sentinel error value
    }
    int item = queue[front];
    printf("[DEQUEUE] Removed %d from index %d\n", item, front);
    front = front + 1;
    if (front > rear)         // Queue became empty after dequeue
        front = rear = -1;
    return item;
}

/* ----- PEEK Operation ----- */
int peek() {
    if (isEmpty()) {
        printf("\n[ERROR] Queue is empty.\n");
        return -999;
    }
    return queue[front];
}

/* ----- DISPLAY Operation ----- */
void display() {
    if (isEmpty()) {
        printf("\nQueue is EMPTY.\n");
        return;
    }
    printf("\nQueue contents (front -> rear): ");
    for (int i = front; i <= rear; i++)
        printf("%d ", queue[i]);
    printf("\n");
}

/* ----- MAIN: Driver Program ----- */
int main() {
    enqueue(10);
    enqueue(20);
    enqueue(30);
    enqueue(40);
    enqueue(50);
    enqueue(60);          // Triggers OVERFLOW

    display();

    printf("Front element = %d\n", peek());

    dequeue();
    dequeue();
    display();

    dequeue();
    dequeue();
    dequeue();
    dequeue();            // Triggers UNDERFLOW

    return 0;
}
```

## 3.2 Execution Trace — Step-by-Step State Transition

Let `SIZE = 5`, with sequence: `enqueue(10), enqueue(20), enqueue(30), dequeue(), dequeue(), enqueue(40)`.

| Step | Operation | front (before) | rear (before) | Action | front (after) | rear (after) | Queue State (left → right) | Result |
|------|-----------|----------------|----------------|--------|----------------|---------------|----------------------------|--------|
| 0 | INIT | $-1$ | $-1$ | Initialize | $-1$ | $-1$ | [ \_ \_ \_ \_ \_ ] | Empty |
| 1 | enqueue(10) | $-1$ | $-1$ | Not full, first insert → front=0, rear=0, arr[0]=10 | $0$ | $0$ | [ **10** \_ \_ \_ \_ ] | OK |
| 2 | enqueue(20) | $0$ | $0$ | rear=1, arr[1]=20 | $0$ | $1$ | [ **10 20** \_ \_ \_ ] | OK |
| 3 | enqueue(30) | $0$ | $1$ | rear=2, arr[2]=30 | $0$ | $2$ | [ **10 20 30** \_ \_ ] | OK |
| 4 | dequeue() | $0$ | $2$ | item=arr[0]=10, front=1 | $1$ | $2$ | [ \_ **20 30** \_ \_ ] | Returns 10 |
| 5 | dequeue() | $1$ | $2$ | item=arr[1]=20, front=2 | $2$ | $2$ | [ \_ \_ **30** \_ \_ ] | Returns 20 |
| 6 | enqueue(40) | $2$ | $2$ | Not full (rear=2 ≠ 4), rear=3, arr[3]=40 | $2$ | $3$ | [ \_ \_ **30 40** \_ ] | OK |

> **Key Observation:** After Step 6, `arr[0]` and `arr[1]` still hold values `10` and `20` (stale data) but are **logically removed**. This demonstrates the **logical deletion** mechanism of array-based queues.

## 3.3 Mathematical Derivation of the Element Count

The number of valid elements in the queue at any moment is derived as:

$$
n = (\text{rear}) - (\text{front}) + 1
$$

**Derivation logic:**
- The front pointer points to the **first valid** element.
- The rear pointer points to the **last valid** element.
- The total number of indices spanned (inclusive of both endpoints) is `rear - front + 1`.
- Example: front = 2, rear = 4 → $n = 4 - 2 + 1 = 3$ elements. ✓

For the **edge case** where the queue is empty (front = $-1$ or front > rear), the formula is **not applied** — we use the `isEmpty()` predicate instead.

## 3.4 Mathematical Derivation of the Full Condition

A linear queue of size `SIZE` has valid array indices: $0, 1, 2, \ldots, \text{SIZE} - 1$.

Since `rear` always points to the **last filled index**, and the last valid index is `SIZE - 1`, the queue is full when:

$$
\text{rear} = \text{SIZE} - 1
$$

Equivalently, the **count** condition:

$$
n = \text{SIZE} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{rear} - \text{front} + 1 = \text{SIZE}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Array-Based Queue Architecture & Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef arraySlot fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pointer fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef opBox fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef errorBox fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    %% Array cells (SIZE = 5)
    idx0["arr[0]"]:::arraySlot
    idx1["arr[1]"]:::arraySlot
    idx2["arr[2]"]:::arraySlot
    idx3["arr[3]"]:::arraySlot
    idx4["arr[4]"]:::arraySlot

    %% Pointers
    frontPtr(["FRONT = 2"]):::pointer
    rearPtr(["REAR = 3"]):::pointer

    %% Operation boxes
    enqOp["ENQUEUE item\n rear = rear + 1\n arr[rear] = item"]:::opBox
    deqOp["DEQUEUE item\n item = arr[front]\n front = front + 1"]:::opBox
    isFullOp{"rear == SIZE-1 ?"}:::opBox
    isEmptyOp{"front == -1 OR front > rear ?"}:::opBox
    overflow["OVERFLOW: Queue FULL\n Insertion REJECTED"]:::errorBox
    underflow["UNDERFLOW: Queue EMPTY\n Deletion REJECTED"]:::errorBox

    %% Connections
    frontPtr --> idx2
    rearPtr --> idx3
    enqOp --> isFullOp
    isFullOp -- YES --> overflow
    isFullOp -- NO --> idx3
    deqOp --> isEmptyOp
    isEmptyOp -- YES --> underflow
    isEmptyOp -- NO --> idx2
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Enqueue/Dequeue Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef start fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    A([START]):::start --> B{Choose Operation}:::decision

    B -- ENQUEUE --> C{isFull ?}:::decision
    C -- YES --> D[[Print OVERFLOW\nReturn]]:::endNode
    C -- NO --> E{front == -1 ?}:::decision
    E -- YES --> F[Set front = 0]:::process
    E -- NO --> G[Skip front reset]:::process
    F --> H[rear = rear + 1]:::process
    G --> H
    H --> I[arr[rear] = item]:::process
    I --> J[[Print SUCCESS\nReturn]]:::endNode

    B -- DEQUEUE --> K{isEmpty ?}:::decision
    K -- YES --> L[[Print UNDERFLOW\nReturn -1]]:::endNode
    K -- NO --> M[item = arr[front]]:::process
    M --> N[front = front + 1]:::process
    N --> O{front > rear ?}:::decision
    O -- YES --> P[front = rear = -1\n Reset queue]:::process
    O -- NO --> Q[Keep pointers]:::process
    P --> R[[Return item]]:::endNode
    Q --> R
```

## 4.3 Visual Schematic — State of the Queue After Operations

```
INITIAL EMPTY STATE (front = -1, rear = -1)
Index:    [0]  [1]  [2]  [3]  [4]
          [ ]  [ ]  [ ]  [ ]  [ ]
          front=-1, rear=-1

AFTER enqueue(10), enqueue(20), enqueue(30)
Index:    [0]  [1]  [2]  [3]  [4]
          [10] [20] [30] [ ]  [ ]
                 ^         ^
              front=0    rear=2

AFTER dequeue(), dequeue()
Index:    [0]  [1]  [2]  [3]  [4]
          [10] [20] [30] [ ]  [ ]   <-- Stale data still in memory
                       ^    ^
                    front=2 rear=2

AFTER enqueue(40)
Index:    [0]  [1]  [2]  [3]  [4]
          [10] [20] [30] [40] [ ]
                       ^         ^
                    front=2    rear=3

Now if enqueue(50), enqueue(60) → queue is FULL (rear = 4 = SIZE-1)
Index:    [0]  [1]  [2]  [3]  [4]
          [10] [20] [30] [40] [50]
                       ^              ^
                    front=2         rear=4
```
