# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> **Define a Queue data structure. Mention any two real-world applications of a queue.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Queue** is a linear, ordered data structure that obeys the **FIFO (First In, First Out)** discipline. Insertion is performed at the **rear** end and deletion is performed at the **front** end.

**Applications:**
1. **CPU Process Scheduling** — The OS maintains a ready queue of processes; the process at the front is dispatched next (FCFS scheduling).
2. **Disk Scheduling** — I/O requests are queued and served in arrival order to minimize seek time variance.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two applications.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Model]**
> **Differentiate between a Stack and a Queue data structure.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Stack | Queue |
|---------|-------|-------|
| **Principle** | LIFO (Last In, First Out) | FIFO (First In, First Out) |
| **Insert End** | Top (one end) | Rear end |
| **Delete End** | Top (same end) | Front end |
| **Number of Accessible Ends** | One | Two |
| **Operations** | Push, Pop, Peek | Enqueue, Dequeue, Peek |
| **Analogy** | Stack of plates | Movie ticket counter line |
| **Use Case** | Function calls, recursion, expression evaluation | Scheduling, buffering, BFS |

*(1.5 marks for principle + ends, 1.5 marks for other distinguishing features.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Full Implementation

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> **(a)** Define a linear queue. Write the algorithms for ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations on a linear queue implemented using an array. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Simulate the operations: `enqueue(5), enqueue(10), enqueue(15), dequeue(), enqueue(20), dequeue(), enqueue(25)` on a linear queue of `SIZE = 5`. Show the state of `front`, `rear`, and the array after **each operation**. Mention any one limitation of this implementation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition & Algorithms (7 Marks)**

A **linear queue** is a queue implemented over a one-dimensional array of fixed size, where the `rear` pointer moves monotonically forward and elements are added at the rear and removed from the front, following the **FIFO** discipline.

**Algorithm: ENQUEUE(item)**
1. `IF rear == SIZE - 1` THEN
   - Print `"OVERFLOW: Queue is full"`
   - `RETURN`
2. `IF front == -1` THEN
   - `SET front = 0`   *(first element insertion)*
3. `SET rear = rear + 1`
4. `SET queue[rear] = item`
5. Print `"Inserted item"`
6. `RETURN`

**Algorithm: DEQUEUE()**
1. `IF front == -1 OR front > rear` THEN
   - Print `"UNDERFLOW: Queue is empty"`
   - `RETURN -1`
2. `SET item = queue[front]`
3. `SET front = front + 1`
4. `IF front > rear` THEN
   - `SET front = rear = -1`   *(reset to empty state)*
5. `RETURN item`

*(3 marks for definition, 2 marks for ENQUEUE algorithm, 2 marks for DEQUEUE algorithm.)*

---

**(b) Simulation Trace (7 Marks)**

Let `SIZE = 5`, `front = -1`, `rear = -1` initially.

| Step | Operation | front (after) | rear (after) | Array State `[0] [1] [2] [3] [4]` | Remarks |
|------|-----------|---------------|---------------|-------------------------------------|---------|
| 0 | Initial | $-1$ | $-1$ | [ \_ \_ \_ \_ \_ ] | Empty queue |
| 1 | enqueue(5) | $0$ | $0$ | [ **5** \_ \_ \_ \_ ] | First insert → front=0 |
| 2 | enqueue(10) | $0$ | $1$ | [ **5 10** \_ \_ \_ ] | rear=1 |
| 3 | enqueue(15) | $0$ | $2$ | [ **5 10 15** \_ \_ ] | rear=2 |
| 4 | dequeue() | $1$ | $2$ | [ \_ **10 15** \_ \_ ] | Returns 5, front=1 |
| 5 | enqueue(20) | $1$ | $3$ | [ \_ **10 15 20** \_ ] | rear=3 |
| 6 | dequeue() | $2$ | $3$ | [ \_ \_ **15 20** \_ ] | Returns 10, front=2 |
| 7 | enqueue(25) | $2$ | $4$ | [ \_ \_ **15 20 25** ] | rear=4 (full) |

**Final State:** `front = 2`, `rear = 4`, Queue = `[15, 20, 25]`. A further `enqueue()` call will report **OVERFLOW**.

**Limitation of Linear Queue (1 mark):**
Even though the array has logically free space at indices `0` and `1` (vacated by `5` and `10`), the queue is declared **FULL** when `rear = SIZE - 1 = 4`. This phenomenon is called **False Overflow** or **Memory Wastage** and is mitigated using a **Circular Queue**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
> **(a)** Explain the array-based implementation of a linear queue with suitable diagrams. Write the `isEmpty()`, `isFull()`, `enqueue()`, and `dequeue()` functions in C. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a queue of maximum size 4, perform the following sequence of operations and show the contents of the queue, `front`, and `rear` after each step: `insert(A), insert(B), insert(C), delete(), insert(D), insert(E), delete(), delete()`. What error condition (if any) occurs? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation + C Functions (7 Marks)**

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes for the array schematic.)*

The array `queue[SIZE]` is paired with two indices, `front` and `rear`. Initially both are `-1` (empty). `rear` moves forward on every insertion; `front` moves forward on every deletion. **FIFO** discipline is maintained.

**C Functions:**
```c
#define SIZE 100
int queue[SIZE];
int front = -1, rear = -1;

int isEmpty() {
    return (front == -1 || front > rear);
}

int isFull() {
    return (rear == SIZE - 1);
}

void enqueue(int x) {
    if (isFull()) {
        printf("Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    if (front == -1) front = 0;
    rear++;
    queue[rear] = x;
}

int dequeue() {
    if (isEmpty()) {
        printf("Underflow\n");
        return -1;
    }
    int x = queue[front];
    front++;
    if (front > rear) front = rear = -1;
    return x;
}
```

*(2 marks for diagram, 1 mark each for `isEmpty`, `isFull`, `enqueue`, `dequeue`.)*

---

**(b) Trace Table (7 Marks)**

Let `SIZE = 4`, `front = -1`, `rear = -1`.

| Step | Operation | front | rear | Array State `[0] [1] [2] [3]` | Error/Note |
|------|-----------|-------|------|-------------------------------|-----------|
| 0 | Initial | $-1$ | $-1$ | [ \_ \_ \_ \_ ] | Empty |
| 1 | insert(A) | $0$ | $0$ | [ **A** \_ \_ \_ ] | OK |
| 2 | insert(B) | $0$ | $1$ | [ **A B** \_ \_ ] | OK |
| 3 | insert(C) | $0$ | $2$ | [ **A B C** \_ ] | OK |
| 4 | delete() | $1$ | $2$ | [ \_ **B C** \_ ] | Returns A |
| 5 | insert(D) | $1$ | $3$ | [ \_ **B C D** ] | OK (rear=3 = SIZE-1) |
| 6 | insert(E) | $1$ | $3$ | [ \_ **B C D** ] | **OVERFLOW** (rear=3 = SIZE-1) |
| 7 | delete() | $2$ | $3$ | [ \_ \_ **C D** ] | Returns B |
| 8 | delete() | $3$ | $3$ | [ \_ \_ \_ **D** ] | Returns C |

**Error Condition:** Step 6 triggers **OVERFLOW** because `rear = 3 = SIZE - 1` (queue is full) and yet only 3 elements are logically present — demonstrating the **False Overflow / Wasted Space** problem of the linear queue.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes — Avoid These!**
> 1. **Forgetting the `front == -1` check in ENQUEUE:** Many students directly do `rear++` even on first insertion, leaving `front = -1`, which makes `dequeue()` incorrectly report underflow. **Always reset `front = 0` on first insertion.**
> 2. **Using `rear == SIZE` instead of `rear == SIZE - 1`:** Since valid indices are `0` to `SIZE - 1`, the full condition is `rear == SIZE - 1`, not `SIZE`. This off-by-one error costs **2 marks** typically.
> 3. **Confusing `isEmpty` conditions:** Use `front == -1 OR front > rear`. Some students only check `front == -1` and miss the case where all elements are deleted (front crosses rear).
> 4. **Not resetting pointers after final dequeue:** If you don't reset `front = rear = -1` when `front > rear`, the queue will erroneously report "not empty" on the next operation since `front != -1`. KTU examiners specifically check this edge case.
> 5. **Confusing the role of `front`:** `front` always points to the **current** front element (not the position *before* the front). Be precise with pointer semantics.
> 6. **In trace questions, forgetting to update BOTH `front` and `rear`:** Always show both pointer values after **every** operation in the trace table.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Queues (Array)**
> - ☐ A **Queue** follows **FIFO** (First In, First Out) discipline.
> - ☐ Two ends: **Rear** (insertion) and **Front** (deletion).
> - ☐ Primitive operations: **Enqueue, Dequeue, Peek, isEmpty, isFull**.
> - ☐ All primitive operations have **O(1)** time complexity.
> - ☐ Space complexity is **O(n)** for an array of size `n`.
> - ☐ Initial state: `front = -1, rear = -1` (empty queue).
> - ☐ **Overflow** check: `rear == SIZE - 1` (queue full).
> - ☐ **Underflow** check: `front == -1 OR front > rear` (queue empty).
> - ☐ Element count: `n = rear - front + 1`.
> - ☐ On first insertion, **both** `front` and `rear` must be initialized (`front = 0, rear = 0`).
> - ☐ After the **last dequeue** (queue becomes empty), reset `front = rear = -1`.
> - ☐ **False Overflow** (memory wastage) is the chief limitation — solved by **Circular Queue** (next module).
> - ☐ Applications: **CPU scheduling, BFS, buffering, disk scheduling, call center systems**.
> - ☐ In C, always use **sentinel value** (e.g., `-1` or `INT_MIN`) to signal underflow errors from `dequeue()`.
> - ☐ Common analogy: **movie ticket line** — first to arrive, first to be served.
> - ☐ Real-time engineering uses: round-robin schedulers, IO request buffers, network packet routers.
> - ☐ **Trace questions** require showing `front`, `rear`, and the array after **every single step** — missing a step loses marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circular Queue implementation using Array
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Circular Queue implementation using Array # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Circular Queue** is a linear data structure that follows the **FIFO (First In First Out)** principle but is implemented in such a way that the **last position is connected back to the first position**, forming a logical circle. It is typically implemented using a **one-dimensional array** where the `rear` and `front` pointers are advanced using **modular arithmetic** (modulo operator, `%`). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *A Circular Queue is a variation of the standard queue in which the last element is logically connected back to the first element, thereby eliminating the "wasted space" problem (also called the "false overflow" problem) of a linear queue implemented using an array.* ## 1.2 Why Do We Need a Circular Queue? (Motivation) Before understanding the circular queue, we must understand the **limitation of a Linear Queue using an array**: | Property | Linear Queue (Array) | Circular Queue (Array) | |----------|----------------------|------------------------| | Memory Reuse | ❌ Wasted after dequeues | ✅ Fully reusable | | Rear Pointer Movement | Only forward (`rear++`) | Wraps around (`rear = (rear+1) % MAX`) | | Front Pointer Movement | Only forward (`front++`) | Wraps around (`front = (front+1) % MAX`) | | False Overflow | **Yes** (occurs even when slots exist) | **No** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **"False Overflow" Explained:** In a linear queue, even if there are empty slots at the front (because of previous dequeues), the queue shows "FULL" once `rear == MAX - 1`. The circular queue **solves this exact problem** by wrapping `rear` back to index `0` using modulo arithmetic. ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy 🎯 > **Analogy: A Circular Dining Table (Round Robin Seating)** > > Imagine **8 friends** sitting around a **circular table** with chairs numbered `0` to `7`. > - A **waiter** takes orders starting from chair `0`. > - When a friend leaves (dequeue), the **next person sits at the next chair clockwise**. > - When the waiter reaches chair `7` and a new person arrives, instead of saying *"No more chairs!"*, the waiter simply **moves to chair `0`** again. > - The table is **"full"** only when every chair is currently occupied (and not when the waiter just reaches the last chair). > > This wrapping behavior, governed by `index = (index + 1) % 8`, is the **essence of a circular queue**. ## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Board Glossary) - **MAX:** The maximum size of the underlying array. - **front:** Index of the element at the **front** of the queue (where deletion happens). - **rear:** Index of the element at the **rear** of the queue (where insertion happens). - **Enqueue (Insert):** Operation to add an element at the `rear`. - **Dequeue (Delete):** Operation to remove an element from the `front`. - **Peek / Front:** Operation to view the element at `front` without removing it. - **Modulo Wrap:** The mathematical operation `index % MAX` that allows index to circle back. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Core Logic — How Does the Circular Movement Work? The fundamental idea is that instead of incrementing indices linearly: $$\text{rear} = \text{rear} + 1$$ we use **modular arithmetic**: $$\text{rear} = (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX}$$ $$\text{front} = (\text{front} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX}$$ This ensures that when `rear` or `front` reaches `MAX - 1`, the next increment wraps it back to `0`. ### 2.1.1 Walkthrough with `MAX = 5` Suppose indices 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 form a circle. | Current `rear` | `(rear + 1) % 5` | New Position | |----------------|------------------|--------------| | 0 | `(0+1) % 5 = 1` | 1 | | 1 | `(1+1) % 5 = 2` | 2 | | 2 | `(2+1) % 5 = 3` | 3 | | 3 | `(3+1) % 5 = 4` | 4 | | **4** | **`(4+1) % 5 = 0`** | **0 (WRAP!)** | > [!TIP] > **Why `% MAX` works mathematically:** The modulo operator returns the **remainder** when one number is divided by another. For an array of size `MAX`, valid indices are `0` to `MAX-1`. The expression `(rear + 1) % MAX` will always produce a value in this range, achieving the wrap-around effect. ## 2.2 The "Full vs Empty" Problem — Three Standard Solutions This is the **most frequently asked KTU question** on circular queues. The challenge is that when `front == rear`, the queue can be either **EMPTY** or **FULL** — these two states are indistinguishable by pointer comparison alone. ### Solution 1: **Wasting One Slot** (Most Common in KTU syllabus) We sacrifice one array slot to differentiate between full and empty. $$\text{Queue is EMPTY} \iff \text{front} == -1$$ $$\text{Queue is FULL} \iff (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} == \text{front}$$ > Maximum usable capacity = `MAX - 1` slots. ### Solution 2: **Using a Count Variable** A separate integer `count` keeps track of the number of elements. $$\text{Queue is EMPTY} \iff \text{count} == 0$$ $$\text{Queue is FULL} \iff \text{count} == \text{MAX}$$ > Maximum usable capacity = `MAX` slots (full utilization). ### Solution 3: **Using a Flag Variable** A boolean `flag` indicates the **last operation performed**. - `flag = 0` after delete, `flag = 1` after insert. - If `front == rear` and `flag == 0` → **Empty** - If `front == rear` and `flag == 1` → **Full** > Maximum usable capacity = `MAX` slots (full utilization). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Reference Table — Memorize this for the exam!** | Operation / Condition | Formula (Slot-Wasting Method) | Notes | |-----------------------|-------------------------------|-------| | Initial State | `front = -1; rear = -1` | Both point to invalid index | | Initialize Enqueue | `front = 0; rear = 0; Q[rear] = x` | First element case | | Initialize Dequeue | `front = 0; rear = -1` (or reset) | Single-element removal | | Enqueue (Insert) | `rear = (rear + 1) % MAX; Q[rear] = x` | Check FULL before | | Dequeue (Delete) | `x = Q[front]; front = (front + 1) % MAX` | Check EMPTY before | | **Is EMPTY?** | `front == -1` | Alternative: `front == rear` (with count) | | **Is FULL?** | `(rear + 1) % MAX == front` | Slot-wasting method | | Effective Capacity | `MAX - 1` | For slot-wasting method | | Number of Elements | `(rear - front + MAX) % MAX` | General formula | | Front Element | `Q[front]` | Peek operation | | Rear Element | `Q[rear]` | Peek operation | | Time Complexity (Enqueue) | **O(1)** | Constant time | | Time Complexity (Dequeue) | **O(1)** | Constant time | | Space Complexity | **O(MAX)** | Static array allocation | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **CPU Scheduling (Round Robin Algorithm):** The OS cycles through processes in a circular fashion, and a circular queue is the natural data structure to maintain the ready queue. 2. **Memory Management (Circular Buffer):** Audio/video streaming applications use circular buffers to maintain a continuous stream of data without stuttering. 3. **Traffic Light Systems:** Traffic signals in a roundabout or junction cycle in a circular pattern. 4. **Disk Scheduling (C-SCAN, C-LOOK):** Disk arm moves circularly to service requests. 5. **Printer Spooling:** Multiple print jobs held in a circular buffer for the printer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Circular queues are the **backbone of real-time embedded systems** where predictable **O(1) latency** for both insertion and deletion is mandatory. Unlike linked lists, the array-based circular queue has better **cache locality**, making it faster in practice. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why `(rear + 1) % MAX == front` Indicates FULL We want to distinguish between an **empty** queue and a **full** queue. Both states have the property that `front == rear` after wrapping, so we cannot use pointer equality alone. **Logical Setup:** - Total array slots: `MAX` (indexed `0` to `MAX - 1`). - If we use **all `MAX` slots**, then when full: the slot **after** the last `rear` position is exactly `front`. - Mathematically, the slot after `rear` is `(rear + 1) % MAX`. **Conclusion:** If we let the queue occupy all `MAX` slots, the empty and full states both reduce to `front == rear`, which is **ambiguous**. **The Fix:** We deliberately leave **one slot empty** when the queue is full. Now: - **Full state:** The slot after `rear` is `front` → `(rear + 1) % MAX == front`. - **Empty state:** `front == -1` (a sentinel value that can never occur during normal use). **Trade-off:** Capacity reduces from `MAX` to `MAX - 1`. ## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Elements Formula We need a formula to compute the number of elements `n` currently in the queue, given `front` and `rear`. **Case 1: `rear >= front` (No wrap-around)** $$n = \text{rear} - \text{front} + 1$$ *Example:* `front = 2, rear = 4, MAX = 7` → `n = 4 - 2 + 1 = 3` elements (at indices 2, 3, 4). ✅ **Case 2: `rear < front` (Wrap-around has occurred)** $$n = \text{rear} - \text{front} + \text{MAX} + 1$$ *Example:* `front = 5, rear = 1, MAX = 7` → `n = 1 - 5 + 7 + 1 = 4` elements (at indices 5, 6, 0, 1). ✅ **Unified Formula (works in both cases):** $$n = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + \text{MAX}) \bmod \text{MAX}$$ Let's verify both cases: **Case 1:** `rear = 4, front = 2, MAX = 7` $$n = (4 - 2 + 7) \bmod 7 = 9 \bmod 7 = 2$$ Wait, this gives 2, but we expected 3. **Correction:** This formula works when `rear` and `front` point to the **last element** and **first element** respectively. In the slot-wasting convention, the *number of occupied slots* is actually `(rear - front + MAX) % MAX` **plus 1** when elements exist, or it's `0` when empty. The cleaner way is to use a `count` variable (incremented on enqueue, decremented on dequeue). **Alternative unified formula for slot-wasting method:** $$\text{Number of elements} = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + 1 + \text{MAX}) \bmod \text{MAX}$$ *Verification Case 1:* `rear = 4, front = 2, MAX = 7` $$(4 - 2 + 1 + 7) \bmod 7 = 10 \bmod 7 = 3 \quad \checkmark$$ *Verification Case 2:* `rear = 1, front = 5, MAX = 7` $$(1 - 5 + 1 + 7) \bmod 7 = 4 \bmod 7 = 4 \quad \checkmark$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Trace — Enqueue/Dequeue Operations Let's perform a **complete trace** with `MAX = 5` (slot-wasting method). The queue can hold at most **4 elements**. | Step | Operation | Status | `front` | `rear` | Queue Contents (Q[0..4]) | Comment | |------|-----------|--------|---------|--------|--------------------------|---------| | 1 | Init | Empty | -1 | -1 | `[_, _, _, _, _]` | Initial state | | 2 | Enqueue 10 | OK | 0 | 0 | `[10, _, _, _, _]` | Special case: both set to 0 | | 3 | Enqueue 20 | OK | 0 | 1 | `[10, 20, _, _, _]` | `rear = (0+1)%5 = 1` | | 4 | Enqueue 30 | OK | 0 | 2 | `[10, 20, 30, _, _]` | `rear = (1+1)%5 = 2` | | 5 | Dequeue | OK, returns 10 | 1 | 2 | `[_, 20, 30, _, _]` | `front = (0+1)%5 = 1` | | 6 | Enqueue 40 | OK | 1 | 3 | `[_, 20, 30, 40, _]` | `rear = (2+1)%5 = 3` | | 7 | Enqueue 50 | OK | 1 | 4 | `[_, 20, 30, 40, 50]` | `rear = (3+1)%5 = 4` | | 8 | **Enqueue 60** | **OVERFLOW!** | 1 | 4 | `[_, 20, 30, 40, 50]` | `(rear+1)%5 = 0`, `front = 1` → not full yet? Wait, **`(4+1)%5 = 0 ≠ 1`** → NOT full! | | 8' | Enqueue 60 (corrected) | OK | 1 | 0 | `[60, 20, 30, 40, 50]` | `rear = (4+1)%5 = 0` (wrap!) | | 9 | **Enqueue 70** | **OVERFLOW!** | 1 | 0 | `[60, 20, 30, 40, 50]` | `(rear+1)%5 = 1 == front` → FULL ✓ | | 10 | Dequeue | OK, returns 20 | 2 | 0 | `[60, _, 30, 40, 50]` | `front = (1+1)%5 = 2` | | 11 | **Enqueue 70** | OK | 2 | 1 | `[60, 70, 30, 40, 50]` | Now `rear` wraps further. `(0+1)%5 = 1` | > [!TIP] > **Critical Insight from the Trace:** Notice how in **Step 8'**, the `rear` pointer successfully wrapped from index `4` back to index `0`. This is exactly what would have been **impossible in a linear queue** — it would have falsely reported "overflow" at this point. ## 3.4 Full C Implementation (KTU Board-Ready) ```c #include #define MAX 100 int queue[MAX]; int front = -1, rear = -1; /* Check if queue is empty */ int isEmpty() { return (front == -1); } /* Check if queue is full (slot-wasting method) */ int isFull() { return ((rear + 1) % MAX == front); } /* Enqueue operation */ void enqueue(int x) { if (isFull()) { printf("Queue Overflow!\n"); return; } if (front == -1) // First element insertion front = 0; rear = (rear + 1) % MAX; queue[rear] = x; printf("%d enqueued.\n", x); } /* Dequeue operation */ int dequeue() { if (isEmpty()) { printf("Queue Underflow!\n"); return -1; } int x = queue[front]; if (front == rear) { // Only one element front = -1; rear = -1; } else { front = (front + 1) % MAX; } return x; } /* Peek front element */ int peek() { if (isEmpty()) { printf("Queue is empty.\n"); return -1; } return queue[front]; } /* Display the queue */ void display() { if (isEmpty()) { printf("Queue is empty.\n"); return; } printf("Queue: "); int i = front; while (1) { printf("%d ", queue[i]); if (i == rear) break; i = (i + 1) % MAX; } printf("\n"); } int main() { enqueue(10); enqueue(20); enqueue(30); display(); printf("Dequeued: %d\n", dequeue()); display(); return 0; } ``` ## 3.5 Complexity Analysis Matrix | Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space | |-----------|-----------|--------------|------------|-------| | Enqueue | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) extra | | Dequeue | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) extra | | Peek | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) extra | | isEmpty | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) extra | | isFull | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) extra | | Display | O(n) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) extra | | **Overall Space** | — | — | — | **O(MAX)** | > [!NOTE] > **Why O(1) for Enqueue/Dequeue?** Each operation only moves the `front` or `rear` pointer by one step using modulo arithmetic. It does **not** depend on the number of elements in the queue. This is a key advantage over linked-list-based queues (which require dynamic memory allocation, O(1) amortized but with overhead). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Circular Queue Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Client Code] --> B{Choose Operation} B -->|Insert| C[enqueue function] B -->|Delete| D[dequeue function] B -->|View| E[peek function] B -->|Check State| F[isEmpty isFull functions] C --> G{isFull check} G -->|Yes| H[Print Overflow Return] G -->|No| I[Update rear using modulo] I --> J[Place element in array] D --> K{isEmpty check} K -->|Yes| L[Print Underflow Return] K -->|No| M[Read element at front] M --> N[Update front using modulo] J --> O[(Array Storage MAX slots)] N --> O E --> O O --> P[front pointer] O --> Q[rear pointer] style A fill:#e1f5ff style O fill:#fff4e1 style P fill:#d4edda style Q fill:#f8d7da ``` ## 4.2 State Diagram — Queue Lifecycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Empty: Initialize front=-1, rear=-1 Empty --> NotEmpty: enqueue first element
front=0, rear=0 NotEmpty --> NotEmpty: enqueue rear=(rear+1)%MAX NotEmpty --> NotEmpty: dequeue front=(front+1)%MAX NotEmpty --> NotEmpty: peek view Q[front] NotEmpty --> Empty: dequeue last element
front=-1, rear=-1 NotEmpty --> Full: enqueue when (rear+1)%MAX==front Full --> Full: peek view Q[front] Full --> NotEmpty: dequeue front=(front+1)%MAX Full --> [*]: enqueue rejected with overflow ``` ## 4.3 Logical Array Layout — Before and After Wrap ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Linear[LINEAR QUEUE Problem] L0[Index 0: 10] L1[Index 1: 20] L2[Index 2: 30] L3[Index 3: EMPTY] L4[Index 4: EMPTY] L5[rear=2 stuck here] L6[Even though slots 3,4 are free] end subgraph Circular[CIRCULAR QUEUE Solution] C0[Index 0: 50] C1[Index 1: EMPTY] C2[Index 2: 10] C3[Index 3: 20] C4[Index 4: 30] C5[rear wraps to 0] C6[front moves to 2] end Linear -.->|rear wraps via modulo| Circular style L5 fill:#ffcccc style L6 fill:#ffcccc style C5 fill:#ccffcc style C6 fill:#ccffcc ``` ## 4.4 Pointer Movement Visualization (Sequential) ```mermaid flowchart LR S0["Step 0: front=-1, rear=-1 EMPTY"] --> S1["Step 1: Insert 10
front=0, rear=0"] S1 --> S2["Step 2: Insert 20
front=0, rear=1"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: Insert 30
front=0, rear=2"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Delete
front=1, rear=2
returns 10"] S4 --> S5["Step 5: Insert 40
front=1, rear=3"] S5 --> S6["Step 6: Insert 50
front=1, rear=4"] S6 --> S7["Step 7: Insert 60
front=1, rear=0
WRAP! rear mod 5"] S7 --> S8["Step 8: Insert 70
OVERFLOW! (rear+1)%5=1==front"] S8 --> S9["Step 9: Delete
front=2, rear=0
returns 20"] S9 --> S10["Step 10: Insert 70
front=2, rear=1
OK!"] style S0 fill:#e0e0e0 style S8 fill:#ffcccc style S7 fill:#ccffcc style S10 fill:#ccffcc ``` ## 4.5 Comparison Block Diagram — Linear vs Circular ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LQ[LINEAR QUEUE] LD[Array: MAX slots] LF[front pointer - only moves right] LR[rear pointer - only moves right] LP[Problem: Wasted space at front] end subgraph CQ[CIRCULAR QUEUE] CD[Array: MAX slots logically circular] CF[front pointer - wraps using modulo] CR[rear pointer - wraps using modulo] CS[Solution: Space fully reused] end LQ -->|rear reaches end| LP CQ -->|rear reaches end| CR CR -->|modulo MAX| CW[rear goes back to 0] CW -->|continues normally| CR LP -->|wasted memory| LX[Result: False Overflow] CW -->|efficient memory| CY[Result: No False Overflow] style LP fill:#ffcccc style LX fill:#ffcccc style CS fill:#ccffcc style CY fill:#ccffcc ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **What is a circular queue? How does it overcome the limitation of a linear queue implemented using an array?** **Model Answer:** A **circular queue** is a linear data structure that follows the **FIFO** principle, implemented using a one-dimensional array where the **last position is logically connected to the first position**, forming a circle. The `rear` and `front` pointers are advanced using **modular arithmetic**: `rear = (rear + 1) % MAX` and `front = (front + 1) % MAX`. **Overcoming Linear Queue Limitation:** In a linear queue, once the `rear` pointer reaches `MAX - 1`, no more elements can be inserted, even if slots at the front have been freed by previous dequeues. This is called the **"false overflow"** problem. In a circular queue, when `rear` reaches `MAX - 1`, the next increment wraps it back to `0` using `% MAX`. This allows the freed slots to be **reused**, eliminating false overflow and ensuring full memory utilization. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]** **Write the conditions to check whether a circular queue (using array, with one slot wasted) is EMPTY and FULL.** **Model Answer:** Let `MAX` be the size of the array, `front` point to the first element, and `rear` point to the last element. **Condition for EMPTY queue:** $$\text{front} == -1$$ (Initially both `front` and `rear` are set to `-1`. The queue becomes empty again only when the last element is dequeued, resetting both pointers to `-1`.) **Condition for FULL queue:** $$(\text{rear} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} == \text{front}$$ (One slot is intentionally left vacant to differentiate FULL from EMPTY. The slot immediately after `rear` is `(rear + 1) % MAX`. If this equals `front`, the queue is full.) **Effective Capacity:** `MAX - 1` elements. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]** **(a)** Explain the concept of a circular queue with a neat diagram. Write the algorithm for ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations on a circular queue implemented using an array, and explain how the "full" and "empty" conditions are handled. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider a circular queue of size **5** implemented using an array with the slot-wasting method. The queue initially contains the elements **10, 20, 30** (with `10` at the front). Perform the following operations in sequence and show the status of `front` and `rear` after each step: 1. Enqueue 40 2. Enqueue 50 3. Dequeue 4. Enqueue 60 5. Enqueue 70 6. Dequeue Clearly indicate when **overflow** or **underflow** occurs. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### **Part (a) — Concept and Algorithm (7 marks)** **Concept:** A circular queue is a FIFO data structure implemented using an array where `rear` and `front` pointers wrap around using modulo arithmetic. It eliminates the **false overflow** problem of linear queues. **Neat Diagram:** ``` front ↓ ┌────┬────┬────┬────┬────┐ │ 20 │ 30 │ 40 │ │ │ ← Array of size 5 └────┴────┴────┴────┴────┘ ↑ rear ``` **ENQUEUE Algorithm:** ``` Algorithm: ENQUEUE(Q, x) 1. IF (rear + 1) % MAX == front THEN 2. PRINT "Queue Overflow" 3. RETURN 4. END IF 5. IF front == -1 THEN // First insertion 6. front = 0 7. END IF 8. rear = (rear + 1) % MAX 9. Q[rear] = x 10. END ``` **DEQUEUE Algorithm:** ``` Algorithm: DEQUEUE(Q) 1. IF front == -1 THEN 2. PRINT "Queue Underflow" 3. RETURN 4. END IF 5. x = Q[front] 6. IF front == rear THEN // Only one element 7. front = -1 8. rear = -1 9. ELSE 10. front = (front + 1) % MAX 11. END IF 12. RETURN x 13. END ``` **EMPTY and FULL Conditions:** - **EMPTY:** `front == -1` (no elements present; both pointers reset). - **FULL:** `(rear + 1) % MAX == front` (one slot is left vacant to distinguish from empty). - One slot is **wasted** to avoid ambiguity. Effective capacity = `MAX - 1`. --- #### **Part (b) — Trace Table (7 marks)** **Initial State:** Queue contains `[10, 20, 30, _, _]`, `front = 0`, `rear = 2`, `MAX = 5`. | Step | Operation | Status | `front` | `rear` | Queue Contents (Q[0..4]) | Explanation | |------|-----------|--------|---------|--------|--------------------------|-------------| | 1 | Enqueue 40 | ✅ OK | 0 | 3 | `[10, 20, 30, 40, _]` | `rear = (2+1)%5 = 3` | | 2 | Enqueue 50 | ✅ OK | 0 | 4 | `[10, 20, 30, 40, 50]` | `rear = (3+1)%5 = 4` | | 3 | Dequeue | ✅ OK, returns 10 | 1 | 4 | `[_, 20, 30, 40, 50]` | `front = (0+1)%5 = 1` | | 4 | Enqueue 60 | ✅ OK (wrap!) | 1 | 0 | `[60, 20, 30, 40, 50]` | `rear = (4+1)%5 = 0` | | 5 | Enqueue 70 | ❌ **OVERFLOW** | 1 | 0 | `[60, 20, 30, 40, 50]` | `(rear+1)%5 = 1 == front` → FULL | | 6 | Dequeue | ✅ OK, returns 20 | 2 | 0 | `[60, _, 30, 40, 50]` | `front = (1+1)%5 = 2` | **Summary of OVERFLOW/UNDERFLOW:** - **Step 5** reports **OVERFLOW** — the queue is full and cannot accept `70`. - No **UNDERFLOW** occurred in this sequence. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Differentiate between a **linear queue** and a **circular queue** implemented using an array. Mention at least **four points** of difference. Explain with a diagram how the **"false overflow"** problem occurs in a linear queue and how a circular queue solves it. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a complete **C program** to implement a circular queue of integers using an array of size `MAX = 10`. Your program must support the following operations: `enqueue`, `dequeue`, `display`, and `isEmpty`. Use the **slot-wasting method** to distinguish between empty and full conditions. Show the output when the following operations are performed: Enqueue(5), Enqueue(10), Enqueue(15), Dequeue(), Enqueue(20), Display(). **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### **Part (a) — Differences and False Overflow (7 marks)** | S.No. | Linear Queue | Circular Queue | |-------|--------------|----------------| | 1 | `rear` and `front` move only in **one direction** (forward). | `rear` and `front` **wrap around** using modulo `% MAX`. | | 2 | Suffers from **false overflow** (wasted memory at front). | **No false overflow** — freed slots are reused. | | 3 | `rear = rear + 1` only. | `rear = (rear + 1) % MAX` (modular). | | 4 | Insertion fails when `rear == MAX - 1`. | Insertion fails only when `(rear+1)%MAX == front`. | | 5 | Simple implementation. | Slightly more complex due to modulo logic. | | 6 | Used in simple buffer scenarios. | Used in **Round Robin scheduling, circular buffers**. | **False Overflow Diagram (Linear Queue):** ``` State: After some enqueues and dequeues front → ┌────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬────┐ │ │ 20 │ 30 │ 40 │ 50 │ 50 │ ← Q[5] is "overflow" └────┴────┴────┴────┴────┴────┘ ↑ rear Slots at indices 0,1 are WASTED but not reusable! ``` **Circular Queue Solution:** ``` front → rear ↓ ↓ ┌────┬────┬────┬────┬────┐ │ 50 │ 20 │ 30 │ 40 │ 50 │ ← rear wraps to index 0 └────┴────┴────┴────┴────┘ ↑ freed slot is REUSED ``` In the circular queue, when `rear` reaches index `5`, instead of stopping, it wraps back to index `0` using `% MAX`, reusing the slot freed by the earlier dequeue. This **eliminates false overflow**. --- #### **Part (b) — C Program (7 marks)** ```c #include #define MAX 10 int queue[MAX]; int front = -1, rear = -1; int isEmpty() { return (front == -1); } int isFull() { return ((rear + 1) % MAX == front); } void enqueue(int x) { if (isFull()) { printf("Overflow!\n"); return; } if (front == -1) front = 0; rear = (rear + 1) % MAX; queue[rear] = x; printf("Enqueued: %d\n", x); } int dequeue() { if (isEmpty()) { printf("Underflow!\n"); return -1; } int x = queue[front]; if (front == rear) { front = -1; rear = -1; } else { front = (front + 1) % MAX; } return x; } void display() { if (isEmpty()) { printf("Queue is empty.\n"); return; } printf("Queue contents: "); int i = front; while (1) { printf("%d ", queue[i]); if (i == rear) break; i = (i + 1) % MAX; } printf("\n"); } int main() { enqueue(5); enqueue(10); enqueue(15); printf("Dequeued: %d\n", dequeue()); enqueue(20); display(); return 0; } ``` **Output:** ``` Enqueued: 5 Enqueued: 10 Enqueued: 15 Dequeued: 5 Enqueued: 20 Queue contents: 10 15 20 ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the first-element case:** Many students write `rear = (rear + 1) % MAX` without checking if `front == -1` first. Result: insertion into `queue[-1]` → undefined behavior / wrong output. **Lose 2 marks.** > 2. **Confusing FULL and EMPTY:** Writing `front == rear` for FULL is **wrong** in slot-wasting method. The correct condition is `(rear + 1) % MAX == front`. **Lose 2-3 marks.** > 3. **Forgetting to reset pointers after last dequeue:** When the queue becomes empty after dequeuing the last element, you **must** reset `front = -1` and `rear = -1`. Otherwise, the next enqueue will fail. **Lose 1 mark.** > 4. **Writing `(rear + 1) == MAX` instead of modulo:** This is the linear queue check, not circular. **Lose 2 marks.** > 5. **Modulo operator with negative numbers:** In C, `-1 % 5 = -1`, not `4`. Always ensure `rear` and `front` are non-negative before applying `%`. The slot-wasting method avoids this pitfall by using `front = -1` only as a sentinel, not in arithmetic. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam!** - ✅ **Circular Queue** = FIFO + array + modulo wrap-around. - ✅ The **fundamental formula** for pointer movement is `index = (index + 1) % MAX`. - ✅ The **"false overflow"** problem in linear queues is the main reason circular queues exist. - ✅ The **EMPTY** condition (slot-wasting) is `front == -1`. - ✅ The **FULL** condition (slot-wasting) is `(rear + 1) % MAX == front`. - ✅ One slot is **always wasted** in the slot-wasting method → effective capacity = `MAX - 1`. - ✅ Alternative methods to distinguish empty/full: **count variable** or **flag variable** (both allow full `MAX` capacity). - ✅ **Time complexity** of Enqueue, Dequeue, Peek, isEmpty, isFull = **O(1)**. - ✅ **Space complexity** = **O(MAX)**. - ✅ Circular queues are used in **CPU scheduling (Round Robin)**, **memory buffers**, **disk scheduling**, and **real-time systems**. - ✅ Number of elements = `(rear - front + 1 + MAX) % MAX` (unified formula for slot-wasting method). - ✅ **Common KTU question types:** Algorithm tracing, code writing, "what if" modifications (e.g., "what if MAX = 7 and you perform 8 enqueues?"), and full vs. empty condition derivations. - ✅ Always **handle the first-element case** separately when `front == -1`. - ✅ **Reset pointers to -1** when the queue becomes empty after the last dequeue. - ✅ The slot-wasting method is the **most commonly tested** in KTU — memorize it thoroughly.

topics.title: Other types of Queues: Dequeue, Priority Queue (Concept only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Other types of Queues: Dequeue, Priority Queue (Concept only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Dequeue (Double-Ended Queue)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)
A **Deque (Double-Ended Queue)**, pronounced *"deck"*, is a generalized queue abstract data type in which elements can be **inserted or deleted from either the front end or the rear end**. It therefore combines the capabilities of both stacks (LIFO) and queues (FIFO) into a single, flexible linear structure.

Mathematically, a Deque of capacity $n$ maintains two moving pointers:
- $F$ — Index of the front element
- $R$ — Index of the rear element

where both ends are independently accessible for both insertion and deletion operations.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 1, the **concept and operations** of Deque are mandatory. Implementation-level array/linked list code is generally assessed under Module 2 (Linear Data Structures).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Way Escalator
Imagine a hallway with **two automatic doors**, one at each end. People may enter or exit from **either door** freely. Unlike a regular queue (where you enter only from the rear and exit only from the front), the deque lets you push in or pop out at both ends. It is essentially a **"super-queue"** that behaves like a queue, a stack, or a hybrid — depending on how you choose to operate it.

### Classification of Deques
There are **two principal variants** of the deque:

1. **Input-Restricted Deque (IRD)** — Insertion is allowed only at the **rear**, but deletion is allowed at **both ends**.
2. **Output-Restricted Deque (ORD)** — Deletion is allowed only at the **front**, but insertion is allowed at **both ends**.

| Variant | Insert at Front | Insert at Rear | Delete from Front | Delete from Rear |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Generalized Deque** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Input-Restricted Deque** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Output-Restricted Deque** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |

> [!NOTE] > A standard **Stack** can be implemented using an Input-Restricted Deque (insert/delete only at one end), and a standard **Queue** can be implemented using an Output-Restricted Deque.

---

## 1.2 Priority Queue

### Formal Definition
A **Priority Queue** is an abstract data type (ADT) in which every element is associated with a **priority value**, and the deletion (or sometimes insertion) operation is performed according to the **highest priority** rather than the order of arrival. The element with the **highest priority** is always removed first, irrespective of when it was inserted.

Formally, a priority queue $Q$ over elements $\{(k_i, v_i)\}$ where $k_i$ is the key (priority) and $v_i$ is the value satisfies:

$$\text{DeleteMax}(Q) = \arg\max_{k_i} \{(k_i, v_i)\}$$

In a **Min-Priority Queue**, the lowest key is dequeued first:

$$\text{DeleteMin}(Q) = \arg\min_{k_i} \{(k_i, v_i)\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > A Priority Queue is **not** a strict FIFO structure. Two elements inserted at different times may be served in an order that does not match their insertion sequence.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room
Consider an **Emergency Room (ER)** at a hospital. Patients arrive at different times, but they are **not treated in arrival order**. Instead, a patient with a **critical (heart attack)** condition is treated immediately, even if 20 other patients arrived earlier. The "priority" here is medical severity. This is precisely how a **Priority Queue** functions inside computer systems — Operating System schedulers, Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm, and Huffman encoding all rely on this principle.

### Key Characteristics
- **Priority assignment:** Each element carries a key/priority — either **numerical** or **ordinal**.
- **Order of service:** Determined by priority, not insertion time.
- **Stability:** A *stable* priority queue preserves the relative insertion order of elements having **equal priority**.
- **Tie-breaking:** When two elements share equal priority, a secondary rule (typically FIFO) is applied.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deque — Operational Logic Decomposition

A deque supports **eight fundamental operations**, partitioned into insertion, deletion, inspection, and utility categories:

### 2.1.1 The Four Core Mutation Operations
1. **`insertFront(x)`** — Inserts element $x$ at the front end.
   - Pre-condition: Queue is **NOT full** (`F \neq (R+1) \bmod n`).
   - Effect: $F \leftarrow (F - 1 + n) \bmod n$; then $A[F] \leftarrow x$.
2. **`insertRear(x)`** — Inserts element $x$ at the rear end.
   - Pre-condition: Queue is **NOT full**.
   - Effect: $R \leftarrow (R + 1) \bmod n$; then $A[R] \leftarrow x$.
3. **`deleteFront()`** — Removes element from the front.
   - Pre-condition: Queue is **NOT empty** (`F \neq R`).
   - Effect: $x \leftarrow A[F]$; then $F \leftarrow (F + 1) \bmod n$.
4. **`deleteRear()`** — Removes element from the rear.
   - Pre-condition: Queue is **NOT empty**.
   - Effect: $x \leftarrow A[R]$; then $R \leftarrow (R - 1 + n) \bmod n$.

### 2.1.2 Inspection Operations
- **`getFront()`** — Returns $A[F]$ without modification.
- **`getRear()`** — Returns $A[R]$ without modification.

### 2.1.3 Utility Operations
- **`isEmpty()`** — Returns TRUE if $F = -1$ (or $F = R = 0$ in alternative conventions).
- **`isFull()`** — Returns TRUE if $(R + 1) \bmod n = F$ (one slot wasted for differentiation).
- **`size()`** — Returns $(R - F + n) \bmod n$.

### 2.1.4 The Circular Logic — Why Modular Arithmetic?
Because a linear array implementation would waste memory after repeated front deletions, the deque uses a **circular array**. The indices wrap around using the modulus operator $\bmod n$, allowing the array to behave as a logically continuous ring buffer. This is what enables both ends to grow indefinitely without shifting elements.

---

## 2.2 Priority Queue — Operational Logic Decomposition

A priority queue supports at least two fundamental operations:

### 2.2.1 Core Operations
1. **`insert(item, priority)`** — Adds a new element with its associated priority.
2. **`extractMax()` / `extractMin()`** — Removes and returns the element with the highest (or lowest) priority.
3. **`peek()` / `getMax()` / `getMin()`** — Inspects the highest-priority element **without removal**.
4. **`increaseKey(i, newKey)`** — Updates the priority of an existing element (used in heap-based implementations).

### 2.2.2 Classification by Ordering Direction

| Type | Service Order | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Max-Heap / Descending Priority Queue** | Highest key first | OS Process Scheduling (priority), Huffman's Max-Heap variant |
| **Min-Heap / Ascending Priority Queue** | Lowest key first | Dijkstra's Algorithm, Prim's MST, Event-driven simulation |

### 2.2.3 Comparison with Ordinary Queue
In a standard FIFO queue, the **arrival time** dictates the **departure order**. In a priority queue, the **priority value** dictates the **departure order**, and arrival time becomes a tie-breaker. This single conceptual shift makes priority queues enormously powerful for optimization problems.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Data Structure | Real-World Application |
|---|---|
| **Deque** | Browser back/forward navigation, A-Steal Job Scheduling, Undo-Redo editors, Sliding window problems in streaming algorithms |
| **Priority Queue** | Dijkstra's & Prim's graph algorithms, Huffman coding compression, OS CPU scheduling (Priority-based), Bandwidth management in routers, A* search algorithm in AI |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Deque — Empty Condition | $F = R = -1$ (or initial sentinel) | $F$ = Front, $R$ = Rear |
| 2 | Deque — Full Condition (Circular) | $(R + 1) \bmod n = F$ | $n$ = capacity |
| 3 | Deque — Number of Elements | $\text{size} = (R - F + n) \bmod n$ | modular arithmetic |
| 4 | Deque — Front Pointer after insertion | $F \leftarrow (F - 1 + n) \bmod n$ | wraps backward |
| 5 | Deque — Rear Pointer after insertion | $R \leftarrow (R + 1) \bmod n$ | wraps forward |
| 6 | Priority Queue — Max-Extraction | $\arg\max_{k \in Q} \; k$ | extract highest priority |
| 7 | Priority Queue — Min-Extraction | $\arg\min_{k \in Q} \; k$ | extract lowest priority |
| 8 | Time Complexity — Insert (Unsorted Array) | $O(1)$ | constant |
| 9 | Time Complexity — Extract (Unsorted Array) | $O(n)$ | linear search |
| 10 | Time Complexity — Insert (Sorted Array) | $O(n)$ | shift elements |
| 11 | Time Complexity — Extract (Sorted Array) | $O(1)$ | direct access |
| 12 | Time Complexity — Heap Operations | $O(\log n)$ | balanced tree height |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deque — Circular Index Derivation

### 3.1.1 Why $(R + 1) \bmod n$ Detects Full State
In a circular array of size $n$, we **intentionally waste one slot** to distinguish the "empty" state from the "full" state without an additional boolean flag.

**Derivation:**

Consider $n = 5$ slots labelled $0, 1, 2, 3, 4$. Initially, $F = 0$ and $R = -1$.

*Step 1: Insert three elements (10, 20, 30) from the rear.*

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{After insertRear(10)}:&\quad R = (R + 1) \bmod 5 = 0 \\
\text{After insertRear(20)}:&\quad R = (R + 1) \bmod 5 = 1 \\
\text{After insertRear(30)}:&\quad R = (R + 1) \bmod 5 = 2 \\
&\quad F = 0,\; R = 2,\; \text{size} = (2 - 0 + 5) \bmod 5 = 2
\end{aligned}$$

*Step 2: Continue inserting to fill the array.*

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{After insertRear(40)}:&\quad R = 3 \\
\text{After insertRear(50)}:&\quad R = 4
\end{aligned}$$

*Step 3: Test the "full" detection formula.*

$$\begin{aligned}
(R + 1) \bmod n &= (4 + 1) \bmod 5 = 5 \bmod 5 = 0 \\
F &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Since $(R + 1) \bmod n = F$, the array is detected as **full**, even though slot $0$ is occupied. This is why one slot must be sacrificed — to use the modular equality as a full-state indicator.

### 3.1.2 Why $(R - F + n) \bmod n$ Calculates Size
**Derivation:**

Without the $+n$ correction, if $R < F$ (which happens after wrap-around), $(R - F)$ would be **negative**. For example, with $F = 4, R = 1, n = 5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(R - F) &= (1 - 4) = -3 \quad \text{(WRONG)} \\
(R - F + n) &= (1 - 4 + 5) = 2 \quad \text{(CORRECT)} \\
(R - F + n) \bmod n &= 2 \bmod 5 = 2
\end{aligned}$$

The expression $(R - F + n) \bmod n$ always produces a value in $[0, n-1]$, correctly representing the logical distance from $F$ to $R$ along the circular ring.

---

## 3.2 Priority Queue — Complexity Analysis Across Representations

### 3.2.1 Comparative Analytical Matrix

The choice of internal representation drastically affects time complexity. The following table — **frequently asked in KTU exams** — must be memorized:

| Representation | Insert | Extract-Max/Min | Peek | Space |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unsorted Array** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Sorted Array (Ascending)** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Unsorted Linked List** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Sorted Linked List (Ascending)** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Binary Heap** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |

### 3.2.2 Derivation of Heap-Based Complexity

A binary heap is a **complete binary tree** stored in an array. The height $h$ of a complete tree with $n$ nodes satisfies:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower bound:} \quad &\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor \leq h \leq \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor \\
\text{Therefore:} \quad &h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor
\end{aligned}$$

The worst-case cost of an `insert` operation is the cost of bubbling the new element up the tree, which traverses at most $h$ levels. Hence:

$$\text{Cost of insert} = O(h) = O(\log_2 n)$$

Similarly, the cost of `extract-min` involves replacing the root with the last leaf and then **sifting down**, again traversing $O(h) = O(\log_2 n)$ levels:

$$\text{Cost of extract-min} = O(h) = O(\log_2 n)$$

This is why the binary heap is the **most efficient** practical implementation of a priority queue among elementary structures.

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example — Priority Queue Insertion Trace

Consider inserting keys $\{20, 15, 30, 5, 10\}$ into an initially empty **Min-Heap** (ascending priority queue):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Insert 20:} \quad &\text{Heap} = [20] \\
\text{Insert 15:} \quad &\text{Heap} = [15, 20] \quad (\text{15 < 20, swap})\\
\text{Insert 30:} \quad &\text{Heap} = [15, 20, 30] \quad (\text{30 > 15, no swap})\\
\text{Insert 5:} \quad &\text{Heap} = [5, 15, 30, 20] \quad (\text{5 < 15, swap; 5 > none, stop})\\
\text{Insert 10:} \quad &\text{Heap} = [5, 10, 30, 20, 15] \quad (\text{10 < 15, swap})
\end{aligned}$$

After the final insertion, the array representation is:

$$A = [5, 10, 30, 20, 15]$$

`extractMin()` returns $5$ (the root), and the heap is rebuilt with $10$ becoming the new minimum.

> [!NOTE] > The parent-child indexing for array-based heap is governed by the relationships: > - Parent of node $i$: $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor$ > - Left child of node $i$: $2i$ > - Right child of node $i$: $2i + 1$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Deque — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following mermaid diagram depicts the conceptual architecture of a Generalized Deque, highlighting how both ends act as **independent access ports** to the underlying container.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["INSERT FRONT"] --> nodeB["DEQUE CONTAINER"]
    nodeC["INSERT REAR"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD["DELETE FRONT"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["DELETE REAR"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["GET FRONT"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["GET REAR"]
    nodeH["ISEMPTY"] --> nodeB
    nodeI["ISFULL"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeJ["SIZE"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The *Deque Container* acts as the central storage. Six modification/access operations are exposed symmetrically — three involving the front (`INSERT FRONT`, `DELETE FRONT`, `GET FRONT`) and three involving the rear (`INSERT REAR`, `DELETE REAR`, `GET REAR`). Two state queries (`ISEMPTY`, `ISFULL`) and one cardinality operator (`SIZE`) complete the ADT.

---

## 4.2 Deque — Variants Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["DEQUE FAMILY"] --> nodeB["GENERALIZED DEQUE"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["INPUT RESTRICTED DEQUE"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["OUTPUT RESTRICTED DEQUE"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Insert Front Allowed"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Insert Rear Allowed"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["Delete Front Allowed"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["Delete Rear Allowed"]
    nodeC --> nodeI["Insert Rear Only"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ["Delete Front and Rear"]
    nodeD --> nodeK["Insert Front and Rear"]
    nodeD --> nodeL["Delete Front Only"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The Input-Restricted Deque is essentially a **stack appended to the rear of a queue**, while the Output-Restricted Deque is a **queue appended to the top of a stack**. The Generalized Deque subsumes both as the unrestricted parent class.

---

## 4.3 Deque — Circular Array State Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["INDEX 0"] --> nodeB["INDEX 1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["INDEX 2"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["INDEX 3"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["INDEX 4"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["WRAPS BACK TO INDEX 0"]
    nodeG["FRONT POINTER F"] --> nodeB
    nodeH["REAR POINTER R"] --> nodeE
```

**Reading the diagram:** This illustrates the **circular wrapping property**. The rear pointer at index $4$ will, on the next `insertRear`, wrap to index $0$ (skipping the empty slot reserved for full-state detection). This single-slot sacrifice enables the elegant full-vs-empty distinction using only modular arithmetic.

---

## 4.4 Priority Queue — Heap-Based Tree Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["10 ROOT MIN ELEMENT"] --> nodeB["20"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["30"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["40"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["50"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["60"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["70"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The root contains the minimum element (10), satisfying the **Min-Heap property**: every parent node is **less than or equal to** its children. The tree is **complete** — filled level by level, left to right. The corresponding array representation is:

$$A = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70]$$

with index $0$ as the root. Children of node $i$ are at indices $2i+1$ and $2i+2$.

---

## 4.5 Priority Queue — Operation Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["PRIORITY QUEUE"] --> nodeB["INSERT element priority"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["EXTRACT MAX or MIN"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["PEEK inspect root"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["INCREASE KEY"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["DECREASE KEY"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["HEAPIFY UP sift upward"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["HEAPIFY DOWN sift downward"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["RETURN A0 constant time"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ["MAINTAIN HEAP PROPERTY"]
    nodeF --> nodeK["MAINTAIN HEAP PROPERTY"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** `INSERT` triggers an **upward sift** to restore the heap invariant, while `EXTRACT` triggers a **downward sift**. Both sift operations are bounded by $O(\log n)$ — the height of the complete binary tree.

---

## 4.6 Comparative Block Diagram — When To Use Which Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["QUEUE FAMILY"] --> nodeB["SIMPLE FIFO QUEUE"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["DEQUE"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["PRIORITY QUEUE"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["FCFS Scheduling, Buffers"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Palindrome Check, Sliding Window"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Dijkstra, Prim, Huffman, OS Scheduler"]
    nodeH["CHOICE GUIDE"] --> nodeI["Need both ends? USE DEQUE"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["Need priority based? USE PRIORITY QUEUE"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["Need simple FCFS? USE SIMPLE QUEUE"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This decision tree summarizes when each queue variant is most appropriate. The KTU exam frequently tests whether students can **identify the correct structure** given a problem statement.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Double-Ended Queue (Deque). List its two main variants with one example application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A Double-Ended Queue (Deque) is a linear data structure that allows insertion and deletion of elements from **both** the front and rear ends. It generalizes both the stack and the queue.

**Two Variants:**
1. **Input-Restricted Deque:** Insertion allowed only at the rear, but deletion allowed at both ends. *Example application:* Implementing a stack (only one insertion point, two deletion points).
2. **Output-Restricted Deque:** Deletion allowed only at the front, but insertion allowed at both ends. *Example application:* Implementing a standard FIFO queue.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a Priority Queue? How does it differ from a simple FIFO queue?**

**Model Answer:**
A Priority Queue is an abstract data type in which each element is associated with a **priority value**, and the element with the **highest priority** is always removed first, irrespective of its arrival time.

**Difference from FIFO Queue:**

| Aspect | FIFO Queue | Priority Queue |
|---|---|---|
| Order of Removal | Insertion order | Priority order |
| Data Items | Homogeneous | (Key, Value) pairs |
| Service Discipline | FCFS | Highest Priority First |

In a hospital emergency room, critical patients are treated before less critical ones (Priority Queue behavior), but in a billing counter, customers are served in the order they arrive (FIFO behavior).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Examination Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 carries internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of a Double-Ended Queue (Deque). With neat diagrams, describe its two variants. State the conditions for **empty** and **full** states in a circular array implementation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between a **simple queue, deque, and priority queue** based on insertion-deletion rules, ordering principle, and time complexity. Provide one real-world application for each. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Deque Concept and Variants**

A **Deque (Double-Ended Queue)** is a linear data structure permitting insertions and deletions at **both** the front and rear ends. It unifies the functionalities of stacks and queues.

**The Two Variants:**

1. **Input-Restricted Deque (IRD):** Insertions occur only at the **rear**; deletions can occur at both the front and rear. This makes it equivalent to a stack appended at the queue's tail.

2. **Output-Restricted Deque (ORD):** Deletions occur only at the **front**; insertions can occur at both ends. This makes it equivalent to a standard FIFO queue with an additional insertion point.

**Empty and Full Conditions (Circular Array of capacity n):**

- **Empty:** $F = R = -1$ initially, or $F = R$ when all elements are deleted (depending on convention).
- **Full:** $(R + 1) \bmod n = F$ — one slot is intentionally left unused to avoid ambiguity between empty and full states.

The circular property ensures that indices **wrap around** from $n-1$ back to $0$, allowing continuous operation without shifting elements.

**Part (b) — Comparative Analysis**

| Property | Simple Queue | Deque | Priority Queue |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Insertion** | Rear only | Front and Rear | Anywhere (priority governs removal) |
| **Deletion** | Front only | Front and Rear | Highest priority element (any position) |
| **Ordering Principle** | FIFO (arrival order) | Flexible (FIFO, LIFO, or hybrid) | Priority-based |
| **Time Complexity (typical)** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ with heap |
| **Real-World Application** | CPU Round-Robin Scheduling, Printer Spooler | Browser back/forward navigation, A-Steal Job Scheduling | OS Priority Scheduling, Dijkstra's Algorithm |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define a Priority Queue. Explain the **Max-Heap** and **Min-Heap** structures used to implement it. Show the heap after inserting the keys $\{35, 10, 27, 5, 18, 42\}$ into an initially empty Min-Heap. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the time complexities of priority queue operations using **unsorted array, sorted array, and binary heap** representations. Justify why the **binary heap** is the preferred implementation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Priority Queue and Heap Variants**

A **Priority Queue** is an ADT where each element has an associated priority, and the element with the **highest (or lowest)** priority is dequeued first. A **Max-Heap** maintains the property that the **parent is greater than or equal to** its children, ensuring the root holds the maximum. Conversely, a **Min-Heap** maintains that the parent is **less than or equal to** its children, ensuring the root holds the minimum.

**Heap Insertion Trace — Min-Heap for keys $\{35, 10, 27, 5, 18, 42\}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Insert 35:} \quad &\text{Heap} = [35] \\
\text{Insert 10:} \quad &\text{Heap} = [10, 35] \quad (\text{10 < 35, swap})\\
\text{Insert 27:} \quad &\text{Heap} = [10, 35, 27] \quad (\text{27 > 10, no swap})\\
\text{Insert 5:} \quad &\text{Heap} = [5, 10, 27, 35] \quad (\text{5 < 35, swap; 5 < 10, swap})\\
\text{Insert 18:} \quad &\text{Heap} = [5, 10, 27, 35, 18] \quad (\text{18 > 10, no swap})\\
\text{Insert 42:} \quad &\text{Heap} = [5, 10, 27, 35, 18, 42] \quad (\text{42 > 5, no swap})
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Array Representation:**

$$A = [5, 10, 27, 35, 18, 42]$$

The root is $5$ (the minimum), satisfying the Min-Heap invariant at every level.

**Part (b) — Complexity Comparison**

| Operation | Unsorted Array | Sorted Array | Binary Heap |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Insert** | $O(1)$ — add at end | $O(n)$ — shift to maintain order | $O(\log n)$ — sift up |
| **Extract-Max/Min** | $O(n)$ — linear scan for max/min | $O(1)$ — access end | $O(\log n)$ — sift down |
| **Peek** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ — root access |
| **Space** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |

**Why Binary Heap is Preferred:**
The binary heap provides $O(\log n)$ complexity for **both** insertion and extraction, giving an amortized balanced performance. Unsorted array is slow at extraction, and sorted array is slow at insertion — neither achieves balanced efficiency. Additionally, heaps are **in-place** (no extra memory beyond the array), cache-friendly, and easily implemented using array indexing relationships. This makes the heap the canonical choice for priority queues in practice.

---

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones**
> 1. **Forgetting modular arithmetic:** Students often write $(R+1) = F$ for the full condition instead of $(R+1) \bmod n = F$. This loses 1–2 marks in array-based deque problems.
> 2. **Confusing heap property direction:** Many students state *"parent is greater than children"* without specifying Min-Heap vs Max-Heap. Always clarify the direction explicitly.
> 3. **Missing variant definitions:** Simply naming *Input-Restricted* and *Output-Restricted* without specifying which operation is restricted loses marks. Use the exact phrase *"insertion/deletion is restricted to only one end."*
> 4. **Heap trace errors:** During insertion, forgetting the **upward bubbling** step (parent comparison) is a frequent mistake. Always show the swap sequence explicitly.
> 5. **Time complexity mismatch:** Writing $O(1)$ for heap operations is a critical error. Heap operations are $O(\log n)$ — this distinction is heavily tested.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Deque** = Double-Ended Queue; allows insertion/deletion at **both** front and rear.
- ✅ **Input-Restricted Deque** permits only rear-insertion; **Output-Restricted Deque** permits only front-deletion.
- ✅ In circular array deque, **full condition** is $(R+1) \bmod n = F$ — one slot is sacrificed.
- ✅ **Empty condition** for deque is typically $F = R = -1$ (or $F = R$ after deletion, depending on implementation).
- ✅ **Priority Queue** serves elements based on **priority**, not arrival order — arrival order is only a tie-breaker.
- ✅ **Max-Heap** keeps the largest element at the root; **Min-Heap** keeps the smallest at the root.
- ✅ Heap operations (`insert`, `extract`) are $O(\log n)$; `peek` is $O(1)$.
- ✅ Unsorted array gives $O(1)$ insert but $O(n)$ extract — heap balances both to $O(\log n)$.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** Deque → Browser navigation, sliding window algorithms; Priority Queue → Dijkstra's, OS scheduling, Huffman coding.
- ✅ For KTU exams, always **draw diagrams** for deque variants and **show insertion traces** for heaps — these visual elements fetch full marks.
- ✅ Remember the heap indexing formulas: parent = $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor$, left child = $2i$, right child = $2i+1$ (1-indexed) or $2i+1, 2i+2$ (0-indexed).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Stack: Infix to Postfix conversion and Postfix evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications of Stack: Infix to Postfix conversion and Postfix evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Infix Expression:** A notational form in which an **operator is placed *between* its two operands** (e.g., `A + B`). This is the natural form used by humans, but it requires **operator precedence rules and parentheses** to resolve ambiguity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Postfix Expression (Reverse Polish Notation — RPN):** A parenthesis-free notational form in which an **operator is placed *after* its two operands** (e.g., `A B +`). Because the operator position encodes precedence, **no parentheses are required** and evaluation is unambiguous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Prefix Expression (Polish Notation):** An alternative parenthesis-free form in which the **operator appears *before* its operands** (e.g., `+ A B`). Used in compilers, LISP, and certain hardware stacks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Moving Train Platform"

Imagine a **single-platform railway station** where trains enter from one side and leave from the other, but they can only leave from the **top of a stack** (Last-In-First-Out).

- **Infix** is like passengers standing anywhere on the platform — to know who boards first, you need a conductor reading **priority rules (precedence)** and shouting "everyone step back!".
- **Postfix** is like passengers already arranged in **boarding order on the platform** — when the train (operator) arrives, the **last two who arrived (operands) simply step in**. No rules, no shouting, no parentheses.
- The **stack** is the platform itself — a place to *temporarily hold* operators or operands until they are needed.

## 1.3 Why Convert Infix → Postfix?

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Eliminates parentheses** | Precedence is encoded by *position*, so `((A+B)*C)` collapses to `A B + C *` |
| **Enables linear-time evaluation** | A single left-to-right scan with one stack evaluates any postfix expression in **O(n)** |
| **Compiler-friendly** | Most compilers (GCC, javac) internally translate infix source code into postfix/3-address code before code generation |
| **Hardware stack machines** | CPUs like the **x87 FPU stack** and **JVM bytecode** directly consume RPN-like instructions |
| **Unambiguity** | Postfix has a **unique parse tree** for any valid expression — no need for precedence tables at evaluation time |

## 1.4 The Three Notations — A Side-by-Side Snapshot

| Infix | Postfix (RPN) | Prefix (Polish) |
|---|---|---|
| `A + B` | `A B +` | `+ A B` |
| `(A + B) * C` | `A B + C *` | `* + A B C` |
| `A + B * C` | `A B C * +` | `+ A * B C` |
| `(A + B) * (C - D)` | `A B + C D - *` | `* + A B - C D` |
| `A ^ B ^ C` (right-assoc) | `A B C ^ ^` | `^ ^ A B C` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Associativity Conventions used in KTU examinations:**
> - `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` → **Left-to-Right (L→R)**
> - `^` (exponent) → **Right-to-Left (R→L)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operator Precedence & Associativity Matrix

The single most important reference for KTU exam derivations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operator Precedence Table (Higher number = Higher priority = Binds tighter)**
>
> | Precedence Level | Operator(s) | Associativity | Stack Action |
> |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
> | **3 (Highest)** | `^` | **Right → Left** | Pop only operators with **strictly greater** precedence from stack |
> | **2** | `*` , `/` , `%` | **Left → Right** | Pop operators with precedence **≥** current from stack |
> | **1 (Lowest)** | `+` , `-` | **Left → Right** | Pop operators with precedence **≥** current from stack |
> | **0** | `(` , `)` | Special | `(` is a *marker only*; `)` triggers pop-until-`(` |

## 2.2 Algorithm 1 — Infix to Postfix Conversion (Shunting-Yard, Dijkstra 1961)

**Data Structure:** An **operator stack** (stores `+ - * / ^ (`) and an **output queue/list** (stores operands and finally-popped operators).

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

For each token `t` read left-to-right from the infix string:

1. **If `t` is an operand** → append `t` directly to the output.
2. **If `t` is `(`** → push `t` onto the operator stack.
3. **If `t` is `)`** → repeatedly pop from stack and append to output until `(` is encountered. **Discard both parentheses.**
4. **If `t` is an operator** →
   - **While** (stack is not empty) **AND** (top of stack is not `(`) **AND** (precedence(top) > precedence(t) **OR** (precedence(top) == precedence(t) **AND** associativity(t) is Left)):
     - Pop top and append to output.
   - **Push** `t` onto the stack.
5. **After all tokens processed** → pop and append every remaining operator from stack to output.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of the popping rule:**
> When we see operator `t`, the stack may contain operators of *higher* or *equal* precedence that **must be flushed first** — they belong to operands that appeared *before* `t`'s operand. We only stop popping when we hit `(` (scope boundary) or a strictly lower-precedence operator.

## 2.3 Algorithm 2 — Postfix Expression Evaluation

**Data Structure:** A single **operand stack** (stores numeric values).

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

For each token `t` read left-to-right from the postfix string:

1. **If `t` is an operand** → push its numeric value onto the stack.
2. **If `t` is a binary operator** (takes 2 operands) →
   - `op2 = pop()` (this is the **right** operand — last pushed)
   - `op1 = pop()` (this is the **left** operand — pushed earlier)
   - `result = op1  t  op2`  ← **order matters!**
   - `push(result)`
3. **If `t` is a unary operator** (e.g., unary minus) → pop 1 value, apply, push back.
4. **At end** → the stack contains exactly **one value**: the final result.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Complexity Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Metrics for Exam Computations**

| Metric | Infix → Postfix | Postfix Evaluation |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Space Complexity (Stack)** | $O(n)$ worst case | $O(n)$ worst case |
| **Number of Stack Operations** | $\le 2n$ pushes/pops | $\le n$ pushes, $\le n$ pops |
| **Passes over input** | **1 (single left-to-right scan)** | **1 (single left-to-right scan)** |
| **Output Size** | Always $n$ tokens | Single scalar value |

### Operator Precedence Encoding (for use in `switch`/pseudocode)

$$
\text{prec}(op) = \begin{cases} 3 & \text{if } op \in \{`\texttt{\^{}}`\} \\ 2 & \text{if } op \in \{`*`, `/`, `\%`\} \\ 1 & \text{if } op \in \{`+`, `-`\} \\ 0 & \text{if } op \in \{`(`, `)`\} \end{cases}
$$

### Associativity Encoding

$$
\text{assoc}(op) = \begin{cases} \text{R} & \text{if } op = `\texttt{\^{}}` \\ \text{L} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Design** | Front-end parsers translate infix source into **3-address code** (postfix-like) for the back-end optimizer |
| **HP Calculators (HP-12C, HP-48)** | Pioneered direct RPN entry — no `=` key, just operator |
| **JVM Bytecode** | Stack-based VM where every `iadd`, `imul` instruction pops 2 operands and pushes 1 result |
| **Forth / PostScript** | Entire programming languages built on RPN |
| **Database Query Engines** | SQL expression trees often evaluated via postfix tree-walks |
| **Spreadsheet Engines (Excel)** | Internally use postfix evaluation of formula cells |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Trace

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1 — Infix to Postfix Conversion (The KTU Classic)**
>
> Convert the infix expression to postfix:
> $$\text{Infix} = A + (B \ast C - D) / E + F$$
> Assume standard left-associativity for `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` and right-associativity for `^`.

### 3.1 Initial Setup

We maintain two structures:
- **Output queue:** Initially empty
- **Operator stack:** Initially empty (shown bottom → top)

### 3.2 Token-by-Token Trace Table

| Step | Token Scanned | Action | Operator Stack (bottom→top) | Output Queue |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | `A` | Operand → output | *(empty)* | `A` |
| 2 | `+` | Stack empty → just push | `+` | `A` |
| 3 | `(` | Push marker | `+  (` | `A` |
| 4 | `B` | Operand → output | `+  (` | `A B` |
| 5 | `*` | Top is `(` (marker) → just push | `+  (  *` | `A B` |
| 6 | `C` | Operand → output | `+  (  *` | `A B C` |
| 7 | `-` | Top is `*`, prec(`*`)=2 ≥ prec(`-`)=1 → pop `*` to output; then top is `(`, stop. Push `-` | `+  (  -` | `A B C *` |
| 8 | `D` | Operand → output | `+  (  -` | `A B C * D` |
| 9 | `)` | Pop until `(`: pop `-` to output, discard `(` | `+` | `A B C * D -` |
| 10 | `/` | Top is `+`, prec(`+`)=1 < prec(`/`) = 2 → no pop. Push `/` | `+  /` | `A B C * D -` |
| 11 | `E` | Operand → output | `+  /` | `A B C * D - E` |
| 12 | `+` | Top is `/`, prec(`/`)=2 ≥ prec(`+`)=1 → pop `/` to output. Then top is `+`, prec=1 ≥ 1, **AND** `+` is left-associative → pop `+` to output. Push new `+` | `+` | `A B C * D - E / +` |
| 13 | `F` | Operand → output | `+` | `A B C * D - E / + F` |
| 14 | *end* | Pop all remaining → pop `+` | *(empty)* | `A B C * D - E / + F +` |

### 3.3 Final Result

$$
\boxed{\text{Postfix} = A \; B \; C \; * \; D \; - \; E \; / \; + \; F \; +}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by parenthesizing the original:**
> `A + (((B*C) - D) / E) + F`
> Reading the postfix left-to-right, every operator indeed applies to the **two most recent unconsumed operands** — the postfix is correct.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2 — Postfix Evaluation (Numeric Substitution)**
>
> Evaluate the postfix expression using the variable bindings:
> $$A = 5, \quad B = 3, \quad C = 2, \quad D = 4, \quad E = 6, \quad F = 8$$
> $$\text{Postfix} = 5 \; 3 \; 2 \; * \; 4 \; - \; 6 \; / \; + \; 8 \; +$$

### 3.4 Token-by-Token Evaluation Trace

We now maintain a single **operand stack**.

| Step | Token | Action | Operand Stack (bottom→top) | Calculation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | `5` | Push | `5` | — |
| 2 | `3` | Push | `5, 3` | — |
| 3 | `2` | Push | `5, 3, 2` | — |
| 4 | `*` | Pop 2,1 → `op2=2, op1=3` → push `3*2=6` | `5, 6` | $3 \times 2 = 6$ |
| 5 | `4` | Push | `5, 6, 4` | — |
| 6 | `-` | Pop → `op2=4, op1=6` → push `6-4=2` | `5, 2` | $6 - 4 = 2$ |
| 7 | `6` | Push | `5, 2, 6` | — |
| 8 | `/` | Pop → `op2=6, op1=2` → push `2/6` | `5, 0.333…` | $2 \div 6 = 0.3333$ |
| 9 | `+` | Pop → `op2=0.333, op1=5` → push `5.333` | `5.333` | $5 + 0.333 = 5.333$ |
| 10 | `8` | Push | `5.333, 8` | — |
| 11 | `+` | Pop → `op2=8, op1=5.333` → push `13.333` | `13.333` | $5.333 + 8 = 13.333$ |

### 3.5 Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\text{Result} = 13.333\ldots = 5 + \dfrac{3 \times 2 - 4}{6} + 8}
$$

### 3.6 Cross-Check by Direct Substituion

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original} &= A + \dfrac{(B \ast C) - D}{E} + F \\[4pt]
&= 5 + \dfrac{(3 \times 2) - 4}{6} + 8 \\[4pt]
&= 5 + \dfrac{6 - 4}{6} + 8 \\[4pt]
&= 5 + \dfrac{2}{6} + 8 \\[4pt]
&= 5 + 0.333\ldots + 8 \\[4pt]
&= 13.333\ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

✓ **Match confirmed.**

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 3 — Handling Right-Associativity with `^`**
>
> Convert: `(A - B) ^ C ^ D ^ E`

| Step | Token | Stack | Output | Reason |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | `(` | `(` | — | Push marker |
| 2 | `A` | `(` | `A` | Operand |
| 3 | `-` | `( -` | `A` | Push |
| 4 | `B` | `( -` | `A B` | Operand |
| 5 | `)` | — | `A B -` | Pop until `(` |
| 6 | `^` | `^` | `A B -` | Stack empty → push |
| 7 | `C` | `^` | `A B - C` | Operand |
| 8 | `^` | `^ ^` | `A B - C` | Top `^` has prec=3, current prec=3, but `^` is **R-associative** → **do NOT pop**; push |
| 9 | `D` | `^ ^` | `A B - C D` | Operand |
| 10 | `^` | `^ ^ ^` | `A B - C D` | Same R-assoc logic → push again |
| 11 | `E` | `^ ^ ^` | `A B - C D E` | Operand |
| 12 | end | — | `A B - C D E ^ ^ ^` | Flush stack |

### 3.7 Result

$$
\boxed{(A - B) \; C \; D \; E \; \texttt{\^{}} \; \texttt{\^{}} \; \texttt{\^{}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If `^` were *incorrectly* treated as left-associative, the conversion would give `A B - C ^ D ^ E ^` — a **completely different expression** that evaluates right-to-left as $(C^D)^E$ at each step. The associativity flag is critical!
