# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Infix to Postfix Conversion (Shunting-Yard)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START: Read infix expression token by token]) --> B{Token type?}
    B -->|Operand| C[Append to OUTPUT]
    B -->|Left Parenthesis| D[Push onto STACK]
    B -->|Right Parenthesis| E[Pop and append until Left Parenthesis found]
    E --> F[Discard the Left Parenthesis]
    B -->|Operator X| G{Is stack empty OR top is Left Parenthesis?}
    G -->|YES| H[Push X onto STACK]
    G -->|NO| I{Precedence of top GREATER than X OR Equal AND X is Left Associative?}
    I -->|YES| J[Pop top and append to OUTPUT]
    J --> G
    I -->|NO| H
    C --> K{All tokens read?}
    F --> K
    H --> K
    K -->|NO| A
    K -->|YES| L[Pop and append all remaining operators]
    L --> M([END: Postfix expression in OUTPUT])
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — Postfix Expression Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START: Read postfix expression token by token]) --> B{Token type?}
    B -->|Operand value| C[Push value onto OPERAND STACK]
    B -->|Binary Operator| D[op2 = Pop top]
    D --> E[op1 = Pop new top]
    E --> F[Compute result = op1 OPERATOR op2]
    F --> G[Push result onto OPERAND STACK]
    B -->|Unary Operator| H[val = Pop top]
    H --> I[Compute result = OPERATOR val]
    I --> G
    C --> J{All tokens read?}
    G --> J
    J -->|NO| A
    J -->|YES| K[Result = Pop top of stack]
    K --> L([END: Final answer])
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Full Compiler Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Code: Infix Expression] --> B[Tokenizer/Lexer]
    B --> C[Token Stream]
    C --> D[Infix to Postfix Converter]
    D --> E[Postfix String]
    E --> F[Postfix Evaluator]
    F --> G[Final Result]
    D -.uses.-> OP[Operator Stack]
    F -.uses.-> VAL[Operand Stack]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Stack-State Evolution Diagram — Visual Trace

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S1[Step 1-3: Push A, +, then start of B*C]
    S1A[Stack: empty] --> S1B[Read A → Output A]
    S1B --> S1C[Read + → Stack: plus]
    S1C --> S1D[Read B → Output A B]
    end
    subgraph S2[Step 4-7: Handle * then - precedence drop]
    S2A[Read * → Stack: plus star] --> S2B[Read C → Output A B C]
    S2B --> S2C[Read - → pop star → Output A B C star, Stack: plus]
    end
    subgraph S3[Step 8-11: Handle / and final flush]
    S3A[Read D → Output A B C star D] --> S3B[Read slash → Stack: plus slash]
    S3B --> S3C[Read E → Output A B C star D E]
    S3C --> S3D[End → pop slash pop plus → Output A B C star D E slash minus]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]`

> **What are infix, prefix, and postfix notations? Convert the expression `A + B * C` into postfix and prefix forms.**

**Model Answer:**

The three notations differ in the **position of the operator** relative to its operands:
- **Infix:** Operator *between* operands → `A + B`
- **Prefix:** Operator *before* operands → `+ A B`
- **Postfix:** Operator *after* operands → `A B +`

For `A + B * C`, since `*` has higher precedence than `+`:
- **Postfix:** `A B C * +`
- **Prefix:** `+ A * B C`

(3 marks — 1 for definitions, 1 each for the two conversions)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`

> **Why is postfix notation preferred over infix notation in compiler design? List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

Postfix notation is preferred because:

1. **No parentheses required** — Precedence is inherently encoded by operator position, eliminating the need for `(` and `)` tokens. This reduces parser complexity.
2. **Linear-time evaluation** — A single left-to-right scan with one stack evaluates any postfix expression in **O(n)** time, with no need for a precedence table or recursive descent.

*(Additional valid points: unique parse tree, suitable for stack-based virtual machines like JVM, easier code generation.)*

(3 marks — 1 mark per advantage, 1 mark for coherence)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Convert the following infix expression into postfix using a stack. Show the **status of the stack at every step**.
> $$Q \ast \left( R - S \right) + \left( T / U - V \ast W \right) / X + Y$$

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Evaluate the following postfix expression using a stack. Show **each stack operation**. Assume the values: $Q=2, R=4, S=2, T=8, U=2, V=2, W=3, X=4, Y=1$.
> $$\text{Postfix: } 6 \; 2 \; 3 \; + \; 3 \; 8 \; 2 \; / \; - \; 2 \; \ast \; +$$

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Parenthesization to fix order of evaluation:**
$$Q \ast (R - S) + \frac{T / U - V \ast W}{X} + Y$$

| Step | Token | Action | Stack (bottom→top) | Output |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | `Q` | Operand → output | — | `Q` |
| 2 | `*` | Stack empty → push | `*` | `Q` |
| 3 | `(` | Push marker | `*  (` | `Q` |
| 4 | `R` | Output | `*  (` | `Q R` |
| 5 | `-` | Top is `(` → push | `*  (  -` | `Q R` |
| 6 | `S` | Output | `*  (  -` | `Q R S` |
| 7 | `)` | Pop `-`; discard `(` | `*` | `Q R S -` |
| 8 | `+` | Top `*`, prec=2 ≥ prec=1, L-assoc → pop `*`. Stack empty → push `+` | `+` | `Q R S - *` |
| 9 | `(` | Push | `+  (` | `Q R S - *` |
| 10 | `T` | Output | `+  (` | `Q R S - * T` |
| 11 | `/` | Top is `(` → push | `+  (  /` | `Q R S - * T` |
| 12 | `U` | Output | `+  (  /` | `Q R S - * T U` |
| 13 | `-` | Top `/`, prec=2 ≥ prec=1 → pop `/`. Top `(` → stop. Push `-` | `+  (  -` | `Q R S - * T U /` |
| 14 | `V` | Output | `+  (  -` | `Q R S - * T U / V` |
| 15 | `*` | Top `-`, prec=1 < prec=2 → no pop. Push `*` | `+  (  -  *` | `Q R S - * T U / V` |
| 16 | `W` | Output | `+  (  -  *` | `Q R S - * T U / V W` |
| 17 | `)` | Pop `*`; pop `-`; discard `(` | `+` | `Q R S - * T U / V W * -` |
| 18 | `/` | Top `+`, prec=1 < prec=2 → no pop. Push `/` | `+  /` | `Q R S - * T U / V W * -` |
| 19 | `X` | Output | `+  /` | `Q R S - * T U / V W * - X` |
| 20 | `+` | Top `/`, prec=2 ≥ prec=1 → pop `/`. Top `+`, prec=1 ≥ prec=1, L-assoc → pop `+`. Push new `+` | `+` | `Q R S - * T U / V W * - X /` |
| 21 | `Y` | Output | `+` | `Q R S - * T U / V W * - X / Y` |
| 22 | end | Pop `+` | — | `Q R S - * T U / V W * - X / Y +` |

**Final Postfix:**
$$
\boxed{Q \; R \; S \; - \; \ast \; T \; U \; / \; V \; W \; \ast \; - \; X \; / \; Y \; +}
$$

(7 marks: 1 for parenthesization, 5 for trace table completeness, 1 for final answer)

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Postfix:** `6 2 3 + 3 8 2 / - 2 * +`

| Step | Token | Action (op1 = first pop, op2 = second pop) | Stack (bottom→top) |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `6` | Push 6 | `6` |
| 2 | `2` | Push 2 | `6, 2` |
| 3 | `3` | Push 3 | `6, 2, 3` |
| 4 | `+` | op2=3, op1=2 → 2+3=5; push 5 | `6, 5` |
| 5 | `3` | Push 3 | `6, 5, 3` |
| 6 | `8` | Push 8 | `6, 5, 3, 8` |
| 7 | `2` | Push 2 | `6, 5, 3, 8, 2` |
| 8 | `/` | op2=2, op1=8 → 8÷2=4; push 4 | `6, 5, 3, 4` |
| 9 | `-` | op2=4, op1=3 → 3−4=−1; push −1 | `6, 5, -1` |
| 10 | `2` | Push 2 | `6, 5, -1, 2` |
| 11 | `*` | op2=2, op1=−1 → −1×2=−2; push −2 | `6, 5, -2` |
| 12 | `+` | op2=−2, op1=5 → 5+(−2)=3; push 3 | `6, 3` |
| 13 | *end* | Pop 3 | `3` |

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{\text{Result} = 3}
$$

**Cross-check by direct evaluation of the infix form** `6 + (2+3) * (3 - 8/2)`:
- Inner: $2+3=5$
- Right bracket: $8/2=4$, then $3-4=-1$
- Multiply: $5 \times (-1) = -2$
- Final: $6 + (-2) = 4$... 

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1 — Operand Order Reversal**
> When evaluating postfix, students frequently compute `op2 - op1` instead of `op1 - op2` for non-commutative operators (`-`, `/`, `^`). The first pop is the **right operand** (it was pushed *last*); the second pop is the **left operand**. For `-`: `pop2 - pop1`, NOT `pop1 - pop2`. This single sign-flip costs **1–2 marks** in a 7-mark question.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2 — The `(` is Not an Operator**
> When scanning `(`, do **not** compare it with operator precedence or pop it. It is purely a *scope marker*. Likewise, when you see `)`, you must pop **until the matching `(`** and discard the `(` — it must NOT appear in the postfix output.
