### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Convert the following infix expression to postfix, showing **all stack states**. Also, write the **equivalent prefix expression**.
> $$P \ast Q - R + S \ast \left( T - U / V \right) + W / X$$

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Evaluate the postfix expression $8 \; 3 \; 4 \; * \; + \; 6 \; 2 \; / \; - \; 9 \; +$ using a stack, showing every push and pop.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Convert to Postfix:**

| Step | Token | Stack | Output |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `P` | — | `P` |
| 2 | `*` | `*` | `P` |
| 3 | `Q` | `*` | `P Q` |
| 4 | `-` | pop `*` (prec 2 ≥ 1, L-assoc); stack empty → push `-` | `P Q *` |
|  |  |  | ` ` |
|  |  | `-` | `P Q *` |
| 5 | `R` | `-` | `P Q * R` |
| 6 | `+` | pop `-` (prec 1 ≥ 1, L-assoc); push `+` | `P Q * R -` |
|  |  | `+` | `P Q * R -` |
| 7 | `S` | `+` | `P Q * R - S` |
| 8 | `*` | top `+`, prec 1 < 2 → push `*` | `P Q * R - S` |
|  |  | `+  *` | `P Q * R - S` |
| 9 | `(` | `+  *  (` | `P Q * R - S` |
| 10 | `T` | `+  *  (` | `P Q * R - S T` |
| 11 | `-` | top `(` → push `-` | `P Q * R - S T` |
|  |  | `+  *  (  -` | ` ` |
| 12 | `U` | `+  *  (  -` | `P Q * R - S T U` |
| 13 | `/` | top `-`, prec 1 < 2 → push `/` | `P Q * R - S T U` |
|  |  | `+  *  (  -  /` | ` ` |
| 14 | `V` | `+  *  (  -  /` | `P Q * R - S T U V` |
| 15 | `)` | pop `/`; pop `-`; discard `(` | `P Q * R - S T U V / -` |
|  |  | `+  *` | ` ` |
| 16 | `+` | top `*`, prec 2 ≥ 1, L-assoc → pop `*`; top `+`, prec 1 ≥ 1, L-assoc → pop `+`; push new `+` | `P Q * R - S T U V / - *` |
|  |  | `+` | ` ` |
| 17 | `W` | `+` | `P Q * R - S T U V / - * W` |
| 18 | `/` | top `+`, prec 1 < 2 → push `/` | `P Q * R - S T U V / - * W` |
|  |  | `+  /` | ` ` |
| 19 | `X` | `+  /` | `P Q * R - S T U V / - * W X` |
| 20 | end | pop `/`; pop `+` | `P Q * R - S T U V / - * W X / +` |

**Postfix:** $P \; Q \; * \; R \; - \; S \; T \; U \; V \; / \; - \; * \; + \; W \; X \; / \; +$

**Step 2 — Convert to Prefix (reverse → convert → reverse):**

1. **Reverse the infix:** $X / W + ) V / U - T ( * S + R - Q * P$
2. **Replace `(` ↔ `)`:**
3. **Convert (reversed) to postfix using a stack:** *(skipped for brevity, yields a postfix of the reversed expression)*
4. **Reverse that result** to get the **prefix.**

The **final prefix** is:
$$
\boxed{+ \; + \; - \; \ast \; P \; Q \; R \; \ast \; S \; - \; T \; / \; U \; V \; / \; W \; X}
$$

*(Or alternatively, apply the same stack algorithm scanning the infix **right-to-left** with reversed precedence logic for `/` and `^`.)*

(7 marks — 5 for postfix trace, 1 for prefix, 1 for clarity)

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Postfix:** `8 3 4 * + 6 2 / - 9 +`

| Step | Token | Action | Stack |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `8` | Push | `8` |
| 2 | `3` | Push | `8, 3` |
| 3 | `4` | Push | `8, 3, 4` |
| 4 | `*` | op2=4, op1=3 → 3×4=12; push | `8, 12` |
| 5 | `+` | op2=12, op1=8 → 8+12=20; push | `20` |
| 6 | `6` | Push | `20, 6` |
| 7 | `2` | Push | `20, 6, 2` |
| 8 | `/` | op2=2, op1=6 → 6÷2=3; push | `20, 3` |
| 9 | `-` | op2=3, op1=20 → 20−3=17; push | `17` |
| 10 | `9` | Push | `17, 9` |
| 11 | `+` | op2=9, op1=17 → 17+9=26; push | `26` |
| 12 | end | Pop | `26` |

**Final Result:** $\boxed{26}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3 — Showing "Stack Empty" for Final Answer**
> A common error is leaving a stray value on the stack without writing the final pop. The valuation key requires **explicitly showing the final pop** that returns the answer. Always end your trace with `Pop → final value`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #4 — Confusing "Operator on Top" with "Higher Precedence on Top"**
> When the algorithm says "pop while top of stack has *higher* or *equal* precedence", students often pop *all* operators regardless of precedence. If the top has **strictly lower** precedence (e.g., `+` on top, current token `*`), you must **stop popping** and push the new operator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Infix to Postfix & Postfix Evaluation**

- ✅ **Three notations** — Infix (operator *between*), Prefix (operator *before*), Postfix (operator *after*).
- ✅ **Postfix is parenthesis-free** — Precedence is encoded by position.
- ✅ **Both algorithms are O(n)** — Single left-to-right scan, one stack each.
- ✅ **Operator Precedence (highest to lowest):** `^` (R-assoc) → `* / %` (L-assoc) → `+ -` (L-assoc) → `( )` (scope marker).
- ✅ **Popping rule (infix → postfix):** Pop operators of **strictly greater** precedence OR **equal precedence with left-associativity**. *Never pop `(`.*
- ✅ **`(` is NOT an operator** — It is pushed/popped only for scope handling; it never appears in postfix output.
- ✅ **Postfix evaluation popping order** — First pop is the **right operand (op2)**, second pop is the **left operand (op1)**. For `-`, `/`, `^`: `op1 - op2`, `op1 / op2`, `op1 ^ op2`.
- ✅ **R-associative `^` is NOT popped on equal precedence** — This is the most-frequently-tested subtlety.
- ✅ **At the end of postfix evaluation, the stack must contain exactly ONE element** — that is the answer.
- ✅ **Real-world usage** — Compilers, JVM bytecode, PostScript, HP calculators, SQL query evaluators.
- ✅ **Conversion to Prefix shortcut** — Reverse the infix string, swap `(` ↔ `)`, convert to postfix, then reverse the result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid — "PRPN LIFO"**
> **P**ostfix **R**everse **P**olish **N**otation is best evaluated **L**eft-to-right **I**n, **F**irst **O**ut (LIFO) — the stack discipline.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Search and Binary Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Search and Binary Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Linear Search (Sequential Search)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Linear Search is a brute-force, sequential searching technique that inspects every element of a data structure (typically a one-dimensional array or a linear linked list) from the **first index (0)** to the **last index (n-1)** until the desired `KEY` is located or the entire structure is exhausted. It is the most primitive search algorithm and is **order-independent** — the data need **not** be sorted.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Terminology:** Examiners frequently use the term *"Sequential Search"* synonymously with *Linear Search*. Both refer to the same algorithm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Linear Search is explicitly mentioned under *Module 2* of PCITT303 in the KTU 2024 scheme. Students are expected to write the algorithm, trace it on small inputs, and analyze its best/average/worst-case time complexity in terms of the **number of key comparisons**.

---

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Lost Key on a Floor"

Imagine you dropped your house key somewhere on a long, dimly lit corridor containing **n** rooms. You have no memory of which room it fell into. What do you do?

- You start at **Room 0** and physically walk into each room, looking under the bed, behind the desk, on the shelf.
- The moment you **spot the key**, you stop.
- If you reach **Room n-1** without finding it, you conclude the key is not in the corridor.

That, in essence, is **Linear Search** — a calm, deterministic sweep that asks, one by one, *"Is this the element? Is this the element? Is this the element?"*

---

## 1.2 Binary Search (Logarithmic Search)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Binary Search is a **divide-and-conquer** searching technique applicable only to a **pre-sorted** (ascending or descending) array. It repeatedly compares the `KEY` with the **middle element** of the current search interval `[low, high]`. Based on the comparison, it **eliminates half of the remaining elements** by adjusting either `low` or `high`, thereby converging exponentially fast toward the target.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Precondition:** The array **must be sorted** in monotonic order (ascending for the standard formulation). If the input is unsorted, Binary Search produces **undefined / incorrect results**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU 2024 Module 2, students must derive the **worst-case recurrence T(n) = T(n/2) + 1**, solve it to obtain **T(n) = O(log₂ n)**, and contrast it with Linear Search's **O(n)** complexity. The logarithmic nature of Binary Search is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**.

---

### Conceptual Analogy — "Finding a Word in a Dictionary"

Open a thick English dictionary at random. The word you are looking for starts with the letter **'M'**.

- The dictionary has roughly **1,000 pages**. You open the **middle page (~500)**.
- That page contains words starting with **'L'**. Since 'M' comes **after** 'L' alphabetically, you **discard the entire left half** — 500 pages gone in one move.
- You now have only ~500 pages left. You open the **middle of the new range**, see 'N' this time, and again discard the upper half.
- After just about **log₂(1000) ≈ 10** such halvings, you have narrowed the search down to a single page.

That is **Binary Search** — a brilliant "halve-and-rule" strategy that turns a linear, time-consuming hunt into a near-instantaneous logarithmic one.

---

## 1.3 Search as a Primitive Operation

In real engineering systems, searching is not a standalone luxury — it is the **backbone** of:

| Application Domain | Use of Search |
|---|---|
| **Database Management Systems (DBMS)** | Index lookups, B-Tree traversals (generalized binary search) |
| **Compiler Design** | Symbol table lookups, identifier resolution |
| **Operating Systems** | Process scheduling, page table entries |
| **Network Routing** | Longest Prefix Match in IP routing tables |
| **Artificial Intelligence** | Adversarial search (Binary Search on game states) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU Cares:** Search complexity directly determines how scalable an application is. A linear search over **1 billion records** is impractical; binary search over the same (sorted) data takes only **~30 comparisons**. This is the difference between an exam and a real engineering decision.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear Search — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

Given an unsorted (or sorted) array `A[0..n-1]` of `n` elements and a search `KEY`:

1. **Initialize** the index counter: `i ← 0`.
2. **Loop while** `i < n`:
   - **Compare** `A[i]` with `KEY`.
   - **If** `A[i] == KEY`, **return** `i` (index where the key was found) and **STOP**.
   - **Else**, increment `i ← i + 1`.
3. **If the loop terminates** without returning, **return** `-1` (search unsuccessful).

**Pseudocode (Standard KTU Format):**

```
ALGORITHM LinearSearch(A[0..n-1], n, KEY)
BEGIN
    FOR i ← 0 TO n-1 DO
        IF A[i] = KEY THEN
            RETURN i
        END IF
    END FOR
    RETURN -1
END
```

### Why does it work?
Because the algorithm is exhaustive — it explicitly inspects **every** element. There is no logical shortcut, no assumption, no prerequisite. It is the most robust (though slowest) search strategy.

---

## 2.2 Binary Search — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

Given a **sorted (ascending)** array `A[0..n-1]` and a search `KEY`:

1. **Initialize**: `low ← 0`, `high ← n-1`.
2. **Loop while** `low ≤ high`:
   - Compute middle index: `mid ← ⌊(low + high) / 2⌋`.
   - **If** `A[mid] == KEY`, **return** `mid` and **STOP**.
   - **Else if** `A[mid] < KEY`, set `low ← mid + 1` (search the right half).
   - **Else** (i.e., `A[mid] > KEY`), set `high ← mid - 1` (search the left half).
3. **If the loop terminates**, **return** `-1` (search unsuccessful).

**Pseudocode (Standard KTU Format):**

```
ALGORITHM BinarySearch(A[0..n-1], n, KEY)
BEGIN
    low ← 0
    high ← n - 1
    WHILE low ≤ high DO
        mid ← ⌊(low + high) / 2⌋
        IF A[mid] = KEY THEN
            RETURN mid
        ELSE IF A[mid] < KEY THEN
            low ← mid + 1
        ELSE
            high ← mid - 1
        END IF
    END WHILE
    RETURN -1
END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Using `(low + high) / 2` is fine for small `n`, but the industry-grade safe formulation is `low + (high - low) / 2` to prevent **integer overflow** in languages like C/C++ when `n` is very large. KTU examiners often test this in 3-mark questions.

### Why does it work?
Because the array is sorted, the relationship of `KEY` to `A[mid]` provides a **provable guarantee** about which half cannot possibly contain the key. We **discard that half entirely** in a single comparison, halving the problem size.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Aspect | Linear Search | Binary Search |
|---|---|---|
| **Precondition** | None (works on unsorted data) | Array **must be sorted** |
| **Strategy** | Sequential / Brute-force | Divide and Conquer |
| **Best-Case Comparisons** | $1$ (key at index 0) | $1$ (key is at `mid` on first try) |
| **Worst-Case Comparisons** | $n$ (key at last index or absent) | $\lfloor \log_{2} n \rfloor + 1$ |
| **Average Comparisons (Successful)** | $\frac{n+1}{2}$ | $\log_{2} n - \frac{n}{2 \log n} + \cdots$ (approx. $\log_{2} n$) |
| **Average Comparisons (Unsuccessful)** | $n$ | $\log_{2} n$ |
| **Time Complexity — Best** | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ |
| **Time Complexity — Average** | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| **Time Complexity — Worst** | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(1)$ — Iterative | $O(1)$ — Iterative; $O(\log n)$ — Recursive (call stack) |
| **Recurrence Relation** | — | $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ |
| **Asymptotic Solution** | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| **Stability** | Deterministic | Deterministic |
| **Variant: Lower Bound of $A[mid]$** | — | $A[\text{mid}] < \text{KEY} \Rightarrow \text{low} \leftarrow \text{mid} + 1$ |
| **Variant: Upper Bound of $A[mid]$** | — | $A[\text{mid}] > \text{KEY} \Rightarrow \text{high} \leftarrow \text{mid} - 1$ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linear Search** is preferred when the dataset is **small**, **unsorted**, or when you need a **single, quick lookup** (e.g., scanning a small contact list).
- **Binary Search** dominates in **large, static, sorted datasets** — e.g., a phone directory, a dictionary, an airline reservation system searching by flight number, or the `bisect` module in Python.
- A hybrid approach (e.g., **Timsort** in Python) uses linear search for small subarrays (where constants dominate) and switches to divide-and-conquer for large ones — combining the strengths of both.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The base of the logarithm in Binary Search is 2 because the problem size is **halved** in every iteration. This is a standard question worth **2–3 marks**: *"Why is the base of the logarithm 2 in binary search complexity?"* Answer: because each step reduces the problem size by a factor of 2.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Time Complexity for Linear Search

Let $n$ denote the number of elements in the array. The algorithm performs **one comparison per loop iteration**.

### 3.1.1 Best-Case Analysis

If the key is located at **index 0**, the algorithm performs exactly **1 comparison** and returns immediately.

$$
T_{\text{best}}(n) = 1 = \Theta(1)
$$

**Explanation:** The `IF A[i] = KEY` test succeeds on the very first iteration; no further iterations occur.

---

### 3.1.2 Worst-Case Analysis

If the key is located at the **last index $(n-1)$**, or if it is **absent** from the array, the algorithm performs exactly **n comparisons**.

$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = n = \Theta(n)
$$

**Explanation:** The `FOR` loop runs through all $n$ elements. Each iteration executes one comparison. After the loop terminates, the function returns $-1$.

---

### 3.1.3 Average-Case Analysis (Successful Search)

Assume the key is equally likely to be at **any of the $n$ positions**. The number of comparisons for a successful match at index $i$ is $i+1$ (because indices start at 0, but we count 1-based comparisons).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}}(n) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (i + 1) \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \cdot \left[ \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} i + \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 1 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \cdot \left[ \frac{(n-1)n}{2} + n \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \cdot \left[ \frac{n^2 - n + 2n}{2} \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{n^2 + n}{2} \\
&= \frac{n + 1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \frac{n + 1}{2} = \Theta(n)
$$

**Explanation of the derivation logic:** We sum the comparison counts $(1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + n)$ using the arithmetic series formula, then divide by $n$ to compute the average. The dominant term is $n/2$, confirming the **linear** average-case behaviour.

---

### 3.1.4 Average-Case Analysis (Unsuccessful Search)

If the key is **not present**, the algorithm performs exactly $n$ comparisons in every invocation.

$$
T_{\text{avg,unsuccessful}}(n) = n = \Theta(n)
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Time Complexity for Binary Search

Binary search is a recursive divide-and-conquer algorithm. The problem size is **halved** at each recursive call, plus a single comparison is performed at the current level.

### 3.2.1 The Recurrence Relation

$$
T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1, \quad T(1) = 1
$$

**Explanation:** Each call to `BinarySearch` does:
- One comparison: `A[mid] = KEY` (this contributes the "+1" term).
- Then it recurses on **at most one half** of the array of size $\frac{n}{2}$.

---

### 3.2.2 Solving the Recurrence Using the Master Theorem

The recurrence $T(n) = a \cdot T(n/b) + f(n)$ has the form:

$$
a = 1, \quad b = 2, \quad f(n) = 1 = n^{0}
$$

Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 1} = n^0 = 1$.

Since $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$, we are in **Case 2** of the Master Theorem.

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log n) = \Theta(1 \cdot \log n) = \Theta(\log n)
$$

**Final Result (Worst-Case):**

$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1 = \Theta(\log n)
$$

---

### 3.2.3 Solving the Recurrence by Repeated Substitution (KTU-Expected Method)

We expand the recurrence step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1 \\
&= \left[ T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 1 \right] + 1 \\
&= T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2 \\
&= \left[ T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 1 \right] + 2 \\
&= T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 3 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + k
\end{aligned}
$$

The recursion bottoms out when $\frac{n}{2^k} = 1$, i.e., when $k = \log_2 n$.

Substituting $k = \log_2 n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(1) + \log_2 n \\
&= 1 + \log_2 n \\
&= \Theta(\log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result (Worst-Case):**

$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(\log_2 n)}
$$

---

### 3.2.4 Best-Case Analysis

The key is found on the first comparison if it happens to be at `A[mid]` initially.

$$
T_{\text{best}}(n) = 1 = \Theta(1)
$$

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Metric | Linear Search | Binary Search | Speedup Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n = 10$ | $10$ comparisons | $\approx 4$ comparisons | $\approx 2.5 \times$ |
| $n = 100$ | $100$ comparisons | $\approx 7$ comparisons | $\approx 14 \times$ |
| $n = 1{,}000$ | $1{,}000$ comparisons | $\approx 10$ comparisons | $\approx 100 \times$ |
| $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ | $1{,}000{,}000$ comparisons | $\approx 20$ comparisons | $\approx 50{,}000 \times$ |
| $n = 1{,}000{,}000{,}000$ | $1 \times 10^9$ comparisons | $\approx 30$ comparisons | $\approx 33{,}000{,}000 \times$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice how Binary Search handles **1 billion records** in just **30 comparisons**, while Linear Search would need **1 billion**. This exponential-vs-linear gap is **the single most important concept** in this module. Examiners expect students to write a **3-4 line justification** of why $O(\log n)$ dramatically outperforms $O(n)$ at scale.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Linear Search — Control Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the iterative control flow of Linear Search, including both the **successful** and **unsuccessful** termination paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B["Initialize i = 0"]
    B --> C{"i less than n?"}
    C -- NO --> D["RETURN -1"]
    D --> E([END UNSUCCESSFUL])
    C -- YES --> F["Compare A i with KEY"]
    F --> G{"A i equals KEY?"}
    G -- YES --> H["RETURN i"]
    H --> I([END SUCCESSFUL])
    G -- NO --> J["Increment i = i + 1"]
    J --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each loop iteration performs **one comparison** at node `G`.
- The diamond `C` (loop condition) is the **terminating gate**.
- Node `D` handles the unsuccessful case after exhausting the array.

---

## 4.2 Binary Search — Control Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B["low = 0, high = n - 1"]
    B --> C{"low less than or equal to high?"}
    C -- NO --> D["RETURN -1"]
    D --> E([END UNSUCCESSFUL])
    C -- YES --> F["mid = floor of low + high over 2"]
    F --> G{"A mid equals KEY?"}
    G -- YES --> H["RETURN mid"]
    H --> I([END SUCCESSFUL])
    G -- NO --> J{"A mid less than KEY?"}
    J -- YES --> K["low = mid + 1"]
    J -- NO --> L["high = mid - 1"]
    K --> C
    L --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Notice the **two-way branch** at node `J`: the key decision that determines which half of the array to discard.
- The `mid` computation at node `F` is the **heart of the algorithm** — every iteration cuts the problem size in half.
- The loop terminates either by finding the key (node `H`) or by exhausting the search interval (node `D`).

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Search Engine Comparison

This high-level block diagram maps the **architectural differences** between the two search strategies as they would be implemented in a search engine subsystem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LINEAR_MODULE["LINEAR SEARCH ENGINE"]
        L1["INPUT: Unsorted Array"] --> L2["Sequential Scanner"]
        L2 --> L3{"Element Match?"}
        L3 -- YES --> L4["OUTPUT: Index"]
        L3 -- NO --> L5{"End of Array?"}
        L5 -- NO --> L2
        L5 -- YES --> L6["OUTPUT: NOT FOUND"]
    end

    subgraph BINARY_MODULE["BINARY SEARCH ENGINE"]
        B1["INPUT: Sorted Array"] --> B2["Sort Validator"]
        B2 -- SORTED --> B3["Mid-Point Calculator"]
        B3 --> B4{"Element Match?"}
        B4 -- YES --> B5["OUTPUT: Index"]
        B4 -- NO --> B6{"Direction?"}
        B6 -- RIGHT --> B7["Discard Left Half"]
        B6 -- LEFT --> B8["Discard Right Half"]
        B7 --> B3
        B8 --> B3
    end

    C1["KTU OBSERVATION: Binary is O log n but requires O n log n one-time sort cost"] -.-> BINARY_MODULE
    C2["KTU OBSERVATION: Linear is O n but has zero preprocessing overhead"] -.-> LINEAR_MODULE
```

**Engineering insight:** Binary search demands a **one-time sorting overhead** of $O(n \log n)$ (e.g., Merge Sort or Heap Sort). Linear search has **no such prerequisite**. For a *static* dataset accessed many times, Binary Search wins overall; for a *dynamic* dataset where insertions dominate, Linear Search (often combined with linked lists) is preferred.

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree for Binary Search (for $n = 8$)

This diagram illustrates how Binary Search splits the array into a balanced decision tree, where each level represents one comparison and each leaf represents a unique position in the array.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["A 0 to 7  mid = 3"] --> L["A 0 to 2  mid = 1"]
    R --> Rt["A 4 to 7  mid = 5"]
    L --> LL["A 0 to 0  mid = 0"]
    L --> LR["A 2 to 2  mid = 2"]
    Rt --> RL["A 4 to 4  mid = 4"]
    Rt --> RR["A 6 to 7  mid = 6"]
    RR --> RRL["A 6 to 6  mid = 6 leaf"]
    RR --> RRR["A 7 to 7  mid = 7 leaf"]
```

**Observations:**
- The **height** of the tree is $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil = 3$ for $n = 8$.
- Each internal node represents **one comparison**.
- The **worst-case number of comparisons equals the height of the tree**, confirming $T_{\text{worst}} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Define Linear Search. List any two advantages and one disadvantage of Linear Search.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Linear Search is a sequential searching technique that examines each element of an array or list one by one, from the first element to the last, until the desired key is found or the entire structure has been traversed.

**Advantages (1 mark each, any two):**
1. It works on **both sorted and unsorted** data — no preprocessing is required.
2. It is **simple to implement** and easy to understand, making it ideal for small datasets.
3. It is well-suited for **linked lists** where random access is not available.

**Disadvantage (1 mark):**
1. It is **inefficient for large datasets** because its time complexity is $O(n)$, leading to long search times as $n$ grows.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024]`

**State the precondition for applying Binary Search. Why is the base of the logarithm in its complexity $2$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Precondition (1 mark):** The array (or data structure) on which Binary Search is applied **must be sorted** in monotonic (ascending or descending) order. Binary Search on an unsorted array produces **incorrect / undefined results**.

**Why base 2 (2 marks):** In each iteration of Binary Search, the search interval is **halved** — i.e., the problem size is divided by a factor of 2. Hence, the recurrence $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ leads to a solution of the form $T(n) = \Theta(\log_2 n)$, where the base 2 directly reflects the halving factor at each step.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Note:** Module 2 of PCITT303 typically pairs Linear Search and Binary Search as a *single 14-mark question with internal choice*. The two sub-parts are usually allocated **7 marks** each.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write the algorithm for **Binary Search** and explain its working with a suitable example. Derive its time complexity in the worst case.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the array `A = [3, 7, 11, 15, 21, 28, 36, 44, 52, 67]` and `KEY = 28`, trace the **Binary Search** algorithm step-by-step. Show all values of `low`, `high`, `mid`, and the comparison result at each iteration.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Algorithm, Explanation, and Complexity Derivation

**Algorithm (3 marks):**

```
ALGORITHM BinarySearch(A[0..n-1], n, KEY)
BEGIN
    low ← 0
    high ← n - 1
    WHILE low ≤ high DO
        mid ← ⌊(low + high) / 2⌋
        IF A[mid] = KEY THEN
            RETURN mid
        ELSE IF A[mid] < KEY THEN
            low ← mid + 1
        ELSE
            high ← mid - 1
        END IF
    END WHILE
    RETURN -1
END
```

**Explanation with Example (2 marks):**

Suppose we have the sorted array `[10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70]` and we want to search for `KEY = 40`.

- Initially: `low = 0`, `high = 6`, `mid = 3`. Compare `A[3] = 40` with `KEY = 40` → **Match found! Return 3**.

Suppose instead `KEY = 25`:
- Iteration 1: `low = 0`, `high = 6`, `mid = 3`, `A[3] = 40 > 25` → `high = 2`.
- Iteration 2: `low = 0`, `high = 2`, `mid = 1`, `A[1] = 20 < 25` → `low = 2`.
- Iteration 3: `low = 2`, `high = 2`, `mid = 2`, `A[2] = 30 > 25` → `high = 1`.
- Now `low = 2 > high = 1` → Loop exits. Return `-1` (not found).

**Worst-Case Complexity Derivation (2 marks):**

The recurrence relation is $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$, with $T(1) = 1$.

By repeated substitution (as derived in Section 3.2.3):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n/2) + 1 \\
&= T(n/4) + 2 \\
&= T(n/8) + 3 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T(n/2^k) + k
\end{aligned}
$$

The base case is reached when $\frac{n}{2^k} = 1$, i.e., $k = \log_2 n$.

Therefore, $T(n) = T(1) + \log_2 n = 1 + \log_2 n = \Theta(\log n)$.

---

#### Part (b) — Step-by-Step Trace

**Given:** `A = [3, 7, 11, 15, 21, 28, 36, 44, 52, 67]`, `KEY = 28`, $n = 10$.

| Iteration | `low` | `high` | `mid = ⌊(low+high)/2⌋` | `A[mid]` | Comparison | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 9 | 4 | 21 | `21 < 28` | `low = mid + 1 = 5` |
| 2 | 5 | 9 | 7 | 44 | `44 > 28` | `high = mid - 1 = 6` |
| 3 | 5 | 6 | 5 | 28 | `28 == 28` | **Return 5 (Found!)** |

**Result:** The key `28` is found at **index 5** after **3 comparisons**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update `low` or `high`**: Many students write the algorithm correctly but make arithmetic mistakes in trace tables, e.g., writing `mid = 4.5` instead of rounding to `4` or `5`. The floor function is **mandatory**. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing**: If a student uses 1-based indexing in the algorithm but the array is given 0-based, the trace will be off by 1. Verify the index convention first.
> 3. **Omitting the base case**: When deriving the recurrence, students often forget to state $T(1) = 1$ or $T(0) = 0$. The Master Theorem application is incomplete without it. Lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing the comparison with `=` instead of `≤`**: In the `WHILE` loop, the condition is `low ≤ high`, **not** `low < high`. A common slip-up costs 0.5–1 mark.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write the algorithm for **Linear Search** and explain its working. Compute the **average number of comparisons** in the successful case.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare Linear Search and Binary Search based on the following criteria: **time complexity (best, average, worst), space complexity, prerequisite on data, and stability**. Which one would you recommend for a dataset of 10 million sorted records, and why?

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Linear Search Algorithm and Average-Case Analysis

**Algorithm (3 marks):**

```
ALGORITHM LinearSearch(A[0..n-1], n, KEY)
BEGIN
    FOR i ← 0 TO n-1 DO
        IF A[i] = KEY THEN
            RETURN i
        END IF
    END FOR
    RETURN -1
END
```

**Explanation (1 mark):** Linear Search sequentially inspects each element of the array. If the key matches the current element, the algorithm returns its index immediately. If the entire array is traversed without a match, it returns $-1$. The algorithm makes **one comparison per iteration**.

**Average-Case Analysis (3 marks):**

Assuming the key is equally likely to be at any of the $n$ positions, the number of comparisons required to find the key at position $i$ is $i + 1$ (using 1-based counting for comparisons).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}}(n) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (i + 1) \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \left[ \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} i + \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 1 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \left[ \frac{(n-1)n}{2} + n \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{n^2 + n}{2} \\
&= \frac{n + 1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \dfrac{n+1}{2} = \Theta(n)$.

---

#### Part (b) — Detailed Comparison

| Criterion | Linear Search | Binary Search |
|---|---|---|
| **Best-Case Time** | $\Theta(1)$ — key at index 0 | $\Theta(1)$ — key at middle |
| **Average-Case Time** | $\Theta(n)$ — $\frac{n+1}{2}$ comparisons | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| **Worst-Case Time** | $\Theta(n)$ — $n$ comparisons | $\Theta(\log n)$ — $\log_2 n + 1$ comparisons |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(1)$ iterative | $O(1)$ iterative; $O(\log n)$ recursive |
| **Prerequisite on Data** | Works on **unsorted or sorted** data | **Must be sorted** |
| **Stability** | Deterministic — same input gives same output | Deterministic — same input gives same output |
| **Ease of Implementation** | Very simple | Moderately simple |
| **Suitability for Large Data** | Poor | Excellent |

**Recommendation (1 mark):**

For a dataset of **10 million sorted records**, **Binary Search is unequivocally recommended**.

- Linear Search would require up to **10,000,000 comparisons** in the worst case.
- Binary Search would require only **$\log_2(10{,}000{,}000) \approx 24$ comparisons** in the worst case.
- Since the dataset is already sorted (a precondition for Binary Search), the one-time $O(n \log n)$ sorting cost is avoided, making Binary Search the clear winner.
- The performance gain is approximately **416,000×**, which is a massive practical advantage in real-time systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (for Part B):**
> 1. **Mixing up "stable" with "sorting stability"**: In the context of search algorithms, "stability" means **deterministic output** — i.e., the same input always yields the same index. It does **not** refer to sorting stability. Many students confuse this and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $\log_2$ to a numerical value**: When asked "how many comparisons for $n = 10$ million?", many students write $\log_2 n$ without computing the actual number. KTU awards marks for the **numerical answer** ($\approx 24$).
> 3. **Failing to mention the one-time sort cost** of Binary Search when comparing it to Linear Search for unsorted data. This is a common theoretical omission.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Linear Search** is a **brute-force**, **sequential** algorithm that works on **unsorted data** and has time complexity $\Theta(n)$ in the average and worst cases.
- **Binary Search** is a **divide-and-conquer** algorithm that **requires sorted input** and has time complexity $\Theta(\log n)$.
- The **average number of comparisons** for Linear Search (successful case) is $\dfrac{n+1}{2}$.
- The **recurrence relation** for Binary Search is $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$, solved to give $T(n) = \Theta(\log_2 n)$.
- The **base of the logarithm is 2** because the problem size is halved at each step.
- The **mid-index formula** is `mid = ⌊(low + high) / 2⌋`. The floor function is **mandatory** for arrays of even length.
- The **safe formulation** `mid = low + (high - low) / 2` is used in production code to prevent integer overflow.
- The **loop condition** in Binary Search is `low ≤ high` (not `low < high`) — a frequent KTU trap.
- **Linear Search** has space complexity $O(1)$ and is preferred for **small or unsorted** datasets and **linked lists**.
- **Binary Search** has space complexity $O(1)$ iterative (or $O(\log n)$ recursive) and is preferred for **large sorted arrays**.
- The **decision tree model** of Binary Search has height $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$, which equals the worst-case number of comparisons.
- For $n = 1$ billion, Binary Search needs only **~30 comparisons**, while Linear Search needs **1 billion** — a 33-million-fold speedup.
- **Master Theorem Case 2** ($f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$) is the classification of Binary Search's recurrence.
- Binary Search on an **unsorted array** produces **incorrect results** — never skip the precondition check.
- The **unsuccessful search** in Linear Search always takes exactly $n$ comparisons; in Binary Search, it takes $\Theta(\log n)$ comparisons.
- KTU **frequently tests** the average-case derivation of Linear Search (3 marks) and the worst-case recurrence solution of Binary Search (3–5 marks) as standalone questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorting: Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Insertion Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sorting: Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Insertion Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Sorting?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sorting** is the fundamental operation in computer science of **rearranging the elements of a list or array into a specific order** — most commonly **ascending (non-decreasing)** or **descending (non-increasing)** order. A sorting algorithm is said to be **in-place** if it uses only **O(1)** auxiliary memory, and **stable** if it preserves the relative order of equal-keyed elements.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCITT303)**, comparison-based elementary sorting algorithms form the foundation upon which advanced algorithms like Merge Sort, Quick Sort, and Heap Sort are built. Mastery of Bubble, Selection, and Insertion Sort is therefore a **mandatory prerequisite** for all subsequent modules.

## 1.2 The Three Algorithms at a Glance

| Algorithm | One-line Definition | Intuitive Analogy |
|-----------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Bubble Sort** | Repeatedly compares and swaps adjacent elements until the array is sorted | **Air bubbles in water** — the largest bubble rises to the surface on every pass |
| **Selection Sort** | Repeatedly selects the minimum (or maximum) from the unsorted portion and places it at its correct position | **Talent show auditions** — pick the shortest contestant, line them up; repeat |
| **Insertion Sort** | Builds the sorted array one element at a time by inserting each new element into its correct position in the already-sorted prefix | **Sorting playing cards in your hand** — pick a card, slide it left until it fits |

## 1.3 Real-World Engineering Context

> [!NOTE]
> - **Bubble Sort** is rarely used in production but is heavily tested in KTU exams and interviews for its **simplicity**.
> - **Selection Sort** minimizes the number of **swaps** — useful in systems where writing to memory is expensive (e.g., **flash memory in embedded systems**).
> - **Insertion Sort** is the algorithm of choice for **small arrays (n < 50)** and is used as the base case in **hybrid sorts** like **Timsort** (used by Python and Java) and **Introsort** (used by C++ STL).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithm 1 — Bubble Sort

### Operational Concept
Bubble Sort works in **n − 1 passes**. In each pass, it iterates over the unsorted portion of the array and **swaps adjacent pairs that are in the wrong order**. After pass *i*, the *i-th largest element* is guaranteed to be in its final sorted position at the end.

### Why It Works (The "Why" Behind It)
After every pass, the **largest unsorted element "bubbles up"** to the end of the unsorted region because it gets repeatedly swapped rightwards whenever a smaller neighbour is found.

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. Start from `i = 0` to `n − 2` (outer loop controls passes).
2. For each pass, set `j = 0` to `n − i − 2` (inner loop, avoiding the already-sorted tail).
3. Compare `a[j]` and `a[j+1]`. If `a[j] > a[j+1]`, **swap** them.
4. **Optimization**: Maintain a `swapped` flag. If an entire pass completes with **zero swaps**, the array is already sorted — terminate early.

### Properties
- **Stable**: ✅ Yes (swapping only when `>` keeps equal elements in original order)
- **In-place**: ✅ Yes
- **Adaptive**: ✅ Yes (best case becomes O(n) with the early-exit flag)

---

## 2.2 Algorithm 2 — Selection Sort

### Operational Concept
Selection Sort divides the array into a **sorted prefix** (left) and an **unsorted suffix** (right). In each pass, it **scans the unsorted suffix to find the minimum element** and **swaps it with the first element of the unsorted portion**.

### Why It Works
Because we always place the *smallest remaining* element at the boundary of the sorted region, the sorted region grows by exactly one element per pass and is always in its **final, correct order** — no further adjustment is needed.

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. For `i = 0` to `n − 2`:
2.   Set `min_idx = i`.
3.   For `j = i + 1` to `n − 1`:
4.      If `a[j] < a[min_idx]`, update `min_idx = j`.
5.   If `min_idx ≠ i`, swap `a[i]` and `a[min_idx]`.

### Properties
- **Stable**: ❌ No (the swap can disrupt the relative order of equal elements)
- **In-place**: ✅ Yes
- **Adaptive**: ❌ No (always performs the same number of comparisons)
- **Minimum swaps**: Only **n − 1** swaps — the best among elementary sorts.

---

## 2.3 Algorithm 3 — Insertion Sort

### Operational Concept
Insertion Sort maintains a **sorted prefix** that grows by one element per iteration. For each new element (the **key**), it **shifts elements in the sorted prefix rightward** until it finds the correct insertion position.

### Why It Works
Each iteration of the outer loop assumes that `a[0..i-1]` is already sorted. The inner loop then finds the unique correct position for `a[i]` by shifting larger elements one slot to the right, preserving the sorted invariant.

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. For `i = 1` to `n − 1`:
2.   `key = a[i]`, `j = i − 1`.
3.   While `j ≥ 0` AND `a[j] > key`:
4.      `a[j+1] = a[j]`, `j = j − 1`.
5.   `a[j+1] = key`.

### Properties
- **Stable**: ✅ Yes (uses strict `>` for shifting, so equal elements are not reordered)
- **In-place**: ✅ Yes
- **Adaptive**: ✅ Yes (best case O(n) on already-sorted input)
- **Online**: ✅ Yes (can sort as data arrives)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single most important summary** for KTU Module 2 examination questions on elementary sorting. **Memorize it.**

| Property | Bubble Sort | Selection Sort | Insertion Sort |
|----------|:-----------:|:-------------:|:--------------:|
| **Best Case Time** | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Average Case Time** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| **Worst Case Time** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **No. of Comparisons (Worst)** | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ |
| **No. of Swaps (Worst)** | $O(n^2)$ | $n-1$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| **Stable?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **In-place?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Adaptive?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Online?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |

### Key Derived Quantities
- **Total comparisons** for any pair-wise pass-based sort:

$$T_{cmp}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2}(n-i-1) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

- **Worst-case inversions** in Insertion Sort = $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ (reverse-sorted input), which directly determines the worst-case shifts.
- **Best-case swaps** in Bubble Sort = $0$ (already sorted, with flag optimization).

### Real-World Utility in Engineering
- **Database systems**: Insertion Sort is used for **small partition sizes** in **Timsort** within Python's `sorted()`.
- **Embedded firmware**: Selection Sort's **minimal swap count** suits **EEPROM/Flash** writes where each write reduces chip lifespan.
- **Network packet scheduling**: Stable sorts preserve FIFO ordering — critical for **fair queueing in routers**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Sorting the Array $[5,\ 3,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$

We will trace all three algorithms on the **same input array** so you can compare their behaviour step-by-step. Let

$$A = [5,\ 3,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4], \quad n = 5$$

---

## 3.2 Bubble Sort — Exhaustive Pass-by-Pass Trace

**Initial State:** $[5,\ 3,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$

**Pass 1 (i = 0):** Compare adjacent pairs from index 0 to 3.

| Step j | Compare (a[j], a[j+1]) | Condition | Array State | Swap? |
|:------:|:----------------------:|:---------:|:-----------:|:-----:|
| 0 | (5, 3) | $5 > 3$ | $[3,\ 5,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$ | ✅ |
| 1 | (5, 8) | $5 < 8$ | $[3,\ 5,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$ | ❌ |
| 2 | (8, 1) | $8 > 1$ | $[3,\ 5,\ 1,\ 8,\ 4]$ | ✅ |
| 3 | (8, 4) | $8 > 4$ | $[3,\ 5,\ 1,\ 4,\ 8]$ | ✅ |

**End of Pass 1:** Largest element **8** is in its final position. Swaps this pass = 3.

**Pass 2 (i = 1):** Compare from index 0 to 2.

| Step j | Compare | Condition | Array State | Swap? |
|:------:|:-------:|:---------:|:-----------:|:-----:|
| 0 | (3, 5) | $3 < 5$ | $[3,\ 5,\ 1,\ 4,\ 8]$ | ❌ |
| 1 | (5, 1) | $5 > 1$ | $[3,\ 1,\ 5,\ 4,\ 8]$ | ✅ |
| 2 | (5, 4) | $5 > 4$ | $[3,\ 1,\ 4,\ 5,\ 8]$ | ✅ |

**End of Pass 2:** **5** is now in its final position. Swaps = 2.

**Pass 3 (i = 2):** Compare from index 0 to 1.

| Step j | Compare | Condition | Array State | Swap? |
|:------:|:-------:|:---------:|:-----------:|:-----:|
| 0 | (3, 1) | $3 > 1$ | $[1,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 8]$ | ✅ |
| 1 | (3, 4) | $3 < 4$ | $[1,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 8]$ | ❌ |

**End of Pass 3:** **3** and **4** are correctly placed. Swaps = 1.

**Pass 4 (i = 3):** Only one comparison.

| Step j | Compare | Condition | Array State | Swap? |
|:------:|:-------:|:---------:|:-----------:|:-----:|
| 0 | (1, 3) | $1 < 3$ | $[1,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 8]$ | ❌ |

**End of Pass 4:** Zero swaps → algorithm **terminates early**.

**Final Sorted Array:** $[1,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 8]$

**Tally for this run:**
- Total comparisons $= 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = \frac{5 \cdot 4}{2} = 10$
- Total swaps $= 3 + 2 + 1 + 0 = 6$

---

## 3.3 Selection Sort — Exhaustive Pass-by-Pass Trace

**Initial State:** $[5,\ 3,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$

**Pass 1 (i = 0):** Search indices 0 to 4 for the minimum.

| Step j | Candidate | a[j] vs a[min_idx] | Action | Array State |
|:------:|:---------:|:------------------:|:------:|:-----------:|
| init | min_idx = 0, a[0] = 5 | — | baseline | $[5,\ 3,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$ |
| 1 | a[1] = 3 | $3 < 5$ | min_idx ← 1 | $[5,\ 3,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$ |
| 2 | a[2] = 8 | $8 > 3$ | no change | $[5,\ 3,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$ |
| 3 | a[3] = 1 | $1 < 3$ | min_idx ← 3 | $[5,\ 3,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$ |
| 4 | a[4] = 4 | $4 > 1$ | no change | $[5,\ 3,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$ |

**After Pass 1:** min_idx = 3. Swap a[0] and a[3] → $[1,\ 3,\ 8,\ 5,\ 4]$. **Swaps = 1.**

**Pass 2 (i = 1):** Search indices 1 to 4.

| Step j | Candidate | Action | Array State |
|:------:|:---------:|:------:|:-----------:|
| init | min_idx = 1, a[1] = 3 | baseline | $[1,\ 3,\ 8,\ 5,\ 4]$ |
| 2 | a[2] = 8 | no change | $[1,\ 3,\ 8,\ 5,\ 4]$ |
| 3 | a[3] = 5 | no change | $[1,\ 3,\ 8,\ 5,\ 4]$ |
| 4 | a[4] = 4 | min_idx ← 4 | $[1,\ 3,\ 8,\ 5,\ 4]$ |

**After Pass 2:** min_idx = 4. Swap a[1] and a[4] → $[1,\ 3,\ 8,\ 5,\ 4]$ becomes $[1,\ 3,\ 8,\ 5,\ 4]$ wait — $a[1]=3, a[4]=4$. Since $3 < 4$, no swap actually. Let me recheck — min_idx ≠ 1, so we do swap. Swap a[1] and a[4] → $[1,\ 4,\ 8,\ 5,\ 3]$. **Swaps = 1.**

**Pass 3 (i = 2):** Search indices 2 to 4.

| Step j | Candidate | Action | Array State |
|:------:|:---------:|:------:|:-----------:|
| init | min_idx = 2, a[2] = 8 | baseline | $[1,\ 4,\ 8,\ 5,\ 3]$ |
| 3 | a[3] = 5 | min_idx ← 3 | $[1,\ 4,\ 8,\ 5,\ 3]$ |
| 4 | a[4] = 3 | min_idx ← 4 | $[1,\ 4,\ 8,\ 5,\ 3]$ |

**After Pass 3:** min_idx = 4. Swap a[2] and a[4] → $[1,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 8]$. Wait: $a[2]=8, a[4]=3$, so after swap → $[1,\ 4,\ 3,\ 5,\ 8]$. Hmm, let me re-verify the array state from Pass 2.

Let me re-trace cleanly:

| Pass | i | min_idx found | Before Swap | After Swap (if any) |
|:----:|:-:|:-------------:|:-----------:|:-------------------:|
| 1 | 0 | 3 | $[5,\ 3,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$ | $[1,\ 3,\ 8,\ 5,\ 4]$ |
| 2 | 1 | 4 | $[1,\ 3,\ 8,\ 5,\ 4]$ | $[1,\ 4,\ 8,\ 5,\ 3]$ |
| 3 | 2 | 4 | $[1,\ 4,\ 8,\ 5,\ 3]$ | $[1,\ 4,\ 3,\ 5,\ 8]$ |
| 4 | 3 | 3 | $[1,\ 4,\ 3,\ 5,\ 8]$ | (no swap, already sorted) |

**Final Sorted Array:** $[1,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 8]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common student error:** Selection Sort performs **n − 1 passes** (not n). In Pass 4 above, the last element is automatically in place — no need to scan further. Examiners will deduct a mark if you show an unnecessary final pass.

**Tally:**
- Comparisons $= 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 10$
- Swaps $= 3$ (which is $n - 1$)

---

## 3.4 Insertion Sort — Exhaustive Pass-by-Pass Trace

**Initial State:** $[5,\ 3,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$

**Outer Loop Iteration i = 1:** `key = a[1] = 3`, `j = 0`.

| Step | Condition `a[j] > key` | Action | Array State |
|:----:|:----------------------:|:------:|:-----------:|
| — | $a[0] = 5 > 3$ ✅ True | Shift a[0] → a[1]; j = -1 | $[5,\ 5,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$ |
| — | $j = -1$, loop exits | Place key at a[0] | $[3,\ 5,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$ |

**Outer Loop Iteration i = 2:** `key = a[2] = 8`, `j = 1`.

| Step | Condition | Action | Array State |
|:----:|:---------:|:------:|:-----------:|
| — | $a[1] = 5 > 8$ ❌ False | No shift | $[3,\ 5,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$ |
| — | Loop exits | Place key at a[2] | $[3,\ 5,\ 8,\ 1,\ 4]$ |

**Outer Loop Iteration i = 3:** `key = a[3] = 1`, `j = 2`.

| Step | Condition | Action | Array State |
|:----:|:---------:|:------:|:-----------:|
| — | $a[2] = 8 > 1$ ✅ True | Shift a[2] → a[3]; j = 1 | $[3,\ 5,\ 8,\ 8,\ 4]$ |
| — | $a[1] = 5 > 1$ ✅ True | Shift a[1] → a[2]; j = 0 | $[3,\ 5,\ 5,\ 8,\ 4]$ |
| — | $a[0] = 3 > 1$ ✅ True | Shift a[0] → a[1]; j = -1 | $[3,\ 3,\ 5,\ 8,\ 4]$ |
| — | $j = -1$, loop exits | Place key at a[0] | $[1,\ 3,\ 5,\ 8,\ 4]$ |

**Outer Loop Iteration i = 4:** `key = a[4] = 4`, `j = 3`.

| Step | Condition | Action | Array State |
|:----:|:---------:|:------:|:-----------:|
| — | $a[3] = 8 > 4$ ✅ True | Shift a[3] → a[4]; j = 2 | $[1,\ 3,\ 5,\ 8,\ 8]$ |
| — | $a[2] = 5 > 4$ ✅ True | Shift a[2] → a[3]; j = 1 | $[1,\ 3,\ 5,\ 5,\ 8]$ |
| — | $a[1] = 3 > 4$ ❌ False | Loop exits | $[1,\ 3,\ 5,\ 5,\ 8]$ |
| — | — | Place key at a[2] | $[1,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 8]$ |

**Final Sorted Array:** $[1,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 8]$

**Tally:**
- Comparisons $= 1 + 1 + 3 + 3 = 8$
- Shifts $= 1 + 0 + 3 + 2 = 6$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pass-Order Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> Observe how the three algorithms reach the same sorted result via **fundamentally different mechanics**. The matrix below highlights the contrast.

| Aspect | Bubble Sort | Selection Sort | Insertion Sort |
|--------|-------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Sorted Region Grows At** | Right end (largest bubbles up) | Left end (minimum selected) | Left end (key inserted) |
| **Work Per Pass** | Variable (may terminate early) | Scanning for minimum | Shifting sorted prefix |
| **Best-Case Behaviour** | O(n) with flag | Always O(n²) | O(n) on sorted input |
| **Final Position Decided** | After each pass (for the *n-i-th* element) | After each pass (for the *i-th* element) | Immediately (key is placed in its final position relative to prefix) |
| **Typical Failure Mode** | Slow on nearly-sorted data without flag | Slow always; not stable | Slow on reverse-sorted data |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bubble Sort — Control Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> initOuterA["outer i = 0"]
    initOuterA --> checkOuterA{i less than n minus 1}
    checkOuterA -- No --> doneA([Return Sorted Array])
    checkOuterA -- Yes --> initInnerA["inner j = 0, swapped = false"]
    initInnerA --> checkInnerA{j less than n minus i minus 1}
    checkInnerA -- No --> checkSwappedA{swapped equals false}
    checkSwappedA -- Yes --> doneA
    checkSwappedA -- No --> incrOuterA["i = i + 1"]
    incrOuterA --> checkOuterA
    checkInnerA -- Yes --> compareA["Compare a j and a j+1"]
    compareA --> decisionA{a j greater than a j+1}
    decisionA -- Yes --> swapA["Swap a j and a j+1, swapped = true"]
    decisionA -- No --> incrInnerA["j = j + 1"]
    swapA --> incrInnerA
    incrInnerA --> checkInnerA
```

## 4.2 Selection Sort — Control Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start]) --> initOuterB["outer i = 0"]
    initOuterB --> checkOuterB{i less than n minus 1}
    checkOuterB -- No --> doneB([Return Sorted Array])
    checkOuterB -- Yes --> setMinB["min_idx = i"]
    setMinB --> initInnerB["inner j = i + 1"]
    initInnerB --> checkInnerB{j less than n}
    checkInnerB -- No --> checkSwapB{min_idx not equal i}
    checkSwapB -- Yes --> swapB["Swap a i and a min_idx"]
    checkSwapB -- No --> incrOuterB
    swapB --> incrOuterB["i = i + 1"]
    incrOuterB --> checkOuterB
    checkInnerB -- Yes --> decisionB{a j less than a min_idx}
    decisionB -- Yes --> updateMinB["min_idx = j"]
    decisionB -- No --> incrInnerB
    updateMinB --> incrInnerB["j = j + 1"]
    incrInnerB --> checkInnerB
```

## 4.3 Insertion Sort — Control Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC([Start]) --> initOuterC["outer i = 1"]
    initOuterC --> checkOuterC{i less than n}
    checkOuterC -- No --> doneC([Return Sorted Array])
    checkOuterC -- Yes --> setKeyC["key = a i, j = i minus 1"]
    setKeyC --> checkInnerC{j greater or equal 0 AND a j greater than key}
    checkInnerC -- No --> placeC["a j+1 = key"]
    checkInnerC -- Yes --> shiftC["a j+1 = a j, j = j minus 1"]
    shiftC --> checkInnerC
    placeC --> incrOuterC["i = i + 1"]
    incrOuterC --> checkOuterC
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comparison Engine

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram depicts how all three algorithms can be unified under a **single comparison-and-restructure engine** — a useful conceptual model for KTU viva questions on algorithmic abstraction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input["Input Array A of size n"] --> dispatcher{"Algorithm Selector"}
    dispatcher -- Bubble --> bubbleEngine["Adjacent Pair Comparator + Swapper"]
    dispatcher -- Selection --> selectionEngine["Min-Finder + Boundary Swapper"]
    dispatcher -- Insertion --> insertionEngine["Key Extractor + Sorted-Prefix Shifter"]
    bubbleEngine --> output["Sorted Array A"]
    selectionEngine --> output
    insertionEngine --> output
    input --> metrics["Metrics Collector"]
    bubbleEngine --> metrics
    selectionEngine --> metrics
    insertionEngine --> metrics
    metrics --> report["Comparisons, Swaps, Time, Stability Report"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define sorting. List any two properties that distinguish a stable sorting algorithm from an unstable one. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (10 lines, 3 marks):**

> **Sorting** is the process of rearranging the elements of a list/array into a specific order, typically ascending or descending.
>
> A sorting algorithm is **stable** if it preserves the **relative order of equal-keyed elements** in the input when producing the output. It is **unstable** if equal elements may appear in a different order after sorting.
>
> **Example of stable algorithm:** Bubble Sort — it only swaps when `a[j] > a[j+1]`, using strict inequality ensures equal elements never cross.
>
> **Example of unstable algorithm:** Selection Sort — the swap between `a[i]` and `a[min_idx]` can move an element past an equal-valued one, breaking original order.
>
> **Other distinguishing properties** include in-place memory usage and adaptability to pre-sorted input.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is an in-place sorting algorithm? Why is Selection Sort called an in-place algorithm?**

**Model Answer (8 lines, 3 marks):**

> An **in-place sorting algorithm** rearranges the input array using only a **constant amount O(1) of extra memory** beyond the input itself, i.e., no additional arrays proportional to the input size *n* are created.
>
> **Selection Sort is in-place** because during execution it performs only two operations that use auxiliary space:
> 1. A few scalar variables (`i`, `j`, `min_idx`) — **O(1) space**.
> 2. An in-array swap using a single temporary variable.
>
> No additional array, list, or recursive call stack proportional to *n* is created. Hence, Selection Sort qualifies as an in-place algorithm.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, every Module 2 question in Part B carries an **internal choice**. You are strongly advised to attempt **both views** during revision.

---

### 📝 Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) Sort the array $[38,\ 27,\ 43,\ 3,\ 9,\ 82,\ 10]$ using Bubble Sort. Show all passes and count the total number of comparisons and swaps. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the Selection Sort algorithm with a suitable example. State its time and space complexity. (7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (a) — Bubble Sort Trace

**Initial Array:** $A = [38,\ 27,\ 43,\ 3,\ 9,\ 82,\ 10]$, $n = 7$

**Pass 1 (i = 0):** 6 comparisons

| j | Compare | Swap? | Array after step |
|:-:|:-------:|:-----:|:-----------------|
| 0 | (38, 27) | ✅ | $[27,\ 38,\ 43,\ 3,\ 9,\ 82,\ 10]$ |
| 1 | (38, 43) | ❌ | $[27,\ 38,\ 43,\ 3,\ 9,\ 82,\ 10]$ |
| 2 | (43, 3) | ✅ | $[27,\ 38,\ 3,\ 43,\ 9,\ 82,\ 10]$ |
| 3 | (43, 9) | ✅ | $[27,\ 38,\ 3,\ 9,\ 43,\ 82,\ 10]$ |
| 4 | (43, 82) | ❌ | $[27,\ 38,\ 3,\ 9,\ 43,\ 82,\ 10]$ |
| 5 | (82, 10) | ✅ | $[27,\ 38,\ 3,\ 9,\ 43,\ 10,\ 82]$ |

**Pass 1 swaps = 4.**

**Pass 2 (i = 1):** 5 comparisons

| j | Compare | Swap? | Array after step |
|:-:|:-------:|:-----:|:-----------------|
| 0 | (27, 38) | ❌ | $[27,\ 38,\ 3,\ 9,\ 43,\ 10,\ 82]$ |
| 1 | (38, 3) | ✅ | $[27,\ 3,\ 38,\ 9,\ 43,\ 10,\ 82]$ |
| 2 | (38, 9) | ✅ | $[27,\ 3,\ 9,\ 38,\ 43,\ 10,\ 82]$ |
| 3 | (38, 43) | ❌ | $[27,\ 3,\ 9,\ 38,\ 43,\ 10,\ 82]$ |
| 4 | (43, 10) | ✅ | $[27,\ 3,\ 9,\ 38,\ 10,\ 43,\ 82]$ |

**Pass 2 swaps = 3.**

**Pass 3 (i = 2):** 4 comparisons

| j | Compare | Swap? | Array after step |
|:-:|:-------:|:-----:|:-----------------|
| 0 | (27, 3) | ✅ | $[3,\ 27,\ 9,\ 38,\ 10,\ 43,\ 82]$ |
| 1 | (27, 9) | ✅ | $[3,\ 9,\ 27,\ 38,\ 10,\ 43,\ 82]$ |
| 2 | (27, 38) | ❌ | $[3,\ 9,\ 27,\ 38,\ 10,\ 43,\ 82]$ |
| 3 | (38, 10) | ✅ | $[3,\ 9,\ 27,\ 10,\ 38,\ 43,\ 82]$ |

**Pass 3 swaps = 3.**

**Pass 4 (i = 3):** 3 comparisons

| j | Compare | Swap? | Array after step |
|:-:|:-------:|:-----:|:-----------------|
| 0 | (3, 9) | ❌ | $[3,\ 9,\ 27,\ 10,\ 38,\ 43,\ 82]$ |
| 1 | (9, 27) | ❌ | $[3,\ 9,\ 27,\ 10,\ 38,\ 43,\ 82]$ |
| 2 | (27, 10) | ✅ | $[3,\ 9,\ 10,\ 27,\ 38,\ 43,\ 82]$ |

**Pass 4 swaps = 1.**

**Pass 5 (i = 4):** 2 comparisons — no swaps.

**Pass 6 (i = 5):** 1 comparison — no swaps.

**Final Sorted Array:** $[3,\ 9,\ 10,\ 27,\ 38,\ 43,\ 82]$

**Totals:**

$$T_{cmp} = 6 + 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = \frac{7 \cdot 6}{2} = 21$$

$$T_{swap} = 4 + 3 + 3 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 11$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly **forget the last pass** when *n* is the array size. Always run the outer loop from $i = 0$ to $i = n - 2$ — that is $n - 1$ passes. Skipping a pass costs 1 mark; miscounting swaps or comparisons costs another 1 mark.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (b) — Selection Sort Explanation

**Definition (1 mark):**
Selection Sort is a comparison-based, in-place sorting algorithm that divides the array into two sub-arrays — a **sorted left sub-array** and an **unsorted right sub-array**. In each iteration, it finds the **minimum element** of the unsorted sub-array and swaps it with the **leftmost element of that sub-array**, thereby extending the sorted region by one.

**Algorithm (3 marks):**

```text
Algorithm: SelectionSort(A[0..n-1])
1.  for i ← 0 to n-2 do
2.      min_idx ← i
3.      for j ← i+1 to n-1 do
4.          if A[j] < A[min_idx] then
5.              min_idx ← j
6.      if min_idx ≠ i then
7.          swap A[i] and A[min_idx]
8.  end
```

**Example (2 marks):** Consider the array $A = [29,\ 10,\ 14,\ 37,\ 13]$.

| Pass i | Unsorted Sub-array | min_idx | After Swap |
|:------:|:------------------:|:-------:|:----------:|
| 0 | $[29,\ 10,\ 14,\ 37,\ 13]$ | 1 (val 10) | $[10,\ 29,\ 14,\ 37,\ 13]$ |
| 1 | $[29,\ 14,\ 37,\ 13]$ | 4 (val 13) | $[10,\ 13,\ 14,\ 37,\ 29]$ |
| 2 | $[14,\ 37,\ 29]$ | 2 (val 14) | $[10,\ 13,\ 14,\ 37,\ 29]$ |
| 3 | $[37,\ 29]$ | 4 (val 29) | $[10,\ 13,\ 14,\ 29,\ 37]$ |

**Final Array:** $[10,\ 13,\ 14,\ 29,\ 37]$ — sorted in ascending order.

**Complexity Analysis (1 mark):**

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2}\sum_{j=i+1}^{n-1} 1 = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2}(n-i-1) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

Hence $T(n) = O(n^2)$ for **best, average, and worst** cases. **Space complexity** = $O(1)$.

---

### 📝 Question B `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) Sort the array $[12,\ 11,\ 13,\ 5,\ 6]$ using Insertion Sort. Show all intermediate steps and count comparisons. (7 marks)**

**(b) Write the algorithm for Bubble Sort and explain its best, average, and worst case time complexity with justification. (7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (a) — Insertion Sort Trace

**Initial Array:** $A = [12,\ 11,\ 13,\ 5,\ 6]$, $n = 5$

| i | key | Sorted prefix before | Shift sequence | Sorted prefix after | Comparisons |
|:-:|:---:|:--------------------:|:---------------|:--------------------|:-----------:|
| 1 | 11 | $[12]$ | 12 → right | $[11,\ 12]$ | 1 |
| 2 | 13 | $[11,\ 12]$ | none | $[11,\ 12,\ 13]$ | 1 |
| 3 | 5 | $[11,\ 12,\ 13]$ | 13, 12, 11 → right | $[5,\ 11,\ 12,\ 13]$ | 3 |
| 4 | 6 | $[5,\ 11,\ 12,\ 13]$ | 13, 12, 11 → right | $[5,\ 6,\ 11,\ 12,\ 13]$ | 3 |

**Detailed Walkthrough for i = 3 (key = 5):**

```
Start: [12, 11, 13, 5, 6]   ← sorted prefix = [12, 11, 13]
Step 1: j=2, a[2]=13 > 5  → shift: [12, 11, 13, 13, 6]
Step 2: j=1, a[1]=11 > 5  → shift: [12, 11, 11, 13, 6]
Step 3: j=0, a[0]=12 > 5  → shift: [12, 12, 11, 13, 6]
Step 4: j=-1, exit loop    → insert: [5, 12, 11, 13, 6]
```

**Detailed Walkthrough for i = 4 (key = 6):**

```
Start: [5, 12, 11, 13, 6]   ← sorted prefix = [5, 12, 11, 13]
Step 1: j=3, a[3]=13 > 6  → shift: [5, 12, 11, 13, 13]
Step 2: j=2, a[2]=11 > 6  → shift: [5, 12, 11, 11, 13]
Step 3: j=1, a[1]=12 > 6  → shift: [5, 12, 12, 11, 13]
Step 4: j=0, a[0]=5 not > 6 → exit loop → insert: [5, 6, 12, 11, 13]
```

**Final Sorted Array:** $[5,\ 6,\ 11,\ 12,\ 13]$

**Total Comparisons:** $1 + 1 + 3 + 3 = 8$

**Total Shifts:** $1 + 0 + 3 + 3 = 7$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to use `≥` in the inner-loop condition instead of `>`. Using `≥` would break the **stability** of Insertion Sort. Always write the condition as `while j ≥ 0 AND a[j] > key`.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (b) — Bubble Sort Algorithm & Complexity

**Algorithm (3 marks):**

```text
Algorithm: BubbleSort(A[0..n-1])
1.  for i ← 0 to n-2 do
2.      swapped ← false
3.      for j ← 0 to n-i-2 do
4.          if A[j] > A[j+1] then
5.              swap A[j] and A[j+1]
6.              swapped ← true
7.      if swapped = false then
8.          break      // Array is already sorted
9.  end
```

**Complexity Analysis (4 marks):**

**Best Case** — Array is already sorted in ascending order.
- The outer loop runs $n - 1$ times.
- In each pass, the inner loop performs $n - i - 1$ comparisons.
- **No swaps** occur; the `swapped` flag is `false` after Pass 1, so the algorithm exits early.
- Total comparisons $= n - 1$.

$$T_{best}(n) = O(n)$$

**Average Case** — Random permutation of elements.
- Roughly half the comparisons lead to a swap.
- On average, $n(n-1)/4$ swaps occur.
- Total work is quadratic.

$$T_{avg}(n) = O(n^2)$$

**Worst Case** — Array is reverse-sorted: $A = [n,\ n-1,\ \dots,\ 1]$.
- Every comparison results in a swap.
- Comparisons: $\sum_{i=0}^{n-2}(n-i-1) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$
- Swaps: $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ (same as comparisons in the worst case).

$$T_{worst}(n) = O(n^2)$$

**Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ — only a few scalar variables and a boolean flag are used.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students incorrectly claim Bubble Sort is **always** $O(n^2)$. You **must** mention the **early-termination optimization** (the `swapped` flag) to justify the $O(n)$ best case. Without this, you lose 1 mark on Part B.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2**

- 📌 **Sorting** = rearranging elements into ascending/descending order. Three elementary comparison-based sorts: **Bubble, Selection, Insertion**.

- 📌 All three algorithms run in **O(n²)** worst-case time but differ in **best-case behaviour**:
  - Bubble Sort: **O(n)** with the `swapped` flag
  - Selection Sort: **O(n²)** always (no early exit)
  - Insertion Sort: **O(n)** on already-sorted input

- 📌 **Space complexity** for all three is **O(1)** — they are **in-place** algorithms.

- 📌 **Stability** matters when sorting records with multiple keys (e.g., sort students first by name, then by roll number). Always preserve the secondary order.
  - **Stable:** Bubble Sort, Insertion Sort
  - **Unstable:** Selection Sort (can be made stable with effort, but is not by default)

- 📌 **Number of comparisons** in the worst case for all three:

$$T_{cmp}(n) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = O(n^2)$$

- 📌 **Number of swaps** is the key differentiator:
  - **Bubble Sort:** up to $O(n^2)$ swaps
  - **Selection Sort:** exactly $n - 1$ swaps (at most)
  - **Insertion Sort:** up to $O(n^2)$ shifts (shifts, not swaps)

- 📌 **Bubble Sort** → largest element *bubbles up* to the right.
- 📌 **Selection Sort** → minimum element *selected* and placed at the left.
- 📌 **Insertion Sort** → new element *inserted* into the sorted left prefix.

- 📌 **Practical usage:**
  - Insertion Sort is used in **Timsort** (Python, Java) for small sub-arrays.
  - Selection Sort is preferred when **swap cost is high** (e.g., flash memory).
  - Bubble Sort is mainly **pedagogical** — not used in production.

- 📌 **KTU exam formula to remember:** $T(n) = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ comparisons; **always show all $n-1$ passes** in trace questions.

- 📌 **Inner-loop bound** in Bubble Sort is `j < n - i - 1` (NOT `j < n - 1`) — the `- i - 1` is critical to skip the already-sorted tail and is a frequent source of errors.

- 📌 **Insertion Sort's inner condition** must be `a[j] > key` (strict), **never** `a[j] ≥ key`, otherwise stability is lost.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Sorting: Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Insertion Sort**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quicksort and Merge Sort (Recursive Algorithms only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quicksort and Merge Sort (Recursive Algorithms only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Quicksort — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quicksort** is a recursive, **divide-and-conquer** sorting algorithm introduced by **C. A. R. Hoare (1961)**. It works by selecting a **pivot element** from the array and **partitioning** the remaining elements into two sub-arrays — those less than the pivot and those greater than the pivot — followed by recursive application of the same procedure on the sub-arrays.

- It is an **in-place** algorithm (uses only **O(log n)** auxiliary stack space for recursion).
- It is **not stable** — equal keys may be reordered.
- It is the **default sorting algorithm** in most standard C/C++ library implementations (`qsort`) because of its excellent cache behaviour and low constant factors.

## 1.2 Merge Sort — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Merge Sort** is a recursive, **divide-and-conquer** sorting algorithm invented by **John von Neumann (1945)**. The array is recursively divided into two halves until each sub-array contains a single element (a trivially sorted list). The sorted sub-arrays are then **merged** back together in sorted order.

- It requires **O(n)** additional auxiliary space (an auxiliary array is used during merging).
- It is a **stable** sort — the relative order of equal elements is preserved.
- It guarantees **O(n log n)** time in **all three cases** (best, average, worst).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Quicksort Analogy — *"The Classroom Line-Up"*:**
> Imagine a teacher who wants to arrange students by height. The teacher picks one student as the **reference (pivot)** and asks everyone shorter to stand on the left and everyone taller to stand on the right. The teacher then repeats this on each side. The pivot is now in its **final correct position** and never needs to move again.

> [!NOTE]
> **Merge Sort Analogy — *"The Sorted Deck of Cards"*:**
> Imagine splitting a deck of 52 shuffled cards into two piles of 26. Keep splitting each pile until you have 52 single-card piles (each is trivially sorted). Now repeatedly merge pairs of piles by comparing the top cards and placing the smaller one in a new pile. The two sorted piles become one larger sorted pile. This merge-and-combine is repeated until a single sorted deck of 52 remains.

## 1.4 Key Performance Constants (at a Glance)

| Algorithm | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space | Stable |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|------------|-------|--------|
| **Quicksort**  | **O(n log n)** | **O(n log n)** | **O(n²)**  | **O(log n)** | **No**  |
| **Merge Sort** | **O(n log n)** | **O(n log n)** | **O(n log n)** | **O(n)**  | **Yes** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Divide-and-Conquer Paradigm (Common to Both)

Both algorithms follow the canonical three-step divide-and-conquer pattern:

1. **Divide:** Split the problem into smaller sub-problems of the **same type**.
2. **Conquer:** Recursively solve each sub-problem.
3. **Combine:** Merge the solutions of sub-problems into the final solution.

> [!NOTE]
> The crucial difference:
> - **Quicksort** performs the **Combine** step *implicitly* (partitioning places elements in their final region — no extra merge pass is needed).
> - **Merge Sort** requires an **explicit, costly Combine** step (the linear-time merge procedure).

## 2.2 Quicksort — Operational Logic (Lomuto Partition Scheme)

**Pseudocode (C-style):**

```
Quicksort(A, low, high):
    if low < high:
        pivotIndex = Partition(A, low, high)
        Quicksort(A, low, pivotIndex - 1)
        Quicksort(A, pivotIndex + 1, high)

Partition(A, low, high):           // Lomuto scheme, pivot = A[high]
    pivot = A[high]
    i = low - 1
    for j = low to high - 1:
        if A[j] <= pivot:
            i = i + 1
            swap(A[i], A[j])
    swap(A[i + 1], A[high])
    return i + 1
```

**Operational Steps:**

- **Why "Partition First"?** Partition does the heavy lifting — it places the pivot in its **final sorted position** and ensures all elements to its left are ≤ pivot and all to its right are > pivot.
- **Why "Recurse on Sub-arrays"?** Because after partitioning, the two sub-arrays are *independently unsorted*. Each must be sorted in place.
- **Why "Base Case low ≥ high"?** A sub-array of size 0 or 1 is trivially sorted.

## 2.3 Hoare Partition Scheme (More Efficient Variant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hoare's scheme** uses **two pointers** moving towards each other from both ends of the array. It performs **~3× fewer swaps** on average than Lomuto and is the basis of most production-grade quicksort implementations (e.g., `glibc` `qsort`).

**Pseudocode (C-style):**

```
Partition_Hoare(A, low, high):
    pivot = A[low]                // First element as pivot
    i = low - 1
    j = high + 1
    loop:
        do j = j - 1 while A[j] > pivot
        do i = i + 1 while A[i] < pivot
        if i < j:
            swap(A[i], A[j])
        else:
            return j
```

## 2.4 Merge Sort — Operational Logic

**Pseudocode (C-style):**

```
MergeSort(A, left, right):
    if left < right:
        mid = (left + right) / 2
        MergeSort(A, left, mid)        // Sort left half
        MergeSort(A, mid + 1, right)   // Sort right half
        Merge(A, left, mid, right)     // Merge sorted halves

Merge(A, left, mid, right):
    n1 = mid - left + 1
    n2 = right - mid
    L[0..n1-1] = A[left..mid]          // Copy to auxiliary arrays
    R[0..n2-1] = A[mid+1..right]
    i = 0, j = 0, k = left
    while i < n1 and j < n2:
        if L[i] <= R[j]:
            A[k] = L[i]; i = i + 1
        else:
            A[k] = R[j]; j = j + 1
        k = k + 1
    Copy remaining elements of L and R into A
```

**Operational Steps:**

- **Why "Divide Till Size 1"?** A single element is trivially sorted; it serves as the base case of the recursion.
- **Why "Merge in Linear Time"?** Since the two halves are already sorted, we can produce a single sorted array using a **two-pointer linear scan** — like merging two sorted decks.
- **Why "Auxiliary Array"?** A merge cannot be done in-place efficiently; we copy to auxiliary arrays `L` and `R` for safe comparison.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all formulas and recurrence relations** required for the KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examination on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Master Theorem** | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ | Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$ |
| 2 | Merge Sort Recurrence | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$ | $a=2, b=2 \Rightarrow$ Case 2 |
| 3 | Merge Sort Time | $T(n) = \Theta(n \log_2 n)$ | Best, Avg, Worst all same |
| 4 | QuickSort Recurrence (General) | $T(n) = T(k) + T(n-k-1) + cn$ | $k$ = pivot's final position |
| 5 | QuickSort Best/Average | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$ | Achieved with balanced partition |
| 6 | QuickSort Worst | $T(n) = T(n-1) + cn$ | Achieved with skewed partition (sorted input, first/last pivot) |
| 7 | QuickSort Worst Time | $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$ | When pivot is min or max |
| 8 | QuickSort Avg Time | $T(n) = \Theta(n \log_2 n)$ | Expected position uniformly distributed |
| 9 | Merge Sort Space | $\Theta(n)$ | Auxiliary array |
| 10 | QuickSort Space | $\Theta(\log n)$ | Recursion stack only |
| 11 | Number of Comparisons (Merge) | $n \log_2 n - n + 1$ | Exact best-case count |
| 12 | Partition Comparisons (Lomuto) | $n - 1$ per partition | Linear in sub-array size |

> [!WARNING]
> In exam answers, **always specify the base case** $T(1) = \Theta(1)$ and the **constant factor** $c$ when writing recurrences. Omitting these is the #1 reason KTU examiners deduct marks.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Quicksort** is the de-facto sort in OS kernels, databases, and in-memory analytics (Apache Spark's `sortByKey`, Java's `Arrays.sort` for primitives) because of its **cache locality** and **in-place behaviour**.
- **Merge Sort** is the standard choice for **external sorting** (sorting data on disk larger than RAM) and for **sorting linked lists** (where random access is expensive). It is also the foundation of the **Counting Sort / Radix Sort** hybrid algorithms used in production string-sorting libraries.
- The **stability** of merge sort makes it the preferred choice for **multi-key sorts** (e.g., sort students by marks, then by name).
