\text{Steps:} & \\
1. & \quad \text{Set newNode->next = NULL: } O(1) \\
2. & \quad \text{Set newNode->prev = TAIL: } O(1) \\
3. & \quad \text{Set TAIL->next = newNode: } O(1) \\
4. & \quad \text{Update TAIL = newNode: } O(1) \\
\hline
\text{Total:} & \quad 4 \cdot O(1) = O(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case C — Circular SLL (with TAIL pointer maintained):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Steps:} & \\
1. & \quad \text{Set newNode->next = TAIL->next (= HEAD): } O(1) \\
2. & \quad \text{Set TAIL->next = newNode: } O(1) \\
3. & \quad \text{Update TAIL = newNode: } O(1) \\
\hline
\text{Total:} & \quad 3 \cdot O(1) = O(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "DLL tail insertion is O(n)" in the exam. This is **wrong if a TAIL pointer is maintained** (which is standard KTU convention). Always state the **precondition** explicitly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Memory Overhead Ratio

The **memory overhead ratio** of a DLL over an SLL is:

$$
R = \frac{M_{\text{DLL}}}{M_{\text{SLL}}} = \frac{N(D + 2P)}{N(D + P)} = \frac{D + 2P}{D + P}
$$

For a typical KTU exam scenario where $D = 4$ bytes (an `int`) and $P = 8$ bytes (64-bit pointer):

$$
R = \frac{4 + 16}{4 + 8} = \frac{20}{12} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667
$$

This means a DLL consumes **66.7% more memory** than an SLL for the same logical data.

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU 14-mark problems, if you present this derivation, **you must show the substitution step explicitly**. Simply stating the final ratio without substitution loses 2 marks under the valuation key.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Singly Linked List (SLL) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD([HEAD ptr]) --> N1["Node1: DATA=A, NEXT ->"]
    N1 --> N2["Node2: DATA=B, NEXT ->"]
    N2 --> N3["Node3: DATA=C, NEXT ->"]
    N3 --> N4["Node4: DATA=D, NEXT ->"]
    N4 --> N5["Node5: DATA=E, NEXT=NULL"]
    N5 -.-> NIL([NULL Terminator])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The diagram shows a 5-node SLL storing data values A, B, C, D, E. The HEAD pointer is the entry point. The final node's NEXT field is explicitly set to `NULL`, unambiguously marking the end. **Traversal must terminate when `current->next == NULL`.**

---

## 4.2 Doubly Linked List (DLL) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD([HEAD ptr]) --> N1["Node1: PREV=NULL, DATA=A, NEXT ->"]
    N1 <--> N2["Node2: PREV <-, DATA=B, NEXT ->"]
    N2 <--> N3["Node3: PREV <-, DATA=C, NEXT ->"]
    N3 <--> N4["Node4: PREV <-, DATA=D, NEXT ->"]
    N4 <--> N5["Node5: PREV <-, DATA=E, NEXT=NULL"]
    N5 --> TAIL([TAIL ptr])
    HEAD --> HPREV([HEAD->prev=NULL])
    N1 -.-> HPREV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The DLL is a **bidirectional** chain. Both `HEAD->prev` and `TAIL->next` are `NULL`. The solid double-arrows between nodes represent the **two-way coupling** via `prev` and `next` pointers. The list can be traversed from either end in **O(1) per step**.

---

## 4.3 Circular Singly Linked List (CSLL) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD([HEAD ptr]) --> N1["Node1: DATA=A, NEXT ->"]
    N1 --> N2["Node2: DATA=B, NEXT ->"]
    N2 --> N3["Node3: DATA=C, NEXT ->"]
    N3 --> N4["Node4: DATA=D, NEXT ->"]
    N4 --> N5["Node5: DATA=E, NEXT ->"]
    N5 -.-> HEAD
    TAIL([TAIL ptr]) --> N5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The CSLL forms a **closed ring** with no NULL terminator. The dotted line from `Node5` back to `HEAD` represents the **circular reconnection**. The list is logically infinite under unbounded traversal — termination must be governed by a **counter** (e.g., "stop after visiting N nodes") rather than a NULL check. **Failure to use a counter results in an infinite loop** — one of the most common bugs in CSLL implementations.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — All Three Types Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SLL ["SINGLY LINKED LIST"]
        S1[Node1: DATA, NEXT] --> S2[Node2: DATA, NEXT]
        S2 --> S3[Node3: DATA, NEXT]
        S3 --> S4[Node4: DATA, NEXT]
        S4 --> SNULL([NULL])
    end

    subgraph DLL ["DOUBLY LINKED LIST"]
        DNULL1([NULL]) --> D1["Node1: PREV, DATA, NEXT"]
        D1 <--> D2["Node2: PREV, DATA, NEXT"]
        D2 <--> D3["Node3: PREV, DATA, NEXT"]
        D3 <--> D4["Node4: PREV, DATA, NEXT"]
        D4 --> DNULL2([NULL])
    end

    subgraph CSLL ["CIRCULAR SLL"]
        C1["Node1: DATA, NEXT"] --> C2["Node2: DATA, NEXT"]
        C2 --> C3["Node3: DATA, NEXT"]
        C3 --> C4["Node4: DATA, NEXT"]
        C4 --> C5["Node5: DATA, NEXT"]
        C5 -.-> C1
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Node Structure Definitions (C — KTU Standard)

```c
/* Singly Linked List Node */
struct SLLNode {
    int data;
    struct SLLNode *next;
};

/* Doubly Linked List Node */
struct DLLNode {
    struct DLLNode *prev;
    int data;
    struct DLLNode *next;
};

/* Circular Singly Linked List Node (structurally identical to SLL) */
struct CSLLNode {
    int data;
    struct CSLLNode *next;
};
/* Note: CSLL differs from SLL ONLY in the linking logic, NOT in node structure. */
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU 14-Mark Questions:** A common trap question is *"Write the structure of a Circular Linked List node."* Many students incorrectly add an extra field. The **node structure of CSLL is identical to SLL** — the circularity is achieved by the **last node pointing to HEAD**, not by any structural change in the node itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: Differentiate between a Singly Linked List and a Doubly Linked List. List any two applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Singly Linked List** | **Doubly Linked List** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of pointers per node | One (`next`) | Two (`prev` and `next`) |
| Traversal direction | Unidirectional (forward only) | Bidirectional (forward and backward) |
| Memory per node | $D + P$ | $D + 2P$ |
| Deletion of a given node (with pointer) | $O(n)$ — predecessor must be found | $O(1)$ — direct unlinking |

**Applications of SLL:** (i) Implementation of stacks and queues. (ii) Polynomial representation / sparse matrix storage.

**Applications of DLL:** (i) Browser back-forward navigation history. (ii) LRU cache implementation in operating systems.

*(3 marks — Awarded: 1 mark for valid difference table, 1 mark for SLL apps, 1 mark for DLL apps)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is a Circular Linked List? Why is there no NULL pointer in a circular linked list?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Circular Linked List** is a variation of a linked list in which the `next` pointer of the **last node** does not point to `NULL`, but instead points back to the **head (first) node**, forming a **closed ring/cycle**. In some implementations, a **TAIL pointer** is also maintained to enable O(1) tail insertions.

There is no `NULL` pointer because the list has **no logical end** — the last node loops back to the first node, creating a continuous cycle. The presence of a `NULL` would break the circular topology. Instead of checking for `NULL`, traversal termination is governed by **counting the number of nodes visited** or by using a **sentinel/flag** to detect when the starting node has been revisited.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks for definition with diagram mention, 1.5 marks for justification of NULL absence)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the classification of linked lists with neat diagrams. Compare singly, doubly, and circular linked lists based on memory consumption, traversal direction, and time complexity of insertion/deletion operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given a Singly Linked List of $N$ nodes, derive the total memory consumed. If a Doubly Linked List is used for the same data, calculate the **additional memory overhead** in both bytes and percentage. Assume: $N = 500$, data field size $D = 4$ bytes, pointer size $P = 8$ bytes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Classification of Linked Lists:**

A linked list is classified based on the **number and direction of pointers** in each node. The three principal classifications are:

1. **Singly Linked List (SLL):** Each node has one pointer (`next`) pointing to the next node. Last node has `next = NULL`. Traversal is **unidirectional**.

2. **Doubly Linked List (DLL):** Each node has two pointers (`prev` and `next`) pointing to previous and next nodes. `HEAD->prev` and `TAIL->next` are `NULL`. Traversal is **bidirectional**.

3. **Circular Singly Linked List (CSLL):** Each node has one pointer (`next`); the last node's `next` points back to `HEAD`, forming a **ring**. No `NULL` is used.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Criterion** | **SLL** | **DLL** | **CSLL** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory per node | $D + P$ | $D + 2P$ | $D + P$ |
| Traversal direction | Forward only | Forward + Backward | Forward (cyclic) |
| Insert at head | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insert at tail (no TAIL ptr) | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Insert at tail (with TAIL ptr) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Delete given node (with ptr) | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |

*(7 marks — 2 marks classification explanation, 2 marks diagrams, 3 marks comparison table)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Memory of SLL**

Each SLL node contains 1 data field ($D$ bytes) and 1 pointer ($P$ bytes).

$$
\text{Memory per SLL node} = D + P
$$

For $N = 500$ nodes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{SLL}} &= N \cdot (D + P) \\
&= 500 \cdot (4 + 8) \\
&= 500 \cdot 12 \\
&= 6000 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Memory of DLL**

Each DLL node contains 1 data field and 2 pointers.

$$
\text{Memory per DLL node} = D + 2P
$$

For $N = 500$ nodes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{DLL}} &= N \cdot (D + 2P) \\
&= 500 \cdot (4 + 16) \\
&= 500 \cdot 20 \\
&= 10{,}000 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Additional Memory Overhead**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta M &= M_{\text{DLL}} - M_{\text{SLL}} \\
&= 10{,}000 - 6000 \\
&= 4000 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Percentage Overhead**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Percentage Overhead} &= \frac{\Delta M}{M_{\text{SLL}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{4000}{6000} \times 100\% \\
&= 66.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Additional memory = **4000 bytes (66.67% overhead)**.

*(7 marks — 2 marks SLL calc, 2 marks DLL calc, 2 marks overhead difference, 1 mark percentage)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure and operations of a **Circular Singly Linked List**. Discuss how insertion at the end and deletion from the beginning differ from a linear SLL. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Circular Singly Linked List with $N = 8$ nodes implementing the **Josephus problem** with step size $k = 3$, determine the **survivor's position** and the **total number of pointer traversals** required. Show the elimination sequence. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Structure of CSLL:**

In a Circular Singly Linked List, every node has a `next` pointer, but the **last node's `next` does not point to NULL** — it points back to the **head node**, forming a logical ring. The list can be entered from any node, and one can traverse infinitely until externally stopped.

**Diagrammatic representation:**

$$
\text{HEAD} \to [A] \to [B] \to [C] \to [D] \to [A] \to \cdots
$$

**Insertion at the End:**

| **Operation** | **Linear SLL** | **Circular SLL (with TAIL ptr)** |
|---|---|---|
| Find last node | Traverse to node with `next == NULL` | `TAIL->next` is the last node — direct access |
| Time complexity | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Update logic | `last->next = newNode; newNode->next = NULL;` | `newNode->next = TAIL->next; TAIL->next = newNode; TAIL = newNode;` |

**Deletion from the Beginning:**

| **Operation** | **Linear SLL** | **Circular SLL (with TAIL ptr)** |
|---|---|---|
| Find predecessor | Not needed | Need TAIL to update `TAIL->next = HEAD->next` |
| Time complexity | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ with TAIL ptr, $O(n)$ without |
| Update logic | `HEAD = HEAD->next; free(oldHead);` | `TAIL->next = HEAD->next; temp = HEAD; HEAD = HEAD->next; free(temp);` |

*(7 marks — 2 marks diagram, 2 marks insertion, 2 marks deletion, 1 mark complexity comparison)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Josephus Problem Setup:**
- $N = 8$ people, numbered $1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8$ in CSLL.
- Step size $k = 3$ (count 3 people, eliminate the 3rd, repeat).
- Initial CSLL: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6 \to 7 \to 8 \to 1$ (back to 1).

**Elimination Sequence:**

| **Round** | **Counting Sequence** | **Eliminated** | **Remaining Nodes** | **Pointer Traversals** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1 \to 2 \to 3$ | 3 | $1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8$ | $2$ |
| 2 | $4 \to 5 \to 6$ | 6 | $1, 2, 4, 5, 7, 8$ | $2$ |
| 3 | $7 \to 8 \to 1$ | 1 | $2, 4, 5, 7, 8$ | $2$ |
| 4 | $2 \to 4 \to 5$ | 5 | $2, 4, 7, 8$ | $2$ |
| 5 | $7 \to 8 \to 2$ | 2 | $4, 7, 8$ | $2$ |
| 6 | $4 \to 7 \to 8$ | 8 | $4, 7$ | $2$ |
| 7 | $4 \to 7 \to 4$ | 4 | $7$ | $2$ |

**Total Pointer Traversals:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} (k - 1) \\
&= (N - 1) \cdot (k - 1) \\
&= (8 - 1) \cdot (3 - 1) \\
&= 7 \cdot 2 \\
&= 14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Survivor:** Position **7**

*(7 marks — 2 marks setup, 3 marks elimination table, 1 mark traversal formula, 1 mark final answer)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing node structure with link logic** (lose 2 marks): Many students claim CSLL has a different node structure than SLL. The node structure is **identical**; only the linking logic differs.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the TAIL pointer precondition** (lose 1–2 marks): O(1) tail insertion in DLL/CSLL is conditional. Always write *"assuming a TAIL pointer is maintained."*
> 3. **Drawing circular lists as terminating in NULL** (lose 2 marks): The defining feature of a CSLL is the **reconnection to HEAD**. Any diagram showing `... -> NULL` is structurally wrong.
> 4. **Skipping unit declarations in numerical problems** (lose 0.5–1 mark): Always write "bytes" or "KB" after numeric answers.
> 5. **Using `^` instead of `NULL`** (lose 0.5 mark): KTU 2024 scheme accepts both, but `NULL` is the modern C standard and preferred.
> 6. **Wrong Josephus counting direction** (lose 2 marks): The elimination is the **k-th person in the count**, not the k-th from a fixed reference. Recount from the **next person after the eliminated one**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ✅

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and review it 24 hours before the exam.**

### 🔑 Structural Distinctions
- **SLL:** 1 pointer, forward-only, ends in `NULL`.
- **DLL:** 2 pointers (`prev`, `next`), bidirectional, both ends in `NULL`.
- **CSLL:** 1 pointer, forward-only, **last node's `next` points to HEAD** — no NULL.

### 🔑 Memory Rules of Thumb
- SLL node = $D + P$
- DLL node = $D + 2P$
- CSLL node = $D + P$ (same as SLL!)
- DLL is **1.5× to 2× the memory** of SLL depending on $D:P$ ratio.

### 🔑 Complexity Golden Rules
- Insert at HEAD: **$O(1)$** for all three types.
- Insert at TAIL: **$O(n)$** without TAIL ptr, **$O(1)$** with TAIL ptr.
- Delete given node ptr: **$O(1)$ for DLL only**; $O(n)$ for SLL and CSLL.
- Reverse traversal: **$O(1)$ per step for DLL only**.

### 🔑 Application Match (High-Yield for Viva)
- Round-robin scheduling → **CSLL**
- Undo-Redo buffer → **DLL**
- Polynomial multiplication → **SLL**
- Josephus problem → **CSLL**
- LRU cache → **DLL**

### 🔑 Common Exam Traps
- A CSLL node structure is **identical to SLL** — only the **link** differs.
- CSLL has **no NULL terminator** — use a **counter** to prevent infinite loops.
- DLL's $O(1)$ deletion at a known node **only works if you have the pointer to that node**; without it, you must still search from HEAD.

### 🔑 One-Line Definitions (For 1-Mark Sub-Parts)
- **SLL:** Linear chain with unidirectional pointers ending in NULL.
- **DLL:** Linear chain with bidirectional pointers, both ends NULL.
- **CSLL:** SLL with last node's `next` redirected to HEAD, forming a closed ring.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations on Singly Linked List - Traversal, Insertion, Deletion and Searching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Operations on Singly Linked List - Traversal, Insertion, Deletion and Searching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Singly Linked List (SLL)** is a linear, dynamic data structure belonging to the family of *linked data structures*. It consists of a sequential chain of **nodes**, where each node is a self-referential structure containing two distinct fields:

1. **DATA field** — stores the actual payload/information (integer, character, object, etc.).
2. **NEXT field** — stores a *pointer/reference* to the next node in the sequence.

The list is accessed via a **HEAD pointer**, which holds the address of the first node. The last node's `NEXT` field is explicitly set to `NULL` (or `None`), acting as the *sentinel terminator* of the list.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"A Singly Linked List is a dynamic, linear collection of nodes where each node contains a data element and a single link pointer to the next node in the sequence, with the final node pointing to NULL and the entire list accessed through a HEAD pointer."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Treasure Hunt Chain"

Imagine a **treasure hunt** in a college campus where a series of clues are hidden in envelopes:

- **Envelope (Node)** = a single clue holder.
- **Clue inside (DATA)** = the actual information/value.
- **Address to next envelope (NEXT pointer)** = the hint telling you *where* the next envelope is hidden.
- **First envelope's location** = the **HEAD pointer** (you must know this to start!).
- **Last envelope has no address** → marked as **"END — No more envelopes here"** (this is `NULL`).

If you lose the HEAD, the entire chain becomes orphaned and inaccessible — exactly like losing the first address in a treasure hunt makes all the subsequent addresses useless, even if the envelopes still physically exist.

### 1.3 The Node Structure (C-Language Representation)

```c
struct Node {
    int data;              // DATA field
    struct Node *next;     // POINTER to next node
};
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **KTU examinations**, the `struct Node` declaration carries **2 marks** in viva/short-answer questions. Students frequently forget the `struct Node` (self-referencing) type instead of writing a forward declaration — *avoid this pitfall*.

### 1.4 Why a Linked List and not an Array?

| Property | Array | Singly Linked List |
|----------|-------|---------------------|
| Memory Allocation | **Static / Contiguous** | **Dynamic / Scattered (heap)** |
| Size | Fixed at compile time | Grows/Shrinks at runtime |
| Random Access | O(1) — `arr[i]` direct | **Not possible** — must traverse |
| Insertion/Deletion at start | O(n) — shift all | O(1) — just rewire HEAD |
| Memory Wastage | Possible (unused slots) | None (allocate as needed) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Singly Linked List Node

A node is the atomic unit of the list. Three critical components:

- **DATA**: The useful information.
- **NEXT**: A pointer of the *same struct type* (self-referential).
- **NULL termination**: The terminator — analogous to the period (`.`) at the end of a sentence.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** SLLs power **undo-redo stacks**, **dynamic memory allocators (free lists)**, **polynomial representation**, **adjacency lists in sparse graphs**, and **LRU caches**.

### 2.2 The Four Cardinal Operations — Logic Breakdown

Every operation on a Singly Linked List can be decomposed into three logical sub-steps, which form the foundation of all algorithmic manipulation:

1. **Allocate / Identify** the relevant node(s) using `malloc()` or traversal.
2. **Rewire** the `NEXT` pointers (this is the *core mutation* step).
3. **Free / Return** the deallocated memory (in deletion) or the found node (in search).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Time-Complexity Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is **examination gold**. KTU examiners frequently award **2 marks** solely for correct time-complexity identification.

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space Used (Aux) |
|-----------|:---------:|:------------:|:----------:|:----------------:|
| **Traversal** | $\Omega(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Search (Linear)** | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Insertion at Head** | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Insertion at Tail** | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Insertion at Position $k$** | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(k)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Deletion at Head** | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Deletion at Tail** | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Deletion at Position $k$** | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(k)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |

### 2.4 Boundary Conditions (Edge Cases)

These are the **defensive programming** scenarios that examiners *love* to test:

- **Empty List**: `HEAD == NULL` — every operation must check this first.
- **Single Node**: `HEAD != NULL` and `HEAD->next == NULL` — deletion must reset `HEAD = NULL`.
- **Insertion/Deletion at position 1**: This is the **head operation**, no traversal needed.
- **Insertion/Deletion at position $n$ (last)**: Requires traversal till the *second-to-last* node.
- **Invalid Position $k > n$ or $k < 1$**: Operation must be safely aborted (or appended at end, per question wording).

### 2.5 The "Two-Pointer" Pattern

A foundational traversal pattern used across all SLL operations:

- **Pointer `temp`** — the *current* node being examined (starts at `HEAD`).
- **Pointer `prev`** — the *previous* node, lagging one step behind `temp` (used in deletion).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Why behind two pointers**: Since SLL has *no backward link*, to modify the *predecessor's* `NEXT` during deletion, we *must* remember it during the walk. This is the central insight of singly-linked list manipulation.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of SLL |
|-------------|-------------|
| **Operating Systems** | Process scheduling linked queues |
| **Compilers** | Symbol table chaining for hash collisions |
| **Music Player** | "Next track" navigation (prev. pointer not needed) |
| **Image Viewer** | Sequential forward slideshow |
| **Polynomial Arithmetic** | Storing sparse polynomials with (coeff, power, next) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Algorithms & Analytical Trace

### 3.1 Operation 1: Traversal

**Goal:** Visit every node from HEAD to the last node, performing some action (print, sum, count, etc.).

#### Algorithm (Pseudocode)

```
ALGORITHM: SLL_Traverse(HEAD)
BEGIN
    IF HEAD == NULL THEN
        PRINT "List is Empty"
        RETURN
    ENDIF
    temp ← HEAD
    WHILE temp != NULL DO
        PROCESS temp->data        // e.g., PRINT
        temp ← temp->next
    ENDWHILE
END
```

#### Step-by-Step Trace (Dry Run)

Let the list be: `HEAD → 10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`

| Step | `temp` | `temp->data` | Action | `temp = temp->next` |
|:----:|:------:|:------------:|:-------|:-------------------:|
| Init | `&node10` | — | Initialize | `&node20` |
| 1 | `&node10` | 10 | Print 10 | `&node20` |
| 2 | `&node20` | 20 | Print 20 | `&node30` |
| 3 | `&node30` | 30 | Print 30 | `NULL` |
| 4 | `NULL` | — | Loop exits | — |

> **Time complexity derivation:** The loop executes exactly once per node, hence $n$ iterations for $n$ nodes.

$$
T_{\text{traverse}}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 1 = n \implies O(n)
$$

---

### 3.2 Operation 2: Insertion

Three sub-cases: **(a) at beginning**, **(b) at end**, **(c) at a given position $k$**.

#### 3.2.1 Insertion at the Beginning

```
ALGORITHM: Insert_At_Begin(HEAD, value)
BEGIN
    newNode ← ALLOCATE node
    newNode->data ← value
    newNode->next ← HEAD        // (Step 1) Wire new node to old head
    HEAD ← newNode              // (Step 2) Move HEAD to new node
END
```

**Pointer Rewiring Logic (Why this works):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{newNode} \cdot \text{next} &\leftarrow \text{HEAD} \\
\text{HEAD} &\leftarrow \text{newNode}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why this order?** If we changed `HEAD` *first*, we would lose the only reference to the old list. Always *rebuild forward links before reassigning the anchor*.

#### 3.2.2 Insertion at the End

```
ALGORITHM: Insert_At_End(HEAD, value)
BEGIN
    newNode ← ALLOCATE node
    newNode->data ← value
    newNode->next ← NULL
    IF HEAD == NULL THEN
        HEAD ← newNode
        RETURN
    ENDIF
    temp ← HEAD
    WHILE temp->next != NULL DO  // Stop at LAST node
        temp ← temp->next
    ENDWHILE
    temp->next ← newNode         // (Step 3) Wire last node to new node
END
```

**Trace:** List `10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`, insert `40`.

| Step | `temp` | `temp->next` | Decision |
|:----:|:------:|:------------:|:---------|
| 1 | `&node10` | `&node20` | Continue |
| 2 | `&node20` | `&node30` | Continue |
| 3 | `&node30` | `NULL` | **Stop** |
| 4 | `&node30` | — | `temp->next = &node40` |

Final list: `10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`

$$
T_{\text{insert\_end}}(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(must walk to last node)}
$$

#### 3.2.3 Insertion at Position $k$ (General Case)

```
ALGORITHM: Insert_At_Position(HEAD, value, k)
BEGIN
    IF k < 1 THEN PRINT "Invalid"; RETURN
    IF k == 1 THEN Call Insert_At_Begin; RETURN
    newNode ← ALLOCATE node
    newNode->data ← value
    temp ← HEAD
    FOR i ← 1 TO (k-2) DO        // Walk to (k-1)th node
        IF temp == NULL THEN PRINT "Out of range"; RETURN
        temp ← temp->next
    ENDFOR
    IF temp == NULL THEN PRINT "Out of range"; RETURN
    newNode->next ← temp->next   // (a) Rewire forward
    temp->next ← newNode         // (b) Rewire backward
END
```

**Trace:** List `10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`, insert `25` at position $k=3$.

| Step | Variable | Value | Action |
|:----:|:--------:|:-----:|:-------|
| 1 | `k=3` | 3 | Not at head, proceed |
| 2 | `temp` | `&node10` | Loop `i=1` (1 to $k-2=1$) |
| 3 | `temp` | `&node20` | `temp = temp->next` |
| 4 | `newNode->next` | `&node30` | Save link to 30 |
| 5 | `temp->next` | `&newNode` | Wire 20 → 25 |

Final list: `10 → 20 → 25 → 30 → NULL` ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rewiring Order:** Always set `newNode->next` *before* breaking the old `temp->next` link. Otherwise the trail to the rest of the list is lost.

---

### 3.3 Operation 3: Deletion

#### 3.3.1 Deletion at the Beginning

```
ALGORITHM: Delete_At_Begin(HEAD)
BEGIN
    IF HEAD == NULL THEN
        PRINT "Underflow — List Empty"
        RETURN
    ENDIF
    temp ← HEAD
    HEAD ← HEAD->next           // Move HEAD forward
    FREE temp                   // Deallocate old head
END
```

$$
T_{\text{del\_begin}}(n) = O(1)
$$

#### 3.3.2 Deletion at the End

```
ALGORITHM: Delete_At_End(HEAD)
BEGIN
    IF HEAD == NULL THEN PRINT "Underflow"; RETURN
    IF HEAD->next == NULL THEN  // Single node case
        FREE HEAD
        HEAD ← NULL
        RETURN
    ENDIF
    prev ← NULL
    temp ← HEAD
    WHILE temp->next != NULL DO
        prev ← temp
        temp ← temp->next
    ENDWHILE
    prev->next ← NULL           // Second-last becomes last
    FREE temp
END
```

**Trace:** List `10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`, delete last.

| Step | `prev` | `temp` | `temp->next` |
|:----:|:------:|:------:|:------------:|
| 1 | `NULL` | `&node10` | `&node20` |
| 2 | `&node10` | `&node20` | `&node30` |
| 3 | `&node20` | `&node30` | `&node40` |
| 4 | `&node30` | `&node40` | `NULL` (stop) |
| Final | `&node30` | `&node40` (free) | `prev->next = NULL` |

Result: `10 → 20 → 30 → NULL` ✓

#### 3.3.3 Deletion at Position $k$

```
ALGORITHM: Delete_At_Position(HEAD, k)
BEGIN
    IF HEAD == NULL OR k < 1 THEN PRINT "Invalid"; RETURN
    IF k == 1 THEN Call Delete_At_Begin; RETURN
    prev ← NULL
    temp ← HEAD
    FOR i ← 1 TO (k-1) DO
        prev ← temp
        temp ← temp->next
        IF temp == NULL THEN PRINT "Out of range"; RETURN
    ENDFOR
    prev->next ← temp->next      // Bypass the deleted node
    FREE temp
END
```

**Trace:** List `10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`, delete position $k=2$ (delete 20).

| Step | `prev` | `temp` | Action |
|:----:|:------:|:------:|:-------|
| Init | `NULL` | `&node10` | Start |
| $i=1$ | `&node10` | `&node20` | Advance |
| Stop | `&node10` | `&node20` | Bypass |
| Final | — | — | `prev->next = temp->next = &node30` |

Result: `10 → 30 → 40 → NULL` ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Memory Leak Alert:** In KTU exams, students often forget the `free(temp)` step. This costs **1 mark** in the `c` programming paper. Always mention deallocation.

---

### 3.4 Operation 4: Searching (Linear Search)

Since SLL has no random access, only **linear search** is possible.

```
ALGORITHM: Search_SLL(HEAD, key)
BEGIN
    IF HEAD == NULL THEN
        PRINT "List is Empty"
        RETURN -1
    ENDIF
    temp ← HEAD
    position ← 1
    WHILE temp != NULL DO
        IF temp->data == key THEN
            RETURN position      // FOUND
        ENDIF
        temp ← temp->next
        position ← position + 1
    ENDWHILE
    RETURN -1                    // NOT FOUND
END
```

**Trace:** List `15 → 25 → 35 → 45 → NULL`, search for `key = 35`.

| Iteration | `temp->data` | `position` | Match? | Return |
|:---------:|:------------:|:----------:|:------:|:------:|
| 1 | 15 | 1 | No | Continue |
| 2 | 25 | 2 | No | Continue |
| 3 | 35 | 3 | **Yes** | **Return 3** |

> **Time complexity:**
> 
> $$
> T_{\text{search}}(n) = \begin{cases} O(1) & \text{best (first element)} \\ O(n) & \text{avg / worst} \end{cases}
> $$

### 3.5 Master Comparison: Insert vs. Delete

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Insertion: } & O(1) \text{ at head, } O(n) \text{ at tail} \\
\text{Deletion: } & O(1) \text{ at head, } O(n) \text{ at tail} \\
\text{Reason: } & \text{Singly-linked = no backward pointer, traversal mandatory for tail}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Anatomy of a Singly Linked List

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HEAD]:::head --> B1[Node 1: data=10 next=ptr]:::node
    B1 --> B2[Node 2: data=20 next=ptr]:::node
    B2 --> B3[Node 3: data=30 next=ptr]:::node
    B3 --> B4[Node 4: data=40 next=NULL]:::node
    B4 --> B5[NULL]:::null

    classDef head fill:#1f3a93,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#1f3a93,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef null fill:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Operation: Insertion at Beginning (Insert `5` before `10`)

**BEFORE:**
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[HEAD]:::head --> N1A[10]:::node --> N2A[20]:::node --> N3A[30]:::node --> NA[NULL]:::null
    classDef head fill:#1f3a93,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#1f3a93
    classDef null fill:#000,color:#fff
```

**AFTER:** Two rewire steps:
- New node's `next` ← old HEAD
- HEAD ← New node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2[HEAD]:::head --> N0B["5 NEW"]:::new --> N1B[10]:::node --> N2B[20]:::node --> N3B[30]:::node --> NB[NULL]:::null
    classDef head fill:#1f3a93,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef new fill:#27ae60,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#1f3a93
    classDef null fill:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Green node** = newly inserted element. Note that no existing node's pointer changed, except the *HEAD anchor* itself.

### 4.3 Operation: Insertion at Position $k=3$ (Insert `25` between `20` and `30`)

**BEFORE:** `10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`

**AFTER:** `10 → 20 → 25 → 30 → 40 → NULL`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H3[HEAD]:::head --> A3[10]:::node --> B3[20]:::node --> C3["25 NEW"]:::new --> D3[30]:::node --> E3[40]:::node --> F3[NULL]:::null
    classDef head fill:#1f3a93,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef new fill:#27ae60,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#1f3a93
    classDef null fill:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Rewire order:** `25→next = 20→next (i.e. &30)`, then `20→next = &25`.

### 4.4 Operation: Deletion at Position $k=2$ (Delete node `20`)

**BEFORE:** `10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`

**AFTER:** `10 → 30 → 40 → NULL`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H4[HEAD]:::head --> A4[10]:::node --> B4["20 X DELETED"]:::deleted --> C4[30]:::node --> D4[40]:::node --> E4[NULL]:::null
    classDef head fill:#1f3a93,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef deleted fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#1f3a93
    classDef null fill:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Pointer bypass:** `prev->next = prev->next->next` (here: `10→next = 20→next = &30`).

### 4.5 Operation: Deletion at End (Delete `40`)

**BEFORE:** `10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`

**AFTER:** `10 → 20 → 30 → NULL` and `30→next = NULL`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H5[HEAD]:::head --> A5[10]:::node --> B5[20]:::node --> C5[30]:::node --> D5["40 X DELETED"]:::deleted --> E5[NULL]:::null
    classDef head fill:#1f3a93,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef deleted fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#1f3a93
    classDef null fill:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Why traversal is needed:** There is no way to reach `30` (the new last node) from `40`; we must walk from HEAD.

### 4.6 Operation: Searching (Linear Walk for `key = 35`)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H6[HEAD]:::head --> S1["15 visit"]:::scan --> S2["25 visit"]:::scan --> S3["35 FOUND"]:::found --> S4["45 not visited"]:::skip --> E6[NULL]:::null
    classDef head fill:#1f3a93,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef scan fill:#f39c12,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef found fill:#27ae60,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef skip fill:#bdc3c7,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    classDef null fill:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Yellow = scanned**, **Green = match found**, **Gray = never reached (early termination)**.

### 4.7 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SLL Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User calls operation]:::stepA --> B{List Empty?}:::stepB
    B -- Yes --> Z[Return Error / Underflow]:::stepZ
    B -- No --> C[Set temp = HEAD]:::stepC
    C --> D{Traversal needed?}:::stepD
    D -- No HEAD op --> E[Perform pointer rewire]:::stepE
    D -- Yes position n op --> F[Walk temp until target node]:::stepF
    F --> E
    E --> G[Update HEAD if needed]:::stepG
    G --> H[Free memory if deletion]:::stepH
    H --> I[Return success status]:::stepI

    classDef stepA fill:#3498db,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef stepB fill:#f1c40f,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef stepC fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef stepD fill:#f1c40f,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef stepE fill:#e67e22,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef stepF fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef stepG fill:#e67e22,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef stepH fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef stepI fill:#27ae60,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef stepZ fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the flow:** Every SLL operation is a pipeline of `Validate → Locate → Rewire → Finalize`. KTU examiners reward answers that follow this exact pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a Singly Linked List. Write the C structure for a node of a singly linked list storing integer data.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Singly Linked List (SLL)** is a dynamic, linear data structure in which each element (called a *node*) contains a `data` field and a `next` pointer that points to the subsequent node in the sequence. The list is accessed via a `HEAD` pointer, and the last node's `next` points to `NULL`.

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};
```

**Mark Split:** Definition — 1 mark | Structure declaration — 2 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State the time complexity of the following operations on a Singly Linked List of size $n$ and justify in one line each: (i) Insertion at the beginning, (ii) Searching for an element, (iii) Deletion at the end.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Insertion at beginning** → $O(1)$: only the HEAD pointer needs to be re-routed; no traversal.
2. **Searching for an element** → $O(n)$: in the worst case, every node must be visited as SLL has no random access.
3. **Deletion at end** → $O(n)$: traversal to the second-last node is required since no backward link exists.

**Mark Split:** 1 mark per correct complexity + justification.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Write a C program (using `struct Node`) to create a singly linked list of $n$ integers entered by the user and **display** all elements using the traversal operation. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Write a C function `void insertAtEnd(struct Node **head, int value)` to insert a new node at the **end** of an existing singly linked list. Show the working with a sample list. **[7 marks]**

---

**Solution (a) — [7 marks]**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};

// Function to insert at end (used during creation)
void append(struct Node **head, int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = NULL;
    if (*head == NULL) { *head = newNode; return; }
    struct Node *temp = *head;
    while (temp->next != NULL) temp = temp->next;
    temp->next = newNode;
}

// Traversal function
void display(struct Node *head) {
    if (head == NULL) { printf("List is empty\n"); return; }
    struct Node *temp = head;
    printf("Singly Linked List: ");
    while (temp != NULL) {
        printf("%d -> ", temp->data);
        temp = temp->next;
    }
    printf("NULL\n");
}

int main() {
    struct Node *head = NULL;
    int n, val;
    printf("Enter number of nodes: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        printf("Enter value %d: ", i + 1);
        scanf("%d", &val);
        append(&head, val);
    }
    display(head);
    return 0;
}
```

**Mark Split:**
- Struct definition — 1 mark
- Insertion logic — 2 marks
- Traversal logic with NULL check — 3 marks
- `main` and I/O handling — 1 mark

---

**Solution (b) — [7 marks]**

```c
void insertAtEnd(struct Node **head, int value) {
    // Step 1: Allocate
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = NULL;

    // Step 2: Handle empty list
    if (*head == NULL) {
        *head = newNode;
        return;
    }

    // Step 3: Traverse to the last node
    struct Node *temp = *head;
    while (temp->next != NULL) {
        temp = temp->next;
    }

    // Step 4: Rewire
    temp->next = newNode;
}
```

**Working with sample list:**

Initial: `HEAD → 10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`
Call: `insertAtEnd(&head, 40);`

| Step | `temp` | `temp->next` |
|:----:|:------:|:------------:|
| 1 | `&node10` | `&node20` (continue) |
| 2 | `&node20` | `&node30` (continue) |
| 3 | `&node30` | `NULL` (stop) |
| 4 | — | `temp->next = &newNode` |

Final: `HEAD → 10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL` ✓

**Time complexity:** $O(n)$, **Space complexity:** $O(1)$.

**Mark Split:**
- Allocation + initial check — 2 marks
- Traversal loop — 2 marks
- Rewiring + correct return — 2 marks
- Time complexity + dry run — 1 mark

---

#### **QUESTION B — Alternative Choice (Internal Choice) [14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **deletion** operation on a singly linked list with algorithms for: (i) deletion at the beginning, (ii) deletion at a given position $k$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Write a C function to perform **linear search** in a singly linked list. The function should return the position of the first occurrence of a key, or $-1$ if not found. Demonstrate with a sample trace. **[7 marks]**

---

**Solution (a) — [7 marks]**

**Algorithm (i) — Deletion at Beginning:**

```
1. IF HEAD == NULL → UNDERFLOW
2. temp = HEAD
3. HEAD = HEAD->next       // Anchor moves forward
4. FREE temp
```

**Algorithm (ii) — Deletion at Position $k$:**

```
1. IF HEAD == NULL OR k < 1 → INVALID
2. IF k == 1 → Call deletion-at-beginning
3. prev = NULL, temp = HEAD
4. FOR i = 1 TO k-1:
       prev = temp
       temp = temp->next
       IF temp == NULL → OUT OF RANGE
5. prev->next = temp->next  // Bypass target
6. FREE temp
```

**Diagram (Before & After deletion at $k=3$):**

```
BEFORE: HEAD → 10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL
                prev   temp
STEP 5:  prev->next = temp->next
         20 -> 30  becomes  20 -> 40
AFTER:  HEAD → 10 → 20 → 40 → NULL
```

**Mark Split:**
- Empty list handling — 1 mark
- Deletion at head logic — 2 marks
- Position-based deletion logic — 3 marks
- Diagram/trace — 1 mark

---

**Solution (b) — [7 marks]**

```c
int searchSLL(struct Node *head, int key) {
    if (head == NULL) return -1;     // Empty list
    struct Node *temp = head;
    int position = 1;
    while (temp != NULL) {
        if (temp->data == key) {
            return position;         // FOUND
        }
        temp = temp->next;
        position++;
    }
    return -1;                       // NOT FOUND
}
```

**Sample Trace:** List `HEAD → 15 → 25 → 35 → 45 → NULL`, search for `key = 35`.

| Iteration | `temp->data` | `position` | Match? | Return |
|:---------:|:------------:|:----------:|:------:|:------:|
| 1 | 15 | 1 | No | — |
| 2 | 25 | 2 | No | — |
| 3 | 35 | 3 | **Yes** | **3** |

**Time complexity:** $O(n)$ worst case, $O(1)$ best case (element at head).

**Mark Split:**
- Empty list check — 1 mark
- Loop with position counter — 3 marks
- Match logic and return values — 2 marks
- Sample trace — 1 mark

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Forgetting `free(temp)` in deletion:** Costs 1 mark — *always deallocate*.
> 2. **Wrong rewiring order in insertion at position:** If you break `prev->next` *before* saving it in `newNode->next`, the rest of the list is lost permanently.
> 3. **Not handling the empty list:** KTU examiners deliberately add `HEAD == NULL` edge case to test. 1 mark is reserved for this.
> 4. **Confusing singly linked with doubly linked:** Students often write *two pointers* (`prev` and `next`) in a single node definition. SLL has only **one** `next` pointer.
> 5. **Returning the wrong position index in search:** Position is **1-based**, not 0-based, in KTU standard answer keys.
> 6. **Missing `struct Node *` type in self-reference:** Forward declaration or proper `struct` keyword is mandatory — `Node *next` without `struct` is a compile error in C.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Singly Linked List** is a chain of `data + next` nodes, terminated by `NULL`, accessed via `HEAD`.
- ✅ The **node structure** is *self-referential*: `struct Node *next`.
- ✅ **Head operations** (insert/delete at start) are $O(1)$ — *the fastest*.
- ✅ **Tail operations** require full traversal: $O(n)$ — *the slowest*.
- ✅ **Search** is always $O(n)$ in the worst case — *no random access exists*.
- ✅ **Rewire order** is critical: in insertion, save the old link *before* breaking it.
- ✅ **Two-pointer pattern** (`prev` + `temp`) is the universal tool for SLL traversal.
- ✅ Always check for **empty list (`HEAD == NULL`)** before any operation.
- ✅ **Deallocate** with `free()` after deletion to prevent memory leaks.
- ✅ Position indexing is **1-based** in KTU answer keys.
- ✅ SLL cannot move **backward** — this is its *fundamental limitation* (solved by Doubly Linked List).
- ✅ Use **dry runs / traces** in exam answers — they earn **easy 1–2 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Linked List: Polynomial representation and manipulation (Addition only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Applications of Linked List: Polynomial representation and manipulation (Addition only) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Polynomial Representation using a Linked List** is a dynamic, memory-efficient technique in which each term (monomial) of a polynomial is stored as a separate node, and the nodes are linked together via pointers to form a complete mathematical expression. A general single-variable polynomial of degree $n$ is mathematically defined as: $$P(x) = a_n x^n + a_{n-1} x^{n-1} + \dots + a_1 x^1 + a_0 x^0$$ In a **singly linked list** representation, every node holds exactly one non-zero term and has the following structure: $$\text{Node} = \big\{ \text{Coefficient} \; (a_i), \; \text{Exponent} \; (e_i), \; \text{Next Pointer} \big\}$$ The list is conventionally maintained in **descending order of exponents**, so that the head node always points to the term with the highest power of $x$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCITT303 - Module 3):** > The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically requires students to master **Polynomial Addition** as a direct application of linked lists. Subtraction, Multiplication, and Evaluation are out of scope for this module. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **train** where every coach is a mathematical term. - Each **coach** has three compartments: 1. **Coefficient** (e.g., the number `5`) 2. **Exponent** (e.g., the number `3`, meaning $x^3$) 3. **A coupling hook** that points to the next coach (the `next` pointer) - The **engine** of the train is the **head pointer** — it tells you where the train starts. - Coaches are arranged from the **biggest exponent at the front** to the **smallest at the rear** (just like engines pull the heaviest load first). If we want to **merge two such trains** (representing $P_1(x)$ and $P_2(x)$), we line up the coaches side-by-side and combine them following strict rules: - Same power coaches → **add the coefficients**. - Different power coaches → **attach the larger one first** to the new train. This "train-merging" is exactly what **Polynomial Addition** does using a linked list. --- ## 1.3 Why Linked List over Array? (KTU Favourite Question) > [!NOTE] > **Advantages of Linked List Representation for Polynomials:** > - **Dynamic Memory**: We allocate only as many nodes as there are non-zero terms. No memory is wasted. > - **Sparse Polynomial Friendly**: For $10x^{1000} + 5x^5$, an array would need 1001 slots, but a linked list uses only **2 nodes**. > - **Easy Insertion / Deletion**: Adding or removing a term costs $O(1)$ once the position is found. > - **No Pre-defined Size**: Unlike arrays, the degree of the polynomial is not bounded in advance. **Disadvantages** (for board exam completeness): - Extra memory for `next` pointers. - No random access — traversal must be sequential ($O(n)$ to reach the $k^{th}$ term). - Slightly more complex implementation than an array. --- ## 1.4 The Node Data Structure (C-style Definition) A polynomial node in C is declared as: ```c struct Node { int coeff; // Coefficient of the term int expo; // Exponent (power of x) struct Node *next; // Pointer to the next term }; ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Convention:** Coefficients can be **positive, negative, or zero**. Exponents are **non-negative integers**. Exponents are stored in **strictly decreasing order** within the list. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Rules of a Polynomial Linked List The following invariants **must** be maintained for a valid polynomial linked list (these are commonly tested in KTU viva and short-answer questions): 1. **Sorted Order:** Nodes are arranged in strictly **decreasing order of exponents** (i.e., $\text{expo}(p) > \text{expo}(p \to next)$). 2. **No Duplicate Exponents:** No two nodes in the same polynomial share the same exponent — they are always combined. 3. **No Zero Coefficients:** A term with coefficient = 0 should not exist as a node; it must be removed to keep the list compact. 4. **Head Pointer:** A pointer `head` (or `poly`) references the first node. An empty polynomial is represented by `head = NULL`. 5. **Tail Termination:** The `next` field of the last node is always `NULL`, marking the end of the polynomial. --- ## 2.2 Memory Layout Comparison | Feature | Array Representation | Linked List Representation | |---|---|---| | **Memory Allocation** | Static / Contiguous | Dynamic / Scattered (Heap) | | **Space Used** | Degree + 1 cells (wasted for zeros) | Exactly the number of non-zero terms | | **Best For** | Dense polynomials | **Sparse polynomials (KTU preferred)** | | **Insertion of New Term** | Costly: shift all elements | Easy once position is known | | **Random Access by Index** | $O(1)$ | $O(k)$ (must traverse) | | **Extra Memory per Term** | 0 | 1 pointer field (typically **4 or 8 bytes**) | | **Total Memory for $n$ terms** | $n \times (\text{sizeof coeff} + \text{sizeof expo})$ | $n \times (\text{sizeof coeff} + \text{sizeof expo} + \text{sizeof pointer})$ | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | |---|---|---| | **General Polynomial** | $P(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i x^i$ | $a_i$ = coefficient, $i$ = exponent | | **Degree of Polynomial** | $\deg(P) = \max \{ e_i \;\vert\; \text{node}_i \in P \}$ | $e_i$ = exponent of $i^{th}$ node | | **Number of Non-Zero Terms** | $N(P) = \vert \{ \text{node}_i \in P \mid a_i \neq 0 \} \vert$ | Cardinality of non-zero terms | | **Addition Rule — Same Exponent** | $c_3 = c_1 + c_2, \quad e_3 = e_1 = e_2$ | $P_3(x) \mathrel{+}= P_1(x) + P_2(x)$ | | **Addition Rule — Different Exponent** | Insert term with $\max(e_1, e_2)$ | Larger exponent wins | | **Sum of Two Polynomials** | $P_3(x) = P_1(x) + P_2(x)$ | Component-wise on terms | | **Time Complexity of Addition** | $T(n) = O(m + n)$ | $m, n$ = number of terms in $P_1, P_2$ | | **Space Complexity of Addition** | $S(n) = O(m + n)$ | Result polynomial size | | **Memory per Node** | $\text{sizeof}(\text{int}) + \text{sizeof}(\text{int}) + \text{sizeof}(\text{ptr})$ | Typically **12 bytes** (32-bit) or **16 bytes** (64-bit) | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Polynomial linked lists are used in: > - **Symbolic Computation Engines** (e.g., MATLAB, Mathematica internals for sparse polynomials). > - **Computer Graphics** — Bézier curves and B-splines are polynomial-based. > - **Cryptography** — Polynomial arithmetic over finite fields (used in NTRU, lattice-based crypto). > - **Signal Processing** — FIR filter design where the impulse response is a polynomial. > - **Compiler Design** — Symbolic differentiation and expression trees. --- ## 2.4 Edge Cases (Frequently Asked in KTU Viva) | Case | Description | Handling | |---|---|---| | **Empty Polynomial** | `head = NULL` | Result is just the other polynomial | | **Both Empty** | Both `head1 = head2 = NULL` | Result is `NULL` | | **All Exponents Mismatched** | No common powers | Result is concatenation of both | | **Result Term has coeff = 0** | e.g., $(5x^2) + (-5x^2)$ | Term is **not inserted** in result | | **Same Polynomial** | $P_1(x) = P_2(x)$ | Every coefficient is doubled | # 3. Step-by-Step Algorithm & Analytical Walkthrough ## 3.1 The Polynomial Addition Algorithm We are given two polynomials $P_1$ and $P_2$, each represented by a sorted linked list, and we must construct a third polynomial $P_3 = P_1 + P_2$ also as a sorted linked list. The algorithm uses **three travelling pointers**: `p1` (on $P_1$), `p2` (on $P_2$), and `p3` (tail of $P_3$). ### 3.1.1 Algorithmic Steps (Logic Flow) **Step 1 — Initialization** - Create a dummy head node for the result list $P_3$. Let `p3` point to it. - Set `p1 = head1`, `p2 = head2`. **Step 2 — Main Comparison Loop** While **both** `p1 ≠ NULL` **and** `p2 ≠ NULL`, compare the exponents of the current nodes: - **Case A:** $\text{p1} \to \text{expo} \;=\; \text{p2} \to \text{expo}$ → Add coefficients: $c = \text{p1} \to \text{coeff} + \text{p2} \to \text{coeff}$ → If $c \neq 0$: create a new node with $(c, \text{p1} \to \text{expo})$ and attach to $P_3$. → If $c = 0$: do **not** create a node (sparse list optimization). → Advance `p1` and `p2`. - **Case B:** $\text{p1} \to \text{expo} \;>\; \text{p2} \to \text{expo}$ → Copy term from $P_1$: new node $(\text{p1} \to \text{coeff}, \; \text{p1} \to \text{expo})$. → Advance only `p1`. - **Case C:** $\text{p1} \to \text{expo} \;<\; \text{p2} \to \text{expo}$ → Copy term from $P_2$: new node $(\text{p2} \to \text{coeff}, \; \text{p2} \to \text{expo})$. → Advance only `p2`. **Step 3 — Drain Remaining Nodes** - If `p1 ≠ NULL`: copy all remaining nodes of $P_1$ to $P_3$. - If `p2 ≠ NULL`: copy all remaining nodes of $P_2$ to $P_3$. **Step 4 — Finalize** - Set `p3 → next = NULL` to terminate the result list. - Discard the dummy head; return `head3 = dummy → next`. --- ### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Numerical Walkthrough Let us work out a fully solved example — a guaranteed KTU board problem. > **Problem:** Add the following two polynomials using linked list representation. > $$P_1(x) = 5x^4 + 3x^2 + 7$$ > $$P_2(x) = 6x^4 + x^3 + 9x^2 + 2x + 4$$ **Initial Linked Lists:** ``` P1: [5, 4] → [3, 2] → [7, 0] → NULL P2: [6, 4] → [1, 3] → [9, 2] → [2, 1] → [4, 0] → NULL ``` (Format: [coefficient, exponent]) **Iteration Trace Table:** | Step | p1 → (c, e) | p2 → (c, e) | Comparison | Action on P3 | New P3 Node | p1 moves? | p2 moves? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | (5, 4) | (6, 4) | e1 = e2 = 4 | c = 5+6 = **11** ≠ 0 | [11, 4] | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | 2 | (3, 2) | (1, 3) | e1=2 < e2=3 | Copy p2 | [1, 3] | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | 3 | (3, 2) | (9, 2) | e1 = e2 = 2 | c = 3+9 = **12** ≠ 0 | [12, 2] | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | 4 | (7, 0) | (2, 1) | e1=0 < e2=1 | Copy p2 | [2, 1] | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | 5 | (7, 0) | (4, 0) | e1 = e2 = 0 | c = 7+4 = **11** ≠ 0 | [11, 0] | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | 6 | NULL | NULL | Loop ends | Drain remaining | (none) | — | — | **Final Result Polynomial:** $$P_3(x) = 11x^4 + x^3 + 12x^2 + 2x + 11$$ **Final Linked List:** ``` P3: [11, 4] → [1, 3] → [12, 2] → [2, 1] → [11, 0] → NULL ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification by direct algebra:** > $$P_1 + P_2 = (5x^4 + 3x^2 + 7) + (6x^4 + x^3 + 9x^2 + 2x + 4)$$ > $$= (5+6)x^4 + (1)x^3 + (3+9)x^2 + (2)x + (7+4)$$ > $$= 11x^4 + x^3 + 12x^2 + 2x + 11 \quad \checkmark$$ --- ### 3.1.3 Worked Example with a Zero-Coefficient Cancellation Let us add: $P_1(x) = 8x^3 + 5x$ and $P_2(x) = -8x^3 + 4x^2 + 2$. | Step | p1 → (c, e) | p2 → (c, e) | Comparison | c (sum) | Action | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | (8, 3) | (-8, 3) | Equal exponents (3) | $8 + (-8) = 0$ | **Skip — no node added** | | 2 | (5, 1) | (4, 2) | e1=1 < e2=2 | — | Copy p2 → [4, 2] | | 3 | (5, 1) | (2, 0) | e1=1 > e2=0 | — | Copy p1 → [5, 1] | | 4 | NULL | (2, 0) | Drain p2 | — | Copy p2 → [2, 0] | **Final Result:** $P_3(x) = 4x^2 + 5x + 2$ **Linked List:** ``` P3: [4, 2] → [5, 1] → [2, 0] → NULL ``` > [!NOTE] > Notice that the $x^3$ term was **completely cancelled** because its combined coefficient was 0. This is why we always check $c \neq 0$ before creating a node — it preserves the "no zero coefficient" invariant. --- ### 3.1.4 Complexity Analysis (Must Appear in KTU Answers) | Metric | Value | Justification | |---|---|---| | **Time Complexity** | $O(m + n)$ | Each node of $P_1$ and $P_2$ is visited at most once. | | **Space Complexity** | $O(m + n)$ | At most $m + n$ new nodes are created for $P_3$. | | **Auxiliary Space** | $O(1)$ extra (excluding result) | Only 3 pointers are maintained during traversal. | where $m$ = number of terms in $P_1$ and $n$ = number of terms in $P_2$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Node Structure (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["COEFF
coefficient
data field"] nodeB["EXPO
exponent
data field"] nodeC["NEXT
pointer
address field"] nodeA --- nodeB --- nodeC nodeC -->|"stores address of next term"| nodeNext["Next Polynomial Term"] ``` > [!NOTE] > Each polynomial node carries **two integers** (coeff, expo) and **one pointer** (next). The `next` pointer of the **last node** stores `NULL`, marking the end of the list. --- ## 4.2 Linked List Representation of a Sample Polynomial Consider the polynomial $P(x) = 7x^4 + 4x^2 + 9x + 1$. ```mermaid flowchart LR HEAD(["HEAD pointer"]) --> N1["Node 1
coeff = 7
expo = 4"] N1 --> N2["Node 2
coeff = 4
expo = 2"] N2 --> N3["Node 3
coeff = 9
expo = 1"] N3 --> N4["Node 4
coeff = 1
expo = 0"] N4 --> NULLNODE["NULL
End of list"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Note the **strictly decreasing exponent order**: $4 \to 2 \to 1 \to 0$. This ordering is mandatory for the addition algorithm to work correctly. --- ## 4.3 Functional Flowchart: Polynomial Addition Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD START(["START"]) --> INIT["Initialize p1 = head1
p2 = head2
p3 = dummy node"] INIT --> LOOPCHK{"p1 != NULL
AND
p2 != NULL ?"} LOOPCHK -->|"No"| DRAIN LOOPCHK -->|"Yes"| CMP{"Compare
p1->expo
vs
p2->expo"} CMP -->|"Equal"| ADDC["c = p1->coeff + p2->coeff
If c != 0: add node (c, expo) to P3
Advance p1, p2"] CMP -->|"p1 > p2"| CPYP1["Copy p1 term to P3
Advance p1"] CMP -->|"p1 < p2"| CPYP2["Copy p2 term to P3
Advance p2"] ADDC --> LOOPCHK CPYP1 --> LOOPCHK CPYP2 --> LOOPCHK DRAIN{"p1 == NULL ?"} -->|"No"| DRAINP1["Copy remaining of P1 to P3"] DRAIN -->|"Yes"| DRAINP2["Copy remaining of P2 to P3"] DRAINP1 --> DRAINP2 DRAINP2 --> FINAL["Set p3->next = NULL
head3 = dummy->next"] FINAL --> STOP(["END"]) ``` --- ## 4.4 Step-by-Step Visual Trace (Adding Two Polynomials) Let $P_1 = 5x^3 + 2x$ and $P_2 = 4x^3 + 6x^2 + 3$. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph P1["Polynomial P1"] A1["5, 3"] --> A2["2, 1"] --> A3["NULL"] end subgraph P2["Polynomial P2"] B1["4, 3"] --> B2["6, 2"] --> B3["3, 0"] --> B4["NULL"] end subgraph P3["Result P3 = P1 + P2"] C1["9, 3"] --> C2["6, 2"] --> C3["2, 1"] --> C4["3, 0"] --> C5["NULL"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > Observe how the **exponent 3 terms merged** ($5 + 4 = 9$), the **exponent 2 term was copied** from $P_2$ (no match in $P_1$), the **exponent 1 term was copied** from $P_1$ (no match in $P_2$), and finally the **constant term was copied** from $P_2$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **Q: Represent the polynomial $P(x) = 6x^4 + 4x^3 - 9x^2 + 5$ using a singly linked list. Draw the node structure and write the linked list.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The polynomial has 4 non-zero terms with exponents in **decreasing order**: $4, 3, 2, 0$. **Node Structure (C):** ```c struct Node { int coeff; int expo; struct Node *next; }; ``` **Linked List Diagram:** ``` HEAD → [6, 4] → [4, 3] → [-9, 2] → [5, 0] → NULL ``` Each node stores one term. The `next` pointer of the last node is `NULL`. The `HEAD` pointer stores the address of the first node. This dynamic representation uses only **4 nodes** instead of an array of 5 cells. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **Q: List any three advantages of representing a polynomial using a linked list over an array.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Memory Efficiency for Sparse Polynomials:** A polynomial like $7x^{1000} + 3x^5$ uses only 2 nodes in a linked list, but needs 1001 cells in an array. **(1 mark)** 2. **No Wasted Space:** Only the non-zero terms are stored; zero coefficients need not occupy memory. **(1 mark)** 3. **Dynamic Size:** The degree of the polynomial is not bounded in advance; new terms can be inserted easily without resizing. **(1 mark)** *(Bonus point may be awarded for mentioning easy insertion/deletion.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students commonly **forget to check whether the resulting coefficient is zero** (e.g., when adding $5x^2$ and $-5x^2$, the term should NOT appear in the result). **[-2 marks]** > - Students often **break the descending order of exponents** in the result list. The output must be sorted in decreasing order of exponents. **[-1 mark]** > - Students forget to **attach remaining nodes** of one list when the other becomes empty. Always perform the "drain" step. **[-2 marks]** > - Writing code with `head` moving forward loses the reference to the list start — use a **tail pointer `p3`** for the result list. **[-1 mark]** > - Confusing `coeff` with `expo` during comparison will yield a wrong output. **[-2 marks]** --- ### **Question B-A** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified] **Q (a)** [7 Marks]: Add the polynomials $P_1(x) = 8x^3 + 4x^2 + 5$ and $P_2(x) = 3x^4 + 6x^3 + 2x + 7$ using a linked list. Show the linked list representation of both polynomials and the final result. **Q (b)** [7 Marks]: Write the C algorithm to add two polynomials represented using linked lists and state its time complexity. --- #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Given:** $$P_1(x) = 8x^3 + 4x^2 + 5$$ $$P_2(x) = 3x^4 + 6x^3 + 2x + 7$$ **Step 1 — Sort in decreasing order of exponents** (already sorted in both cases). **Step 2 — Linked list representation:** ``` P1: HEAD1 → [8, 3] → [4, 2] → [5, 0] → NULL P2: HEAD2 → [3, 4] → [6, 3] → [2, 1] → [7, 0] → NULL ``` **Step 3 — Addition using comparison:** | Step | p1 (c, e) | p2 (c, e) | Compare | Action | P3 new node | p1 | p2 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | (8, 3) | (3, 4) | 3 < 4 | Copy p2 | [3, 4] | ❌ | ✅ | | 2 | (8, 3) | (6, 3) | 3 = 3 | c = 8+6 = 14 | [14, 3] | ✅ | ✅ | | 3 | (4, 2) | (2, 1) | 2 > 1 | Copy p1 | [4, 2] | ✅ | ❌ | | 4 | (5, 0) | (2, 1) | 0 < 1 | Copy p2 | [2, 1] | ❌ | ✅ | | 5 | (5, 0) | (7, 0) | 0 = 0 | c = 5+7 = 12 | [12, 0] | ✅ | ✅ | | 6 | NULL | NULL | — | Drain | (none) | — | — | **Step 4 — Final Result:** $$P_3(x) = 3x^4 + 14x^3 + 4x^2 + 2x + 12$$ **Result Linked List:** ``` P3: HEAD3 → [3, 4] → [14, 3] → [4, 2] → [2, 1] → [12, 0] → NULL ``` **Verification (algebraic check):** $$P_1 + P_2 = (8x^3 + 4x^2 + 5) + (3x^4 + 6x^3 + 2x + 7)$$ $$= 3x^4 + (8+6)x^3 + 4x^2 + 2x + (5+7)$$ $$= 3x^4 + 14x^3 + 4x^2 + 2x + 12 \quad \checkmark$$ **(7 marks)** — Full marks for correct linked list diagrams, the trace table, the final polynomial, and the algebraic verification. --- #### **Model Solution for (b):** **C Algorithm:** ```c #include #include struct Node { int coeff; int expo; struct Node *next; }; struct Node* createNode(int c, int e) { struct Node* newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); newNode->coeff = c; newNode->expo = e; newNode->next = NULL; return newNode; } void insertTerm(struct Node** tail, int c, int e) { struct Node* newNode = createNode(c, e); (*tail)->next = newNode; *tail = newNode; } struct Node* addPolynomials(struct Node* p1, struct Node* p2) { struct Node* dummy = createNode(0, 0); // Dummy head struct Node* p3 = dummy; while (p1 != NULL && p2 != NULL) { if (p1->expo == p2->expo) { int sum = p1->coeff + p2->coeff; if (sum != 0) insertTerm(&p3, sum, p1->expo); p1 = p1->next; p2 = p2->next; } else if (p1->expo > p2->expo) { insertTerm(&p3, p1->coeff, p1->expo); p1 = p1->next; } else { insertTerm(&p3, p2->coeff, p2->expo); p2 = p2->next; } } // Drain remaining nodes while (p1 != NULL) { insertTerm(&p3, p1->coeff, p1->expo); p1 = p1->next; } while (p2 != NULL) { insertTerm(&p3, p2->coeff, p2->expo); p2 = p2->next; } struct Node* result = dummy->next; free(dummy); return result; } ``` **Time Complexity Analysis:** Let $m$ = number of terms in $P_1$ and $n$ = number of terms in $P_2$. - The main loop runs at most $\min(m, n)$ times. Each iteration performs $O(1)$ work. - The two drain loops together process at most $m + n$ nodes in total. - Therefore, total time = $O(m) + O(n) = \mathbf{O(m + n)}$. **Space Complexity:** $O(m + n)$ for the result polynomial (excluding the $O(1)$ auxiliary pointers). **(7 marks)** — Algorithm, correct use of pointers, and complexity analysis with proper justification. --- ### **Question B-B** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified] — *Internal Choice Alternative* **Q (a)** [7 Marks]: Represent the polynomial $P(x) = 12x^5 - 4x^4 + 7x^2 - 3$ using a linked list. How many nodes are required and why is this better than an array of size 6? **Q (b)** [7 Marks]: Add $P_1(x) = 4x^5 + 2x^3 - x$ and $P_2(x) = -4x^5 + 6x^4 + 3x^3 + x + 5$ using linked list representation. Show the step-by-step trace. --- #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Node Structure:** Same as defined earlier (coeff, expo, next). **Linked List Representation:** The polynomial has 4 non-zero terms: $12x^5, -4x^4, 7x^2, -3x^0$. ``` HEAD → [12, 5] → [-4, 4] → [7, 2] → [-3, 0] → NULL ``` **Number of nodes required = 4** (only non-zero terms). **Why linked list is better than an array of size 6:** - An array of size 6 would need slots for $x^5, x^4, x^3, x^2, x^1, x^0$. Since the terms $x^3$ and $x^1$ are absent, two array cells will be **wasted (store 0)**. **(2 marks)** - A linked list allocates only **4 nodes**, exactly one per non-zero term, saving memory. **(2 marks)** - The array size is **fixed at compile time** and cannot grow; the linked list can grow dynamically if a new term (e.g., $8x^6$) is added later. **(2 marks)** - For very large gaps in exponents (e.g., $x^{1000}$), arrays become highly inefficient while linked lists remain unaffected. **(1 mark)** **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** $$P_1(x) = 4x^5 + 2x^3 - x$$ $$P_2(x) = -4x^5 + 6x^4 + 3x^3 + x + 5$$ **Linked List Diagrams:** ``` P1: [4, 5] → [2, 3] → [-1, 1] → NULL P2: [-4, 5] → [6, 4] → [3, 3] → [1, 1] → [5, 0] → NULL ``` **Step-by-Step Trace:** | Step | p1 (c, e) | p2 (c, e) | Compare | c = sum | Action | P3 Node | p1 | p2 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | (4, 5) | (-4, 5) | 5 = 5 | 4 + (-4) = **0** | **Skip (no node)** | — | ✅ | ✅ | | 2 | (2, 3) | (6, 4) | 3 < 4 | — | Copy p2 | [6, 4] | ❌ | ✅ | | 3 | (2, 3) | (3, 3) | 3 = 3 | 2 + 3 = **5** | Add node | [5, 3] | ✅ | ✅ | | 4 | (-1, 1) | (1, 1) | 1 = 1 | -1 + 1 = **0** | **Skip (no node)** | — | ✅ | ✅ | | 5 | NULL | (5, 0) | Drain | — | Copy p2 | [5, 0] | — | ✅ | | 6 | NULL | NULL | End | — | Done | — | — | — | **Final Result:** $$P_3(x) = 6x^4 + 5x^3 + 5$$ **Result Linked List:** ``` P3: [6, 4] → [5, 3] → [5, 0] → NULL ``` **Verification:** $$P_1 + P_2 = (4x^5 + 2x^3 - x) + (-4x^5 + 6x^4 + 3x^3 + x + 5)$$ $$= (4 - 4)x^5 + 6x^4 + (2 + 3)x^3 + (-1 + 1)x + 5$$ $$= 6x^4 + 5x^3 + 5 \quad \checkmark$$ Note how the $x^5$ and $x$ terms both **cancelled out** (sum of coefficients became zero) and were correctly omitted from the result. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Polynomial Addition Using Linked Lists** > > - [x] A polynomial is stored as a **linked list of terms**, where each term is a node with three fields: `coeff`, `expo`, `next`. > - [x] The list must always be in **strictly decreasing order of exponents** — this is the most important invariant. > - [x] The **head pointer** points to the term with the highest degree; the last node's `next` is `NULL`. > - [x] Polynomial addition uses **two input pointers (`p1`, `p2`)** and **one tail pointer (`p3`)** for the result. > - [x] Three cases: **equal exponents** (add coefficients), **p1 > p2** (copy from $P_1$), **p1 < p2** (copy from $P_2$). > - [x] **Always check** if the summed coefficient is **zero** — if so, do not insert that term into the result. > - [x] After the main loop, **drain** the remaining nodes of whichever list is not yet empty. > - [x] The algorithm runs in $O(m + n)$ **time** and uses $O(m + n)$ **space** for the result. > - [x] Linked list representation is **superior to arrays for sparse polynomials** (large gaps in exponents). > - [x] Polynomial addition is a **classic KTU 14-mark question** — practice trace tables thoroughly. > - [x] Common pitfalls: forgetting the drain step, breaking the descending order, missing zero-coefficient handling, and losing the list head by moving it instead of using a separate pointer. --- > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 3 Notes — Polynomial Representation and Addition** > *Prepared strictly as per KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCITT303). All algorithms, examples, and complexity analyses are aligned with KTU Board Examiner expectations and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L3 — Apply, L4 — Analyze, L5 — Evaluate).*

topics.title: Operations on Doubly Linked List - Traversal, Insertion, Deletion and Searching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Operations on Doubly Linked List - Traversal, Insertion, Deletion and Searching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Doubly Linked List (DLL)** is a dynamic linear data structure composed of a sequence of nodes, where each node contains three fields:

1. **Data field** — stores the actual element/value.
2. **Next pointer (forward link)** — stores the address of the succeeding node.
3. **Prev pointer (backward link)** — stores the address of the preceding node.

The list is anchored by a `head` pointer (first node) and the last node's `next` field points to `NULL`. Symmetrically, the first node's `prev` field points to `NULL`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Strict Recall):** A Doubly Linked List is a linked list in which traversal is possible in **both forward and backward directions**, because every node maintains **two pointers** — one to its successor and one to its predecessor.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Train with Two Couplers**
> Imagine a train where every coach is connected to the coach *in front of it* AND the coach *behind it*. This is exactly how a Doubly Linked List works:
> - Each node (coach) knows who is **before** it (`prev` link) and who is **after** it (`next` link).
> - A **Singly Linked List** is like a train where only the front coupler exists — you can move forward but to go backward, you must restart from the engine.
> - The **Head** of the list is the engine of the train. There is no coach before the engine (`prev = NULL`), just like the first node has no predecessor.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Node size (memory per node):** `sizeof(data) + 2 × sizeof(pointer)`.
- **Time to access nth element:** **O(n)** — no random access.
- **Time to insert/delete at known position:** **O(1)** — once the position is found.
- **Time to traverse the entire list:** **O(n)**.

### 1.4 Node Structure (C Declaration)

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *prev;   // Pointer to previous node
    struct Node *next;   // Pointer to next node
};
```

### 1.5 Generic Diagram of a Doubly Linked List

```
NULL <-> [prev | 10 | next] <-> [prev | 20 | next] <-> [prev | 30 | next] <-> NULL
   ^            HEAD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Doubly Linked List over Singly Linked List?**
> - **Backward traversal** is supported natively.
> - **Deletion of a given node (pointer known)** becomes **O(1)** — no need to traverse to find the predecessor.
> - The trade-off is **extra memory** for the `prev` pointer and **extra pointer updates** during insertion/deletion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Core Operations — Concept Breakdown

#### **A. Traversal**
- **Forward Traversal:** Start at `head`, follow `next` pointers until `NULL` is reached.
- **Backward Traversal:** Start at `tail` (or traverse to the end once, then move backward), follow `prev` pointers until `NULL` is reached.
- **Why:** To access, print, or process every element exactly once.

#### **B. Insertion**
A new node can be inserted in **three positions**:
1. **At the beginning (before head):** Update `head`'s `prev` and the new node's `next`.
2. **At the end (after tail):** Update `tail`'s `next` and the new node's `prev`.
3. **At a given position k:** Insert between node `(k-1)` and node `k`. **Four pointer updates** required.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of DLL Insertion:** Whenever you insert a node between `A` and `B`, the four pointer links — `A->next`, `newNode->prev`, `newNode->next`, `B->prev` — must be set in the correct order to avoid losing the reference to `B`.

#### **C. Deletion**
A node can be deleted from **three positions**:
1. **Delete head node:** Update `head = head->next` and `head->prev = NULL`.
2. **Delete tail node:** Update `tail->prev->next = NULL`.
3. **Delete a node at position k:** Bypass the node — `node->prev->next = node->next` and `node->next->prev = node->prev`, then free memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Deletion in a DLL (when node pointer is given) is **O(1)** because the predecessor is directly accessible via `node->prev`. In a Singly Linked List, this same operation is **O(n)** because you must traverse to find the predecessor.

#### **D. Searching**
- Linear scan from `head`, comparing `node->data` with the target `key`.
- Return the node pointer if found, else `NULL`.
- **Time Complexity: O(n)** in the worst and average case.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Time Complexity Table

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Auxiliary Space | Stable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Traversal | $\Omega(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes |
| Backward Traversal | $\Omega(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes |
| Insert at Beginning | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | — |
| Insert at End (with tail ptr) | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | — |
| Insert at End (without tail ptr) | $\Omega(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | — |
| Insert at Position k | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(k)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | — |
| Delete Head | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | — |
| Delete Tail (with tail ptr) | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | — |
| Delete Node (ptr known) | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | — |
| Delete at Position k | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(k)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | — |
| Search | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Footprint Formula:** For a list of $n$ nodes each storing one integer and two pointers on a 64-bit system,
>
> $$\text{Total Memory} = n \times \left( \text{sizeof(int)} + 2 \times \text{sizeof(pointer)} \right) = n \times (4 + 16) = 20n \text{ bytes}$$

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Browser History (Back/Forward navigation):** Each web page visited is a DLL node; the `prev` pointer takes you to the previous page, `next` to the next.
- **Undo/Redo functionality** in editors like MS Word, Photoshop.
- **LRU Cache implementation** in operating systems and databases.
- **Music Player playlists** (previous track / next track).
- **Polynomial arithmetic** and **deque (double-ended queue)** implementation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section exhaustively derives each operation using C-style pseudocode. Every algorithmic transition is written explicitly.

---

### 3.1 Operation 1 — Forward Traversal

**Goal:** Visit and print every node from `head` to the last node.

**Algorithm (Step-by-Step):**

```
ALGORITHM: forwardTraversal(head)
INPUT: head — pointer to the first node
OUTPUT: Prints all elements

Step 1: IF head == NULL THEN
            PRINT "List is empty"
            RETURN
        END IF

Step 2: curr ← head
        // Initialize a temporary pointer at the start.

Step 3: WHILE curr != NULL DO
            PRINT curr->data
            curr ← curr->next
            // Move one step forward using the next pointer.
        END WHILE

Step 4: RETURN
```

**Analytical Trace on list `[10] <-> [20] <-> [30] <-> NULL`:**

| Iteration | `curr` address | `curr->data` | `curr->next` | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Node@1000 | 10 | Node@2000 | `10` |
| 2 | Node@2000 | 20 | Node@3000 | `20` |
| 3 | Node@3000 | 30 | NULL | `30` |
| 4 | NULL | — | — | Loop exits |

**Time Complexity:** Number of loop iterations $= n$. Therefore $T(n) = O(n)$.

---

### 3.2 Operation 2 — Backward Traversal

**Goal:** Visit every node from the last node back to the first node.

**Algorithm:**

```
ALGORITHM: backwardTraversal(head)
INPUT: head — pointer to the first node
OUTPUT: Prints all elements in reverse order

Step 1: IF head == NULL THEN RETURN END IF

Step 2: curr ← head
Step 3: WHILE curr->next != NULL DO
            curr ← curr->next
            // Walk to the last node using next pointers.
        END WHILE

Step 4: WHILE curr != NULL DO
            PRINT curr->data
            curr ← curr->prev
            // Walk backward using prev pointers.
        END WHILE

Step 5: RETURN
```

---

### 3.3 Operation 3 — Insertion at the Beginning

**Goal:** Insert a new node as the new `head`.

```
ALGORITHM: insertAtBeginning(head, value)
INPUT: head, value — integer to insert
OUTPUT: Updated head pointer

Step 1: newNode ← CREATE_NODE
Step 2: newNode->data ← value
Step 3: newNode->prev ← NULL
        // The new head has no predecessor.

Step 4: IF head == NULL THEN
            newNode->next ← NULL
            head ← newNode
            RETURN head
        END IF

Step 5: newNode->next ← head
        // New node points forward to the old head.

Step 6: head->prev ← newNode
        // Old head now points backward to the new node.

Step 7: head ← newNode
        // Update head to the new node.

Step 8: RETURN head
```

**Before:** `NULL <- [HEAD|10|next] <-> [20] <-> [30] <-> NULL`
**After inserting 5:** `NULL <- [HEAD|5|next] <-> [10] <-> [20] <-> [30] <-> NULL`

**Time Complexity:** $T(n) = O(1)$ — pointer updates are constant.

---

### 3.4 Operation 4 — Insertion at the End

```
ALGORITHM: insertAtEnd(head, value)
INPUT: head, value
OUTPUT: Updated head pointer

Step 1: newNode ← CREATE_NODE
Step 2: newNode->data ← value
Step 3: newNode->next ← NULL
        // The new tail has no successor.

Step 4: IF head == NULL THEN
            newNode->prev ← NULL
            head ← newNode
            RETURN head
        END IF

Step 5: curr ← head
Step 6: WHILE curr->next != NULL DO
            curr ← curr->next
        END WHILE
        // Traverse to the last node.

Step 7: curr->next ← newNode
        // Old tail points forward to new node.

Step 8: newNode->prev ← curr
        // New node points backward to old tail.

Step 9: RETURN head
```

**Time Complexity:** Without a `tail` pointer, $T(n) = O(n)$ due to the traversal loop.

---

### 3.5 Operation 5 — Insertion at a Given Position k

**Goal:** Insert a new node such that it becomes the $k^{th}$ node (1-indexed).

```
ALGORITHM: insertAtPosition(head, k, value)
INPUT: head, k (1-indexed position), value
OUTPUT: Updated head pointer, status flag

Step 1: IF k == 1 THEN
            RETURN insertAtBeginning(head, value)
        END IF

Step 2: newNode ← CREATE_NODE
Step 3: newNode->data ← value

Step 4: curr ← head
Step 5: FOR i ← 1 TO (k - 2) DO
            IF curr == NULL THEN
                PRINT "Invalid position"
                RETURN head
            END IF
```
