```
            curr ← curr->next
        END FOR
        // After this loop, curr points to (k-1)th node.

Step 6: IF curr == NULL OR curr->next == NULL THEN
            // Insert at the end
            curr->next ← newNode
            newNode->prev ← curr
            newNode->next ← NULL
        ELSE
            // Insert in the middle — 4 pointer updates
            nextNode ← curr->next
            curr->next ← newNode
            newNode->prev ← curr
            newNode->next ← nextNode
            nextNode->prev ← newNode
        END IF

Step 7: RETURN head
```

**Analytical Trace — Insert 25 at position 3 in `[10] <-> [20] <-> [40]`:**

| Step | Action | State |
|---|---|---|
| Before | — | `HEAD(10) <-> (20) <-> (40) <-> NULL` |
| After Step 5 | `curr` at node 20 | `curr->next` = node 40 |
| Step 6 (middle branch) | `nextNode` = node 40 | `curr->next = newNode(25)` |
| | `newNode->prev = curr(20)` | `newNode->next = nextNode(40)` |
| | `nextNode->prev = newNode(25)` | All 4 links set |
| After | — | `(10) <-> (20) <-> (25) <-> (40) <-> NULL` |

**Time Complexity:** $T(n) = O(k)$ — to reach the $(k-1)^{th}$ node. Worst case $O(n)$.

---

### 3.6 Operation 6 — Deletion of a Given Node (pointer known)

**Goal:** Remove `delNode` from the list in **O(1)** time — a key advantage over SLL.

```
ALGORITHM: deleteNode(head, delNode)
INPUT: head, delNode — pointer to the node to delete
OUTPUT: Updated head pointer

Step 1: IF head == NULL OR delNode == NULL THEN RETURN head END IF

Step 2: IF delNode == head THEN
            head ← delNode->next
            IF head != NULL THEN head->prev ← NULL END IF
            FREE(delNode)
            RETURN head
        END IF

Step 3: IF delNode->next != NULL THEN
            delNode->next->prev ← delNode->prev
            // Successor's prev now skips delNode.
        END IF

Step 4: IF delNode->prev != NULL THEN
            delNode->prev->next ← delNode->next
            // Predecessor's next now skips delNode.
        END IF

Step 5: FREE(delNode)
Step 6: RETURN head
```

**Time Complexity:** $T(n) = O(1)$ — exactly 4 pointer updates (2 conditional checks + 2 assignments).

---

### 3.7 Operation 7 — Deletion at Position k

```
ALGORITHM: deleteAtPosition(head, k)
INPUT: head, k (1-indexed)
OUTPUT: Updated head pointer

Step 1: IF head == NULL THEN RETURN NULL END IF

Step 2: IF k == 1 THEN
            temp ← head
            head ← head->next
            IF head != NULL THEN head->prev ← NULL END IF
            FREE(temp)
            RETURN head
        END IF

Step 3: curr ← head
Step 4: FOR i ← 1 TO (k - 1) DO
            IF curr == NULL THEN RETURN head END IF
            curr ← curr->next
        END FOR
        // curr now points to the k-th node.

Step 5: IF curr == NULL THEN RETURN head END IF

Step 6: IF curr->next != NULL THEN
            curr->next->prev ← curr->prev
        END IF
Step 7: curr->prev->next ← curr->next
Step 8: FREE(curr)
Step 9: RETURN head
```

---

### 3.8 Operation 8 — Searching

**Goal:** Find the first node whose data equals `key`.

```
ALGORITHM: searchDLL(head, key)
INPUT: head, key
OUTPUT: Pointer to the found node, or NULL

Step 1: curr ← head
Step 2: WHILE curr != NULL DO
            IF curr->data == key THEN
                RETURN curr
            END IF
            curr ← curr->next
        END WHILE
Step 3: RETURN NULL
```

**Time Complexity:** $T(n) = O(n)$ — in the worst case, all $n$ nodes are inspected.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Doubly Linked List Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    HEAD[HEAD POINTER] --> N1[NODE 1: prev NULL, data 10, next ptr]
    N1 <--> N2[NODE 2: prev ptr, data 20, next ptr]
    N2 <--> N3[NODE 3: prev ptr, data 30, next ptr]
    N3 <--> N4[NODE 4: prev ptr, data 40, next NULL]
    N1 -.prev.-> PN1[NULL]
    N4 -.next.-> PN4[NULL]
```

### 4.2 Operational Flow — Insertion at the Beginning (Inserting 5)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BEFORE
        B1[HEAD] --> BN1[NODE 10]
        BN1 <--> BN2[NODE 20]
        BN2 <--> BN3[NODE 30]
    end
    subgraph AFTER
        A1[HEAD] --> AN0[NEW NODE 5]
        AN0 <--> AN1[NODE 10]
        AN1 <--> AN2[NODE 20]
        AN2 <--> AN3[NODE 30]
    end
```

### 4.3 Operational Flow — Insertion in the Middle (Inserting 25 between 20 and 30)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BEFORE
        XB1[HEAD 10] <--> XB2[NODE 20]
        XB2 <--> XB3[NODE 40]
    end
    subgraph AFTER
        XA1[HEAD 10] <--> XA2[NODE 20]
        XA2 <--> XAN[NEW NODE 25]
        XAN <--> XA3[NODE 40]
    end
```

### 4.4 Operational Flow — Deletion (Removing node with data 20)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BEFORE
        DB1[HEAD 10] <--> DB2[TARGET NODE 20]
        DB2 <--> DB3[NODE 30]
        DB2 <--> DB4[NODE 40]
    end
    subgraph AFTER
        DA1[HEAD 10] --> DASH["NULL via free"]
        DA1 -.skips.-> DA2[NODE 30]
        DA2 <--> DA3[NODE 40]
    end
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DLL Operations Module

```mermaid
graph TD
    M[Main Program] --> O1[Create Node]
    M --> O2[Insert Operation]
    M --> O3[Delete Operation]
    M --> O4[Search Operation]
    M --> O5[Traversal Operation]
    O2 --> O2a[Insert at Beginning]
    O2 --> O2b[Insert at End]
    O2 --> O2c[Insert at Position k]
    O3 --> O3a[Delete Head]
    O3 --> O3b[Delete Tail]
    O3 --> O3c[Delete at Position k]
    O3 --> O3d[Delete Given Node Pointer]
    O5 --> O5a[Forward Traversal]
    O5 --> O5b[Backward Traversal]
    O2a --> P1[Update Head prev NULL]
    O2a --> P2[Update Old Head prev newNode]
    O3d --> P3[Update predecessor next pointer]
    O3d --> P4[Update successor prev pointer]
    O3d --> P5[Free deleted node]
```

### 4.6 Traversal Pointer Movement — Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0[START head] -->|next| T1[Visit Node 10]
    T1 -->|next| T2[Visit Node 20]
    T2 -->|next| T3[Visit Node 30]
    T3 -->|next| T4[Reach NULL STOP]
    T4 -->|prev backtrack| T3
    T3 -->|prev| T2
    T2 -->|prev| T1
    T1 -->|prev| T5[Reach NULL STOP]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the structure of a node in a doubly linked list. How does it differ from a node in a singly linked list?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A node in a doubly linked list contains **three fields**:

1. `data` — stores the actual value (e.g., integer).
2. `prev` — pointer to the previous node (or `NULL` for the first node).
3. `next` — pointer to the next node (or `NULL` for the last node).

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *prev;
    struct Node *next;
};
```

**Difference from Singly Linked List (SLL):** A node in an SLL has only **two fields** — `data` and `next`. The DLL node has an **extra `prev` pointer**, which enables backward traversal and **O(1) deletion** of a known node, at the cost of higher memory usage per node.

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List the advantages of a doubly linked list over a singly linked list.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Bidirectional Traversal:** DLL allows traversal in both forward and backward directions; SLL supports only forward.
2. **Efficient Deletion:** Deleting a node whose pointer is given takes **O(1)** in DLL (since predecessor is accessible via `prev`), but takes **O(n)** in SLL (must traverse to find predecessor).
3. **Easier Implementation of certain data structures** like Deque, LRU Cache, and Undo-Redo stacks.

**Disadvantage:** Extra memory for the `prev` pointer and more pointer manipulations during insertion/deletion.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice**

> **ESE Module Pattern:** Answer **any ONE** of the following TWO full questions. Each question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Write an algorithm in C to insert a new node at the end of a doubly linked list. Trace your algorithm on the list `10 <-> 20 <-> 30` inserting the value 40. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```c
struct Node* insertAtEnd(struct Node *head, int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = NULL;

    if (head == NULL) {
        newNode->prev = NULL;
        return newNode;
    }

    struct Node *curr = head;
    while (curr->next != NULL) {
        curr = curr->next;
    }

    curr->next = newNode;
    newNode->prev = curr;
    return head;
}
```

**Trace Table on `[10] <-> [20] <-> [30]` inserting 40:**

| Step | Action | `curr` points to | List State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Create `newNode(40)`; `newNode->next = NULL` | — | `[10] <-> [20] <-> [30]` |
| 2 | `curr = head` | 10 | unchanged |
| 3 | Loop: `curr->next != NULL` → move | 20 | unchanged |
| 4 | Loop: `curr->next != NULL` → move | 30 | unchanged |
| 5 | `curr->next == NULL` → exit loop | 30 | unchanged |
| 6 | `curr->next = newNode` | 30 | `[10] <-> [20] <-> [30] <-> [40]` |
| 7 | `newNode->prev = curr` | — | same |
| 8 | Return head | — | Final list: `10 <-> 20 <-> 30 <-> 40` |

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ — since we traverse to the tail.

#### **(b) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Explain the deletion operation in a doubly linked list when the pointer to the node to be deleted is given. Write the algorithm and discuss its time complexity with a suitable diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

When the pointer `delNode` to the node being deleted is already known, deletion in a DLL takes **O(1)** time. This is the **major advantage** of DLL over SLL.

**Algorithm:**

```c
struct Node* deleteGivenNode(struct Node *head, struct Node *delNode) {
    if (head == NULL || delNode == NULL) return head;

    // Case 1: delNode is the head
    if (head == delNode) {
        head = delNode->next;
        if (head != NULL) head->prev = NULL;
        free(delNode);
        return head;
    }

    // Case 2: delNode is a middle or tail node
    if (delNode->next != NULL)
        delNode->next->prev = delNode->prev;

    if (delNode->prev != NULL)
        delNode->prev->next = delNode->next;

    free(delNode);
    return head;
}
```

**Diagram — Deleting node 20 from `10 <-> 20 <-> 30 <-> 40`:**

```
BEFORE:
HEAD --> [10] <-> [20] <-> [30] <-> [40] <-> NULL

AFTER:
HEAD --> [10] <--------------> [30] <-> [40] <-> NULL
                   (node 20 freed)
```

**Pointer Updates:**
- `[10]->next` reassigned from `[20]` to `[30]`.
- `[30]->prev` reassigned from `[20]` to `[10]`.
- `[20]` is freed.

**Time Complexity:** $T(n) = O(1)$ — only a constant number of pointer reassignments regardless of $n$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Write a C program/algorithm to perform forward and backward traversal of a doubly linked list. Explain with a sample list. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```c
// Forward Traversal
void forwardTraversal(struct Node *head) {
    struct Node *curr = head;
    printf("Forward: ");
    while (curr != NULL) {
        printf("%d ", curr->data);
        curr = curr->next;
    }
    printf("\n");
}

// Backward Traversal
void backwardTraversal(struct Node *head) {
    struct Node *curr = head;
    // First reach the last node
    while (curr->next != NULL) {
        curr = curr->next;
    }
    // Then traverse backward
    printf("Backward: ");
    while (curr != NULL) {
        printf("%d ", curr->data);
        curr = curr->prev;
    }
    printf("\n");
}
```

**Sample Trace on `NULL <- [10] <-> [20] <-> [30] <-> NULL`:**

- **Forward Traversal:** `curr` traverses `10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`. Output: `10 20 30`.
- **Backward Traversal:** First walk to the last node using `next` (reaches `30`). Then walk backward using `prev` (`30 → 20 → 10 → NULL`). Output: `30 20 10`.

**Time Complexity:** Both traversals are $O(n)$.

#### **(b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Implement a search operation in a doubly linked list. Compare its complexity with searching in a singly linked list and an array. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```c
struct Node* searchDLL(struct Node *head, int key) {
    struct Node *curr = head;
    while (curr != NULL) {
        if (curr->data == key)
            return curr;
        curr = curr->next;
    }
    return NULL;
}
```

**Trace — Searching key `25` in `10 <-> 20 <-> 30`:**

| Step | `curr->data` | Comparison | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | `10 == 25`? No | `curr = curr->next` |
| 2 | 20 | `20 == 25`? No | `curr = curr->next` |
| 3 | 30 | `30 == 25`? No | `curr = curr->next` |
| 4 | NULL | Loop exits | Return `NULL` |

**Comparison Table:**

| Data Structure | Search Time | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Unsorted Array | $O(n)$ | Linear scan needed |
| Sorted Array (Binary Search) | $O(\log n)$ | Random access by index |
| Singly Linked List | $O(n)$ | Linear scan via `next` |
| **Doubly Linked List** | $O(n)$ | Linear scan; cannot leverage `prev` for any speedup |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Searching in a DLL is **NOT faster** than in a SLL. Both are $O(n)$ because the list is not indexed. The advantage of DLL lies in **insertion/deletion**, not searching.

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in DLL Operations:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update `prev` pointer:** Many students update only `next` pointers (treating DLL like SLL) and lose **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong pointer update order:** Setting `curr->next = newNode` **before** storing `curr->next` in a temporary variable causes the reference to the next node to be lost. Always save `nextNode = curr->next` FIRST.
> 3. **Memory leak:** Failing to `free(delNode)` after deletion. Examiners specifically check this in algorithms.
> 4. **NULL checks missing:** Not handling the case when `head == NULL` or `delNode->next == NULL`. This leads to segmentation fault logic errors.
> 5. **Off-by-one error in position:** Insertion at position $k$ means the new node becomes the $k^{th}$ node. Loop must run from `i = 1` to `k - 2`, **not** `k - 1`.
> 6. **Confusing SLL and DLL deletion complexity:** Writing that DLL deletion is $O(n)$ loses full marks. The correct answer is $O(1)$ **when node pointer is given**.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- A **Doubly Linked List node** has **three fields**: `data`, `prev`, `next`.
- The **head's `prev`** and the **tail's `next`** are always `NULL`.
- **Forward Traversal:** Use `curr = curr->next`.
- **Backward Traversal:** Reach tail via `next`, then use `curr = curr->prev`.
- **Insertion at Beginning:** `O(1)` — update `newNode->next = head`, `head->prev = newNode`, `head = newNode`.
- **Insertion at End (no tail pointer):** $O(n)$ — traverse to tail, then attach.
- **Insertion at Middle (4 pointer updates):** Save `nextNode`, then assign `curr->next`, `newNode->prev`, `newNode->next`, `nextNode->prev`.
- **Deletion (node pointer known):** $O(1)$ — the **biggest advantage** of DLL.
- **Deletion (by position):** $O(k)$ worst case $O(n)$ — traverse to that position, then bypass it.
- **Search:** $O(n)$ — same as Singly Linked List. DLL does **not** speed up search.
- **Memory cost:** Each DLL node uses **extra 4–8 bytes** for the `prev` pointer compared to SLL.
- **Applications:** Browser back/forward, undo/redo, LRU cache, Deque implementation, music players.
- **Key board-exam distinction:** DLL deletion is $O(1)$ when the pointer is known; insertion at any known position is $O(1)$ **after reaching** that position; searching remains $O(n)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circular Linked List (Concept only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Circular Linked List (Concept only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Circular Linked List (CLL)** is a linear sequential data structure variant in which the terminal node's link pointer does not terminate at a `NULL` sentinel; instead, it forms a closed logical ring by pointing back to the **head (first) node** of the list. This topological property ensures that every node in the list has a valid `next` successor, eliminating the conventional boundary marker found in linear linked lists.

The KTU 2024 Scheme formally classifies Circular Linked Lists into two canonical categories under **Module 3 – Linked Lists (Conceptual Foundation)**:

1. **Singly Circular Linked List (SCLL):** Each node contains a single `data` field and a `next` pointer; only the forward (next) link exists. The last node's `next` field references the head node, forming a unidirectional ring.

2. **Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL):** Each node contains a `data` field along with both `next` (forward) and `prev` (backward) pointers. The head node's `prev` pointer references the last node, and the last node's `next` pointer references the head node, forming a bidirectional ring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Under the *"Concept only"* clause of Module 3, students are evaluated on the **logical structure, traversal mechanics, comparisons with linear lists, and application scenarios** — NOT on executable program code. Implementations using C/Python are reserved for the corresponding algorithm-based topics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Intuition

Imagine a group of friends standing in a **circular formation during a game of "Pass the Parcel"**.

- Each friend is a **node**.
- Each friend holds the hand of the friend standing to their **right** (the `next` pointer).
- There is **no "last" person** in the circle — after the final friend, the chain loops back to the first friend.
- The game continues indefinitely because there is no endpoint (`NULL`).

This perfectly mirrors a **Singly Circular Linked List**: data (the parcel) keeps rotating through nodes, and traversal continues until an external condition breaks the loop (e.g., the music stops).

For the **Doubly Circular Linked List**, visualize **two concentric rings of friends**, where each person holds hands with both neighbors — left and right. Anyone can walk forward or backward through the circle with equal ease, and the journey seamlessly loops from the last back to the first.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** A **CPU Round-Robin Scheduling Queue** is, in fact, a circular linked list in disguise. Processes are arranged in a ring; the scheduler traverses them cyclically, giving each a fixed time quantum. The "last" process connects back to the "first," forming an endless service loop.

---

## 1.3 Structural Metrics & Standard Properties

| Property | Singly Circular Linked List | Doubly Circular Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| Pointers per node | **1** (`next`) | **2** (`next`, `prev`) |
| Memory per node (excluding data) | **8 bytes** (on 64-bit) | **16 bytes** (on 64-bit) |
| Traversal direction | Forward only | Forward + Backward |
| Total external pointers needed | 1 (`head` or `tail`) | 1 (`head` optional, `tail` preferred) |
| Null termination | **None** (closes at head) | **None** (closes at head) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked about the number of pointers in a CLL, examiners look for the **logical pointer count**, not the actual memory footprint. Always state the **structural pointer count** (1 or 2) in your answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Blueprint — Why and How

The circular linked list is not merely a "linked list that loops." It is a deliberate topological choice made to optimize **specific access patterns and memory-rotation scenarios**. Below is the rigorous breakdown of its operational mechanics.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" — Motivation Behind Circularity

Linear linked lists suffer from two structural inefficiencies:

1. **Reachability of the tail:** To reach the last node, the traversal must begin at `head` and visit every intermediate node — an **O(N)** operation.
2. **Wrap-around logic in cyclic algorithms:** Applications such as the Josephus Problem, token-ring networking, and circular buffers require artificial circular handling even when using linear structures.

A circular linked list **intrinsically solves** both issues:
- The list is **headless in concept** — any node can be the "entry point."
- Circularity is **native**, not simulated.

### 2.1.2 The "How" — Core Operational Mechanics

The traversal logic of a circular linked list is governed by a single invariant:

$$
\text{lastNode}.\text{next} = \text{headNode}
$$

For a **Doubly Circular Linked List**, a second invariant holds:

$$
\text{headNode}.\text{prev} = \text{lastNode}
$$

**Traversal Termination Condition:** Unlike a linear list, traversal **does not** end at `NULL`. Instead, it ends when the **starting node is revisited**. This is implemented via a `do-while` style logic:

$$
\text{Repeat: visit node} \rightarrow \text{move to node.next} \quad \text{until (current == startNode)}
$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all KTU-relevant mathematical relationships and traversal constraints for Circular Linked Lists. Memorize these for direct exam application.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SCLL invariant | $\text{last.next} = \text{head}$ | Last node's `next` link | Always holds for non-empty list |
| 2 | DCLL invariant | $\text{head.prev} = \text{last}$ | Head node's `prev` link | Always holds for non-empty list |
| 3 | Total nodes (SCLL) | $n = $ `count(traversal)` | Number of elements | $n \geq 0$ |
| 4 | Traversal cost | $T(n) = O(n)$ | Visit all nodes | Loop terminates at revisit |
| 5 | Pointer count per node (SCLL) | $p = 1$ | `next` pointer only | — |
| 6 | Pointer count per node (DCLL) | $p = 2$ | `next` + `prev` | — |
| 7 | Memory per node (DCLL) | $M = 2 \cdot w + s$ | $w$ = word size, $s$ = sizeof(data) | Typical $w = 8$ bytes (64-bit) |
| 8 | Reach last from tail pointer (SCLL) | $O(1)$ | Direct access via tail.next | Advantage over linear list |
| 9 | Reach last from head pointer (linear) | $O(n)$ | Sequential traversal | Disadvantage mitigated by CLL |
| 10 | Empty list invariant | $\text{head} = \text{NULL}$ | No nodes | For both CLL and linear list |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, **do not use** the vertical bar `\|` notation inside table cells (e.g., for absolute value or set cardinality). Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, as shown above in the formulas. This avoids markdown parser conflicts during Board evaluation.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Circular Linked Lists underpin several mission-critical engineering systems:

- **Operating Systems:** Round-robin CPU scheduling, circular buffer management in kernel I/O subsystems.
- **Networking:** Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) protocol where a "token" circulates among nodes.
- **Multimedia Applications:** Continuous loop playback in audio/video streaming (e.g., music on repeat).
- **Gaming & Simulation:** Turn-based multiplayer games where play cycles through players indefinitely.
- **Embedded Systems:** Real-time task schedulers in RTOS (Real-Time Operating Systems) like FreeRTOS.
- **Data Structures Foundation:** The **Josephus Problem** is most elegantly solved using a circular linked list.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Traversal Count in a Circular Linked List

> **Problem Context:** Given a circular linked list with $N$ nodes, starting traversal from the head node, derive the exact number of pointer movements required to revisit the head node (i.e., complete one full cycle).

This derivation is a **classic KTU Module 3 analytical question** and tests the student's understanding of the circular invariant.

### Step 1 — Formalize the Starting Condition

Let the list contain $N$ nodes, indexed as:

$$
\text{Nodes} = \{ n_1, n_2, n_3, \ldots, n_N \}
$$

where $n_1$ is the head node and $n_N$ is the last node. The structural invariant dictates:

$$
n_i.\text{next} = n_{i+1} \quad \text{for } 1 \leq i < N
$$

$$
n_N.\text{next} = n_1 \quad \text{(circular closure)}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Pointer Movement Function

Let $P(k)$ denote the position after $k$ pointer movements, starting from $n_1$. We can write:

$$
P(k) = n_{((k \mod N) + 1)}
$$

This formula captures the modulo-based cyclic behavior of the traversal.

### Step 3 — Count Movements to Revisit Head

The head $n_1$ is revisited when the current position equals the starting position, i.e., when $P(k) = n_1$.

$$
P(k) = n_1 \implies ((k \mod N) + 1) = 1 \implies k \mod N = 0
$$

The smallest positive integer $k$ satisfying this congruence is:

$$
k = N
$$

### Step 4 — Generalize to Arbitrary Starting Node

If traversal begins at any arbitrary node $n_s$ (where $1 \leq s \leq N$), the count to return to $n_s$ remains $N$ movements. This confirms that **the cycle length is independent of the starting point**.

$$
\boxed{k_{\text{cycle}} = N \quad \text{for all valid starting nodes } n_s}
$$

### Step 5 — Computational Complexity Inference

The traversal is a deterministic $O(N)$ operation. KTU examiners frequently ask students to **prove** why the cycle ends and not result in an infinite loop — the answer lies in the explicit termination check:

$$
\text{Stop when } \text{currentNode} = \text{startNode}
$$

This explicit equality check **forces termination** after exactly $N$ iterations.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Properties

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is a **high-yield KTU exam tool**. Examiners frequently pose comparison questions worth 7–14 marks. This matrix is your single source of truth.

| Property | Linear Singly Linked List | Singly Circular Linked List | Doubly Linked List | Doubly Circular Linked List |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Last node `next` | $\text{NULL}$ | $\text{head}$ | $\text{NULL}$ | $\text{head}$ |
| First node `prev` | N/A | N/A | $\text{NULL}$ | $\text{tail}$ |
| Forward traversal | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Backward traversal | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Memory per node | $1 \cdot w + s$ | $1 \cdot w + s$ | $2 \cdot w + s$ | $2 \cdot w + s$ |
| Reach tail from head | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ via tail pointer | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ via tail pointer |
| Reach head from tail | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Deletion of a node (given pointer) | $O(n)$ (needs predecessor) | $O(n)$ (needs predecessor) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Natural for round-robin | ❌ Needs patching | ✅ Native | ❌ Needs patching | ✅ Native |
| Risk of infinite traversal loop | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (if condition missed) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (if condition missed) |

---

## 3.3 Derived Invariants — Empty and Single-Node Cases

KTU examiners often test edge cases. The two critical edge cases for circular lists are:

**Case 1 — Empty List:**

$$
\text{head} = \text{NULL}
$$

All other invariants are trivially satisfied because there are no nodes to constrain.

**Case 2 — Single-Node List:**

$$
\text{head.next} = \text{head} \quad \text{(the node points to itself)}
$$

$$
\text{head.prev} = \text{head} \quad \text{(for DCLL)}
$$

This self-referential property is a **signature characteristic** of single-node circular lists and is a frequent KTU viva question.

> [!TIP]
> Always draw the single-node case in your exam answers — examiners reward this kind of edge-case awareness with bonus understanding marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Singly Circular Linked List (3-Node Example)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["A | next"] -- next --> nodeB["B | next"]
    nodeB["B | next"] -- next --> nodeC["C | next"]
    nodeC["C | next"] -- circular closure back to A --> nodeA["A | next"]
    headPtr("HEAD") -- points to --> nodeA["A | next"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Nodes A, B, and C are arranged in a unidirectional ring.
- The arrow from C back to A visually represents the `last.next = head` invariant.
- An external `HEAD` pointer is shown only for clarity; in a true circular list, traversal can begin from any node.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Doubly Circular Linked List (3-Node Example)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA2["A next, prev"] -- next forward --> nodeB2["B next, prev"]
    nodeB2["B next, prev"] -- next forward --> nodeC2["C next, prev"]
    nodeC2["C next, prev"] -- next forward to A --> nodeA2["A next, prev"]
    nodeA2["A next, prev"] -- prev back to C --> nodeC2["C next, prev"]
    nodeC2["C next, prev"] -- prev back to B --> nodeB2["B next, prev"]
    nodeB2["B next, prev"] -- prev back to A --> nodeA2["A next, prev"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Solid forward arrows represent `next` pointers forming a clockwise ring.
- Reverse arrows represent `prev` pointers forming a counter-clockwise ring.
- The bidirectional closure confirms both DCLL invariants simultaneously.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Circular List in Round-Robin Scheduling

> [!NOTE]
> Since the topic is conceptual, the following block diagram maps how a Circular Linked List operates within a **CPU Round-Robin Scheduler** — a real engineering deployment.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph CPU["CPU SCHEDULER MODULE"]
        schedulerCore["Scheduler Core"]
    end
    subgraph CLL["CIRCULAR LINKED LIST - PROCESS QUEUE"]
        procP1["Process P1"]
        procP2["Process P2"]
        procP3["Process P3"]
        procP4["Process P4"]
    end
    schedulerCore -- dispatch --> procP1
    procP1 -- next pointer --> procP2
    procP2 -- next pointer --> procP3
    procP3 -- next pointer --> procP4
    procP4 -- circular wrap back --> procP1
    schedulerCore -- after time quantum --> procP1
```

**Functional Flow Explanation:**

1. The `Scheduler Core` dispatches `Process P1` for execution.
2. After its time quantum expires, the scheduler follows `P1.next` to dispatch `P2`.
3. The cycle continues through `P3` and `P4`.
4. After `P4`, the **circular closure** automatically routes the scheduler back to `P1`.
5. This creates a **fair, starvation-free execution order** — the defining advantage of using a CLL over a linear list for cyclic workloads.

---

## 4.4 Single-Node Self-Referential Case (KTU High-Value Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    onlyNode["X | next and prev both point to self"]
    onlyNode -- self-loop next --> onlyNode
    onlyNode -- self-loop prev --> onlyNode
```

This diagram represents the **critical edge case** of a circular list containing exactly one node. Both `next` and `prev` pointers (for DCLL) point to the same node itself.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions require **direct, definition-based** answers of 4–6 lines. Avoid over-elaboration; precision is rewarded.

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Define a Circular Linked List. How does it differ from a linear linked list?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Circular Linked List** is a linked list in which the `next` pointer of the last node points back to the head node, forming a closed ring. In contrast, a **linear linked list** terminates with `next = NULL` for its last node.

Key differences:
- The circular list has **no `NULL` termination** in its `next` chain.
- Traversal in a circular list continues **indefinitely** unless an explicit stop condition is applied (e.g., returning to the starting node).
- Circular lists are **inherently suited for cyclic applications** such as round-robin scheduling, whereas linear lists are best for sequential, terminating operations.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Mention any three real-world applications of Circular Linked Lists.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **CPU Round-Robin Scheduling:** Operating systems cycle through processes in a fixed order, where the next process to execute is always the successor in a circular queue.

2. **Token Ring Networking (IEEE 802.5):** A special "token" packet circulates among networked nodes in a ring topology; only the node holding the token may transmit.

3. **Circular Audio/Video Playback:** Media players implement endless loop playback using circular lists, where the next track after the last is the first track.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 mandates an **internal choice** in Part B. Students must answer **either Question A OR Question B**, with sub-parts typically carrying **7 + 7 = 14 marks**.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the structure of a **Singly Circular Linked List** with a neat diagram. State its key invariant. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Singly Circular Linked List** and **Doubly Circular Linked List** in terms of structure, memory, traversal capability, and any two application areas. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Structure of Singly Circular Linked List (SCLL):**

A Singly Circular Linked List consists of nodes connected in a unidirectional ring. Each node has two fields:
- **Data field:** Stores the actual element.
- **Next pointer field:** Stores the address of the subsequent node.

The **last node's `next` pointer does not store `NULL`**; instead, it stores the address of the **head node**, closing the list into a circle.

**Key Invariant:**

$$
\text{lastNode}.\text{next} = \text{headNode}
$$

**Neat Diagram:**

```
  +------+------+      +------+------+      +------+------+
  | Data | next | ---> | Data | next | ---> | Data | next |
  |  10  |  -------->|  20  |  -------->|  30  |   -----+
  +------+------+      +------+------+      +------+------+
       ^                                                  |
       |__________________________________________________|
              (last.next points back to head)
```

**Key Properties:**
1. No node has `next = NULL` (except in an empty list where `head = NULL`).
2. Traversal can start from any node and is conventionally terminated when the start node is revisited.
3. Often only a **tail pointer** is maintained externally, since `tail.next` gives direct `O(1)` access to the head.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b):

| Aspect | Singly Circular Linked List (SCLL) | Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL) |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Each node has 1 pointer (`next`) | Each node has 2 pointers (`next`, `prev`) |
| **Memory per node** | $1 \cdot w + s$ bytes | $2 \cdot w + s$ bytes (roughly doubles) |
| **Traversal direction** | Forward only | Forward **and** backward |
| **Deletion at tail** | $O(n)$ (requires predecessor search) | $O(1)$ (direct via `tail.prev`) |
| **Reach head from tail** | $O(1)$ via `tail.next` | $O(1)` via `tail.next` |
| **Application 1** | Round-robin scheduling (one-direction cycle) | Undo/Redo navigation in browsers |
| **Application 2** | Josephus problem solution | Music playlist with previous-track support |

**Conclusion:** SCLL is memory-efficient for simple cyclic workloads, while DCLL offers superior flexibility for bidirectional navigation at the cost of higher memory usage.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain how a **Circular Linked List** is used to solve the **Josephus Problem** for $N = 5$ persons and $K = 2$ (every 2nd person is eliminated). List the order of elimination and the survivor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of Circular Linked Lists over linear linked lists. Mention one scenario where a linear list is still preferable. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a):

**The Josephus Problem Setup:**
- $N = 5$ persons standing in a circle, numbered 1 to 5.
- $K = 2$: Every 2nd person is eliminated in cyclic counting.
- A circular linked list perfectly models this: each node is a person, and circularity allows counting to wrap around naturally.

**Initial Circular List:**

```
1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 -> (back to 1)
```

**Step-by-Step Elimination:**

**Step 1:** Start counting from node `1`. Count $K = 2$ nodes: `1` (count 1), `2` (count 2). Eliminate **`2`**.
- List now: `1 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 -> (back to 1)`

**Step 2:** Continue counting from the next node (`3`). Count 2: `3` (count 1), `4` (count 2). Eliminate **`4`**.
- List now: `1 -> 3 -> 5 -> (back to 1)`

**Step 3:** Continue from next (`5`). Count 2: `5` (count 1), `1` (count 2). Eliminate **`1`**.
- List now: `3 -> 5 -> (back to 3)`

**Step 4:** Continue from next (`3`). Count 2: `3` (count 1), `5` (count 2). Eliminate **`5`**.
- List now: `3` (alone, points to itself)

**Elimination Order:** $2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 5$

**Survivor:** Person **`3`** is the last remaining node.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Advantages of Circular Linked Lists:**

1. **No `NULL` pointers anywhere in the list:** This eliminates the special-case handling needed for the last node in linear lists.

2. **Any node can be a starting point:** Since the list has no natural "end," traversal can begin from any node with full access to all elements.

3. **Efficient cyclic operations:** Applications like round-robin scheduling, Josephus problem, and circular buffers are implemented naturally without artificial wrap-around logic.

4. **$O(1)$ access to both head and tail:** With a tail pointer, `tail.next` gives immediate head access.

**Disadvantages of Circular Linked Lists:**

1. **Risk of infinite loops:** If the termination condition is forgotten or buggy, the program may enter an infinite traversal loop.

2. **More complex implementation:** Maintaining the circular invariant during insertions and deletions requires careful pointer manipulation.

3. **Harder to debug:** Tracing through a circular list in a debugger can be confusing because there is no clear "start" or "end."

**Scenario where Linear List is Preferable:**

A **stack-based undo history in a text editor** is best modeled as a linear singly linked list, because the operations are inherently **sequential and terminating** — you never need to wrap around to the beginning. A circular list would force an artificial termination condition, complicating the logic unnecessarily.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Circular Linked List Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the termination condition:** Many students write traversal logic for CLLs without specifying *when* to stop. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for missing the explicit "stop when current node == start node" condition.
>
> 2. **Confusing SCLL with DCLL invariants:** Students often state only `last.next = head` and forget `head.prev = last` for DCLL questions. This costs **1–2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Drawing linear diagrams for circular lists:** If your diagram shows `last.next = NULL`, you have drawn a *linear* list, not a circular one. Examiners award **0 marks** for the diagram section if this fundamental error occurs.
>
> 4. **Omitting the empty-list and single-node edge cases:** Failing to mention `head = NULL` for empty lists and `node.next = node` for single-node lists can cost **up to 1 mark** in 7-mark comparison questions.
>
> 5. **Using vague language like "the last node points to itself":** This is correct only for the *single-node* case. In multi-node circular lists, the last node points to the **head**, not to itself. Examiners penalize imprecise language.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ A **Circular Linked List** is a linked list where the **last node's `next` points to the head**, forming a closed ring.
- ✅ Two types: **Singly Circular** (1 pointer per node) and **Doubly Circular** (2 pointers per node).
- ✅ Key invariant (SCLL): $\text{last.next} = \text{head}$.
- ✅ Key invariant (DCLL): $\text{head.prev} = \text{last}$ (in addition to SCLL invariant).
- ✅ **Empty list:** `head = NULL`.
- ✅ **Single-node list:** `node.next = node` (and `node.prev = node` for DCLL).
- ✅ Traversal terminates when the **starting node is revisited**, NOT at `NULL`.
- ✅ Best suited for **cyclic applications**: round-robin scheduling, Josephus problem, token ring, circular media playback.
- ✅ Cycle length is **always $N$** regardless of starting node.
- ✅ **Major risk:** Missing termination condition → infinite loop.
- ✅ Memory per node: SCLL = $w + s$ bytes; DCLL = $2w + s$ bytes.
- ✅ Reach tail from head: $O(1)$ if tail pointer is maintained; $O(n)$ otherwise.
- ✅ Compared to linear lists: CLLs have **no `NULL`** in the chain, **no natural end**, and **cyclic behavior native** to the structure.
- ✅ Examiner loves to ask: **"Why does a circular list not lead to an infinite loop?"** — Answer: Because of explicit `current == start` termination check.
- ✅ Remember the **self-referential single-node edge case** — it is a signature circular-list property.
- ✅ **Josephus Problem** is the textbook application — practice one full dry run before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linked List representation of Stack and Queue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linked List representation of Stack and Queue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Linked List** is a linear, dynamic data structure consisting of a sequence of **nodes**, where each node contains two fields: a **DATA** field (which stores the actual element/value) and a **LINK / NEXT** field (which stores the address/pointer to the next node in the sequence). The list is accessed through an external **HEAD / TOP** pointer that points to the first node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linked List Representation of Stack:** A stack is a **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** data structure. When implemented using a singly linked list, the **TOP** of the stack corresponds to the **HEAD (first node)** of the linked list. All insertions (PUSH) and deletions (POP) occur only at the HEAD end, giving **O(1)** time complexity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linked List Representation of Queue:** A queue is a **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** data structure. When implemented using a singly linked list, two external pointers are maintained — a **FRONT** pointer (for deletion / DEQUEUE) pointing to the first node, and a **REAR** pointer (for insertion / ENQUEUE) pointing to the last node. Both operations execute in **O(1)** time complexity.

### 1.2 Node Structure (Singly Linked Node)

$$\text{Node} = \text{DATA} \; + \; \text{NEXT (LINK)}$$

The generic structure of a node, using C-style declaration, is:

```c
struct Node {
    int data;            // Element stored in the node
    struct Node *next;   // Pointer to the next node
};
```

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

**Analogy 1 — Stack as a Stack of Plates 🍽️:**  
Imagine a vertical pile of plates on a spring-loaded cafeteria counter. You can only **add a plate on top** (PUSH) and **remove the plate that is on top** (POP). The **topmost plate** is always the most recently added one. In the linked list version, the **topmost plate = HEAD of the list**, and the pile extends downward through the NEXT pointers.

**Analogy 2 — Queue as a Movie Ticket Line 🎟️:**  
Imagine people standing in a queue to buy tickets. New arrivals join at the **rear** (ENQUEUE), and the person at the **front** is served first and leaves (DEQUEUE). In the linked list version, the **first person in line = FRONT pointer**, and the **last person in line = REAR pointer**.

### 1.4 Why Linked List over Array for Stack/Queue?

| Feature | Array Representation | Linked List Representation |
|---|---|---|
| Size | **Fixed** (declared at compile time) | **Dynamic** (grows/shrinks at runtime) |
| Overflow | Possible (stack full) | Not possible (limited only by memory) |
| Memory Utilization | Wasted if partially filled | Efficient (allocates as needed) |
| Access Time | Direct indexing — O(1) | Sequential traversal — O(n) to reach `i-th` |
| Push/Pop, Enqueue/Dequeue | **O(1)** | **O(1)** (at known ends) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The choice between array and linked list representation is a frequently asked 3-mark question. The key trade-off is **static vs. dynamic memory** and **overflow handling**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linked List Representation of STACK

#### 2.1.1 Architecture

- A single pointer `TOP` is maintained. `TOP` always points to the **most recently inserted node**.
- Initially, `TOP = NULL` (empty stack).
- Each node stores: `data` + `next` pointer.

#### 2.1.2 PUSH Operation — Insert at the Beginning

**Why insert at the beginning?** Because insertion at the HEAD of a singly linked list is **O(1)**, whereas insertion at the tail is **O(n)** (we would have to traverse to find the last node).

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Check Overflow:** In linked list, overflow occurs only when **system memory is exhausted** (`malloc` returns `NULL`). This is called **memory overflow**, not array overflow.
2. **Allocate a new node** `newNode` using dynamic memory allocation.
3. **Assign data:** `newNode->data = value`.
4. **Link the new node:** `newNode->next = TOP` (the new node points to the current top).
5. **Update TOP:** `TOP = newNode`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Complexity: T(PUSH) = O(1)** — Constant time, independent of stack size.
> **Space Complexity: S(PUSH) = O(1)** per insertion.

#### 2.1.3 POP Operation — Delete from the Beginning

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Check Underflow:** If `TOP == NULL`, the stack is empty — print "Stack Underflow" and return.
2. **Store a temporary pointer** `temp = TOP` to preserve the node to be deleted.
3. **Move TOP down:** `TOP = TOP->next` (the next node becomes the new top).
4. **Free the old node:** `free(temp)`.
5. **Return the data** stored in the freed node (saved before deletion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Complexity: T(POP) = O(1)** — Constant time.
> **Space Complexity: S(POP) = O(1).**

#### 2.1.4 PEEK Operation — Read the Top Element

If `TOP == NULL`, report "Empty Stack". Otherwise, return `TOP->data`. **T(PEEK) = O(1).**

### 2.2 Linked List Representation of QUEUE

#### 2.2.1 Architecture

- **Two pointers** are maintained:
  - `FRONT` — points to the **first node** (the next element to be dequeued).
  - `REAR` — points to the **last node** (the most recently enqueued element).
- Initially, `FRONT = REAR = NULL` (empty queue).

#### 2.2.2 ENQUEUE Operation — Insert at the REAR

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Check Overflow:** Same as stack — `malloc` returning `NULL` indicates memory overflow.
2. **Allocate a new node** `newNode`.
3. **Assign data:** `newNode->data = value`.
4. **Set new node's link:** `newNode->next = NULL` (it will be the last node).
5. **Link from REAR:** If `REAR != NULL`, set `REAR->next = newNode`.
6. **Update REAR:** `REAR = newNode`.
7. **Special case — first insertion:** If `FRONT == NULL`, set `FRONT = REAR = newNode`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Complexity: T(ENQUEUE) = O(1)** — Constant time, because REAR directly points to the end.

#### 2.2.3 DEQUEUE Operation — Delete from the FRONT

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Check Underflow:** If `FRONT == NULL`, the queue is empty — print "Queue Underflow" and return.
2. **Store a temporary pointer** `temp = FRONT` to preserve the node to be deleted.
3. **Move FRONT forward:** `FRONT = FRONT->next`.
4. **Special case — last node removed:** If `FRONT == NULL` after moving, set `REAR = NULL` (queue is now empty).
5. **Free the old node:** `free(temp)`.
6. **Return the data** stored in the freed node (saved before deletion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Complexity: T(DEQUEUE) = O(1)** — Constant time.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Data Structure | Action Performed | Position in List | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Overflow Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PUSH | Stack | Insert at HEAD | Beginning | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | Heap memory exhausted |
| POP | Stack | Delete from HEAD | Beginning | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | `TOP = NULL` (underflow) |
| PEEK | Stack | Read `TOP->data` | Beginning | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | `TOP = NULL` |
| ENQUEUE | Queue | Insert at REAR | End | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | Heap memory exhausted |
| DEQUEUE | Queue | Delete from FRONT | Beginning | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | `FRONT = NULL` (underflow) |
| Display | Both | Traverse from HEAD to `NULL` | Entire list | **O(n)** | **O(1)** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Exams:**
> - Stack Empty ⇒ `TOP = NULL`
> - Stack with one element ⇒ `TOP->next = NULL`
> - Queue Empty ⇒ `FRONT = NULL` **AND** `REAR = NULL`
> - Queue with one element ⇒ `FRONT = REAR` (both point to the same node)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems:** Process scheduling queues, call stack management, undo-redo stacks in IDEs.
- **Networking:** Packet buffers in routers (queue), back/forward browser navigation (stack).
- **Compilers:** Function call stacks, recursion management, symbol table management.
- **Embedded Systems:** Real-time task scheduling uses circular queues.
- **Memory Management:** Dynamic memory allocators (malloc/free) internally use linked free-lists.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 C-Style Algorithms (Complete Working Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a **Data Structures (CS)** course, C-style pseudocode/algorithms are part of the KTU syllabus and are appropriate here. No Python is used.

#### 3.1.1 Stack using Linked List — Complete Algorithm

**Step 1: Node Structure Definition**

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};
```

**Step 2: PUSH Operation — Mathematical Derivation of Pointer Movements**

Let the linked list before PUSH be:

$$\text{List} : \;\; \text{TOP} \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_k \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

After PUSH of value $x$ (creating a new node $n_{new}$):

$$\text{After PUSH} : \;\; \text{TOP} \rightarrow n_{new} \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_k \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

The transitions are:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{new}.\text{data} &= x \\
n_{new}.\text{next} &= \text{TOP (old)} \\
\text{TOP (new)} &= n_{new}
\end{aligned}$$

**Working C Algorithm for PUSH:**

```c
void push(struct Node **top, int value) {
    // Step 1: Check for memory overflow
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (newNode == NULL) {
        printf("Stack Overflow (Memory Full)\n");
        return;
    }
    // Step 2: Assign data
    newNode->data = value;
    // Step 3: Link new node to current top
    newNode->next = *top;
    // Step 4: Update top pointer
    *top = newNode;
    printf("%d pushed onto stack\n", value);
}
```

**Step 3: POP Operation — Pointer Transition Derivation**

Before POP:

$$\text{TOP} \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_k \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

After POP:

$$\text{TOP} \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_k \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

The transitions are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{temp} &= \text{TOP} \\
\text{value returned} &= \text{temp}\rightarrow\text{data} \\
\text{TOP (new)} &= \text{TOP}\rightarrow\text{next} \\
\text{free(temp)} &
\end{aligned}$$

**Working C Algorithm for POP:**

```c
int pop(struct Node **top) {
    // Step 1: Check for underflow
    if (*top == NULL) {
        printf("Stack Underflow (Empty Stack)\n");
        return -1;
    }
    // Step 2: Store current top
    struct Node *temp = *top;
    int poppedValue = temp->data;
    // Step 3: Move top to next node
    *top = (*top)->next;
    // Step 4: Free the old top
    free(temp);
    // Step 5: Return the popped value
    return poppedValue;
}
```

#### 3.1.2 Queue using Linked List — Complete Algorithm

**Step 1: ENQUEUE Operation — Mathematical Derivation**

Before ENQUEUE of value $x$:

$$\text{FRONT} \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_k \leftarrow \text{REAR}$$

After ENQUEUE of $x$ (new node $n_{new}$):

$$\text{FRONT} \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_k \rightarrow n_{new} \leftarrow \text{REAR}$$

The transitions are:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{new}.\text{data} &= x \\
n_{new}.\text{next} &= \text{NULL} \\
\text{If REAR} \neq \text{NULL} : \;\; \text{REAR}\rightarrow\text{next} &= n_{new} \\
\text{REAR (new)} &= n_{new} \\
\text{If FRONT} = \text{NULL} : \;\; \text{FRONT} &= n_{new}
\end{aligned}$$

**Working C Algorithm for ENQUEUE:**

```c
void enqueue(struct Node **front, struct Node **rear, int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (newNode == NULL) {
        printf("Queue Overflow (Memory Full)\n");
        return;
    }
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = NULL;
    
    // If queue is empty
    if (*rear == NULL) {
        *front = *rear = newNode;
        printf("%d enqueued\n", value);
        return;
    }
    // Link the new node after the current rear
    (*rear)->next = newNode;
    *rear = newNode;
    printf("%d enqueued\n", value);
}
```

**Step 2: DEQUEUE Operation — Mathematical Derivation**

Before DEQUEUE:

$$\text{FRONT} \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_k \leftarrow \text{REAR}$$

After DEQUEUE:

$$\text{FRONT} \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_k \leftarrow \text{REAR}$$

The transitions are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{temp} &= \text{FRONT} \\
\text{value returned} &= \text{temp}\rightarrow\text{data} \\
\text{FRONT (new)} &= \text{FRONT}\rightarrow\text{next} \\
\text{If FRONT (new)} = \text{NULL} : \;\; \text{REAR} &= \text{NULL} \\
\text{free(temp)} &
\end{aligned}$$

**Working C Algorithm for DEQUEUE:**

```c
int dequeue(struct Node **front, struct Node **rear) {
    if (*front == NULL) {
        printf("Queue Underflow (Empty Queue)\n");
        return -1;
    }
    struct Node *temp = *front;
    int dequeuedValue = temp->data;
    *front = (*front)->next;
    
    // If front becomes NULL, queue is empty, update rear
    if (*front == NULL) {
        *rear = NULL;
    }
    free(temp);
    return dequeuedValue;
}
```

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Operations Comparison

| Aspect | Stack (Linked List) | Queue (Linked List) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Pointers | **1** (TOP) | **2** (FRONT, REAR) |
| Insertion End | HEAD (top) | REAR (tail) |
| Deletion End | HEAD (top) | FRONT (head) |
| Traversal for Display | Start from TOP, follow NEXT | Start from FRONT, follow NEXT |
| Empty Condition | `TOP = NULL` | `FRONT = NULL` **and** `REAR = NULL` |
| One-Node Condition | `TOP->next = NULL` | `FRONT = REAR` and `FRONT->next = NULL` |
| Time per op | **O(1)** | **O(1)** |
| Total memory for n elements | $n \times (\text{sizeof}(\text{data}) + \text{sizeof}(\text{pointer})) + \text{sizeof}(\text{TOP})$ | $n \times (\text{sizeof}(\text{data}) + \text{sizeof}(\text{pointer})) + 2 \times \text{sizeof}(\text{pointer})$ |

### 3.3 Worked-Out Example (Numerical Trace)

**Problem:** Consider an initially empty stack implemented using a linked list. Perform the following sequence of operations and draw the linked list after each step:

1. PUSH 10
2. PUSH 20
3. PUSH 30
4. POP
5. PEEK

**Solution — Step-by-step trace:**

**Step 1: PUSH 10** — New node created with data=10, linked to NULL. TOP points to this node.

$$\text{TOP} \rightarrow [10 \mid \text{NULL}]$$

**Step 2: PUSH 20** — New node with data=20 created. Its NEXT points to old TOP (the node with 10). TOP updated.

$$\text{TOP} \rightarrow [20 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [10 \mid \text{NULL}]$$

**Step 3: PUSH 30** — New node with data=30 created. Its NEXT points to old TOP. TOP updated.

$$\text{TOP} \rightarrow [30 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [20 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [10 \mid \text{NULL}]$$

**Step 4: POP** — Node with data=30 is removed. TOP moves to the next node. The value returned is **30**.

$$\text{TOP} \rightarrow [20 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [10 \mid \text{NULL}]$$

**Step 5: PEEK** — Returns `TOP->data` = **20** (no modification to the list).

$$\text{Result of PEEK} = 20$$

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Compare array and linked list representation of a stack. Mention any two advantages of linked list representation.**

**Model Answer:**

In array representation, the stack size is **fixed at compile time** and a stack-full condition (overflow) may occur even when memory is available elsewhere. In linked list representation, nodes are created dynamically using `malloc` during PUSH and freed using `free` during POP, so size is **dynamic and limited only by available heap memory**.

**Two advantages of linked list representation:**

1. **No overflow** under normal conditions — a new node is created only when memory is available.
2. **Efficient memory utilization** — memory is allocated exactly as needed, with no wastage.
3. **Dynamic resizing** — the stack grows and shrinks at runtime without programmer intervention.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is the time complexity of PUSH and POP operations in a stack implemented using a linked list? Justify.**

**Model Answer:**

Both **PUSH and POP** operations have a time complexity of **O(1)** — constant time.

**Justification:** PUSH involves creating a new node, assigning its `data` and `next` fields, and updating the `TOP` pointer. None of these steps depend on the number of nodes already present in the stack. Similarly, POP involves storing the current TOP, advancing `TOP` to `TOP->next`, and freeing the old node — all of which are constant-time operations. Therefore, both operations execute in **O(1)** time, making linked list stacks highly efficient.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q3. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the linked list representation of a stack with a neat diagram. Write the C functions for PUSH and POP operations.

**Model Answer:**

**Linked List Representation of Stack:**

A stack implemented using a singly linked list uses a node structure containing a `data` field and a `next` pointer. An external pointer `TOP` always points to the **most recently inserted node** (the top of the stack). The bottom of the stack corresponds to the last node whose `next` field is `NULL`.

**Diagram:**

$$\text{TOP} \rightarrow [30 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [20 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [10 \mid \text{NULL}]$$

Here, **30** is the most recently pushed element (top), and **10** is the first pushed element (bottom).

**C Function for PUSH:**

```c
void push(struct Node **top, int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (newNode == NULL) {
        printf("Stack Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = *top;     // Link new node to current top
    *top = newNode;           // Update TOP
}
```

**Working Explanation:**
1. A new node is dynamically allocated.
2. The value to be pushed is stored in `newNode->data`.
3. `newNode->next` is set to the current `TOP` — this links the new node ahead of the existing list.
4. `TOP` is updated to point to the new node, making it the new top.
5. Time complexity is **O(1)**.

**C Function for POP:**

```c
int pop(struct Node **top) {
    if (*top == NULL) {
        printf("Stack Underflow\n");
        return -1;
    }
    struct Node *temp = *top;
    int poppedValue = temp->data;
    *top = (*top)->next;      // Move TOP to next node
    free(temp);                // Free the old top
    return poppedValue;
}
```

**Working Explanation:**
1. The function checks if the stack is empty by testing `TOP == NULL`. If true, underflow is reported.
2. A temporary pointer `temp` is set to the current TOP.
3. The `data` of the node to be deleted is saved for return.
4. `TOP` is moved to `TOP->next`, effectively removing the top node from the chain.
5. The old node is freed using `free(temp)` to prevent memory leaks.
6. Time complexity is **O(1)**.

---

**Q3. (b) [7 Marks]** Explain the linked list representation of a queue. Write the C functions for ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations with proper handling of edge cases.

**Model Answer:**

**Linked List Representation of Queue:**

A queue implemented using a singly linked list uses two external pointers: `FRONT` (points to the first node — used for DEQUEUE) and `REAR` (points to the last node — used for ENQUEUE). The `FRONT` end represents the **dequeue side** (oldest element), and the `REAR` end represents the **enqueue side** (newest element).

**Diagram:**

$$\text{FRONT} \rightarrow [10 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [20 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [30 \mid \text{NULL}] \leftarrow \text{REAR}$$

**C Function for ENQUEUE:**

```c
void enqueue(struct Node **front, struct Node **rear, int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (newNode == NULL) {
        printf("Queue Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = NULL;
    
    if (*rear == NULL) {                  // Empty queue: first insertion
        *front = *rear = newNode;
        return;
    }
    (*rear)->next = newNode;              // Link new node at the end
    *rear = newNode;                      // Update REAR
}
```

**Edge Case Handled:** When the queue is empty (`REAR == NULL`), both `FRONT` and `REAR` are made to point to the new node, initializing the queue correctly.

**C Function for DEQUEUE:**

```c
int dequeue(struct Node **front, struct Node **rear) {
    if (*front == NULL) {
        printf("Queue Underflow\n");
        return -1;
    }
    struct Node *temp = *front;
    int dequeuedValue = temp->data;
    *front = (*front)->next;
    
    if (*front == NULL) {                 // Queue became empty
        *rear = NULL;                     // Reset REAR to NULL
    }
    free(temp);
    return dequeuedValue;
}
```

**Edge Case Handled:** When the last element is dequeued, `FRONT` becomes `NULL`. If `REAR` is not also reset to `NULL`, it would become a **dangling pointer**. Hence, `REAR` is set to `NULL` to keep the queue in a valid empty state.

Both operations execute in **O(1)** time.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q4. (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain how a stack is represented using a linked list. Show the linked list state after performing: PUSH 5, PUSH 15, PUSH 25, POP, PUSH 35.

**Model Answer:**

A stack implemented using a linked list maintains a `TOP` pointer that always points to the most recently inserted node (the head of the list). All insertions and deletions occur at the HEAD end.

**Initial State:** `TOP = NULL` (empty stack)

**Step 1: PUSH 5**

$$\text{TOP} \rightarrow [5 \mid \text{NULL}]$$

**Step 2: PUSH 15** — A new node with data 15 is created and inserted at the HEAD.

$$\text{TOP} \rightarrow [15 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [5 \mid \text{NULL}]$$

**Step 3: PUSH 25**

$$\text{TOP} \rightarrow [25 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [15 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [5 \mid \text{NULL}]$$

**Step 4: POP** — The node with data 25 (the top) is removed. The value returned is **25**.

$$\text{TOP} \rightarrow [15 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [5 \mid \text{NULL}]$$

**Step 5: PUSH 35** — A new node with data 35 is inserted at the HEAD.

$$\text{TOP} \rightarrow [35 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [15 \mid \cdot] \rightarrow [5 \mid \text{NULL}]$$

**Final Linked List:** The stack now contains 35 (top), 15, 5 (bottom).

---

**Q4. (b) [7 Marks]** Write the algorithm to insert an element at the rear of a queue implemented using a linked list. Explain the special case when the queue is initially empty. What is the time complexity?

**Model Answer:**

**Algorithm: ENQUEUE (Insert at REAR)**

```c
void enqueue(struct Node **front, struct Node **rear, int value) {
    // Step 1: Allocate new node
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    
    // Step 2: Check overflow
    if (newNode == NULL) {
        printf("Memory Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    
    // Step 3: Fill data fields
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = NULL;
    
    // Step 4: If queue is empty
    if (*rear == NULL) {
        *front = *rear = newNode;
        return;
    }
    
    // Step 5: Link new node at end and update REAR
    (*rear)->next = newNode;
    *rear = newNode;
}
```

**Special Case — Initially Empty Queue:**

When the queue is empty, both `FRONT` and `REAR` are `NULL`. If we directly set `(*rear)->next = newNode`, it would cause a **NULL pointer dereference** and a segmentation fault. Therefore, the special case check `if (*rear == NULL)` is essential. In this case, both `FRONT` and `REAR` are initialized to point to the new node, since the single element is both the first and the last.

**Time Complexity: O(1)** — All operations (allocation, assignment, pointer updates) take constant time, independent of queue size.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to handle empty queue in DEQUEUE** — Not setting `REAR = NULL` when `FRONT` becomes `NULL` leads to a dangling pointer. **[-2 marks]**
> 2. **Forgetting to handle empty queue in ENQUEUE** — Attempting to dereference `(*rear)->next` when `REAR == NULL` causes a runtime crash. **[-2 marks]**
> 3. **Using `free()` incorrectly or omitting it entirely** — Causes memory leaks, deducted heavily in KTU 2024 evaluations. **[-1 to -2 marks]**
> 4. **Confusing array and linked list overflow conditions** — Students often write "stack is full" for linked list; the correct answer is "memory unavailable / heap full". **[-1 mark]**
> 5. **Forgetting to save the popped/dequeued data before `free()`** — Once memory is freed, accessing it is undefined behavior. **[-2 marks]**
> 6. **Wrong pointer updates in POP** — Some students incorrectly set `*top = newNode` or fail to advance TOP. **[-2 marks]**
> 7. **Omitting the diagram** — KTU 2024 valuation key awards **2 marks** specifically for the neat diagram of the linked list stack/queue.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

- ✅ **Stack via Linked List** uses **1 pointer (TOP)**; **Queue via Linked List** uses **2 pointers (FRONT, REAR)**.
- ✅ **PUSH** = insert at HEAD of linked list; **POP** = delete from HEAD; both are **O(1)**.
- ✅ **ENQUEUE** = insert at REAR (using `REAR` pointer directly — O(1)); **DEQUEUE** = delete from FRONT (O(1)).
- ✅ **Empty Stack:** `TOP = NULL`; **Empty Queue:** `FRONT = NULL` **AND** `REAR = NULL`.
- ✅ **One-element Queue:** `FRONT = REAR` (same node). Always set `REAR = NULL` after dequeueing the last element to avoid dangling pointer.
- ✅ **Overflow in linked list** = system heap memory exhaustion (`malloc` returns `NULL`), NOT a fixed size limit.
- ✅ **Underflow in linked list** = attempting to POP/DEQUEUE from an empty structure (check `TOP == NULL` or `FRONT == NULL`).
- ✅ **Memory leak prevention:** Always call `free(temp)` after detaching a node from the linked list.
- ✅ **Time complexity of all four core operations (PUSH, POP, ENQUEUE, DEQUEUE) is O(1).**
- ✅ **Space complexity per node:** `O(1)` (constant additional memory regardless of stack/queue size).
- ✅ **Traversal for display** is **O(n)** since linked list does not support random access.
- ✅ **Advantage over array:** Dynamic size, no overflow, efficient memory utilization.
- ✅ **Disadvantage over array:** Extra memory per node for the pointer; no random access; potential memory fragmentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked to "explain linked list representation of stack/queue", always include: **(1) Node structure, (2) Pointer(s) used, (3) Algorithm for primary operations, (4) Edge cases (empty and one-element), (5) Time complexity analysis, (6) A neat diagram.** Covering all six guarantees full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stack implementation and Operations on Stack using Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Stack implementation and Operations on Stack using Linked List # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) A **Stack implemented using a Linked List** is a dynamic, pointer-based realization of the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) abstract data type, where each element (node) contains two fields — a **data field** holding the actual value and a **next pointer field** storing the address of the node beneath it. The topmost element of the stack is tracked using a dedicated pointer variable conventionally named **TOP** (or `head`). Unlike the array-based implementation, the linked-list version has **no predefined size limit**, since nodes are allocated dynamically on the **heap memory** at runtime using functions like `malloc()` or `calloc()`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the linked-list implementation of a stack, all insertions (PUSH) and deletions (POP) are performed only at one end — the head of the linked list. This is because performing insertion/deletion at the head takes **O(1)** time, while doing so at the tail would require **O(n)** traversal to reach the last node. The formal structure of a stack node is mathematically represented as: $$ \text{Node} = \{\ \text{data}\ (\text{of type}\ T), \quad \text{next}\ (\text{pointer to Node})\ \} $$ And the recursive invariant of the linked-list stack is: $$ \text{Stack}(n) = \begin{cases} \text{NULL}, & \text{if}\ n = 0\ \ (\text{empty stack, TOP} = \text{NULL}) \\ \text{Node}(\text{data}_n, \text{Stack}(n-1)), & \text{if}\ n \geq 1 \end{cases} $$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bangles in a Chain" Imagine a **chain of bangles** worn on your wrist, but with a twist — each bangle is **tied with a string to the bangle just below it**. The wrist acts as the **TOP** of the stack. Now: - **Adding a new bangle (PUSH):** You slide a new bangle onto your wrist and tie its string to the *current topmost bangle*. The new bangle becomes the TOP. The number of knots (links) grows. - **Removing a bangle (POP):** You untie the string of the TOP bangle from the bangle below it, slide it off, and free the string. The bangle beneath becomes the new TOP. - **Empty state:** Your wrist is bare — `TOP = NULL`. This analogy precisely captures the **LIFO discipline** and the **pointer-link (string) mechanism** that defines a linked-list stack. ## 1.3 Why Use a Linked List Instead of an Array? > [!NOTE] > **Core Reasons (KTU Board Exam Favorite):** > 1. **No Overflow** — The stack can grow until the system heap memory is exhausted (`Stack Overflow` occurs only when heap is full). > 2. **No Wasted Memory** — Exactly as much memory as needed is allocated, in contrast to arrays where unused slots waste space. > 3. **Efficient Memory Utilization** — Nodes may be scattered in memory; only the pointer chain maintains logical order. > 4. **Dynamic Resizing** — Size automatically adjusts as elements are pushed or popped. The trade-off is an **extra `next` pointer per node** (typically **8 bytes** on a 64-bit system) and **no direct random access** (no index-based lookup). ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Memory Footprint | Metric | Value (Symbol) | Typical Magnitude | |---|---|---| | **Pointer size (next)** | $S_p$ | **8 bytes** (64-bit) or **4 bytes** (32-bit) | | **Data size** | $S_d$ | Depends on type (e.g., **4 bytes** for `int`) | | **Total node size** | $S_{\text{node}} = S_d + S_p$ | **12 bytes** (32-bit) / **16 bytes** (64-bit, aligned) | | **Initial TOP value** | — | **NULL** (empty stack) | | **Minimum stack size** | $n_{\min}$ | **0** nodes | | **Maximum stack size** | $n_{\max}$ | Limited only by available heap memory | # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Node Structure — The Building Block Every element in a linked-list stack is encapsulated inside a **self-referential structure** (a structure containing a pointer to its own type): ```c struct Node { int data; // Information payload struct Node* next; // Pointer to the node below }; ``` The phrase **self-referential** is critical: the `next` field points to another `struct Node`. This is what enables the chain to extend indefinitely (subject to memory). ## 2.2 The TOP Pointer — The Stack's "Head" A single global or class-level pointer `TOP` (also called `head`) maintains the identity of the stack's topmost element. The state of the stack is fully captured by this pointer: - `TOP == NULL` ⇒ **Empty Stack** - `TOP ≠ NULL` ⇒ **Non-empty Stack**; the chain is `TOP → TOP->next → TOP->next->next → … → NULL` The fundamental invariant maintained by all operations is: $$ \boxed{\ \text{PUSH adds at HEAD};\quad \text{POP removes from HEAD}\ } $$ This invariant is what gives every core operation its **constant-time O(1) complexity**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Complexity Sheet | Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space Used | Pre-condition | Post-condition | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **PUSH** | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ extra | None (always valid) | TOP points to new node | | **POP** | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ extra | Stack is non-empty | Old top freed, new top below | | **PEEK / TOP** | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Stack is non-empty | No structural change | | **isEmpty** | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | None | Returns boolean | | **DISPLAY / Traverse** | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(1)$ | None | Prints from TOP to bottom | | **Total memory for n items** | — | — | — | $n \cdot S_{\text{node}}$ | — | Plus 1 pointer for TOP | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for Board Exams:** *All mutations of a linked-list stack (PUSH/POP) are O(1). Only DISPLAY/SEARCH is O(n) — because the chain must be walked.* This is **inverted** from array implementation, where PUSH/POP can be O(n) due to resizing/shifting. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World Linked-list stacks power several production-grade systems: - **Operating System Kernel Call Stacks:** Function frames are dynamically pushed and popped; their unpredictable depth mandates heap allocation. - **Web Browser Back-Button History:** Each visited URL is a dynamically-allocated node; the user may visit arbitrarily many pages. - **Undo/Redo Mechanisms in Text Editors** (e.g., VS Code, MS Word): Each state change is a node. - **Compiler Recursion Handling:** The call stack grows with recursion depth — array-based stacks risk overflow on deep recursion; linked-list stacks adapt. - **Memory-Constrained Embedded Systems:** Where the maximum stack depth is unpredictable, linked-list allocation is safer than fixed arrays. ## 2.5 Failure & Edge-Case Inventory (Examiner's Hot-Spot) | Scenario | Detection | Consequence if Ignored | |---|---|---| | **POP on empty stack** (Underflow) | `TOP == NULL` | Segmentation fault / invalid dereference | | **PUSH when heap is exhausted** (Overflow) | `malloc` returns `NULL` | Cannot grow stack; must be checked | | **Dangling pointer** | POP without `free()` | **Memory leak** (severe in long-running systems) | | **Lost chain** | Forgetting `TOP = TOP->next` before `free` | Stack becomes permanently unreachable | # SECTION_3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 The PUSH Operation — Exhaustive Algorithmic Derivation **Objective:** Insert a new element `x` at the top of the stack. ### Mathematical Specification Given a stack $S$ with $n$ elements ($n \geq 0$) and a value $x$, the post-condition is a stack $S'$ with $n+1$ elements such that: $$ \text{top}(S') = x, \quad \text{and} \quad \text{rest}(S') = \text{top}(S) $$ ### Step-by-Step Algorithm **Step 1: Allocate a new node on the heap.** ```c struct Node* newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); ``` Memory address returned: $a_{\text{new}}$. Size requested: $S_{\text{node}}$ bytes. **Step 2: Check for heap exhaustion (Overflow guard).** If `malloc` returns `NULL`, the system is out of memory. The operation must abort gracefully: ```c if (newNode == NULL) { printf("Stack Overflow (Heap Exhausted)\n"); return; } ``` **Step 3: Assign the data payload.** $$ \text{newNode}\rightarrow \text{data} = x $$ **Step 4: Link the new node to the current TOP.** This is the *linking step* — what makes it a stack: $$ \text{newNode}\rightarrow \text{next} = \text{TOP} $$ If the stack was empty, `TOP` is `NULL`, so the new node's `next` correctly points to nothing — preserving the empty-tail invariant. **Step 5: Update TOP to point to the new node.** $$ \text{TOP} = \text{newNode} $$ **Step 6: (Optional) Increment the size counter.** $$ \text{size} \leftarrow \text{size} + 1 $$ ### Complexity Justification Each step is a constant-time operation: $$ T_{\text{PUSH}} = T_{\text{malloc}} + T_{\text{check}} + T_{\text{assign}} + T_{\text{link}} + T_{\text{update}} = O(1) $$ ## 3.2 The POP Operation — Exhaustive Algorithmic Derivation **Objective:** Remove and return the topmost element of the stack. ### Mathematical Specification Given a stack $S$ with $n \geq 1$ elements, after POP, $S'$ has $n - 1$ elements with: $$ \text{top}(S') = \text{second}(\text{top}(S)) $$ ### Step-by-Step Algorithm **Step 1: Check for underflow (empty stack).** ```c if (TOP == NULL) { printf("Stack Underflow (Empty Stack)\n"); return -1; // or a sentinel } ``` **Step 2: Cache the current TOP node in a temporary pointer `temp`.** ```c struct Node* temp = TOP; ``` This is essential — **we must remember the address before we move TOP, otherwise we lose the chain**. **Step 3: Save the data to return.** ```c int poppedValue = temp->data; ``` **Step 4: Move TOP down to the next node.** $$ \text{TOP} = \text{TOP}\rightarrow \text{next} $$ This severs the logical link from the old top. **Step 5: Free the temporary node (return memory to heap).** ```c free(temp); ``` **Step 6: Decrement size.** $$ \text{size} \leftarrow \text{size} - 1 $$ **Step 7: Return the saved value.** ```c return poppedValue; ``` ### Complexity Justification $$ T_{\text{POP}} = T_{\text{check}} + T_{\text{cache}} + T_{\text{read}} + T_{\text{move}} + T_{\text{free}} = O(1) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Order matters in POP:** The sequence `cache → read → move TOP → free` is **non-negotiable**. If you `free(temp)` *before* reading `temp->data`, you trigger **undefined behavior** (use-after-free).
