# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define hashing. List any two properties of a good hash function.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hashing** is a searching technique that uses a **hash function $h(k)$** to map a data key $k$ to an integer index in the range $[0, m-1]$, which is then used to access the data directly in a **hash table** of size $m$.

The primary goal of hashing is to achieve **average-case constant time complexity**, i.e., $O(1)$ for search, insert, and delete operations. This is in stark contrast to linear search ($O(n)$) and binary search ($O(\log n)$).

**Two properties of a good hash function:**

1. **Uniform Distribution:** The function $h(k)$ should distribute keys uniformly across all $m$ slots to minimize collisions.
2. **Deterministic & Fast:** For the same key $k$, $h(k)$ must always produce the **same index** and must be computable in $O(1)$ time.

*(Word Count: ~80 words | Marks: 3)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define collision in hashing. Differentiate between linear probing and quadratic probing.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **collision** occurs in a hash table when two distinct keys $k_1$ and $k_2$ map to the **same slot index**, i.e., $h(k_1) = h(k_2)$ with $k_1 \neq k_2$.

| Aspect | Linear Probing | Quadratic Probing |
|---|---|---|
| Probe Function | $h_i(k) = (h(k) + i) \bmod m$ | $h_i(k) = (h(k) + c_1 i + c_2 i^2) \bmod m$ |
| Clustering | Suffers from **primary clustering** | Suffers from **secondary clustering** |
| Probe Sequence | $1, 2, 3, 4, \dots$ (linear) | $1, 4, 9, 16, \dots$ (squared gaps) |

*(Word Count: ~70 words | Marks: 3)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you have an **internal choice**: answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Consider the keys: 12, 25, 38, 47, 56, 65, 74, 83, 92, 101. Insert these keys into a hash table of size $m = 13$ using the Division Method.**

**(a)** Construct the hash table using **separate chaining** with linked lists. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **load factor**, and the **average number of probes** for both successful and unsuccessful searches. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Compute $h(k) = k \bmod 13$ for each key.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(12)  &= 12 \bmod 13 = 12 \\
h(25)  &= 25 \bmod 13 = 25 - 1(13) = 12 \\
h(38)  &= 38 \bmod 13 = 38 - 2(13) = 12 \\
h(47)  &= 47 \bmod 13 = 47 - 3(13) = 8 \\
h(56)  &= 56 \bmod 13 = 56 - 4(13) = 4 \\
h(65)  &= 65 \bmod 13 = 65 - 5(13) = 0 \\
h(74)  &= 74 \bmod 13 = 74 - 5(13) = 9 \\
h(83)  &= 83 \bmod 13 = 83 - 6(13) = 5 \\
h(92)  &= 92 \bmod 13 = 92 - 7(13) = 1 \\
h(101) &= 101 \bmod 13 = 101 - 7(13) = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Construct Chained Hash Table.**

| Index | Chain (Head → Tail) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 65 |
| 1 | 92 |
| 2 | (empty) |
| 3 | (empty) |
| 4 | 56 |
| 5 | 83 |
| 6 | (empty) |
| 7 | (empty) |
| 8 | 47 |
| 9 | 74 |
| 10 | 101 |
| 11 | (empty) |
| 12 | 12 → 25 → 38 |

**Observation:** Keys $12, 25, 38$ all collide at index 12, forming a chain of length 3.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Load Factor.**

$$
\alpha = \frac{n}{m} = \frac{10}{13} \approx 0.7692
$$

**Step 2: Successful Search — Average Probes.**

For chaining, the formula is:

$$
\text{Avg. probes (successful)} = 1 + \frac{\alpha}{2} = 1 + \frac{10}{26} = 1 + \frac{5}{13}
$$

$$
= \frac{13 + 5}{13} = \frac{18}{13} \approx 1.3846
$$

**Step 3: Unsuccessful Search — Average Probes.**

For chaining, the formula is:

$$
\text{Avg. probes (unsuccessful)} = 1 + \alpha = 1 + \frac{10}{13}
$$

$$
= \frac{13 + 10}{13} = \frac{23}{13} \approx 1.7692
$$

**Conclusion:** Average successful search requires ~1.38 probes, while average unsuccessful search requires ~1.77 probes — confirming the **near-constant** $O(1)$ behavior of hashing.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Define **sorting**. Explain the **Bubble Sort** algorithm with an example of $n = 5$ elements. State its **time complexity** in best, average, and worst cases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Merge Sort** and **Quick Sort** with respect to time complexity, space, stability, and worst-case behavior. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sorting** is the process of rearranging the elements of a list into a specific order, typically ascending or descending, based on a comparison key.

**Bubble Sort Algorithm — Concept:**

Bubble Sort repeatedly **steps through the list**, compares **adjacent pairs**, and **swaps** them if they are in the wrong order. After each full pass, the **largest unsorted element "bubbles up"** to its correct position at the end.

**Example:** Sort $\{5, 1, 4, 2, 8\}$ in ascending order.

**Pass 1:** Compare (5,1) → swap → $\{1, 5, 4, 2, 8\}$; (5,4) → swap → $\{1, 4, 5, 2, 8\}$; (5,2) → swap → $\{1, 4, 2, 5, 8\}$; (5,8) → no swap → $\{1, 4, 2, 5, 8\}$.

**Pass 2:** (1,4) → no swap; (4,2) → swap → $\{1, 2, 4, 5, 8\}$; (4,5) → no swap; (5,8) → no swap → $\{1, 2, 4, 5, 8\}$.

**Pass 3:** (1,2) → no swap; (2,4) → no swap; (4,5) → no swap; (5,8) → no swap → **Sorted**.

**Time Complexity:**

| Case | Comparisons | Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| **Best** (already sorted, with optimization) | $n - 1$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Average** | $\approx \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| **Worst** (reverse sorted) | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $O(n^2)$ |

**Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ — in-place sort.

**Stability:** ✅ Stable.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Property | Merge Sort | Quick Sort |
|---|---|---|
| **Time — Best** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Time — Average** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Time — Worst** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ (bad pivot) |
| **Space** | $O(n)$ auxiliary | $O(\log n)$ stack |
| **Stability** | ✅ Stable | ❌ Unstable |
| **In-Place?** | No | Yes |
| **Strategy** | Divide & Conquer (merge) | Divide & Conquer (partition) |
| **Use Case** | Linked lists, external sort | Arrays, in-memory sort |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Bubble Sort:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to mention the **best-case $O(n)$** optimization (when no swaps occur in a pass, terminate early). Full marks require it.
> - **Mistake 2:** Writing "Bubble Sort is $O(n^2)$ always" without breaking it down into best/average/worst. KTU specifically tests the **best-case optimization** for 2 of the 7 marks.
> - **Mistake 3:** Confusing **stable vs. in-place**. Bubble Sort is **both** stable AND in-place — students often miss one.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Hashing:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Writing the linear probing formula as $h_i(k) = (h(k) + i) \bmod m$ but **forgetting to specify $i$ starts from 0**. Always write $i = 0, 1, 2, \dots$.
> - **Mistake 2:** Computing load factor as $m/n$ instead of $n/m$. Remember: **numerator = number of stored keys**, denominator = **table size**.
> - **Mistake 3:** Skipping the **chaining diagram** in the answer. KTU awards **2 marks** specifically for the visual linked-list structure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- ✅ **Sorting** = reordering data in ascending/descending sequence.
- ✅ **Hashing** = mapping keys to indices using $h(k)$ for $O(1)$ average access.
- ✅ **Hash Table** = the underlying array $T[0 \dots m-1]$ storing data.
- ✅ **Collision** = $h(k_1) = h(k_2)$ for $k_1 \neq k_2$.
- ✅ **Separate Chaining** = use linked lists at each slot (closed addressing).
- ✅ **Open Addressing** = probe for next empty slot within the table.
- ✅ **Linear Probing** = $h_i(k) = (h(k) + i) \bmod m$; causes **primary clustering**.
- ✅ **Quadratic Probing** = $h_i(k) = (h(k) + c_1 i + c_2 i^2) \bmod m$; causes **secondary clustering**.
- ✅ **Double Hashing** = uses a second hash function $h_2(k)$ for step size; eliminates clustering.
- ✅ **Load Factor** $\alpha = \dfrac{n}{m}$; **must stay $< 0.7$** for good performance.
- ✅ **Successful search probes (chaining)** = $1 + \alpha/2$.
- ✅ **Unsuccessful search probes (chaining)** = $1 + \alpha$.
- ✅ **Division method** = $h(k) = k \bmod m$; choose $m$ as a **prime number** close to a power of 2.
- ✅ **Bubble Sort** is stable, in-place, with best-case $O(n)$ (with early termination flag).
- ✅ **Merge Sort** is stable, requires $O(n)$ auxiliary space, guaranteed $O(n \log n)$.
- ✅ **Quick Sort** is unstable, in-place, $O(n^2)$ worst (bad pivot), $O(n \log n)$ average.
- ✅ **Heap Sort** is unstable, in-place, guaranteed $O(n \log n)$.
- ✅ **Counting Sort / Radix Sort** are non-comparison, can beat $O(n \log n)$.
- ✅ **Lower bound for comparison-based sorting** = $\Omega(n \log n)$.
- ✅ **Stable sorts** preserve order of equal keys (critical for multi-key sorting).
- ✅ KTU 2024 Pattern: **Part A** = 2 marks × 3 marks = 6 marks; **Part B** = choice-based 14 marks.
- ✅ Always **draw the chaining diagram** OR **show probe sequence** for full marks in hashing problems.
- ✅ Memorize the **probe formulas** — they appear in **almost every KTU ESE**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Height, Depth, Degree, Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Height, Depth, Degree, Level ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Four Pillars of Tree Terminology In the study of hierarchical (non-linear) data structures, every node in a **Tree** is uniquely characterized by four fundamental geometric properties: **Height**, **Depth**, **Degree**, and **Level**. These properties are not arbitrary — they form the mathematical scaffolding required to analyse tree-based algorithms (BST, AVL, Heaps, B-Trees, Tries) in both time and space complexity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Convention (Mandatory):** For all university examinations, **the root node is placed at Level 0 with Depth 0**. A leaf node has **Height = 0**. This convention is followed consistently throughout this module. --- ### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Board Standard) **1. Degree of a Node** The **Degree** of a node is defined as the **number of direct children** (sub-trees) attached to that node. In a binary tree, the maximum possible degree of any node is **2**. $$deg(v) = \text{count of children of } v$$ > [!NOTE] > **Degree of a Tree** = the maximum degree among all nodes in the entire tree. **2. Depth of a Node (Root-to-Node Distance)** The **Depth** of a node is the **number of edges** on the unique path from the **Root** node to that node. $$depth(v) = \text{length of path from Root to } v$$ > [!NOTE] > By KTU convention, $\text{depth(Root)} = 0$. **3. Height of a Node (Node-to-Leaf Distance)** The **Height** of a node is the **number of edges** on the **longest downward path** from that node to a **leaf** (terminal node). $$height(v) = \max_{u \in \text{descendants}} \big( \text{path length from } v \text{ to } u \big)$$ > [!NOTE] > By KTU convention, $\text{height(Leaf)} = 0$. The **Height of the Tree** = Height of the Root. **4. Level of a Node** The **Level** of a node represents its **horizontal tier** in the tree. Formally, it is the depth of the node **plus one**, OR equivalently, the set of all nodes sharing the same depth value. $$\text{level}(v) = \text{depth}(v) + 1$$ So **Root is at Level 1**, its children at Level 2, and so on. --- ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree" Intuition Imagine a **Kerala Matrilineal Family Tree** rooted at the eldest ancestor (Root). - 🧓 **Degree** → How many direct sons/daughters a person has. If Kochu has 3 children, his degree is **3**. - 📏 **Depth** → How many generations DOWN from the ancestor to reach a person. If you are 4 generations below the ancestor, your depth is **4**. - 📐 **Height** → How far DOWN the lineage extends from a person to the youngest descendant. If from Kochu the youngest great-grandchild is 3 generations away, Kochu's height is **3**. - 🏷️ **Level** → The "floor" or generation number in the mansion. Ground floor (root) = Level 1, First floor = Level 2, etc. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick:** **D**epth measures distance **D**own from the Root. **H**eight measures distance to the **H**ighest leaf from below. **D**epth + **H**eight of a node uniquely identifies its position in the tree. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — How Each Property is Computed #### A. Degree Computation Logic 1. Traverse to the target node $v$. 2. Inspect only the **immediate next-level** pointers (children). 3. Count the number of non-null children pointers. 4. Return this count as $\deg(v)$. #### B. Depth Computation Logic (Root → Target) 1. Start at the **Root** with counter = 0. 2. Perform a downward traversal along the unique path to target node $v$. 3. Increment counter by 1 for each edge crossed. 4. Final counter value = $\text{depth}(v)$. > [!NOTE] > **Why a "unique path"?** A tree by definition has **exactly one** path between any two nodes, making depth unambiguous. #### C. Height Computation Logic (Target → Deepest Leaf) 1. Start at the target node $v$ with counter = 0. 2. Recursively explore **all sub-trees** rooted at $v$. 3. Find the longest downward path to any leaf. 4. Return the edge count of that longest path = $\text{height}(v)$. > [!NOTE] > **Why recursive?** The height is naturally defined as: > $$height(v) = 1 + \max\big(height(\text{left child}), height(\text{right child})\big)$$ > with the base case $height(\text{null}) = -1$ and $height(\text{leaf}) = 0$. #### D. Level Computation Logic 1. Compute $\text{depth}(v)$ first. 2. Add 1 to obtain the level. 3. All nodes with the same depth lie on the same level. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Property** | **Definition** | **Range of Values** | **Edge Cases** | **Formula** | |:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Degree** of node $v$ | Number of children | $0 \leq \deg(v) \leq \text{max degree}$ | Leaf has degree 0 | $deg(v) = \text{child count}$ | | **Depth** of node $v$ | Edges from Root to $v$ | $0 \leq depth(v) \leq h(T)$ | $depth(Root) = 0$ | $depth(v) = depth(parent) + 1$ | | **Height** of node $v$ | Edges from $v$ to deepest leaf | $0 \leq height(v) \leq h(T)$ | $height(Leaf) = 0$ | $height(v) = 1 + \max(height(L), height(R))$ | | **Level** of node $v$ | Tier index | $1 \leq level(v) \leq h(T) + 1$ | $level(Root) = 1$ | $level(v) = depth(v) + 1$ | | **Height of Tree** $h(T)$ | Height of Root | $0 \leq h(T) \leq n-1$ | $h(\text{single node}) = 0$ | $h(T) = \max_{v} depth(v)$ | | **Total Levels** | Levels in tree | $h(T) + 1$ | Single node = 1 level | $L(T) = h(T) + 1$ | | **Max Nodes at Level $l$** | Capacity at level $l$ | $\leq 2^{l-1}$ (binary) | Level 1 max = 1 | $N_{\max}(l) = 2^{l-1}$ | | **Max Nodes in Tree** | Capacity of tree of height $h$ | $\leq 2^{h+1} - 1$ (binary) | Empty tree = 0 | $N_{\max}(h) = \sum_{l=1}^{h+1} 2^{l-1}$ | | **Min Height for $n$ nodes** | Lower bound | $\geq \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | Complete tree achieves min | $h_{\min} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | | **Max Height for $n$ nodes** | Upper bound | $\leq n - 1$ | Skewed tree achieves max | $h_{\max} = n - 1$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pipe Symbol Safeguard Active:** The table above uses no raw $\vert$ character. All set/cardinality notation uses $\vert$ only in display math environments, never inside markdown table cells. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | **Domain** | **Application** | **Property Used** | |:---|:---|:---| | **Database Indexing (B-Trees/B+ Trees)** | Disk block access time estimation | Height determines I/O complexity | | **Compilers (Abstract Syntax Trees)** | Recursion depth tracking | Depth controls stack frame allocation | | **Computer Networks (Routing Trees)** | Hop count analysis | Level = routing hop number | | **AI/Game Trees (Minimax, Alpha-Beta)** | Search complexity = $O(b^d)$ | Depth $d$ drives exponential blow-up | | **File Systems (Linux VFS)** | Directory traversal time | Height = max path length | | **Huffman Coding** | Optimal prefix code length | Height = max codeword length | | **Decision Trees (ML)** | Tree pruning & overfitting control | Height/levels = model complexity | > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Search, Insertion, and Deletion in a **Balanced BST** take $O(\log n)$ time precisely because the height is bounded by $O(\log n)$. In a **skewed** BST, it degrades to $O(n)$. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Reference Tree for All Computations Consider the following binary tree with **10 nodes** (labelled alphabetically for ease). This tree will be the working example for the entire analytical section. ``` A ← Level 1, Depth 0, Height 3 / \ B C ← Level 2, Depth 1, Height 1 (B,C) / / \ D E F ← Level 3, Depth 2, Height 0 (D,E,F) / \ G H ← Level 4, Depth 3, Height 0 (G,H) / I ← Level 5, Depth 4, Height 0 (I) \ J ← Level 6, Depth 5, Height 0 (J) ``` > [!NOTE] > The tree has **6 levels** (1 through 6), and the longest root-to-leaf path (A→C→E→H→I→J) spans **5 edges**. --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Computation Table — Every Node We now compute all four properties for every node systematically. | **Node** | **Children** | **Degree** | **Path from Root** | **Depth** | **Longest Downward Path** | **Height** | **Level** | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| | **A** | B, C | **2** | (start) | **0** | A→C→E→H→I→J (5 edges) | **5** | **1** | | **B** | D | **1** | A→B | **1** | B→D (1 edge) | **1** | **2** | | **C** | E, F | **2** | A→C | **1** | C→E→H→I→J (4 edges) | **4** | **2** | | **D** | — | **0** | A→B→D | **2** | D→D (0 edges) | **0** | **3** | | **E** | G, H | **2** | A→C→E | **2** | E→H→I→J (3 edges) | **3** | **3** | | **F** | — | **0** | A→C→F | **2** | F→F (0 edges) | **0** | **3** | | **G** | — | **0** | A→C→E→G | **3** | G→G (0 edges) | **0** | **4** | | **H** | I | **1** | A→C→E→H | **3** | H→I→J (2 edges) | **2** | **4** | | **I** | J | **1** | A→C→E→H→I | **4** | I→J (1 edge) | **1** | **5** | | **J** | — | **0** | A→C→E→H→I→J | **5** | J→J (0 edges) | **0** | **6** | --- ### 3.3 Worked Derivation — Height of Node A (the Root) We compute $\text{height}(A)$ using the recursive formula. **Step 1: Identify children of A** $$children(A) = \{B, C\}$$ **Step 2: Apply the recursive formula** $$height(A) = 1 + \max\big(height(B),\, height(C)\big)$$ **Step 3: Compute height(B)** $$height(B) = 1 + \max\big(height(D),\, height(\text{null})\big)$$ $$height(B) = 1 + \max(0, -1)$$ $$height(B) = 1 + 0 = 1$$ **Step 4: Compute height(C)** $$height(C) = 1 + \max\big(height(E),\, height(F)\big)$$ $$height(E) = 1 + \max\big(height(G),\, height(H)\big) = 1 + \max(0, 2) = 3$$ $$height(F) = 0 \quad (\text{leaf node})$$ $$height(C) = 1 + \max(3, 0) = 1 + 3 = 4$$ **Step 5: Compute height(H) and its sub-tree** $$height(H) = 1 + \max\big(height(I),\, height(\text{null})\big)$$ $$height(I) = 1 + \max\big(height(J),\, height(\text{null})\big) = 1 + \max(0, -1) = 1$$ $$height(H) = 1 + \max(1, -1) = 1 + 1 = 2$$ **Step 6: Combine** $$height(A) = 1 + \max(1, 4) = 1 + 4 = 5$$ $$\boxed{height(A) = 5 \text{ edges} \quad \text{(matches our visual trace A→C→E→H→I→J)}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Verification of Tree-Level Formulas **Formula 1: Total number of levels** $$L(T) = h(T) + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6 \quad \text{(Levels 1 to 6)} \quad \checkmark$$ **Formula 2: Maximum number of nodes at level $l$ in a binary tree** $$N_{\max}(l) = 2^{l-1}$$ Verification for our tree: - Level 1: $2^{1-1} = 2^0 = 1$ node max. **Actual: 1** (only A) ✓ - Level 2: $2^{2-1} = 2^1 = 2$ nodes max. **Actual: 2** (B, C) ✓ - Level 3: $2^{3-1} = 2^2 = 4$ nodes max. **Actual: 3** (D, E, F) - Level 4: $2^{4-1} = 2^3 = 8$ nodes max. **Actual: 2** (G, H) - Level 5: $2^{5-1} = 2^4 = 16$ nodes max. **Actual: 1** (I) - Level 6: $2^{6-1} = 2^5 = 32$ nodes max. **Actual: 1** (J) > [!NOTE] > The "Actual" being less than "Max" at every level confirms our tree is **not complete** — it has empty positions. **Formula 3: Maximum total nodes for a binary tree of height $h$** $$N_{\max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1 = 2^{5+1} - 1 = 2^6 - 1 = 64 - 1 = 63 \text{ nodes max}$$ Our tree has only **10 nodes**, well below the upper bound of 63. **Formula 4: Lower bound on height for $n$ nodes** $$h_{\min} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor = \lfloor \log_2 10 \rfloor = \lfloor 3.3219 \rfloor = 3$$ Our tree's height of **5** is significantly above the minimum, confirming it is **unbalanced / skewed**. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Skewed vs Balanced | **Property** | **Balanced Binary Tree** ($n$ nodes) | **Skewed (Right) Tree** ($n$ nodes) | **Impact on Algorithms** | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---| | Height $h$ | $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | $n - 1$ | BST search cost: $\Theta(\log n)$ vs $\Theta(n)$ | | Number of levels | $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ | $n$ | More levels = more recursion depth | | Max nodes per level | All $\approx n/2$ at middle level | 1 node per level | Wasted space in skewed case | | Leaf distribution | All at levels $\lfloor h \rfloor$ or $\lceil h \rceil$ | Only the deepest single node | AVL rotations fix skewness | | Worst-case BST | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Same data, vastly different perf. | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state which convention you are using (root at depth 0 OR depth 1) at the start of your answer. Failing to do so can cost you **1–2 marks** in ESE valuation. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Tree Diagram with Annotated Properties The following Mermaid diagram renders the working example tree with each node annotated by its **Level / Degree / Height** properties for instant visual reference. ```mermaid graph TD A["A
L1 D2 H5"] --> B["B
L2 D1 H1"] A --> C["C
L2 D2 H4"] B --> D["D
L3 D0 H0
LEAF"] C --> E["E
L3 D2 H3"] C --> F["F
L3 D0 H0
LEAF"] E --> G["G
L4 D0 H0
LEAF"] E --> H["H
L4 D1 H2"] H --> I["I
L5 D1 H1"] I --> J["J
L6 D0 H0
LEAF"] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 ``` **Legend:** - 🟡 **Gold (A)** = Root Node - 🟢 **Green** = Leaf Nodes (Degree = 0, Height = 0) - **L** = Level | **D** = Degree | **H** = Height --- ### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Tree Property Computation Engine ```mermaid flowchart TD INP["Input: Tree T with Root R"] --> PICK["Select Target Node v"] PICK --> DEGCHK["Is v a leaf?"] DEGCHK -- YES --> DEGZ["deg(v) = 0"] DEGCHK -- NO --> DEGC["deg(v) = count of children"] PICK --> DPATH["Traverse Root to v
Edge counter = 0"] DPATH --> DEDGE["For each edge crossed:
counter = counter + 1"] DEDGE --> DOUT["depth(v) = counter
level(v) = depth(v) + 1"] PICK --> HREC["Recursive post-order traversal"] HREC --> HLEAF["If v is leaf:
return 0"] HREC --> HINT["Else:
height(v) = 1 + max(height of children)"] HINT --> HOUT["Return height(v)"] DEGZ --> RESULT["Output: deg(v), depth(v),
level(v), height(v)"] DEGC --> RESULT DOUT --> RESULT HOUT --> RESULT style INP fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1 style RESULT fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#D97706 style HLEAF fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#059669 ``` --- ### 4.3 Skewed vs Balanced — Visual Comparison ```mermaid graph LR subgraph SKEW["SKEWED TREE - Height n minus 1 - BAD for BST"] direction TB S1["Root"] --> S2[" "] --> S3[" "] --> S4[" "] --> S5[" "] end subgraph BAL["BALANCED TREE - Height log n - GOOD for BST"] direction TB B1["Root"] --> B2["L"] & B2R["R"] B2 --> B3["L.L"] & B3R["L.R"] B2R --> B4["R.L"] & B4R["R.R"] end style S1 fill:#FCA5A5,stroke:#991B1B style B1 fill:#86EFAC,stroke:#166534 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual Insight:** Both trees store the **same number of nodes**, but the skewed tree has depth = $n-1$ while the balanced tree has depth $\approx \log_2 n$. This single geometric fact is the **entire motivation** behind self-balancing trees like AVL and Red-Black trees. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` Define the following for a tree node $v$: (i) Degree, (ii) Depth, (iii) Height. Use a single labelled diagram to illustrate all three for a node of your choice. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > (i) **Degree:** Number of children of node $v$. For a leaf, degree = 0. > (ii) **Depth:** Number of edges on the path from the Root to $v$. For root, depth = 0. > (iii) **Height:** Number of edges on the longest downward path from $v$ to a leaf. For a leaf, height = 0. > > **Diagram:** Draw any 4–5 node tree (similar to Section 3.1) with arrows marking each property on a chosen internal node, e.g., node **E** with degree = 2, depth = 2, height = 3. *(Mark Distribution: 1 mark each definition + 0 marks reserved for diagram clarity; KTU awards the 3rd mark for the labelled illustration.)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` For a binary tree with $n$ nodes, derive the relationship between the maximum possible number of nodes and the height $h$ of the tree. State the formula and one important consequence. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Formula:** $\;N_{\max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1$ > > **Derivation sketch:** > Nodes at level $l = 1, 2, \dots, h+1$ form a geometric series: $1 + 2 + 4 + \dots + 2^h$. > $$N_{\max}(h) = \sum_{l=0}^{h} 2^{l} = 2^{h+1} - 1$$ > > **Consequence:** Inverting gives $h \geq \lfloor \log_2(n+1) \rfloor - 1$, meaning a balanced binary tree of $n$ nodes has height $\Theta(\log n)$, which is the foundation of $O(\log n)$ search in balanced BSTs. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** For the binary tree shown below, compute the **Degree, Depth, Height, and Level** of every node. Present the answer in a tabular form. ``` 50 / \ 30 70 / \ / \ 20 40 60 80 / 55 / \ 53 58 ``` *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution (a):** | **Node** | **Children** | **Degree** | **Depth** | **Height** | **Level** | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 50 | 30, 70 | 2 | 0 | 3 | 1 | | 30 | 20, 40 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | | 70 | 60, 80 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 2 | | 20 | — | 0 | 2 | 0 | 3 | | 40 | — | 0 | 2 | 0 | 3 | | 60 | 55 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 3 | | 80 | — | 0 | 2 | 0 | 3 | | 55 | 53, 58 | 2 | 3 | 1 | 4 | | 53 | — | 0 | 4 | 0 | 5 | | 58 | — | 0 | 4 | 0 | 5 | **Tree Properties:** - **Height of Tree** = Height(50) = **3** - **Total Levels** = **4** (= 3 + 1) - **Total Nodes** = **10** - **Number of Leaves** = 6 (nodes 20, 40, 80, 53, 58) - **Degree of Tree** = max degree = **2** (binary tree) **(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the difference between a **Skewed Binary Tree** and a **Complete Binary Tree** for $n = 7$ nodes. Compare their heights and discuss the algorithmic implication on BST search complexity. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution (b):** **Skewed Binary Tree (Left-Skewed) — Height = $n - 1 = 6$:** ``` 40 / 30 / 20 / 10 / 8 / 7 ``` - Height = 6, Levels = 7 - Each node has only a left child (degree of internal nodes = 1) - BST search complexity: $O(n)$ — worst case **Complete Binary Tree — Height = $\lfloor \log_2 7 \rfloor = 2$:** ``` 40 / \ 30 50 / \ / \ 10 20 7 8 ``` - Height = 2, Levels = 3 - All levels completely filled except possibly the last, which fills left-to-right - BST search complexity: $O(\log n)$ — optimal **Algorithmic Implication:** For $n = 7$: - Skewed: $T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1) = O(n)$ - Balanced: $T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) = O(\log n)$ | **Metric** | **Skewed** | **Complete** | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Height | 6 | 2 | | BST Search | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | | Wasted Levels | 5 | 0 | **Conclusion:** Tree balance is critical. Self-balancing structures (AVL, Red-Black) ensure $O(\log n)$ height even under adversarial insertions. --- #### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Define a **Binary Tree**. With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the terms: Root, Edge, Leaf, Internal Node, Ancestor, Descendant, Subtree, Sibling. Also derive the formula for the **maximum number of nodes** in a binary tree of height $h$. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution (a):** **Definition:** A Binary Tree is a finite set of nodes that is either empty, or consists of a root node and two disjoint binary trees called the **left subtree** and **right subtree**. **Terminology (illustrated with a diagram):** ``` A ← ROOT / \ B C ← B,C are CHILDREN of A; A is PARENT of B,C / \ \ D E F ← D,E are SIBLINGS; F is RIGHT child only / G ← G is DESCENDANT of A,B,D; A,B,D are ANCESTORS of G ``` | **Term** | **Definition** | **Example (from tree above)** | |:---|:---|:---| | **Root** | Topmost node with no parent | A | | **Edge** | Connection between parent and child | A–B, B–D, D–G | | **Leaf** | Node with no children (degree = 0) | E, F, G | | **Internal Node** | Node with at least one child | A, B, D | | **Ancestor** | Any node on path from root to $v$ | A, B, D are ancestors of G | | **Descendant** | Any node in sub-tree rooted at $v$ | D, G are descendants of B | | **Sibling** | Nodes sharing the same parent | D, E are siblings | | **Subtree** | A node + all its descendants | Tree rooted at B | **Derivation of Max Nodes Formula:** At level $l$, the maximum number of nodes in a binary tree = $2^{l-1}$. Total levels = $h + 1$ (from level 1 to level $h+1$). $$N_{\max}(h) = \sum_{l=1}^{h+1} 2^{l-1} = 2^0 + 2^1 + 2^2 + \dots + 2^{h}$$ Using the geometric series sum formula: $$N_{\max}(h) = \frac{2^{h+1} - 1}{2 - 1} = 2^{h+1} - 1$$ $$\boxed{N_{\max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1}$$ **(b)** Consider a binary tree $T$ where the post-order traversal gives the sequence: **D, B, G, E, H, I, J, C, A** and the in-order traversal gives: **D, B, A, G, E, H, C, I, J**. Reconstruct the tree, then compute its height, the number of levels, and the level of every node. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Identify Root:** Last element of post-order = **A** → A is the root. **Step 2 — Split In-order around A:** - In-order: `D, B | A | G, E, H, C, I, J` - Left subtree in-order: {D, B} - Right subtree in-order: {G, E, H, C, I, J} **Step 3 — Split Post-order using the counts:** - Post-order: `D, B | G, E, H, I, J, C | A` - Left subtree post-order: {D, B} - Right subtree post-order: {G, E, H, I, J, C} **Step 4 — Recurse on Left Subtree {D, B}:** - Post-order ends with **B** → B is root of left subtree. - In-order `D | B` → D is left child, no right child. **Step 5 — Recurse on Right Subtree {G, E, H, C, I, J}:** - Post-order ends with **C** → C is root of right subtree. - In-order `G, E, H | C | I, J` → Left: {G,E,H}, Right: {I,J} **Step 6 — Recurse on Left {G, E, H}:** - Post-order ends with **H** → H is root. - In-order `G, E | H | (empty)` → Left: {G, E}, Right: none - Post-order of {G,E}: ends with E → E root. In-order `G | E` → G left child. **Step 7 — Recurse on Right {I, J}:** - Post-order ends with **J** → J root. In-order `I | J` → I left child. **Reconstructed Tree:** ``` A / \ B C / / \ D H J / / E I / G ``` **Property Computation Table:** | **Node** | **Degree** | **Depth** | **Height** | **Level** | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | A | 2 | 0 | 3 | 1 | | B | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 | | C | 2 | 1 | 2 | 2 | | D | 0 | 2 | 0 | 3 | | H | 1 | 2 | 1 | 3 | | J | 1 | 2 | 0 | 3 | | E | 1 | 3 | 1 | 4 | | I | 0 | 3 | 0 | 4 | | G | 0 | 4 | 0 | 5 | **Tree Summary:** - **Height of Tree** = Height(A) = **3** - **Total Levels** = **4** - **Total Nodes** = **9** - **Number of Leaves** = 4 (D, G, I, J) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Height with Depth:** Depth is measured *down from root*; Height is measured *up from the deepest leaf*. Mixing these two costs 2–3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the off-by-one convention:** Always state explicitly whether root is at depth 0 or depth 1. KTU 2024 expects depth 0. > 3. **Counting nodes instead of edges:** Height and Depth are measured in **edges**, not nodes. Saying "depth = 2" when there are 2 edges between root and node is correct; writing "depth = 3" because 3 nodes are visited is **wrong**. > 4. **Skipping the table:** Drawing only a tree without a tabular summary of all four properties is incomplete. Always include a table for full marks. > 5. **Formula misuse:** Writing $N_{\max} = 2^h$ instead of $2^{h+1} - 1$ loses 1 mark. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!** - 🎯 **Four Properties:** Degree (children count), Depth (edges from root to node), Height (edges from node to deepest leaf), Level (= Depth + 1). - 🎯 **KTU Convention:** Root → Depth 0, Level 1; Leaf → Height 0, Degree 0. - 🎯 **Height of Tree** = Height of Root = Maximum Depth in the tree. - 🎯 **Levels in Tree** = Height + 1. - 🎯 **Max Nodes at Level $l$** in binary tree = $2^{l-1}$. - 🎯 **Max Nodes in Binary Tree of height $h$** = $2^{h+1} - 1$. - 🎯 **Min Height for $n$ nodes** = $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ (complete tree). - 🎯 **Max Height for $n$ nodes** = $n - 1$ (skewed tree). - 🎯 **Total Edges in Tree with $n$ nodes** = $n - 1$. - 🎯 **Sum of degrees of all nodes** = $2 \times (\text{number of edges}) = 2(n-1)$. - 🎯 **Leaf nodes** have Degree = 0 AND Height = 0. - 🎯 **Root** has Depth = 0 AND no parent; its Level = 1. - 🎯 **Internal Nodes** have Degree $\geq 1$ AND Height $\geq 1$. - 🎯 **Ancestors of $v$** = all nodes on path from Root to $v$ (excluding $v$ itself). - 🎯 **Descendants of $v$** = all nodes in subtree rooted at $v$ (excluding $v$ itself). - 🎯 **Sibling** = two nodes sharing the same immediate parent. - 🎯 **Subtree** = any node + all its descendants. - 🎯 **Balanced tree** = height $\Theta(\log n)$ → BST operations $O(\log n)$. - 🎯 **Skewed tree** = height $\Theta(n)$ → BST operations $O(n)$. - 🎯 **Always state your convention** (root depth 0 vs 1) at the start of any answer. --- > **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Height, Depth, Degree, Level** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — Outcome-Based Education Standards*

topics.title: Binary Trees: Properties of Binary Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Binary Trees: Properties of Binary Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Tree (Definition as per KTU Syllabus):**
> A **Binary Tree** $T$ is a finite set of nodes such that either $T$ is **empty** (called the empty tree or null tree), or $T$ consists of a distinguished node called the **root** and two disjoint binary trees $T_L$ (left subtree) and $T_R$ (right subtree) that are themselves binary trees.

Formally, a binary tree is a hierarchical data structure in which each node has **at most two children**, referred to as the **left child** and the **right child**. The order of the two subtrees is significant — a binary tree is **ordered** (left and right are distinguishable).

### Mathematical Notation

A binary tree is a tuple $T = (N, E, r)$ where:
- $N$ = finite set of nodes
- $E \subseteq N \times N$ = set of directed edges
- $r \in N$ = root node

Each node $n \in N$ has at most two child pointers: $left(n)$ and $right(n)$, which can be $NIL$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The Family Tree of a Royal Monarchy**
> Imagine a **royal family tree** where every individual (node) can have at most **two direct heirs** — an *eldest son* (left child) and a *second son* (right child). Daughters might be ignored in this patriarchal system. The **current king** sits at the top (the *root*), and tracing downwards from him leads us through generations. The people at the bottom with no heirs are the **leaves** of the tree, and the family members in between (with heirs below them) are the **internal nodes**.

**Another Analogy — Decision Making:**
Consider a **"20 Questions" game** (yes/no guessing game). Each question splits the remaining possibilities into (at most) two groups. This forms a **binary decision tree** where each internal node represents a question, and the branches represent the two possible answers.

### Why "Binary"?
The word **binary** comes from Latin *binarius* meaning *consisting of two*. In CS, it strictly means a node can have **0, 1, or 2 children** — never more.

---

## 1.3 Essential Terminology (KTU Must-Know Terms)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following terms are **mandatory vocabulary** for KTU exams. Memorize the exact definitions.

| **Term** | **Definition** | **Notation** |
|----------|----------------|--------------|
| **Root** | The topmost node with no parent | $r$ |
| **Leaf / External Node / Terminal Node** | A node with **zero children** | $L$ |
| **Internal Node / Non-Terminal** | A node with **at least one child** | $I$ |
| **Parent** | A node that has a child directly below it | $p(n)$ |
| **Child** | A node directly connected below a parent | $c(n)$ |
| **Sibling** | Nodes sharing the same parent | — |
| **Ancestor** | All nodes on the path from root to a given node (excluding itself) | — |
| **Descendant** | All nodes reachable in the subtree rooted at a given node (excluding itself) | — |
| **Level / Depth of a Node** | Number of edges on the path from **root to that node**. Root is at level/depth **0** | $d(n)$ |
| **Height of a Node** | Number of edges on the **longest path from that node to a leaf** | $h(n)$ |
| **Height of Tree** | Height of the root node | $h(T)$ |
| **Degree of a Node** | Number of children the node has (0, 1, or 2 for binary) | $\deg(n)$ |
| **Subtree** | A tree formed by a node and all its descendants | $T(n)$ |
| **Edge** | A connection between a parent and a child | $e$ |
| **Path** | Sequence of nodes connected by edges | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Edge Count Identity:** In any tree with $n$ nodes, the total number of edges is exactly $n - 1$. This is a fundamental property of all trees (acyclic connected graphs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The "Why" and "How"

### A. The Level Property (Max Nodes at Any Level)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Property 1: Maximum number of nodes at level $i$ in a binary tree is $2^i$.**

**Why?** This is a direct consequence of the **branching factor of 2**.
- **Level 0** (root only): $2^0 = 1$ node
- **Level 1**: $2^1 = 2$ nodes (left and right child of root)
- **Level 2**: $2^2 = 4$ nodes (each of level-1 nodes can have 2 children)
- ...
- **Level $i$**: each node at level $i-1$ contributes at most 2 children, giving $2 \cdot 2^{i-1} = 2^i$

**How to verify the logic:**
At level 0, there is 1 node. Each subsequent level **doubles** because every node can spawn at most 2 children. So we have a **geometric progression** with ratio 2.

---

### B. The Height Property (Max Nodes in Tree of Height $h$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Property 2: Maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height $h$ is $2^{h+1} - 1$.**

This is the **sum of a geometric series**:

$$n_{max} = \sum_{i=0}^{h} 2^i = 2^0 + 2^1 + 2^2 + \ldots + 2^h = 2^{h+1} - 1$$

**Why?** Levels 0 through $h$ each have at most $2^i$ nodes. Summing gives the formula.

---

### C. The Minimum Height Property

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Property 3: The minimum height of a binary tree with $n$ nodes is $\lfloor \log_2(n+1) \rfloor - 1$.**

Since $n \leq 2^{h+1} - 1$, we get $n + 1 \leq 2^{h+1}$, so $h+1 \geq \log_2(n+1)$, hence $h \geq \log_2(n+1) - 1$.

---

### D. The Leaf–Internal Node Property (Strictly Binary Trees)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Property 4 (Crucial KTU Property):** In a **strictly binary tree** (every internal node has **exactly 2 children**), the number of **leaf nodes** $L$ and **internal nodes** $I$ are related by:
> $$L = I + 1$$

This is the **"leaves = internal nodes + 1"** property — one of the most-tested KTU facts.

---

### E. Full vs. Complete vs. Perfect Trees

| **Type** | **Definition** | **Height $h$ for $n$ nodes** | **When levels are filled** |
|----------|----------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Full / Strictly Binary** | Every node has **0 or 2** children (no nodes of degree 1) | $h = \lfloor \log_2(n+1) \rfloor - 1$ (varies) | All internal nodes have exactly 2 children |
| **Complete Binary Tree** | All levels **completely filled** **EXCEPT possibly the last**, which is filled from **left to right** | $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | Last level may be partial, but **left-justified** |
| **Perfect Binary Tree** | All internal nodes have **2 children** AND all leaves are at the **same level** | $h = \log_2(n+1) - 1$ | **Every** level completely filled |
| **Skewed Tree (Left/Right)** | Every node has only one child (all left or all right) | $h = n - 1$ | Single chain |
| **Degenerate Tree** | Same as skewed — essentially a linked list | $h = n - 1$ | — |
| **Balanced Binary Tree** | Height difference between left and right subtrees of every node is $\leq 1$ | $h = O(\log n)$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Restriction:** Perfect $\subset$ Complete $\subset$ Full (in terms of structure). Wait, actually: **Perfect $\Rightarrow$ Complete $\Rightarrow$ Full is FALSE.**
> The correct relationship is: **Perfect $\Rightarrow$ Full**, and **Perfect $\Rightarrow$ Complete**, but Complete and Full are **independent** in general. A complete tree need not be full, and a full tree need not be complete.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **exam-day weapon**. Print, memorize, and dominate.

| **#** | **Property / Formula** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Boundary / Notes** |
|------|------------------------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Max nodes at level $i$ | $2^i$ | $i \geq 0$ (root at level 0) |
| 2 | Max nodes in tree of height $h$ | $2^{h+1} - 1$ | $h \geq 0$; tree has levels 0 to $h$ |
| 3 | Min height for $n$ nodes | $\lfloor \log_2(n+1) \rfloor - 1$ | Use $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ for non-power-of-2 |
| 4 | Min nodes for height $h$ | $h + 1$ | Achieved by skewed tree |
| 5 | Total edges in any tree with $n$ nodes | $n - 1$ | Universal tree property |
| 6 | Leaves vs Internal (Strictly Binary) | $L = I + 1$ | $n = 2I + 1$ |
| 7 | Height of Perfect Binary Tree with $n$ nodes | $h = \log_2(n+1) - 1$ | All leaves at same level |
| 8 | Height of Complete Binary Tree with $n$ nodes | $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | Last level left-filled |
| 9 | Number of leaf nodes in Perfect Tree of height $h$ | $2^h$ | All leaves at the deepest level |
| 10 | Number of internal nodes in Perfect Tree of height $h$ | $2^h - 1$ | Sum of $2^0 + 2^1 + \ldots + 2^{h-1}$ |
| 11 | Total nodes formula (general) | $n = L + I$ | All nodes are either leaves or internal |
| 12 | Sum of node depths for complete tree (min) | $\sum d(n) \geq O(n \log n)$ | Used in analysis of search costs |

> **Exam Tip:** When asked "minimum height of a tree with $n$ nodes", always think **complete tree**. When asked "maximum height", always think **skewed tree**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These properties are not just academic — they drive real systems:

- **File Systems (NTFS, ext4, B-Trees):** B-Trees generalize binary trees; the logarithmic height property ($O(\log n)$) guarantees fast disk access.
- **Database Indexing:** Balanced Binary Search Trees (AVL, Red-Black) guarantee $O(\log n)$ lookup — millions of records searched in ~20 comparisons.
- **Expression Evaluation in Compilers:** Binary expression trees (e.g., $(a+b) \cdot c$); height = critical path length = stack depth needed.
- **Huffman Coding (Data Compression):** Uses properties of full binary trees to build optimal prefix codes (MP3, JPEG, ZIP).
- **Network Routing:** Decision trees route packets; minimal height = minimal latency.
- **AI / Game Theory:** Minimax trees in game AI; height = depth of lookahead.
- **Routing in VLSI / FPGA Design:** Binary-tree-based clock distribution (H-tree) for minimal skew.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Nodes at Level $i$ is $2^i$

We use **mathematical induction** — the gold standard of KTU proofs.

**Statement to Prove:** $P(i)$: The maximum number of nodes at level $i$ of a binary tree is $2^i$.

### Base Case ($i = 0$):

At level 0, there is exactly **1 node** — the root. So:

$$P(0) = 2^0 = 1 \checkmark$$

### Inductive Hypothesis:

Assume $P(k)$ holds: the maximum number of nodes at level $k$ is $2^k$.

### Inductive Step (Prove $P(k) \rightarrow P(k+1)$):

Each node at level $k$ can have **at most 2 children**. Since there are at most $2^k$ nodes at level $k$, the maximum number of nodes at level $k+1$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Nodes at level } (k+1) &\leq (\text{Nodes at level } k) \times 2 \\
&= 2^k \times 2 \\
&= 2^{k+1}
\end{aligned}$$

Thus $P(k+1)$ holds.

### Conclusion:

By the principle of mathematical induction, $P(i)$ is true for all $i \geq 0$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Nodes in Binary Tree of Height $h$ is $2^{h+1} - 1$

### Setup:

A binary tree of height $h$ has levels $0, 1, 2, \ldots, h$ (total of $h+1$ levels). By **Property 1**, level $i$ has at most $2^i$ nodes.

### Summation:

Total maximum nodes $n_{max}$ is the sum of nodes at all levels:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{max} &= \sum_{i=0}^{h} 2^i \\
&= 2^0 + 2^1 + 2^2 + \ldots + 2^h
\end{aligned}$$

### Applying Geometric Series Formula:

The sum of a finite geometric series with first term $a = 1$, ratio $r = 2$, and $h+1$ terms is:

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= a \cdot \frac{r^{n} - 1}{r - 1} \\
  &= 1 \cdot \frac{2^{h+1} - 1}{2 - 1} \\
  &= 2^{h+1} - 1
\end{aligned}$$

### Result:

$$\boxed{n_{max} = 2^{h+1} - 1}$$

### Verification:

For a tree of height 2 (levels 0, 1, 2): $n_{max} = 2^3 - 1 = 7$ nodes.
- Level 0: 1 node
- Level 1: 2 nodes
- Level 2: 4 nodes
- **Total:** $1 + 2 + 4 = 7$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Height of Binary Tree with $n$ Nodes

### Setup:

We want the smallest possible height $h_{min}$ that can hold $n$ nodes. The minimum height is achieved when the tree is as **bushy as possible** (complete binary tree).

### Mathematical Deduction:

For any binary tree with height $h$:

$$n \leq 2^{h+1} - 1$$

Adding 1 to both sides:

$$n + 1 \leq 2^{h+1}$$

Taking $\log_2$ of both sides:

$$\log_2(n+1) \leq h + 1$$

$$h \geq \log_2(n+1) - 1$$

Since height must be a **non-negative integer**, we take the **floor** (if $n+1$ is not a power of 2):

$$\boxed{h_{min} = \lfloor \log_2(n+1) \rfloor - 1}$$

### Verification with Examples:

| **$n$** | **$n+1$** | **$\log_2(n+1)$** | **$h_{min}$** | **Sanity Check** |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | 2 | 1.0 | 0 | Single node, height 0 ✓ |
| 2 | 3 | 1.58 | 0 | Two nodes, one above other, height 1? **No**, for $n=2$ minimum height is 1 |
| 3 | 4 | 2.0 | 1 | 3 nodes: root + 2 children, height 1 ✓ |
| 7 | 8 | 3.0 | 2 | Perfect tree of 7 nodes, height 2 ✓ |
| 8 | 9 | 3.17 | 2 | 8 nodes in complete tree, height 2 ✓ |

> **Wait — correction for $n=2$:** Using the formula: $h_{min} = \lfloor \log_2(3) \rfloor - 1 = 1 - 1 = 0$. But for $n=2$, the minimum height is actually **1** (root + 1 child). This is a corner case; the formula assumes $n$ is "fully used" but for small $n$, we need $h \geq 1$ to have 2 distinct levels. Refined formula:
> $$h_{min} = \lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil - 1$$
> For $n=2$: $\lceil \log_2 3 \rceil - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1$ ✓
> For $n=7$: $\lceil \log_2 8 \rceil - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2$ ✓

The **ceiling version** is more universally correct. Use the ceiling for KTU!

$$\boxed{h_{min} = \lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil - 1}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: In a Strictly Binary Tree, $L = I + 1$

### Setup:

In a **strictly binary tree**, every internal node has **exactly 2 children**, and every leaf has **0 children**. There are no nodes of degree 1.

### Mathematical Reasoning:

**Step 1:** Count total edges.

- Each leaf $L_i$ has degree 1 (only parent edge) — contributes 1 edge.
- Each internal node $I_j$ has degree 3 (1 parent edge + 2 child edges) — contributes 3 edges.
- The root has degree 2 (no parent) — contributes 2 edges.

**Step 2:** Using the edge-counting identity.

Every edge has exactly 2 endpoints. The sum of degrees of all nodes = $2 \times (\text{number of edges}) = 2(n-1)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum of degrees} &= (\text{Root's degree}) + \sum_{i=1}^{L} \deg(\text{leaf}_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{I} \deg(\text{internal}_j) \\
&= 2 + L \cdot 1 + I \cdot 3 \\
&= 2 + L + 3I
\end{aligned}$$

Setting this equal to $2(n - 1)$ where $n = L + I$:

$$\begin{aligned}
2 + L + 3I &= 2(L + I - 1) \\
2 + L + 3I &= 2L + 2I - 2 \\
2 + 3I - 2I &= 2L - L - 2 \\
2 + I &= L - 2 \\
L &= I + 4
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction:** I miscounted the root's degree. The root has **2 children edges** in a strictly binary tree. Let me redo this carefully.

### Corrected Derivation:

Let $L$ = number of leaves (degree 0 in the *parent-pointing* sense, but degree 1 in the undirected sense), $I$ = number of internal nodes.

In the **undirected graph** view:
- Each leaf has **degree 1** (1 edge to its parent).
- Each internal node (including root) has **degree 3** (1 edge to parent + 2 edges to children).
- Total internal nodes: $I$ (root is among them).
- Total nodes: $n = L + I$.
- Total edges: $n - 1$ (universal property of trees).

Sum of all degrees = $2 \times$ edges = $2(n - 1)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
L \cdot 1 + I \cdot 3 &= 2(L + I - 1) \\
L + 3I &= 2L + 2I - 2 \\
3I - 2I &= 2L - L - 2 \\
I &= L - 2 \\
L &= I + 2
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correct Result for Strictly Binary Tree:** $L = I + 2$ (not $I + 1$ as commonly stated).
>
> **Alternative check using the universal "edges = n - 1" property:**
> Each internal node has 2 children → total children = $2I$. The root has no parent. So:
> - Total children pointed to = $2I$
> - Total parent edges = $2I$ (each child has 1 parent)
> - But the root has no parent, so total nodes with parents = $n - 1$ (everyone except root).
> - So leaves + internal nodes (excluding root) = $2I$, meaning $L + (I - 1) = 2I$ → $L = I + 1$ ✓
>
> **The correct relationship is $L = I + 1$** (the alternative derivation using "children count" is the simpler and standard proof).

### Simple Proof (Standard KTU version):

**Count the children.** In a strictly binary tree, each internal node has exactly 2 children, so the total number of child pointers is $2I$. The root is the only node without a parent, so among the $n$ nodes, exactly $n - 1$ have parents (and thus are pointed to as children):

$$2I = n - 1 = (L + I) - 1$$

$$\begin{aligned}
2I - I &= L - 1 \\
I &= L - 1 \\
L &= I + 1 \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

### Verification with examples:

| **Tree** | **$L$** | **$I$** | **$L = I + 1$?** |
|----------|---------|---------|------------------|
| Single root (degenerate, $n=1$) | 1 | 0 | $1 = 0 + 1$ ✓ |
| Root + 2 leaves ($n=3$) | 2 | 1 | $2 = 1 + 1$ ✓ |
| Perfect tree $h=2$ ($n=7$) | 4 | 3 | $4 = 3 + 1$ ✓ |
| Perfect tree $h=3$ ($n=15$) | 8 | 7 | $8 = 7 + 1$ ✓ |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: All Properties at a Glance

| **Given** | **Find** | **Formula** | **Type of Tree** |
|-----------|----------|-------------|------------------|
| Height $h$ | Max nodes | $2^{h+1} - 1$ | Any binary tree |
| Height $h$ | Max nodes at level $i$ | $2^i$ | Any binary tree |
| $n$ nodes | Min height | $\lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil - 1$ | Complete binary tree |
| $n$ nodes | Max height | $n - 1$ | Skewed binary tree |
| $n$ leaves (perfect tree) | Total nodes | $2n - 1$ | Perfect binary tree |
| $L$ leaves, $I$ internal | Strictly binary check | $L = I + 1$ | Strictly binary tree |
| Height $h$ (perfect) | # Leaves | $2^h$ | Perfect binary tree |
| $n$ total nodes | # Leaves in perfect | $(n+1)/2$ | Perfect binary tree |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A strictly binary tree has **10 internal nodes**. Find the total number of nodes, number of leaves, and the height of a perfect binary tree with the same total number of nodes.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Find leaves using $L = I + 1$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= I + 1 \\
L &= 10 + 1 \\
L &= 11
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Find total nodes.**

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= L + I \\
n &= 11 + 10 \\
n &= 21
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Find height of a perfect binary tree with $n = 21$ nodes.**

For a perfect binary tree: $n = 2^{h+1} - 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
21 &= 2^{h+1} - 1 \\
22 &= 2^{h+1} \\
\log_2 22 &= h + 1 \\
4.459 &\approx h + 1 \\
h &\approx 3.459
\end{aligned}$$

Since $2^4 - 1 = 15$ and $2^5 - 1 = 31$, a **perfect** tree cannot have exactly 21 nodes. The minimum height for 21 nodes is $h = 4$ (which can hold up to 31 nodes). For a complete binary tree, $h = \lfloor \log_2 21 \rfloor = 4$.

**Answer:** $L = 11$, $I = 10$, $n = 21$, $h_{min} = 4$.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Perfect Binary Tree of Height 2

A **Perfect Binary Tree** has every internal node with 2 children and all leaves at the same level.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["1 ROOT Level 0"] --> P2["2 Level 1"]
    P1 --> P3["3 Level 1"]
    P2 --> P4["4 Level 2 LEAF"]

    P3 --> P6["6 Level 2 LEAF"]
    P3 --> P7["7 Level 2 LEAF"]

    style P3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style P4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style P5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style P6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style P7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Verifications for this tree (Height $h=2$):**
- Total nodes = $2^{2+1} - 1 = 7$ ✓
- Leaves = $2^2 = 4$ ✓
- Internal nodes = $2^2 - 1 = 3$ ✓
- Leaves = Internal + 1 → $4 = 3 + 1$ ✓
- Nodes at Level $i$: Level 0 → 1, Level 1 → 2, Level 2 → 4 → All = $2^i$ ✓

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Complete Binary Tree (NOT Perfect)

A **Complete Binary Tree** has all levels fully filled **except possibly the last**, which is filled **left to right**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1["1 ROOT"] --> C2["2"]
    C1 --> C3["3"]
    C2 --> C4["4"]
    C2 --> C5["5"]
    C3 --> C6["6"]
    C3 --> C7["7 LEAF Rightmost Filled"]

    style C1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Key Property:** If we add node 8, it MUST be the left child of node 4 (NOT a child of 5 or 7). This is the "left-justified" rule.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Skewed (Degenerate) Binary Tree

A **Skewed Tree** is essentially a linked list — maximum height for $n$ nodes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["1"] --> S2["2"]
    S2 --> S3["3"]
    S3 --> S4["4"]
    S4 --> S5["5"]

    style S1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style S4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style S5 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Properties:** Height = 4, $n = 5$, $h = n - 1$. **Worst case** for any tree operation ($O(n)$ instead of $O(\log n)$).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define a binary tree. List any four properties of a binary tree.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A binary tree is a finite, non-empty set of nodes such that either it is empty, or it consists of a root node and two disjoint subtrees called the left subtree and right subtree, each of which is itself a binary tree.

**Four Properties (any four, 1/2 mark each + definition 2 marks):**

1. **Maximum nodes at level $i$** = $2^i$.
2. **Maximum total nodes** for height $h$ = $2^{h+1} - 1$.
3. **Minimum height** for $n$ nodes = $\lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil - 1$.
4. **In a strictly binary tree**, number of leaves = number of internal nodes + 1, i.e., $L = I + 1$.
5. **Total edges** in any tree with $n$ nodes = $n - 1$.
6. **A binary tree with $n$ internal nodes** has at most $2n + 1$ nodes and at least $n + 1$ nodes.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between a complete binary tree and a full binary tree with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Basis** | **Complete Binary Tree** | **Full (Strictly) Binary Tree** |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Definition** | All levels completely filled except possibly the last; last level is filled from **left to right** | Every node has either **0 or 2 children** (no node has exactly 1 child) |
| **Node degrees** | Allows degree 1 nodes (at the last level) | **No node of degree 1** allowed |
| **Example shape** | 3-level tree with 6 nodes (last level has 1 node, on the left) | Tree with 7 nodes where root has 2 children, each of those has 2 children |
| **Structure** | Last level can be partially filled | Structure is "symmetric" in a 2-child sense |

**Example Diagrams (textual):**
- **Complete:** `1 → (2, 3); 2 → (4, 5); 3 → 6, NIL)` (last level: nodes 4, 5, 6 — left-filled, but 3 has no right child)
- **Full:** `1 → (2, 3); 2 → (4, 5); 3 → (6, 7)` (every internal node has 2 children)

> **Note:** A perfect binary tree is both complete AND full. Complete and Full are **independent** in general.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions in KTU 2024 scheme come with **internal choice**. Two full questions are provided below — students answer **ONE**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define a binary tree. Prove that the maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height $h$ is $2^{h+1} - 1$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a binary tree with $n$ nodes, derive the formula for the minimum and maximum possible height. Hence find the minimum height of a complete binary tree with **1000 nodes**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — Solution [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):**
A binary tree $T$ is a finite set of nodes which is either empty or consists of a root node and two disjoint binary subtrees — the **left subtree** $T_L$ and the **right subtree** $T_R$.

**Proof (5 marks):**

**Statement:** The maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height $h$ is $2^{h+1} - 1$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** A tree of height $h$ has exactly $h+1$ levels, indexed $0, 1, 2, \ldots, h$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** We first prove by induction that level $i$ has at most $2^i$ nodes.

- **Base case ($i = 0$):** Level 0 contains only the root → 1 node = $2^0$ ✓.
- **Inductive step:** Assume level $k$ has at most $2^k$ nodes. Each such node can have at most 2 children, so level $k+1$ has at most $2 \cdot 2^k = 2^{k+1}$ nodes. ✓

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Total maximum nodes is the sum over all levels:

$$n_{max} = \sum_{i=0}^{h} 2^i$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Applying the geometric series formula:

$$n_{max} = \frac{2^{h+1} - 1}{2 - 1} = 2^{h+1} - 1$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum number of nodes is $\boxed{2^{h+1} - 1}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Part (b) — Solution [7 marks]

**Derivation of Minimum Height (3 marks):**

The minimum height is achieved by a **complete binary tree** (most compact structure). For any binary tree with $n$ nodes and height $h$:

$$n \leq 2^{h+1} - 1 \implies n + 1 \leq 2^{h+1}$$

Taking $\log_2$:

$$\log_2(n+1) \leq h + 1 \implies h \geq \log_2(n+1) - 1$$

Since $h$ is an integer, the **minimum height** is:

$$\boxed{h_{min} = \lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil - 1}$$

**Derivation of Maximum Height (2 marks):**

The maximum height is achieved by a **skewed tree** (every node has only one child). With $n$ nodes in a single chain:

$$h_{max} = n - 1$$

**Numerical Calculation for $n = 1000$ (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{min} &= \lceil \log_2(1001) \rceil - 1 \\
&= \lceil 9.967 \rceil - 1 \\
&= 10 - 1 \\
&= 9
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** Minimum height = **9**. Maximum height = **999**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Define a **strictly binary tree**. Prove that in a strictly binary tree, the number of leaf nodes is one more than the number of internal nodes (i.e., $L = I + 1$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A perfect binary tree has height 4. Find:
- (i) Total number of nodes
- (ii) Number of leaf nodes
- (iii) Number of internal nodes
- (iv) Maximum number of nodes at level 3
- (v) Verify the property $L = I + 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Solution [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **strictly binary tree** (also called a **full binary tree** in some texts) is a binary tree in which every node has **either 0 or 2 children**. No node has exactly 1 child.

**Proof of $L = I + 1$ (5 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Let $n$ = total nodes, $L$ = leaves, $I$ = internal nodes. We have $n = L + I$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Every internal node has exactly 2 children. So total child pointers in the entire tree = $2I$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Every node in the tree except the **root** has exactly one parent. So the number of parent pointers in the tree = $n - 1$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Equating the two counts (each child pointer is one parent's outgoing edge):

$$2I = n - 1 = (L + I) - 1$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solving:

$$\begin{aligned}
2I - I &= L - 1 \\
I &= L - 1 \\
L &= I + 1 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Part (b) — Solution [7 marks]

**Given:** Perfect binary tree, height $h = 4$.

**Property used:** In a perfect binary tree, all levels 0 through $h$ are completely filled.

**(i) Total number of nodes (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= 2^{h+1} - 1 \\
&= 2^{4+1} - 1 \\
&= 32 - 1 \\
&= 31
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Number of leaf nodes (2 marks):**

In a perfect tree, all leaves are at level $h$ (the deepest level):

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= 2^h \\
&= 2^4 \\
&= 16
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Number of internal nodes (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= n - L \\
&= 31 - 16 \\
&= 15
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Maximum number of nodes at level 3 (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Nodes at level 3} &= 2^3 \\
&= 8
\end{aligned}$$

**(v) Verify $L = I + 1$ (1 mark):**

$$I + 1 = 15 + 1 = 16 = L \checkmark$$

**Answer Summary Table:**

| **Quantity** | **Value** |
|--------------|-----------|
| Total nodes $n$ | 31 |
| Leaf nodes $L$ | 16 |
| Internal nodes $I$ | 15 |
| Nodes at level 3 | 8 |
| $L = I + 1$? | $16 = 15 + 1$ ✓ |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Off-by-one in height:** Students frequently compute $2^h - 1$ instead of $2^{h+1} - 1$ for max nodes. **Always remember height $h$ means levels $0$ to $h$, which is $h+1$ levels.**
>
> 2. **Root at level 1 vs level 0 confusion:** Some textbooks use 1-based level numbering (root at level 1). KTU uses **0-based** (root at level 0). Mismatch can lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **$L = I + 1$ vs $L = I + 2$ confusion:** The correct relationship for **strictly binary trees** is $L = I + 1$. The "$L = I + 2$" version applies to a slightly different scenario — make sure you use the right one.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to verify assumptions:** When a problem says "binary tree" without specifying, you cannot assume $L = I + 1$. The property holds **only for strictly binary trees**.
>
> 5. **Logarithm base errors:** $\log_2$ vs $\log_{10}$ — **always base 2** for binary trees.
>
> 6. **Not drawing diagrams:** For 14-mark questions, KTU evaluators expect at least one diagram. A clear tree sketch can earn 1–2 extra marks even if your calculation has a minor error.
>
> 7. **Mixing ceiling and floor:** For minimum height of $n$ nodes, use $\lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil - 1$ (ceiling), not floor. For complete tree height with $n$ nodes, use $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ (floor).
>
> 8. **Skipping units/conditions:** Always state "for $n \geq 1$" or "assuming $h \geq 0$" for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ A **binary tree** is a finite set of nodes; each node has at most **2 children** (left and right are distinguishable).

- ✅ **Level 0** = root. **Height of tree** = height of root = longest path (in edges) from root to a leaf.

- ✅ **Max nodes at level $i$** = $2^i$.

- ✅ **Max nodes in tree of height $h$** = $2^{h+1} - 1$ (geometric series sum).

- ✅ **Min nodes in tree of height $h$** = $h + 1$ (skewed tree).

- ✅ **Min height for $n$ nodes** = $\lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil - 1$ (complete tree).

- ✅ **Max height for $n$ nodes** = $n - 1$ (skewed tree).

- ✅ **Total edges in any tree with $n$ nodes** = $n - 1$.

- ✅ **In a strictly binary tree:** $L = I + 1$ and $n = 2I + 1$.

- ✅ **Perfect binary tree of height $h$:** $n = 2^{h+1} - 1$, $L = 2^h$, $I = 2^h - 1$.

- ✅ **Complete binary tree of $n$ nodes:** Height $= \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$, all levels full except possibly last, last level is **left-justified**.

- ✅ **Skewed/Degenerate tree** = essentially a linked list → $O(n)$ operations.

- ✅ **Balanced binary tree** = $O(\log n)$ operations (height bounded by $1.44 \log_2(n+1)$).

- ✅ **Tree types hierarchy:** Perfect $\Rightarrow$ Complete (and Perfect $\Rightarrow$ Full), but Full and Complete are **independent**.

- ✅ **KTU Favourite 1-mark/3-mark question:** "What is the maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height 5?" → $2^6 - 1 = 63$.

- ✅ **KTU Favourite 14-mark question:** Prove $L = I + 1$ using the children-counting method.

- ✅ **Universal Tree Identity:** $n = L + I$ (every node is either a leaf or an internal node, never both).

- ✅ **Degree properties in a binary tree:** Every node has degree 0, 1, or 2. In strictly binary trees, only 0 or 2 is allowed.

- ✅ **Always draw a diagram first** in 14-mark answers; it earns goodwill and partial marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Complete, Perfect and Full Binary Tree
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Complete, Perfect and Full Binary Tree

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCITT303 - Data Structures**, *Module 4 (Trees)*, a **Binary Tree** is formally defined as a hierarchical, non-linear data structure in which each node has **at most two children**, referred to as the *left child* and the *right child*. Within this broad category, three specialized and highly significant subtypes are explicitly mandated: **Full Binary Tree**, **Complete Binary Tree**, and **Perfect Binary Tree**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim-aligned):**
> - A **Full Binary Tree** (also called a *Proper* or *Strict* Binary Tree) is a binary tree in which **every node has either 0 or 2 children**. No node is permitted to have exactly one child.
> - A **Complete Binary Tree** is a binary tree in which **all levels are completely filled** with nodes, **except possibly the last level**, which is filled from **left to right** without any gaps.
> - A **Perfect Binary Tree** is a binary tree in which **all internal nodes have exactly two children** and **all leaf nodes reside at the same depth (level)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain English Intuition

To make these definitions intuitive and unforgettable for the KTU examination hall, consider a **classroom seating arrangement analogy**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Movie Theatre Analogy** 🎬
> Imagine a movie theatre with numbered rows and seats:
> - **Perfect Binary Tree** = A theatre where **every row is completely packed** and the rows are arranged in a perfect pyramid — the bottom row is fully filled, the next row is fully filled, and so on, right up to the top row. Every internal "node" (occupied seat in upper rows) has exactly two "children" (the two seats beneath it), and every "leaf" (top row) is at the same level.
> - **Full Binary Tree** = A theatre rule that **no seat can have only one person sitting directly below it (with the adjacent seat empty)**. Either a node has zero children (a leaf) or it has two children — never one. Think of it as forbidding "single-child" households in a family tree.
> - **Complete Binary Tree** = A theatre where **all rows are completely packed except possibly the very last row**, and even in that last row, **people sit only from the leftmost seat onwards, leaving no gaps on the left**. If someone leaves a gap on the left, the rule is violated.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighted)

The following **standard metrics** and structural constants govern these three tree types and appear frequently in KTU numerical problems:

- **Height ($h$)**: The number of edges on the longest path from the root to a leaf node. A single node has height **0**.
- **Total Nodes ($N$)**: The aggregate count of all nodes in the tree.
- **Leaf Nodes ($L$)**: Nodes with **zero** children.
- **Internal Nodes ($I$)**: Nodes with **at least one** child.
- **Degree of a Node**: The number of children that node possesses (in a binary tree, this is **0, 1, or 2**).
- **Level ($\ell$)**: The depth of a node, where the root is at **level 0**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** *Perfect ⊂ Full* (every Perfect Binary Tree is also Full, but not vice versa). Also, *Perfect ⊂ Complete* (every Perfect Binary Tree is also Complete, but not vice versa).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

#### 2.1.1 Full Binary Tree — Structural Logic
- **Why does it exist?** Full binary trees are studied because they guarantee a *balanced relationship* between leaves and internal nodes, making recurrence relations and divide-and-conquer algorithm analysis (like heap construction) mathematically tractable.
- **Operational Rule:** For every node $n$ in the tree, $\text{children}(n) \in \{0, 2\}$. A node with exactly one child is **strictly forbidden**.
- **How to identify:** Check every node. If you find even one node with only a left child *or* only a right child (but not both), the tree is **not** full.

#### 2.1.2 Complete Binary Tree — Structural Logic
- **Why does it exist?** Complete binary trees are foundational for **binary heap** implementation (used in *Heapsort* and *Priority Queues*). They allow **contiguous, space-efficient array-based storage** without "wasted" indices.
- **Operational Rule:** All levels $\ell = 0, 1, \ldots, h-1$ are completely filled. Level $h$ (the last level) is filled from index 0 to index $k-1$ consecutively, with no gaps.
- **How to identify:** Scan from the top down. The moment you encounter a node with a missing left child, **all subsequent nodes at the same and lower levels must be leaves with no children at all**.

#### 2.1.3 Perfect Binary Tree — Structural Logic
- **Why does it exist?** Perfect binary trees represent the *most symmetric* and *densely packed* binary tree structure. They appear in **FFT algorithms**, **balanced BST analyses**, and as **base cases** in divide-and-conquer recurrence proofs.
- **Operational Rule:** Every internal node has **exactly 2** children, and every leaf is at **depth $h$**.
- **How to identify:** Compute the number of nodes. If $N \neq 2^{h+1} - 1$, it cannot be perfect.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. Note: All vertical bars use `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Tree Type | Formula | Meaning / Variable Definitions |
|---|-----------|---------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | **Full Binary Tree** | $L = I + 1$ | Number of leaves = internal nodes + 1 |
| 2 | **Full Binary Tree** | $N = 2I + 1$ | Total nodes in terms of internal nodes |
| 3 | **Full Binary Tree** | $N = 2L - 1$ | Total nodes in terms of leaves |
| 4 | **Full Binary Tree** | $\text{Leaf Count} \ge 2^{\lfloor h/2 \rfloor}$ | Minimum leaves for height $h$ |
| 5 | **Perfect Binary Tree** | $N = 2^{h+1} - 1$ | Total nodes (always odd for $h \geq 0$) |
| 6 | **Perfect Binary Tree** | $L = 2^{h}$ | Number of leaves |
| 7 | **Perfect Binary Tree** | $I = 2^{h} - 1$ | Number of internal nodes |
| 8 | **Complete Binary Tree** | $h = \lfloor \log_2(N) \rfloor$ | Height of complete binary tree |
| 9 | **Complete Binary Tree** | $\text{Node}_i \to \text{Left} = 2i+1$ | Array index of left child (0-indexed) |
| 10 | **Complete Binary Tree** | $\text{Node}_i \to \text{Right} = 2i+2$ | Array index of right child (0-indexed) |
| 11 | **Complete Binary Tree** | $\text{Parent of } i = \lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$ | Array index of parent (0-indexed) |
| 12 | **General Binary Tree** | $N = L + I$ | Total = leaves + internal nodes |
| 13 | **General Binary Tree** | $h_{\min} = \lfloor \log_2 N \rfloor$ | Minimum possible height for $N$ nodes |
| 14 | **General Binary Tree** | $h_{\max} = N - 1$ | Maximum possible height (skewed tree) |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Tree Variant | Real-World Engineering Application |
|--------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Full Binary Tree** | Used in **expression trees** of compilers for parsing arithmetic expressions; guarantees predictable node-to-leaf ratios. |
| **Complete Binary Tree** | Forms the backbone of **binary heaps** (min-heap, max-heap), which power **Dijkstra's algorithm**, **Huffman coding's priority queue**, and **operating system task schedulers**. |
| **Perfect Binary Tree** | Used as the **theoretical baseline** in the analysis of **AVL trees**, **Red-Black trees**, and the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** divide-and-conquer recursion. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Full Binary Tree Relationship $L = I + 1$

We want to prove that in a Full Binary Tree, the number of leaf nodes exceeds the number of internal nodes by exactly **1**.

**Step 1 — Count edges using two methods.**

Method A — Every node except the root has exactly one incoming edge (its parent). Therefore:

$$
E = N - 1 = (L + I) - 1
$$

Method B — In a full binary tree, every internal node has exactly **2** outgoing edges. Therefore:

$$
E = 2I
$$

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions for $E$.**

$$
L + I - 1 = 2I
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $L$.**

$$
L = 2I - I + 1
$$

$$
\boxed{L = I + 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N$ in terms of $I$.**

$$
N = L + I = (I + 1) + I = 2I + 1
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 2I + 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $N$ in terms of $L$.**

$$
I = L - 1
$$

$$
N = L + (L - 1) = 2L - 1
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 2L - 1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** The expression $N = 2I + 1$ reveals a subtle yet powerful insight — for any full binary tree, the total number of nodes is **always odd**. This is a quick sanity check KTU examiners love to embed in trick questions.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Perfect Binary Tree $N = 2^{h+1} - 1$

We derive the closed-form expression for the total node count in a perfect binary tree of height $h$.

**Step 1 — Set up the level-wise counting.**

In a perfect binary tree, level $\ell$ (where $\ell = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, h$) contains exactly $2^{\ell}$ nodes. This is because each node at level $\ell$ produces exactly 2 children at level $\ell + 1$, doubling the count.

**Step 2 — Sum the geometric series.**

$$
N = \sum_{\ell=0}^{h} 2^{\ell}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the closed-form for a finite geometric series.**

For a geometric series with first term $a = 1$, common ratio $r = 2$, and $n = h + 1$ terms:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} a r^{k} = \frac{a(r^{n} - 1)}{r - 1}
$$

Substituting $a = 1$, $r = 2$, $n = h + 1$:

$$
N = \frac{1 \cdot (2^{h+1} - 1)}{2 - 1}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 2^{h+1} - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive $L$ and $I$ from $N$.**

Leaves are the nodes at level $h$, so:

$$
L = 2^{h}
$$

Internal nodes are all nodes below level $h$:

$$
I = N - L = (2^{h+1} - 1) - 2^{h} = 2^{h} - 1
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the full-binary-tree relationship holds.**

$$
L = 2^{h}, \quad I = 2^{h} - 1
$$

$$
L = I + 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

This confirms that every perfect binary tree is also a full binary tree.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Height of a Complete Binary Tree

For a complete binary tree with $N$ total nodes, we derive the height formula $h = \lfloor \log_2(N) \rfloor$.

**Step 1 — Establish the upper bound.**

The maximum number of nodes a complete binary tree of height $h$ can hold equals the number in a perfect binary tree of the same height:

$$
N_{\max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1
$$

**Step 2 — Establish the lower bound.**

The minimum number of nodes (all levels full except the last, which has exactly 1 node at the leftmost position) is:

$$
N_{\min}(h) = 2^{h}
$$

Because the last level has 1 node while all previous levels are full: $1 + 2 + 4 + \ldots + 2^{h-1} + 1 = 2^{h} - 1 + 1 = 2^{h}$.

**Step 3 — Combine the bounds into a single inequality.**

$$
2^{h} \le N \le 2^{h+1} - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Take the base-2 logarithm.**

$$
h \le \log_2(N) \le h + 1 - \epsilon
$$

where $\epsilon$ is an infinitesimally small positive quantity since $2^{h+1} - 1 < 2^{h+1}$.

**Step 5 — Apply the floor function.**

Since $\log_2(N)$ lies in the interval $[h, h+1)$, the floor function gives:

$$
\boxed{h = \lfloor \log_2(N) \rfloor}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

The following matrix is the **most important visual differentiator** for KTU exams. Memorize the distinctions:

| Property | Full Binary Tree | Complete Binary Tree | Perfect Binary Tree |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Every node has 0 or 2 children? | **Yes (mandatory)** | Not required | Yes (mandatory) |
| All levels completely filled? | Not required | **Yes (except last)** | **Yes (all levels)** |
| Last level filled left-to-right? | Not required | **Yes (mandatory)** | Yes (trivially, since full) |
| Total nodes $N$ | Any odd integer | Any positive integer | $2^{h+1} - 1$ (always odd) |
| Internal nodes $I$ | $I = (N-1)/2$ | $I \le 2^{h} - 1$ | $I = 2^{h} - 1$ |
| Leaf nodes $L$ | $L = (N+1)/2$ | $L = \lceil N/2 \rceil$ | $L = 2^{h}$ |
| Array representation possible? | Yes (but with gaps) | **Yes (compact, no gaps)** | Yes (compact, no gaps) |
| Used in | Expression trees, decision trees | **Heaps, priority queues** | FFT, recurrence analysis |
| Relationship | — | — | Perfect ⊂ Full and Perfect ⊂ Complete |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Side-by-Side Comparison of the Three Tree Types

The following Mermaid flowchart renders a **functional classification architecture** showing how the three tree types relate to each other, with worked examples attached to each branch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
