\theta\; \text{Inorder}(\text{right}) \\ \text{Left subtree} &: \text{Inorder}((+\ a\ b)) = (a + b) \\ \text{Right subtree} &: \text{Inorder}((*\ (+ c d)\ e)) = ((c + d) * e) \\ \text{Whole tree} &: ((a + b)) * ((c + d) * e) \end{aligned} $$ **Final Infix Expression:** $$ (a + b) \ast ((c + d) \ast e) $$ ### 3.1.4 (b) Prefix Expression — Preorder Traversal (Root, Left, Right) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Preorder} &= \theta\ \text{Preorder}(\text{left})\ \text{Preorder}(\text{right}) \\ &= *\ +\ a\ b\ *\ +\ c\ d\ e \end{aligned} $$ **Final Prefix Expression:** $$ \texttt{* + a b * + c d e} $$ ### 3.1.5 (c) Evaluation — Postorder Traversal (Left, Right, Root) Substitute the values $a = 3,\ b = 2,\ c = 4,\ d = 1,\ e = 5$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Postorder evaluation: } & a\ b\ +\ c\ d\ +\ e\ *\ * \\ \text{Step 1: } & 3\ 2\ +\ \Rightarrow\ 3 + 2 = 5 \\ \text{Step 2: } & 5\ 4\ 1\ +\ *\ * \Rightarrow\ 4 + 1 = 5 \\ \text{Step 3: } & 5\ 5\ 5\ *\ * \Rightarrow\ 5 \times 5 = 25 \\ \text{Step 4: } & 5\ 25\ * \Rightarrow\ 5 \times 25 = 125 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Evaluated Result:** $$ (a + b) \ast ((c + d) \ast e) = (3 + 2) \ast ((4 + 1) \ast 5) = 5 \ast 25 = \mathbf{125} $$ ### 3.1.6 Verification Using Tree Structure We can verify by computing the tree bottom-up: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Leaf } c &= 4 \\ \text{Leaf } d &= 1 \\ \text{Node } (+,\ c,\ d) &= 4 + 1 = 5 \\ \text{Leaf } e &= 5 \\ \text{Node } (*,\ (+ c d),\ e) &= 5 \times 5 = 25 \\ \text{Leaf } a &= 3 \\ \text{Leaf } b &= 2 \\ \text{Node } (+,\ a,\ b) &= 3 + 2 = 5 \\ \text{Root } (*,\ (+ a b),\ (* (+ c d) e)) &= 5 \times 25 = 125 \end{aligned} $$ **Result matches: $125$.** ✓ ## 3.2 Worked Example — Constructing from Prefix (Secondary Case) > **Problem:** Construct the expression tree for the prefix expression: $\texttt{* + a b - c d}$ where $a = 5, b = 3, c = 8, d = 2$. Scan from **right to left**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: } & d \rightarrow \text{push leaf}(d) &\text{Stack: } [d] \\ \text{Step 2: } & c \rightarrow \text{push leaf}(c) &\text{Stack: } [d, c] \\ \text{Step 3: } & -\ : \text{pop } c \text{ (left)}, \text{pop } d \text{ (right)}, \text{create } (- c d) &\text{Stack: } [(- c d)] \\ \text{Step 4: } & b \rightarrow \text{push leaf}(b) &\text{Stack: } [(- c d), b] \\ \text{Step 5: } & a \rightarrow \text{push leaf}(a) &\text{Stack: } [(- c d), b, a] \\ \text{Step 6: } & +\ : \text{pop } a \text{ (left)}, \text{pop } b \text{ (right)}, \text{create } (+ a b) &\text{Stack: } [(- c d), (+ a b)] \\ \text{Step 7: } & *\ : \text{pop } (+ a b) \text{ (left)}, \text{pop } (- c d) \text{ (right)}, \text{create } (* (+ a b) (- c d)) &\text{Stack: } [(* (+ a b) (- c d))] \end{aligned} $$ **Evaluation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Inorder: } & (a + b) * (c - d) = (5 + 3) * (8 - 2) = 8 * 6 = \mathbf{48} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Pseudocode for Construction (Algorithm — C Style, KTU Board Standard) > [!NOTE] > Although the KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes algorithmic reasoning, the following generic pseudocode is the standard answer format expected in the examination. ``` ALGORITHM: BuildExpressionTree(postfix) INPUT: postfix — string of space-separated tokens OUTPUT: root pointer of constructed expression tree 1. CREATE an empty stack S 2. FOR each token t in postfix (left to right) DO 3. IF t is an operand THEN 4. node ← NEW Node(t) 5. node.left ← NULL 6. node.right ← NULL 7. PUSH(S, node) 8. ELSE // t is an operator 9. right ← POP(S) 10. left ← POP(S) 11. node ← NEW Node(t) 12. node.left ← left 13. node.right ← right 14. PUSH(S, node) 15. END IF 16. END FOR 17. root ← POP(S) // final stack entry 18. RETURN root ``` **Evaluation Pseudocode (Recursive Postorder):** ``` FUNCTION Evaluate(node) 1. IF node.left = NULL AND node.right = NULL THEN 2. RETURN lookup(node.value) // operand 3. END IF 4. leftVal ← Evaluate(node.left) 5. rightVal ← Evaluate(node.right) 6. RETURN Apply(node.value, leftVal, rightVal) END FUNCTION ``` ## 3.4 Algorithmic Correctness Argument (Why It Works) - **Base case:** A single operand is trivially represented as a leaf node. ✓ - **Inductive step:** Assume the algorithm correctly builds trees for all sub-expressions ending before position $i$. When it encounters operator $\theta_i$ at position $i$, both sub-expressions $E_1$ and $E_2$ have already been processed and are on the stack. Combining them under $\theta_i$ produces a correct tree for $E_1\ \theta\ E_2$. ✓ - **Termination:** Each symbol is processed exactly once, so the algorithm terminates after $2n - 1$ steps. ✓ - **Time complexity:** $T(n) = O(n)$ — one pass, constant work per symbol. ✓ - **Space complexity:** $O(n)$ — stack holds at most $n$ nodes. ✓ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Final Expression Tree for `ab+cde+**` ```mermaid graph TD A0["STAR_OP"] --> A1["PLUS_OP"] A0 --> A2["STAR_OP_2"] A1 --> A3["a"] A1 --> A4["b"] A2 --> A5["PLUS_OP_3"] A2 --> A6["e"] A5 --> A7["c"] A5 --> A8["d"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the tree:** > - Root = `*` (outermost multiplication) > - Left child of root = `+` (handles `a + b`) > - Right child of root = `*` (handles `(c + d) * e`) > - Leaves `a, b, c, d, e` are the operands ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Stack State Evolution During Construction ```mermaid graph LR S1["Step 1-2: Push a, b"]:::step --> S2["Step 3: Operator + → pop b, pop a, push + node"] S2 --> S3["Step 4-5: Push c, d"] S3 --> S4["Step 6: Operator + → pop d, pop c, push + node"] S4 --> S5["Step 7: Push e"] S5 --> S6["Step 8: Operator * → pop e, pop +node, push * node"] S6 --> S7["Step 9: Operator * → pop *node, pop +node, push ROOT * node"] S7 --> S8["Final Stack: One Root Node"] classDef step fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#0B2545 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Compiler's Use of Expression Trees ```mermaid graph TD SRC["Source Code
if x > a + b * c"] --> LEX["Lexical Analyzer
Token Stream"] LEX --> PARSE["Parser
Recursive Descent"] PARSE --> AST["Expression Tree
>
/
x +
/
a *
/
b c"] AST --> OPT["Optimizer
Constant Folding
Strength Reduction"] OPT --> CG["Code Generator
Three-Address Code
t1 = b * c
t2 = a + t1
IF x > t2 GOTO L1"] CG --> ASM["Assembly / Machine Code"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > This block diagram illustrates the **expression tree's central role** in a compiler's front-end → back-end pipeline. The same data structure you construct in this KTU module is the **exact intermediate representation** used by production-grade compilers like GCC and Clang. ## 4.4 Traversal-to-Expression Mapping (Visual Mnemonic) ```mermaid graph TD T["Inorder Traversal"]:::io --> IE["INFIX: a + b * c
Needs Parentheses"] P["Preorder Traversal"]:::po --> PE["PREFIX: + a * b c
Operator BEFORE operands"] PO["Postorder Traversal"]:::pt --> POE["POSTFIX: a b c * +
Operator AFTER operands
Used in Stack Machines"] classDef io fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#D97706,color:#7C2D12 classDef po fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0284C7,color:#075985 classDef pt fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#16A34A,color:#14532D ``` ## 4.5 Stack Operation Sequence (For `ab+cde+**`) ```mermaid graph TD P1["Push a"] --> P2["Push b"] P2 --> P3["Pop b, Pop a, Push +"] P3 --> P4["Push c"] P4 --> P5["Push d"] P5 --> P6["Pop d, Pop c, Push +"] P6 --> P7["Push e"] P7 --> P8["Pop e, Pop +, Push *"] P8 --> P9["Pop *, Pop +, Push ROOT *"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] **Define an expression tree. State and justify the relationship between the three depth-first traversals of an expression tree and the three notations of an arithmetic expression.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **expression tree** is a binary tree in which **leaf nodes represent operands** and **internal nodes represent operators**, with the left and right subtrees of an operator representing its left and right sub-expressions respectively. The three depth-first traversals correspond to the three notations as follows: | Traversal | Order | Expression Form | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Inorder | Left $\to$ Root $\to$ Right | **Infix** | $a + b \ast c$ | | Preorder | Root $\to$ Left $\to$ Right | **Prefix** | $+\ a\ \ast\ b\ c$ | | Postorder | Left $\to$ Right $\to$ Root | **Postfix** | $a\ b\ c\ \ast\ +$ | The justification is that in each traversal, the **root (operator)** is visited either between, before, or after its two operand subtrees — which directly matches the syntactic position of the operator in the three notations. > [!NOTE] > **Mark split:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the table, 1 mark for the justification/example. --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **For an expression tree built from an arithmetic expression containing $n$ operands, what is the total number of nodes? Justify your answer.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Total number of nodes = $2n - 1$.** **Justification:** - Every operand occupies a **leaf node** $\Rightarrow$ $n$ leaf nodes. - Every binary operator occupies an **internal node** $\Rightarrow$ $(n - 1)$ internal nodes, because a well-formed arithmetic expression with $n$ operands has exactly $(n - 1)$ binary operators (each operator reduces two sub-expressions into one). - Therefore, total nodes: $$ \text{Total} = n + (n - 1) = 2n - 1 $$ **Example:** The expression $a + b \ast c$ has 3 operands and 2 operators, so the tree has $2(3) - 1 = 5$ nodes. ✓ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A — Module 4 Choice 1** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] **(a)** Construct the expression tree for the postfix expression: $\texttt{ab*c+}\ \text{where}\ a = 5,\ b = 3,\ c = 2$. Show the stack state at each step. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Using the constructed tree, derive: (i) the **infix expression**, (ii) the **prefix expression**, and (iii) the **evaluated result**. **[7 Marks]** --- #### **Model Solution (a) — Stack-Based Construction** **Step-by-step table:** | Step | Symbol | Action | Stack (top at right) | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `a` | Operand → push leaf(`a`) | `[a]` | | 2 | `b` | Operand → push leaf(`b`) | `[a, b]` | | 3 | `*` | Pop `b` (right), Pop `a` (left), create `(*, a, b)`, push | `[(* a b)]` | | 4 | `c` | Operand → push leaf(`c`) | `[(* a b), c]` | | 5 | `+` | Pop `c` (right), Pop `(* a b)` (left), create `(+, (* a b), c)`, push | `[(+ (* a b) c)]` | **Final Tree Structure:** ``` (+) / \ [(*)] c / \ a b ``` **Verification of node count:** 3 operands + 2 operators = $2(3) - 1 = 5$ nodes ✓ --- #### **Model Solution (b) — Traversals and Evaluation** **(i) Infix Expression (Inorder Traversal):** Walking Left $\to$ Root $\to$ Right and adding parentheses: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Inorder} &= \text{Inorder}(\text{left})\ \theta\ \text{Inorder}(\text{right}) \\ &= ((a \ast b)) + (c) \\ &= (a \ast b) + c \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\text{Infix: } (a \ast b) + c}$$ **(ii) Prefix Expression (Preorder Traversal):** Walking Root $\to$ Left $\to$ Right: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Preorder} &= \theta\ \text{Preorder}(\text{left})\ \text{Preorder}(\text{right}) \\ &= +\ \ast\ a\ b\ c \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\text{Prefix: } +\ \ast\ a\ b\ c}$$ **(iii) Evaluated Result (Postorder Traversal):** Substitute $a = 5,\ b = 3,\ c = 2$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Postfix: } & a\ b\ \ast\ c\ + \\ &= 5\ 3\ \ast\ 2\ + \\ &= 15\ 2\ + \\ &= 17 \end{aligned} $$ **Verification by tree bottom-up:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Node } (*, a, b) &= 5 \times 3 = 15 \\ \text{Root } (+, 15, c) &= 15 + 2 = 17 \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\text{Result: } 17}$$ --- ### **Question B — Module 4 Choice 2 (Alternative)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **(a)** Construct the expression tree for the prefix expression: $\texttt{- * a b / c d}$, where $a = 8,\ b = 2,\ c = 10,\ d = 5$. Show the stack state at each step. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Using the constructed tree, derive: (i) the **infix expression**, (ii) the **postfix expression**, and (iii) the **evaluated result**. **[7 Marks]** --- #### **Model Solution (a) — Stack-Based Construction (Right-to-Left Scan)** | Step | Symbol | Action | Stack (top at right) | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `d` | Operand → push leaf(`d`) | `[d]` | | 2 | `c` | Operand → push leaf(`c`) | `[d, c]` | | 3 | `/` | Pop `c` (left), Pop `d` (right), create `(/, c, d)`, push | `[(*, c, d)]` wait, the op is `/` | | 4 | `b` | Operand → push leaf(`b`) | `[(/ c d), b]` | | 5 | `a` | Operand → push leaf(`a`) | `[(/ c d), b, a]` | | 6 | `*` | Pop `a` (left), Pop `b` (right), create `(*, a, b)`, push | `[(/ c d), (* a b)]` | | 7 | `-` | Pop `(* a b)` (left), Pop `(/ c d)` (right), create `(-, (* a b), (/ c d))`, push | `[(- (* a b) (/ c d))]` | **Final Tree Structure:** ``` (-) / \ [*] [/] / \ / \ a b c d ``` --- #### **Model Solution (b) — Traversals and Evaluation** **(i) Infix Expression:** $$ \begin{aligned} &= \text{Inorder}((*,\ a,\ b))\ -\ \text{Inorder}((/,\ c,\ d)) \\ &= (a \ast b) - (c\ /\ d) \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\text{Infix: } (a \ast b) - (c\ /\ d)}$$ **(ii) Postfix Expression:** Walking Left $\to$ Right $\to$ Root: $$ \begin{aligned} &= \text{Postorder}((*,\ a,\ b))\ \text{Postorder}((/,\ c,\ d))\ - \\ &= a\ b\ \ast\ c\ d\ /\ - \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\text{Postfix: } a\ b\ \ast\ c\ d\ /\ -}$$ **(iii) Evaluated Result:** Substitute $a = 8,\ b = 2,\ c = 10,\ d = 5$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Postfix evaluation: } & 8\ 2\ \ast\ 10\ 5\ /\ - \\ &= 16\ 10\ 5\ /\ - \\ &= 16\ 2\ - \\ &= 14 \end{aligned} $$ **Verification by tree bottom-up:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Node } (*, a, b) &= 8 \times 2 = 16 \\ \text{Node } (/, c, d) &= 10 \div 5 = 2 \\ \text{Root } (-, 16, 2) &= 16 - 2 = 14 \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\text{Result: } 14}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in Expression Tree Questions:** > > 1. **Wrong child assignment (–2 to –3 marks):** When popping two subtrees for a postfix operator, the **first pop is the RIGHT subtree** and the **second pop is the LEFT subtree**. Reversing this gives a wrong tree, and consequently wrong infix/prefix/postfix outputs. > > 2. **Forgetting parentheses in infix (–1 mark):** Inorder traversal gives a *parenthesized* infix. Writing just `a + b * c` instead of `(a * b) + c` loses marks because operator precedence is ambiguous without parentheses. > > 3. **Stack underflow in prefix construction (–2 marks):** When scanning prefix right-to-left, some students forget to scan **right-to-left** and instead scan left-to-right, causing incorrect pop order and a malformed tree. > > 4. **Confusing traversal with expression form (–1 mark):** Writing "infix = postorder" is a common slip. Memorize: **Inorder ↔ Infix, Preorder ↔ Prefix, Postorder ↔ Postfix**. > > 5. **Skipping the stack-state table (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU examiners *require* the explicit stack trace. A final tree without the step-by-step construction usually receives only 4 out of 7 marks. > > 6. **Arithmetic errors in evaluation (–1 mark):** Re-verify the postfix evaluation by the tree method to catch computational slips. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Expression tree** = binary tree with **operands as leaves** and **operators as internal nodes**. - **Total nodes** for an expression with $n$ operands = $2n - 1$. - **Construction from postfix** uses a stack: **operands push**, **operators pop two (right first, then left) and push a new node**. - **Construction from prefix** is the mirror: **scan right-to-left**, pop in **(left first, then right)** order. - **Inorder traversal** of the tree = **infix expression** (with parentheses). - **Preorder traversal** of the tree = **prefix expression**. - **Postorder traversal** of the tree = **postfix expression**. - **Evaluation** is a recursive postorder traversal: evaluate children first, then apply the operator. - **Time complexity** of construction = $O(n)$; space complexity = $O(n)$ for the stack. - **Real-world use:** Compilers (AST), calculators (RPN), spreadsheets, database optimizers. - **Mnemonic:** "**PO**storder = **PO**stfix" (both start with the same two letters). - **Mnemonic:** "**Pre**order = **Pre**fix" (both start with "**Pre**"). - **KTU must-show:** Stack state table at *every* step, the final tree, all three traversals, and the evaluated result.

topics.title: Binary Search Tree: Creation, Insertion, Deletion, and Search operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Binary Search Tree: Creation, Insertion, Deletion, and Search operations # 📘 MODULE 4: BINARY SEARCH TREE (BST) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 🎯 Formal Academic Definition A **Binary Search Tree (BST)** is a node-based binary tree data structure that satisfies the **BST Ordering Property (also called the BST Invariant)**. For every node `N` in the tree: 1. All keys (data values) in the **left subtree** of `N` are **strictly less than** the key of `N`. 2. All keys in the **right subtree** of `N` are **strictly greater than** the key of `N`. 3. Both the left and right subtrees must themselves be **Binary Search Trees**. 4. The tree must not contain **duplicate keys** (in the standard definition used at KTU). Formally, for a node `N` with key `k`: $$\text{key}(L) < k < \text{key}(R) \quad \forall L \in \text{LeftSubtree}(N), \; \forall R \in \text{RightSubtree}(N)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** BST is the foundational search structure for Module 4. You must be able to (a) **create** a BST from a given sequence of keys, (b) perform **insertion** of new nodes, (c) perform **deletion** while preserving the BST property, and (d) implement **search** operations. Examiner questions frequently include tracing operations step-by-step on a given BST. --- ### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — The "Phone Book Dictionary" Model Imagine a **college library** where books are organized as follows: The librarian sits at the **main desk (root)**. If you ask for a book with a call number **smaller** than the one on the desk, the librarian points you to the **left aisle**; if **larger**, the **right aisle**. At each subsequent desk, the same rule applies. This is exactly how a BST works — at every node, the **left child has a smaller value** and the **right child has a larger value**. To find a book, you don't search every shelf (which would be `O(n)` like a linear search); you make smart, branching decisions (`O(log n)` on average). > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** A BST enables **binary search on a dynamic, mutable data structure** that supports efficient insertions and deletions — something a sorted array cannot do as efficiently. --- ### 📊 Key Properties & Standard Metrics - **Height (h):** Length of the longest path from root to a leaf. For a balanced BST with `n` nodes, $h = O(\log n)$. - **Depth of a node:** Distance from the root (root has depth 0). - **Total nodes `n`** in a BST of height `h` satisfies: $\min(h+1, 1) \le n \le 2^{h+1} - 1$. - **Average-case time complexity** for search/insert/delete: $O(\log n)$. - **Worst-case time complexity:** $O(n)$ — happens when the BST degenerates into a **skewed tree** (essentially a linked list). ### 🏗️ Node Structure (C-style) A typical BST node has three fields: ```c struct Node { int key; struct Node *left; struct Node *right; }; ``` For exam purposes, draw a BST as a set of circles connected by arrows — **left child goes down-left, right child goes down-right**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 📐 The Four Core Operations — Logic Breakdown ### 🟢 Operation 1: CREATION of a BST A BST is **created** by inserting keys one at a time into an initially empty tree. The order of insertion directly determines the **shape** of the tree. **Logic Steps:** 1. Start with an **empty BST** (`root = NULL`). 2. Read the first key — it becomes the **root**. 3. For every subsequent key, call the **Insert** procedure. 4. After all insertions, the tree is said to be "constructed." > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** Different insertion orders yield **different BST shapes** for the same set of keys. For example, inserting `50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80` produces a **balanced** tree, while inserting `20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80` produces a **right-skewed degenerate tree** (worst case). --- ### 🟢 Operation 2: INSERTION **Logic Steps (Algorithmic):** 1. **Start** at the **root node**. 2. If the root is `NULL`, create a new node and make it the root. **Return.** 3. Compare the new key `K` with the current node's key. 4. If `K < current.key`: - If `current.left == NULL`, insert `K` as the left child. - Else, **recurse** into the left subtree. 5. If `K > current.key`: - If `current.right == NULL`, insert `K` as the right child. - Else, **recurse** into the right subtree. 6. If `K == current.key` → **Duplicate detected** → typically rejected (or handled as per problem statement). **Why this works:** Because we always descend to a leaf position, the BST ordering property is automatically maintained at every step — the new node has no children, so no invariant is violated above it. --- ### 🟢 Operation 3: SEARCH **Logic Steps:** 1. Begin at the **root**. 2. If root is `NULL` → **Not Found**. Return `NULL`. 3. If `K == root.key` → **Found!** Return root. 4. If `K < root.key` → **Recurse** into the **left subtree**. 5. If `K > root.key` → **Recurse** into the **right subtree**. The search proceeds in a single **top-down path**, eliminating roughly half the remaining nodes at each step (when balanced). **Three outcomes of search:** - ✅ **Successful Search** — key found at some node. - ❌ **Unsuccessful Search** — reached a `NULL` pointer; key absent. - 🔁 **Duplicate Search** — finds an existing key (same as successful). --- ### 🟢 Operation 4: DELETION (The Most Exam-Critical Operation) Deletion is the **trickiest** BST operation because removing a node can break the BST ordering property. It has **three distinct cases** based on the number of children of the node to be deleted. #### Case A: Node is a **LEAF** (0 children) - **Logic:** Simply remove the node by setting its parent's pointer to `NULL`. - **Cost:** $O(1)$ — no restructuring needed. #### Case B: Node has **EXACTLY ONE CHILD** (left or right) - **Logic:** Replace the deleted node with its single child. Bypass the node in its parent. - **Cost:** $O(1)$ — one pointer adjustment. #### Case C: Node has **TWO CHILDREN** ⭐ (Most Important!) - **Logic:** Replace the node's key with either: - The **Inorder Successor** (smallest key in the right subtree), OR - The **Inorder Predecessor** (largest key in the left subtree). - Then **recursively delete** the successor/predecessor from the subtree (which will fall into Case A or B). - **Why this works:** The inorder successor is the **next larger key**, so the BST ordering remains valid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inorder Successor of a node `N`** = leftmost node in `N`'s right subtree. To find it: go to `N.right`, then keep going left until `NULL`. > > **Inorder Predecessor of a node `N`** = rightmost node in `N`'s left subtree. To find it: go to `N.left`, then keep going right until `NULL`. --- ## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | |---|---|---| | BST Ordering | $\text{key}(L) < k < \text{key}(R)$ | $L$ = left subtree, $R$ = right subtree | | Min nodes for height $h$ | $n_{\min} = h + 1$ | Skewed (worst case) | | Max nodes for height $h$ | $n_{\max} = 2^{h+1} - 1$ | Perfect/full tree (best case) | | Height (balanced, $n$ nodes) | $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | Average case | | Average search/insert/delete | $O(\log n)$ | Balanced BST | | Worst-case search/insert/delete | $O(n)$ | Skewed BST | | Inorder Traversal of BST | Produces **sorted ascending** sequence | Crucial property | | Time to find inorder successor | $O(h)$ | $h$ = height | ### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Database indexing** (B-trees are generalizations of BSTs). - **File system directories** for fast lookup. - **Auto-complete / spell checkers** (sorted dictionary lookups). - **Compiler symbol tables** for variable/identifier storage. - **Network routing tables** for IP address lookups. - **Priority schedulers** in operating systems. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples ## 🧪 Worked Example 1: BST CREATION from a Key Sequence **Problem:** Construct a BST by inserting the keys in this order: $$50, \; 30, \; 70, \; 20, \; 40, \; 60, \; 80, \; 35$$ ### Step-by-Step Trace **Step 1:** Insert `50` → Tree is empty, so `50` becomes the **root**. $$T = \{50\}$$ **Step 2:** Insert `30`. Compare with `50`: $30 < 50$, so go left. Left is empty, insert as left child. $$T = \{50 \to L:30\}$$ **Step 3:** Insert `70`. Compare with `50`: $70 > 50$, so go right. Right is empty, insert. $$T = \{50 \to L:30, \; R:70\}$$ **Step 4:** Insert `20`. $20 < 50$ → go left to `30`. $20 < 30$ → go left. Empty, insert. **Step 5:** Insert `40`. $40 < 50$ → go left to `30`. $40 > 30$ → go right. Empty, insert. **Step 6:** Insert `60`. $60 > 50$ → go right to `70`. $60 < 70$ → go left. Empty, insert. **Step 7:** Insert `80`. $80 > 50$ → go right to `70`. $80 > 70$ → go right. Empty, insert. **Step 8:** Insert `35`. $35 < 50$ → left to `30`. $35 > 30$ → right to `40`. $35 < 40$ → left. Empty, insert. **Final BST:** ``` 50 / \ 30 70 / \ / \ 20 40 60 80 / 35 ``` **Verification (Inorder Traversal gives sorted order):** $$20, \; 30, \; 35, \; 40, \; 50, \; 60, \; 70, \; 80 \; \checkmark$$ --- ## 🧪 Worked Example 2: SEARCH Operation **Problem:** Search for key `35` in the BST above. ### Step-by-Step Trace | Step | Current Node | Comparison | Action | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `50` | $35 < 50$? **Yes** | Go to **left** child | | 2 | `30` | $35 > 30$? **Yes** | Go to **right** child | | 3 | `40` | $35 < 40$? **Yes** | Go to **left** child | | 4 | `35` | $35 == 35$? **Yes** | ✅ **FOUND!** Return node `35` | **Total comparisons:** 4 **Path:** `50 → 30 → 40 → 35` --- ## 🧪 Worked Example 3: DELETE Operation (All Three Cases) Using the BST above, perform these deletions **in sequence**: $$\text{Delete } 20, \; \text{Delete } 70, \; \text{Delete } 50$$ ### 🔸 Delete 20 (Case A — Leaf Node) `20` is a leaf (no children). Simply remove it by setting its parent's left pointer to `NULL`. ``` Before: After: 50 50 / \ / \ 30 70 30 70 / \ / \ / \ / \ [20] 40 60 80 (X) 40 60 80 / / 35 35 ``` **Result:** `20` removed cleanly. BST property preserved. ✓ --- ### 🔸 Delete 70 (Case B — One Child) `70` has only a left child (`60`). Replace `70` with `60` in the tree. **Logic:** - Find inorder successor of `70`: leftmost of right subtree of `70`. But right subtree of `70` is empty (since `80` is right child of `70`, not of any node under it). Wait — `70` has left child `60` and right child `80`. To delete `70` with one child via standard "bypass" technique: since `70` has two children here actually, let me reconsider. **Re-examination:** `70` has left child `60` and right child `80` → **TWO children** → This is **Case C**, not Case B! Let me redo this properly: **Case C — Two Children:** Find inorder successor of `70` = leftmost node in right subtree of `70`. Right subtree root = `80`. Leftmost of `80` = `80` itself (since `80` has no left child). So **inorder successor = 80**. **Replacement:** Copy `80` into node `70`. Then delete original `80` from right subtree (which is a leaf → Case A). ``` Before: After: 50 50 / \ / \ 30 70 30 80 / \ / \ / \ / (X) 40 60 80 (X) 40 60 (X) / / 35 35 ``` --- ### 🔸 Delete 50 (Case C — Two Children, Root) `50` (the root) has two children: `30` and `80`. **Step 1:** Find **inorder successor** of `50` = leftmost node in right subtree of `50`. Right subtree of `50` has root `80`. Left child of `80` = `60`. Left child of `60` = `NULL`. So inorder successor = `60`. **Step 2:** Copy `60` into root's key position. Root now has key `60`. **Step 3:** Delete the original `60` from its position. Original `60` is a leaf (no children) → **Case A**, just remove. ``` Before: After: 50 60 / \ / \ 30 80 30 80 / \ / / \ / (X) 40 60 (X) (X) 40 (X) (X) / / 35 35 ``` **Final BST After All Deletions:** ``` 60 / \ 30 80 \ / 40 (X) / 35 ``` **Verification (Inorder):** $30, 35, 40, 60, 80$ ✓ Sorted order preserved. --- ## 📊 Analytical Matrix: Complexity Summary | Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space | |---|---|---|---|---| | Search | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ recursion stack | | Insert | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ recursion stack | | Delete | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ recursion stack | | Inorder Traversal | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ recursion stack | | Find Min/Max | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ recursion stack | Here, $n$ = number of nodes, $h$ = height of tree. For balanced BST, $h \approx \log_2 n$. --- ## 🧪 Worked Example 4: Mathematical Height & Node Relation **Problem:** A balanced BST has **height $h = 3$**. Find the **minimum and maximum number of nodes**. ### Derivation **Minimum nodes** (skewed tree with height 3, one node per level): $$n_{\min} = h + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \; \text{nodes}$$ **Maximum nodes** (perfect binary tree, all levels completely filled): $$n_{\max} = 2^{h+1} - 1 = 2^{3+1} - 1 = 2^4 - 1 = 16 - 1 = 15 \; \text{nodes}$$ $$\boxed{4 \le n \le 15}$$ **Interpretation:** A height-3 BST can hold between 4 and 15 nodes while maintaining height 3. > [!NOTE] > **Reverse problem:** If a BST has $n = 1000$ nodes and is balanced, the height is: > > $$h \approx \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor = \lfloor \log_2 1000 \rfloor = \lfloor 9.97 \rfloor = 9$$ > > So search takes at most 9 comparisons — extremely efficient compared to linear search's 1000 comparisons! # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 🌳 Diagram 1: A Sample BST (Generic) ```mermaid graph TD root50((50)) left30((30)) right70((70)) node20((20)) node40((40)) node60((60)) node80((80)) null1[ ]:::invisible null2[ ]:::invisible null3[ ]:::invisible null4[ ]:::invisible root50 --> left30 root50 --> right70 left30 --> node20 left30 --> node40 right70 --> node60 right70 --> node80 node20 -.-> null1 node40 -.-> null2 node60 -.-> null3 node80 -.-> null4 classDef invisible fill:none,stroke:none ``` **Reading the diagram:** `50` is the root. Its left child `30` is smaller; its right child `70` is larger. Subtrees follow the same rule. --- ## 🌳 Diagram 2: INSERTION Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start: New Key K arrives]) --> B{Root NULL?} B -- Yes --> C[Create new node
Make it the root
Return] B -- No --> D{K < current.key?} D -- Yes --> E{current.left
NULL?} D -- No --> F{current.right
NULL?} E -- Yes --> G[Insert K as
current.left
Return] E -- No --> H[Move to
current.left
Loop back to D] F -- Yes --> I[Insert K as
current.right
Return] F -- No --> J[Move to
current.right
Loop back to D] classDef startend fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef process fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 class A,C,G,I startend class B,D,E,F decision class H,J process ``` --- ## 🌳 Diagram 3: DELETION Case Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start: Delete Key K]) --> B[Search for node N
with key K] B --> C{N found?} C -- No --> D[Return
Not Found] C -- Yes --> E{N has how
many children?} E -- Zero children
LEAF --> F[Case A:
Remove N
parent ptr = NULL] E -- One child --> G[Case B:
Replace N with
its single child] E -- Two children --> H[Case C:
Find inorder
successor S] H --> I[Copy S.key
into N.key] I --> J[Recursively
delete S from
right subtree] J --> K[Case A or B
applies to S] F --> L([End]) G --> L K --> L classDef startend fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef caseBox fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef process fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 class A,L startend class F,G,H caseBox class B,I,J,K process ``` --- ## 🌳 Diagram 4: BST Search Path (Visual Trace) ```mermaid graph TD s([Search Key: 35]) --> r((50)) r -- 35 less than 50
GO LEFT --> n30((30)) n30 -- 35 greater than 30
GO RIGHT --> n40((40)) n40 -- 35 less than 40
GO LEFT --> n35((35)) n35 -- 35 equals 35 --> found((FOUND)) classDef foundStyle fill:#00FF00,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef pathStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000 class found foundStyle class r,n30,n40,n35 pathStyle ``` **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow is labeled with the comparison result, showing the **single top-down path** traversed during the search. --- ## 🏗️ Diagram 5: Functional Architecture of BST Operations ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input[Input Layer] I1[Key Sequence] I2[Single Key Insert] I3[Search Key] I4[Delete Key] end subgraph Core[Core BST Engine] C1[Insert Routine] C2[Search Routine] C3[Delete Routine] C4[Tree Traversal
Inorder, Preorder,
Postorder] end subgraph Invariant[BST Invariant Checker] V1{L : less than : R
maintained?} end subgraph Output[Output Layer] O1[Constructed BST] O2[Position: Found
Not Found] O3[Restructured BST] O4[Sorted Key List] end I1 --> C1 I2 --> C1 I3 --> C2 I4 --> C3 C1 --> V1 C3 --> V1 V1 -- Yes --> O1 V1 -- Yes --> O3 C2 --> O2 C1 --> C4 C3 --> C4 C4 --> O4 classDef inputStyle fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000 classDef coreStyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000 classDef validStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000 classDef outputStyle fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000 class I1,I2,I3,I4 inputStyle class C1,C2,C3,C4 coreStyle class V1 validStyle class O1,O2,O3,O4 outputStyle ``` **Interpretation:** This shows how all four operations (create, insert, search, delete) feed into a single BST engine, which must always satisfy the BST invariant at every step, and produces the final tree or query result. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style] **Q: Define a Binary Search Tree. State the BST ordering property with an example.** **Model Answer:** A **Binary Search Tree (BST)** is a binary tree data structure in which for every node, all keys in its left subtree are **strictly less** than the node's key, and all keys in its right subtree are **strictly greater** than the node's key. **BST Ordering Property:** For any node `N` with key `k`: - $\text{key}(\text{left subtree}) < k$ - $\text{key}(\text{right subtree}) > k$ **Example:** ``` 50 / \ 30 70 / \ / \ 20 40 60 80 ``` Here, at node `50`: left subtree $\{30, 20, 40\}$ all $< 50$ ✓; right subtree $\{70, 60, 80\}$ all $> 50$ ✓. The property holds recursively at every node. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style] **Q: What is the inorder successor of a node in a BST? How is it used in deletion?** **Model Answer:** The **inorder successor** of a node `N` in a BST is the node with the **smallest key greater than `N.key`**. Algorithmically, it is the **leftmost node in the right subtree of `N`**. **Procedure to find inorder successor of `N`:** 1. Move to `N.right`. 2. Keep moving to the left child until the left child is `NULL`. 3. The last non-`NULL` node is the inorder successor. **Use in Deletion:** When a node `N` to be deleted has **two children**, we cannot simply remove it. Instead, we: 1. Find the inorder successor `S` of `N`. 2. Copy `S.key` into `N.key` (replacing `N`'s value). 3. Recursively delete `S` from the right subtree. This preserves the BST ordering property because `S` is the **next larger key**, so replacing `N` with `S` keeps the sorted inorder sequence intact. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### 🔷 Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style] **Q: (a)** Construct a Binary Search Tree (BST) by inserting the following keys in the given order: $$50, \; 25, \; 75, \; 10, \; 30, \; 60, \; 90, \; 27, \; 65$$ Show the BST after each insertion. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Perform the following operations on the resulting BST and show the tree after each operation: 1. Search for the key `30`. 2. Delete the key `25`. 3. Delete the key `50` (the root). Show the deletion steps clearly, identifying which case (A, B, or C) applies in each deletion. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A **Part (a) — Step-by-Step BST Construction** | Step | Key Inserted | Action | Tree State | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `50` | Empty tree → root = 50 | `{50}` | | 2 | `25` | $25 < 50$ → left of 50 | `{50, L:25}` | | 3 | `75` | $75 > 50$ → right of 50 | `{50, L:25, R:75}` | | 4 | `10` | $10 < 50 \to 10 < 25$ → left of 25 | 4 nodes | | 5 | `30` | $30 < 50 \to 30 > 25$ → right of 25 | 5 nodes | | 6 | `60` | $60 > 50 \to 60 < 75$ → left of 75 | 6 nodes | | 7 | `90` | $90 > 50 \to 90 > 75$ → right of 75 | 7 nodes | | 8 | `27` | $27 < 50 \to 27 > 25 \to 27 < 30$ → left of 30 | 8 nodes | | 9 | `65` | $65 > 50 \to 65 < 75 \to 65 > 60$ → right of 60 | 9 nodes | **Final BST after Part (a):** ``` 50 / \ 25 75 / \ / \ 10 30 60 90 / \ 27 65 ``` --- **Part (b) — Operations on the BST** **Operation 1: Search for `30`** Compare with root `50`: $30 < 50$ → go left to `25`. Compare with `25`: $30 > 25$ → go right to `30`. Compare with `30`: $30 == 30$ → ✅ **FOUND at node 30**. **Path traversed:** `50 → 25 → 30`. Number of comparisons: **3**. --- **Operation 2: Delete `25`** Node `25` has two children (`10` and `30`) → **Case C: Two children**. **Find inorder successor of `25`:** - Right subtree of `25` has root `30`. - Leftmost node in this subtree: start at `30`, go left → `27`. Left of `27` is `NULL`. - So inorder successor = `27`. **Replacement:** Copy `27` into node `25`. Node `25` now has key `27`. **Recursive deletion:** Delete original `27`. It is a **leaf (Case A)** → simply remove. **Tree after deleting `25`:** ``` 50 / \ 27 75 / \ / \ 10 30 60 90 \ 65 ``` **Inorder check:** $10, 27, 30, 50, 60, 65, 75, 90$ ✓ Sorted. --- **Operation 3: Delete `50` (the root)** Root `50` has two children (`27` and `75`) → **Case C: Two children**. **Find inorder successor of `50`:** - Right subtree of `50` has root `75`. - Leftmost node: start at `75`, go left → `60`. Left of `60` is `NULL`. - So inorder successor = `60`. **Replacement:** Copy `60` into root. Root now has key `60`. **Recursive deletion:** Delete original `60`. It has one child (`65`, the right child) → **Case B: One child**. **Case B handling:** Replace `60` with its right child `65`. The parent of original `60` (which is `75`) now points to `65` instead of `60`. **Final Tree after deleting `50`:** ``` 60 / \ 27 75 / \ \ 10 30 90 \ 65 ``` **Inorder check:** $10, 27, 30, 60, 65, 75, 90$ ✓ Sorted. BST property preserved. --- ### 🔷 Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style] **Q: (a)** What is a Binary Search Tree? Explain the **deletion operation** in a BST in detail, covering all three cases (leaf node, node with one child, node with two children) with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given the following BST: ``` 45 / \ 30 60 / \ / \ 20 35 50 70 \ 55 ``` Perform the following operations sequentially and show the resulting tree after each step: 1. Insert `40` 2. Insert `25` 3. Delete `45` (the root) 4. Delete `30` State which deletion case applies in steps 3 and 4, and show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B **Part (a) — Detailed Explanation of BST Deletion** *(Definition of BST — as given in Part A Q1 above.)* The **deletion** operation in a BST removes a specified key while preserving the BST ordering property. There are **three cases** based on the number of children of the node to be deleted: **Case 1 — Leaf Node (0 children):** The node has no children. - **Action:** Simply remove the node by setting its parent's corresponding child pointer to `NULL`. - **Example:** Deleting `20` from the BST — just remove the leaf. **Case 2 — Node with One Child (left or right, not both):** - **Action:** Replace the deleted node with its single child (bypass the node). - **Example:** Deleting a node that has only a left child — the left child takes the deleted node's place. **Case 3 — Node with Two Children (most complex):** - **Action:** 1. Find the **inorder successor** `S` (leftmost node in right subtree) — or alternatively, the **inorder predecessor**. 2. Copy `S.key` into the node being deleted. 3. Recursively delete `S` from the right subtree (which will fall into Case 1 or Case 2). - **Example:** Deleting a node with both children — replace its key with the inorder successor's key, then delete the successor. **Why Case 3 works:** The inorder successor is the smallest key greater than the deleted node's key. Replacing maintains the sorted inorder property. --- **Part (b) — Step-by-Step Operations on the Given BST** **Initial BST:** ``` 45 / \ 30 60 / \ / \ 20 35 50 70 \ 55 ``` **Step 1: Insert `40`** Trace path: $40 < 45$ → left to `30`. $40 > 30$ → right to `35`. $40 > 35$ → right of `35` (empty). Insert. ``` 45 / \ 30 60 / \ / \ 20 35 50 70 \ \ 40 55 ``` **Step 2: Insert `25`** Trace path: $25 < 45$ → left to `30`. $25 < 30$ → left to `20`. $25 > 20$ → right of `20` (empty). Insert. ``` 45 / \ 30 60 / \ / \ 20 35 50 70 \ \ \ 25 40 55 ``` **Step 3: Delete `45` (root)** Node `45` has two children → **Case C applies**. **Find inorder successor of `45`:** - Right subtree of `45` has root `60`. - Leftmost in this subtree: go to `60` → left child is `50` → left child of `50` is `NULL`. - Inorder successor = `50`. **Replacement:** Copy `50` into root. Root now has key `50`. **Recursive deletion of original `50`:** Original `50` has one right child (`55`) → **Case B applies**. **Case B handling:** Replace `50` with its right child `55`. So `60`'s left child becomes `55`. ``` 50 / \ 30 60 / \ \ 20 35 70 \ \ / 25 40 55 ``` **Step 4: Delete `30`** Node `30` has two children (`20` and `35`) → **Case C applies**. **Find inorder successor of `30`:** - Right subtree of `30` has root `35`. - Leftmost: go to `35` → left child is `40` → left child of `40` is `NULL`. - Inorder successor = `40`. **Replacement:** Copy `40` into node `30`. Node `30` now has key `40`. **Recursive deletion of original `40`:** Original `40` is a leaf (no children) → **Case A applies**. **Case A handling:** Remove `40` by setting its parent's right pointer to `NULL`. So `35`'s right child becomes `NULL`. **Final Tree after all operations:** ``` 50 / \ 40 60 / \ \ 20 35 70 \ / 25 55 ``` **Inorder verification:** $20, 25, 35, 40, 50, 55, 60, 70$ ✓ Strictly ascending. BST property holds at every node. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to handle root deletion specially:** When the root has two children, students often forget to update the `root` pointer after replacement. Always explicitly note the new root. > > 2. **Confusing Inorder Successor with Inorder Predecessor:** Successor = leftmost in **right** subtree. Predecessor = rightmost in **left** subtree. Mixing these up gives an incorrect tree. > > 3. **Not recursing after replacement in Case C:** After copying the successor's value, you MUST recursively delete the original successor. Students often leave a duplicate node. > > 4. **Skipping the inorder verification:** KTU examiners love awarding the final 1-2 marks for verifying that the resulting tree's inorder traversal is sorted. Always include this check. > > 5. **Drawing arrows incorrectly:** Children must go **down-left** or **down-right** of the parent. Avoid horizontal or upward arrows. > > 6. **Assuming duplicates are allowed:** The standard BST definition (and KTU convention) **rejects duplicate keys**. If a duplicate insertion is requested, state that it is rejected. > > 7. **Forgetting to count comparisons in search:** The number of comparisons equals the **path length** in the tree, and examiners often award a mark for stating this explicitly. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ A **BST** is a binary tree where `Left < Node < Right` at every node, with no duplicates. - ✅ **Creation** of a BST is done by repeated **insertion** of keys. - ✅ The **order of insertion** determines the shape — sorted insertion produces a **skewed (worst-case)** tree. - ✅ **Inorder traversal** of a BST always yields keys in **strictly ascending sorted order** — use this to verify correctness. - ✅ **Search** is a top-down traversal following a single path: $O(\log n)$ average, $O(n)$ worst. - ✅ **Insertion** finds the correct `NULL` position and creates a new leaf node there. - ✅ **Deletion has 3 cases:** - **Case A (Leaf):** Just remove the node. - **Case B (One Child):** Replace the node with its child (bypass). - **Case C (Two Children):** Replace with **inorder successor** (or predecessor), then recursively delete the successor. - ✅ **Inorder Successor** of a node = **leftmost node in its right subtree**. - ✅ **Inorder Predecessor** of a node = **rightmost node in its left subtree**. - ✅ **Balanced BST:** $h = O(\log n)$, all operations $O(\log n)$. - ✅ **Skewed BST:** $h = O(n)$, all operations $O(n)$ — same as a linked list. - ✅ **Height-node relation:** - Minimum nodes for height $h$: $n_{\min} = h + 1$ (skewed). - Maximum nodes for height $h$: $n_{\max} = 2^{h+1} - 1$ (perfect tree). - ✅ **Node structure** has three fields: `key`, `left pointer`, `right pointer`. - ✅ **Duplicate keys** are rejected in the standard KTU definition. - ✅ After **every deletion**, verify the BST property by checking inorder traversal. - ✅ KTU exam questions on this topic typically carry **3 marks** (Part A — definition/short trace) or **7–14 marks** (Part B — full construction + multi-step operations). - ✅ Always **label which deletion case (A/B/C)** applies — examiners allocate marks specifically for this identification. - ✅ **Memory hook for deletion:** *"Leaf out, One bypass, Two replace"*: Leaf → remove; One child → bypass; Two children → replace with successor.

topics.title: Heap: Creation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heap: Creation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Heap - Creation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Heap):** A *heap* is a **complete binary tree** in which the value of every parent node is either **greater than or equal to** (Max-Heap) or **less than or equal to** (Min-Heap) the values of its children. This invariant is called the **Heap Property**.

A **complete binary tree** is a binary tree in which every level, except possibly the last, is completely filled, and all nodes in the last level are as far left as possible. The heap is the foundational data structure used to implement **Priority Queues**, **Heapsort**, and **Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm**.

### 1.2 Two Variants of Heap

| Variant | Property | Root Element | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Max-Heap** | $\text{parent} \ge \text{children}$ | Maximum element | Heapsort (ascending), Priority queue (extract max) |
| **Min-Heap** | $\text{parent} \le \text{children}$ | Minimum element | Heapsort (descending), Priority queue (extract min) |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "VIP Hospital Triage":** Imagine an emergency room where patients are triaged. The most critical patient (highest priority) is **always** moved to the front of the queue, regardless of when they arrived. When the doctor is free, the sickest patient is treated first. A *Max-Heap* operates exactly this way — the **highest priority element is always at the root (front)**, and inserting a new patient triggers a quick "bubble-up" to re-establish order. The structure is **always balanced** because patients fill beds row by row, left to right — this is the *complete binary tree* property.

### 1.4 Why Array Representation (Not Pointers)?

Because a heap is a **complete binary tree**, it has no gaps. This allows it to be stored in a **contiguous array** without any pointers, saving memory and improving cache locality. The trade-off is fixed maximum size.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Heap:** For a heap with $n$ elements stored 0-indexed:
> - **Height of heap:** $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$
> - **Number of leaf nodes:** $\lceil n/2 \rceil$
> - **Number of internal nodes:** $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Array Indexing Rules (0-Indexed) — The Most Tested Formulas

For any node at index $i$ in the array:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parent}(i) &= \left\lfloor \frac{i-1}{2} \right\rfloor \\[4pt]
\text{LeftChild}(i) &= 2i + 1 \\[4pt]
\text{RightChild}(i) &= 2i + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1-Indexed variant (also asked in KTU):**
> - $\text{Parent}(i) = \lfloor i/2 \rfloor$
> - $\text{LeftChild}(i) = 2i$
> - $\text{RightChild}(i) = 2i + 1$
>
> **Always confirm whether the question uses 0-based or 1-based indexing before writing the answer.**

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Height of heap $h$ | $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | Last level may be partially filled |
| Leaf node indices | $i \in [\lfloor n/2 \rfloor, n-1]$ | They have no children to violate property |
| Last internal node | $i = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1$ | The starting index for **Build-Heap** |
| Build-Heap (Floyd) | $O(n)$ | Bottom-up heapify — *not* $O(n \log n)$ |
| Insert (single key) | $O(\log n)$ | Bubble up through $\le h$ levels |
| Extract-Min/Max | $O(\log n)$ | Replace root + sift down |
| Heap Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | Build heap + $n$ extractions |
| Space Complexity | $O(n)$ | In-place array storage |

### 2.3 The Two Strategies for Heap Creation

**Strategy A — Top-Down (Repeated Insertion):**
- Insert keys one-by-one into an initially empty heap.
- After each insert, perform **Heapify-Up (Sift-Up / Bubble-Up)**: compare the inserted key with its parent and swap if the heap property is violated, until it reaches the root or finds its correct position.
- Total cost: $O(n \log n)$ — *not optimal* for bulk creation.

**Strategy B — Bottom-Up (Floyd's Build-Heap):**
- Place all $n$ keys into the array (forming a complete binary tree that may violate the heap property).
- Start from the **last non-leaf node** at index $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1$ and move **backwards to index 0**.
- At each index, call **Heapify-Down (Sift-Down / Bubble-Down)**: push the node down to its correct position by repeatedly swapping with the larger (max-heap) or smaller (min-heap) child.
- Total cost: $O(n)$ — **the preferred method** and a **favourite KTU question**.

### 2.4 Why is Build-Heap $O(n)$ and not $O(n \log n)$?

> [!NOTE]
> Although each individual *Heapify-Down* call costs $O(h) = O(\log n)$, most nodes are near the bottom of the tree. Roughly $n/2$ nodes are leaves (cost 0), $n/4$ are one level above (cost 1), $n/8$ cost 2, etc. The total work is bounded by the geometric series:
> $$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{h} \frac{n}{2^{i+1}} \cdot i \le n \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \frac{i}{2^{i+1}} = 2n = O(n)$$

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems:** Linux kernel uses a heap-like structure for task scheduling.
- **Network Routing:** Dijkstra's shortest path uses a min-heap for the frontier set.
- **Data Stream Processing:** Online median maintenance uses **two heaps** (max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half).
- **Event-driven simulation:** Discrete event systems maintain a min-heap of pending events keyed on time.
- **External sorting:** Replacement selection in $K$-way merge uses a tournament tree (heap variant).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Worked Example

### 3.1 The Heapify-Down Procedure (Max-Heap)

The **Heapify-Down** routine is the workhorse of Build-Heap. Given an array `A`, size `n`, and an index `i` that may violate the max-heap property, it recursively restores the property.

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**
```
HEAPIFY-DOWN(A, n, i):
    largest = i
    left    = 2*i + 1
    right   = 2*i + 2

    if left  < n AND A[left]  > A[largest]: largest = left
    if right < n AND A[right] > A[largest]: largest = right

    if largest != i:
        swap A[i] and A[largest]
        HEAPIFY-DOWN(A, n, largest)
```

**Build-Heap (Bottom-Up):**
```
BUILD-MAX-HEAP(A, n):
    for i = floor(n/2) - 1 downto 0:
        HEAPIFY-DOWN(A, n, i)
```

### 3.2 C Implementation (KTU-Standard)

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void swap(int *x, int *y) {
    int t = *x; *x = *y; *y = t;
}

void heapifyDown(int A[], int n, int i) {
    int largest = i;
    int left  = 2*i + 1;
    int right = 2*i + 2;

    if (left  < n && A[left]  > A[largest]) largest = left;
    if (right < n && A[right] > A[largest]) largest = right;

    if (largest != i) {
        swap(&A[i], &A[largest]);
        heapifyDown(A, n, largest);
    }
}

void buildMaxHeap(int A[], int n) {
    for (int i = n/2 - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        heapifyDown(A, n, i);
    }
}

void printArray(int A[], int n) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) printf("%d ", A[i]);
    printf("\n");
}
```

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example (Max-Heap Construction)

**Problem:** Construct a max-heap from the input array using Floyd's bottom-up method.

**Initial array:** $A = [4, 10, 3, 5, 1, 15, 7]$, so $n = 7$.

**Step 0 — Identify the starting index.**
$$\text{Last non-leaf index} = \left\lfloor \frac{n}{2} \right\rfloor - 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{7}{2} \right\rfloor - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2$$

So we call `heapifyDown` at $i = 2, 1, 0$ in that order.

**Initial Tree (before any operation):**
```
            4  (i=0)
          /   \
        10(i1)  3(i=2)
        / \    / \
      5(i3) 1(i4) 15(i5) 7(i6)
```

**Step 1 — Heapify-Down at $i = 2$ (value 3):**
- Left child = $A[5] = 15$, Right child = $A[6] = 7$.
- Largest of $\{3, 15, 7\}$ is $15$ at index 5. Since $5 \neq 2$, swap $A[2]$ and $A[5]$.
- Array becomes $[4, 10, 15, 5, 1, 3, 7]$.
- Recurse on index 5: children of 5 are $A[11]$ and $A[12]$ — both out of bounds. **Stop.**

```
           4
         /   \
       10     15
       / \    / \
      5   1  3   7
```

**Step 2 — Heapify-Down at $i = 1$ (value 10):**
- Left child = $A[3] = 5$, Right child = $A[4] = 1$.
- Largest of $\{10, 5, 1\}$ is $10$ at index 1. No swap. **Stop.**

```
           4
         /   \
       10     15
       / \    / \
      5   1  3   7
```

**Step 3 — Heapify-Down at $i = 0$ (value 4):**
- Left child = $A[1] = 10$, Right child = $A[2] = 15$.
- Largest of $\{4, 10, 15\}$ is $15$ at index 2. Swap $A[0]$ and $A[2]$.
- Array becomes $[15, 10, 4, 5, 1, 3, 7]$.
- Recurse on index 2: Left = $A[5] = 3$, Right = $A[6] = 7$. Largest = 7 at index 6. Swap.
- Array becomes $[15, 10, 7, 5, 1, 3, 4]$.
- Recurse on index 6: no children. **Stop.**

```
            15
          /    \
        10      7
       /  \    / \
      5   1   3   4
```

**Final Max-Heap Array:** $A = [15, 10, 7, 5, 1, 3, 4]$.

### 3.4 Verification — Parent ≥ Children

| Index $i$ | Value | Parent Idx | Parent Val | Satisfies Max-Heap? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 15 | — | — | Root |
| 1 | 10 | 0 | 15 | $15 \ge 10$ ✓ |
| 2 | 7 | 0 | 15 | $15 \ge 7$ ✓ |
| 3 | 5 | 1 | 10 | $10 \ge 5$ ✓ |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | 10 | $10 \ge 1$ ✓ |
| 5 | 3 | 2 | 7 | $7 \ge 3$ ✓ |
| 6 | 4 | 2 | 7 | $7 \ge 4$ ✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heap Property verified.** The maximum element (15) is at the root, which is the defining feature of a max-heap.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Block Diagram of Build-Heap Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Input Array A of size n]) --> fillArr[Place all n elements into array]
    fillArr --> calcLast["Compute last = floor of n/2 minus 1"]
    calcLast --> loopCheck{For i = last down to 0}
    loopCheck -- yes --> callHeap[Call Heapify Down on A, n, i]
    callHeap --> findLargest[Find largest among A i, A left, A right]
    findLargest --> compareCheck{Largest is not i}
    compareCheck -- yes --> swapNode[Swap A i and A largest]
    swapNode --> recurse[Recurse Heapify Down on new index]
    recurse --> findLargest
    compareCheck -- no --> nextI[Decrement i]
    nextI --> loopCheck
    loopCheck -- no --> doneA([Max Heap Built])
    nextI --> nextI
    style startA fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style doneA fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style callHeap fill:#f57c00,color:#ffffff
    style swapNode fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Final Max-Heap Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    root15["15 ROOT INDEX 0"] --> left10["10 INDEX 1"]
    root15 --> right7["7 INDEX 2"]
    left10 --> n5["5 INDEX 3"]
    left10 --> n1["1 INDEX 4"]
    right7 --> n3["3 INDEX 5"]
    right7 --> n4["4 INDEX 6"]
    style root15 fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:3px
    style left10 fill:#b3e5fc
    style right7 fill:#b3e5fc
    style n5 fill:#c8e6c9
    style n1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style n3 fill:#c8e6c9
    style n4 fill:#c8e6c9
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Memory Layout (Array View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    arr0["A 0  15"] --> arr1["A 1  10"] --> arr2["A 2  7"] --> arr3["A 3  5"] --> arr4["A 4  1"] --> arr5["A 5  3"] --> arr6["A 6  4"]
    style arr0 fill:#fff59d
    style arr1 fill:#fff59d
    style arr2 fill:#fff59d
    style arr3 fill:#fff59d
    style arr4 fill:#fff59d
    style arr5 fill:#fff59d
    style arr6 fill:#fff59d
```

### 4.4 Recurrence Relation for Build-Heap Cost

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1} O(h - \text{level}(i)) \le \sum_{k=0}^{h} \frac{n}{2^{k+1}} \cdot k
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &\le n \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{k}{2^{k+1}} \\[4pt]
&= n \cdot 2 \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{k+1} \\[4pt]
&= n \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{1/2}{(1 - 1/2)^2} = 2n
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $T(n) = O(n)$, proving that **Build-Heap is linear time**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*Define a max-heap. State two applications of a heap data structure.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **max-heap** is a complete binary tree in which the value at every parent node is **greater than or equal to** the values at its two child nodes. The largest element of the heap is therefore always at the root.

Two applications:
1. **Priority Queue** implementation — the maximum (or minimum) priority element is always accessible in $O(1)$ at the root.
2. **Heap Sort** — sorts an array in $O(n \log n)$ time using in-place heap operations.

*(Other valid answers: Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm, Top-K elements, Median maintenance, Huffman-like scheduling.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]**
*For an array representation of a max-heap, given a node at index $i$ (0-based), write the formulas for its parent and children. Why is a heap stored as an array rather than a linked structure?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Formulas (0-indexed):
$$\text{Parent}(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{i-1}{2} \right\rfloor, \quad \text{Left}(i) = 2i+1, \quad \text{Right}(i) = 2i+2$$

A heap is stored as an array because it is always a **complete binary tree**, meaning there are no gaps. This allows the parent-child relationship to be computed arithmetically using the above formulas, eliminating the need for explicit pointers. This saves memory ($O(n)$ vs $2n$ pointers) and improves **cache locality**, which makes heap operations faster in practice.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Choice)

> **ESE Module Choice Pattern:** Answer **any one** full question from this module. Each question carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Main Exam]

**(a) (7 marks)** Construct a **max-heap** from the following array using the **bottom-up (Floyd's) heapify** method. Show every step clearly with intermediate array states and the final heap tree.

$$A = [20, 15, 30, 5, 10, 25, 40, 35]$$

**(b) (7 marks)** Write the **C function** to construct a max-heap using bottom-up heapify. What is its time complexity? Justify.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Step-by-step construction:**

$n = 8$. Last non-leaf index = $\lfloor 8/2 \rfloor - 1 = 3$.

**Step 1 — Heapify at $i=3$ (value 5):**
- Left $= A[7] = 35$, no right child. Largest = 35 at index 7. Swap.
- Array: $[20, 15, 30, 35, 10, 25, 40, 5]$. No children of 7 — stop.

**Step 2 — Heapify at $i=2$ (value 30):**
- Left $= A[5] = 25$, Right $= A[6] = 40$. Largest = 40 at index 6. Swap.
- Array: $[20, 15, 40, 35, 10, 25, 30, 5]$. Children of 6 out of bounds — stop.

**Step 3 — Heapify at $i=1$ (value 15):**
- Left $= A[3] = 35$, Right $= A[4] = 10$. Largest = 35 at index 3. Swap.
- Array: $[20, 35, 40, 15, 10, 25, 30, 5]$.
- Recurse on $i=3$: Left $= A[7] = 5$, no right. Largest = 15 itself. **Stop.**

**Step 4 — Heapify at $i=0$ (value 20):**
- Left $= A[1] = 35$, Right $= A[2] = 40$. Largest = 40 at index 2. Swap.
- Array: $[40, 35, 20, 15, 10, 25, 30, 5]$.
- Recurse on $i=2$: Left $= A[5] = 25$, Right $= A[6] = 30$. Largest = 30 at index 6. Swap.
- Array: $[40, 35, 30, 15, 10, 25, 20, 5]$. Children of 6 out of bounds — stop.

**Final Max-Heap:** $A = [40, 35, 30, 15, 10, 25, 20, 5]$.

**Final Heap Tree:**
```
              40
            /    \
          35      30
         /  \    /  \
        15  10  25  20
       /
      5
```

**Part (b) — Code:**
```c
void buildMaxHeap(int A[], int n) {
    for (int i = n/2 - 1; i >= 0; i--)
        heapifyDown(A, n, i);
}
```
*(Use the `heapifyDown` function defined in Section 3.2.)*

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$. Justification: The geometric series argument — most nodes are near the leaves and perform $O(1)$ work, while few nodes near the root do $O(\log n)$ work. Summing gives $\sum_{k=0}^{h} \frac{n}{2^{k+1}} \cdot k \le 2n = O(n)$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Exam]

**(a) (7 marks)** Compare **Top-Down** and **Bottom-Up** heap construction methods. Show the heap built by the **Top-Down (insert + heapify-up) method** for the sequence of insertions: $25, 35, 20, 30, 10, 40$.

**(b) (7 marks)** A min-heap is stored in the array: $[2, 7, 5, 10, 12, 6, 8, 15, 14]$. After inserting key **3**, what is the new array? Show all intermediate bubble-up steps.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparison:**

| Aspect | Top-Down (Insertion) | Bottom-Up (Build-Heap) |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Insert one key at a time; bubble up | Place all keys; heapify-down from last non-leaf |
| Start index | i = 0 (insertion point) | i = floor(n/2) − 1 |
| Time complexity | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Best use | Online / streaming insertions | Bulk construction from existing data |

**Top-down construction for the sequence $25, 35, 20, 30, 10, 40$:**

1. Insert 25: `[25]`
2. Insert 35: `[25, 35]` → bubble up → `[35, 25]`
3. Insert 20: `[35, 25, 20]`
4. Insert 30: `[35, 25, 20, 30]` → bubble up at 3 → swap 30, 25 → `[35, 30, 20, 25]`
5. Insert 10: `[35, 30, 20, 25, 10]`
6. Insert 40: `[35, 30, 20, 25, 10, 40]` → bubble up at 5 → swap with 20 → `[35, 30, 40, 25, 10, 20]` → swap with 35 → `[40, 30, 35, 25, 10, 20]`

**Final Max-Heap (Top-Down):** `[40, 30, 35, 25, 10, 20]`

**Part (b) — Min-Heap insertion of 3:**

Initial: `[2, 7, 5, 10, 12, 6, 8, 15, 14]`. After inserting 3, array becomes:
`[2, 7, 5, 10, 12, 6, 8, 15, 14, 3]` (index 9).

- **Bubble-up step 1:** Compare $A[9] = 3$ with parent $A[4] = 12$. $3 < 12$, so swap.
- Array: `[2, 7, 5, 10, 3, 6, 8, 15, 14, 12]`
- **Bubble-up step 2:** Compare $A[4] = 3$ with parent $A[1] = 7$. $3 < 7$, so swap.
- Array: `[2, 3, 5, 10, 7, 6, 8, 15, 14, 12]`
- **Bubble-up step 3:** Compare $A[1] = 3$ with parent $A[0] = 2$. $3 > 2$, so **stop** (heap property satisfied).

**Final Min-Heap Array:** `[2, 3, 5, 10, 7, 6, 8, 15, 14, 12]`.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that cost 2–3 marks each in KTU evaluation:**
> 1. **Indexing confusion (0-based vs 1-based):** The most common error. If the question uses 1-based, parent is $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor$. If 0-based, parent is $\lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$. Writing the wrong formula loses full marks for that step.
> 2. **Starting index of Build-Heap:** Students often start at $i = 0$ (this is *correct* in terms of coverage, but wastes work since leaves are already trivially heaps) or $i = n/2$ (this is **wrong** — it skips the actual last internal node). The correct start is $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1$.
> 3. **Confusing Heapify-Up with Heapify-Down:** Top-down insertion uses **sift-up**; bottom-up construction uses **sift-down**. Using the wrong one gives a heap, but built by the wrong method, losing the method-mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to recurse after a swap:** After swapping with the largest child, you **must recursively call heapify on the new position**, or the property may still be violated further down.
> 5. **Swapping the wrong child:** Always swap with the **larger (max-heap) or smaller (min-heap)** child, not arbitrarily. The wrong choice can violate the property in a deeper subtree.
> 6. **Time-complexity claim of $O(n \log n)$ for Build-Heap:** This is wrong. It is $O(n)$. The $O(n \log n)$ answer applies to the **top-down** method only.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Before you move on, make sure you can tick every box below:**

- [ ] A heap is a **complete binary tree** satisfying the **heap property** (max-heap or min-heap).
- [ ] The **array representation** works because the tree has no gaps — every level is filled left-to-right.
- [ ] **Indexing formulas (0-based):** Parent $= (i-1)/2$, Left $= 2i+1$, Right $= 2i+2$.
- [ ] **Indexing formulas (1-based):** Parent $= \lfloor i/2 \rfloor$, Left $= 2i$, Right $= 2i+1$.
- [ ] **Height** of a heap of $n$ elements: $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$.
- [ ] **Last non-leaf (internal) node index:** $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1$ (0-based) or $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ (1-based).
- [ ] **Heapify-Down (Sift-Down):** Used in Build-Heap; pushes a violating node downward by swapping with the **largest child** (max-heap).
- [ ] **Heapify-Up (Sift-Up / Bubble-Up):** Used in Insert; pushes a violating node upward by swapping with its **parent**.
- [ ] **Top-Down (insert) Build:** $O(n \log n)$ time.
- [ ] **Bottom-Up (Floyd) Build-Heap:** $O(n)$ time — preferred.
- [ ] **Build-Heap algorithm:** loop `i` from `floor(n/2)-1` down to `0`, calling heapify-down at each step.
- [ ] **Heap Sort** runs in $O(n \log n)$ worst-case — better than quicksort's $O(n^2)$ worst case.
- [ ] **Space complexity** of a heap is $O(n)$ — no pointer overhead.
- [ ] **Real-world uses:** priority queues, Dijkstra, event-driven simulation, Huffman, Top-K, median maintenance.
- [ ] The **root always holds the extremum** (max for max-heap, min for min-heap).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graph: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Graph: Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Graph

A **Graph** is a non-linear, non-hierarchical data structure consisting of a finite set of **vertices** (also called nodes) and a finite set of **edges** (also called arcs or links) that connect pairs of vertices. Formally, it is mathematically represented as an ordered pair:

$$G = (V, E)$$

Where:
- $V$ is a **non-empty, finite set of vertices** (nodes). That is, $V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, \dots, v_n\}$
- $E$ is a **set of edges** (connections). Each edge $e \in E$ is a pair $(v_i, v_j)$ representing a connection between two vertices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A graph is the most general data structure in computer science. Unlike trees (which are hierarchical and acyclic), graphs can model **arbitrary relationships** between entities, including cycles, self-loops, and multiple connections between the same pair of nodes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a road trip across Kerala. You have a map with **cities** like Kochi, Trivandrum, Kozhikode, and Thrissur. The **roads** connecting these cities don't follow a strict tree-like hierarchy — they form a web where:
- A city can connect to **multiple other cities** (Kochi connects to both Thrissur and Alappuzha).
- Roads can be **one-way** (Kochi → Munnar) or **two-way** (Kochi ↔ Thrissrum).
- You can return to a starting city via different paths (forming a **cycle**).
- Two cities may be connected by **multiple roads** (e.g., NH-66 and NH-544 between Kochi and Kozhikode).

In this analogy:
- **Cities = Vertices (Nodes)**
- **Roads = Edges**
- The entire **map = Graph**

### 1.3 Fundamental Components of a Graph

A graph is composed of two primary elements. Understanding these is **critical for KTU board exams**:

| Component | Symbol | Description | Example (Road Map) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vertex / Node** | $V$ | A fundamental unit or entity in the graph | A city (Kochi, Trivandrum) |
| **Edge / Arc** | $E$ | A connection or relationship between two vertices | A road connecting two cities |
| **Adjacent Vertices** | — | Two vertices connected by an edge | Kochi and Thrissur |
| **Degree of Vertex** | $\deg(v)$ | Number of edges incident to a vertex | Kochi has 4 connecting roads |
| **Path** | — | A sequence of vertices where each adjacent pair is connected | Kochi → Alappuzha → Trivandrum |
| **Cycle** | — | A path that starts and ends at the same vertex | Kochi → Thrissur → Kochi |

> [!NOTE]
> **Empty Graph:** A graph with $n$ vertices and **zero edges** is called an *empty graph* or *null graph*, denoted as $N_n$ or simply a graph with $E = \emptyset$.

### 1.4 Order and Size of a Graph

Two important metrics used in KTU numerical problems:

- **Order of a graph ($n$ or $\vert V \vert$)**: The total number of vertices.
- **Size of a graph ($m$ or $\vert E \vert$)**: The total number of edges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an undirected graph with no self-loops or multi-edges, the maximum number of edges is bounded by the combinatorial formula:
>
> $$\vert E \vert_{max} = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$
>
> This represents a **complete graph** $K_n$, where every vertex is connected to every other vertex.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Graphs (KTU High-Priority Topic)

Graphs can be classified based on **directionality** and **connectivity multiplicity**:

#### A. Based on Edge Direction

- **Undirected Graph**: Edges have no direction. If there is an edge between $u$ and $v$, you can traverse it in both directions. Mathematically: $(u, v) \equiv (v, u)$.
- **Directed Graph (Digraph)**: Edges have a specific direction. An edge from $u$ to $v$ is written as $\langle u, v \rangle$ or $(u, v)$ with an arrow, meaning traversal is only allowed from $u$ to $v$.

#### B. Based on Edge Multiplicity

- **Simple Graph**: An undirected graph with **no self-loops** and **no multiple edges** (no parallel edges between the same pair of vertices).
- **Multigraph**: A graph that **allows parallel edges** (multiple edges between the same pair of vertices) but typically no self-loops.
- **Pseudograph**: A graph that allows **both self-loops and parallel edges**.

#### C. Based on Connectivity

- **Connected Graph**: A graph in which there is a **path between every pair of vertices**. In a disconnected graph, there exist at least two vertices with no path connecting them.
- **Complete Graph ($K_n$)**: A simple undirected graph in which **every pair of distinct vertices is connected by an edge**.
- **Bipartite Graph**: A graph whose vertex set can be partitioned into two disjoint sets $V_1$ and $V_2$ such that every edge connects a vertex in $V_1$ to one in $V_2$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Maximum edges (undirected simple graph)** | $\vert E \vert_{max} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Upper bound for edges in a simple undirected graph | Very High |
| **Maximum edges (directed simple graph)** | $\vert E \vert_{max} = n(n-1)$ | Upper bound for edges in a simple directed graph | Very High |
| **Handshaking Lemma** | $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2\vert E \vert$ | Sum of degrees of all vertices = twice the number of edges | Very High |
| **In-degree of a vertex** | $\deg^-(v)$ | Number of edges coming **into** $v$ | High |
| **Out-degree of a vertex** | $\deg^+(v)$ | Number of edges going **out of** $v$ | High |
| **Sum of in-degrees (digraph)** | $\sum_{v \in V} \deg^-(v) = \vert E \vert$ | Total in-degrees equals number of edges | High |
| **Sum of out-degrees (digraph)** | $\sum_{v \in V} \deg^+(v) = \vert E \vert$ | Total out-degrees equals number of edges | High |
| **Number of edges in $K_n$** | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Edges in a complete graph on $n$ vertices | Medium |
| **Number of edges in $K_{m,n}$** | $m \times n$ | Edges in a complete bipartite graph | Medium |
| **Isolated vertex** | $\deg(v) = 0$ | A vertex with no incident edges | Medium |
| **Pendant vertex** | $\deg(v) = 1$ | A vertex with exactly one incident edge | Low |

### 2.3 The Handshaking Lemma — Engineering Utility

The **Handshaking Lemma** is one of the most frequently tested theorems in KTU board exams. It states:

> **Theorem:** The sum of the degrees of all vertices in an undirected graph is always **even** and equals **twice the number of edges**.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \deg(v_i) = 2 \cdot \vert E \vert$$

**Why this matters in engineering:**
- **Network Design**: When designing a computer network (LAN/WAN topology), the Handshaking Lemma helps verify if a proposed topology is feasible. If the sum of connection points is odd, the network is impossible to construct without redundant links.
- **Social Network Analysis**: In platforms like LinkedIn or Facebook, the lemma helps estimate the total number of friendships in a closed group.
- **Compiler Design**: Used in register allocation and intermediate code optimization to analyze variable usage graphs.

### 2.4 Real-World Applications of Graphs

| Application Domain | Vertex Represents | Edge Represents |
|---|---|---|
| **Google Maps / Navigation** | Intersections / Cities | Roads / Highways |
| **Social Networks (Facebook)** | Users | Friendship / Follow relationship |
| **World Wide Web** | Web pages | Hyperlinks |
| **Computer Networks** | Routers / Devices | Network connections |
| **Operating Systems** | Processes | Resource requests / Deadlock cycles |
| **Compiler Design** | Variables / Functions | Dependencies |
| **Database Systems** | Tables | Foreign key relationships |
| **AI / Machine Learning** | States | Transitions (search algorithms) |
| **Electrical Circuits** | Components (resistors) | Wires |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of Edges in an Undirected Simple Graph

**Problem Statement:** Given $n$ vertices, derive the maximum number of edges possible in a simple undirected graph.

**Step 1: Identify the problem constraints**
- A **simple graph** prohibits self-loops (no edge from a vertex to itself).
- A **simple graph** prohibits multiple edges (at most one edge between any pair of vertices).
- Each edge connects **exactly two distinct vertices**.

**Step 2: Count the number of ways to choose 2 vertices from $n$ vertices**

Since each edge requires selecting a pair of distinct vertices, this is a problem of **combinations**, not permutations, because the edge $(u, v)$ is the same as the edge $(v, u)$ in an undirected graph.

$$\text{Number of ways to choose 2 vertices from } n = \binom{n}{2}$$

**Step 3: Apply the binomial coefficient formula**

The binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{2}$ is defined as:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n!}{2! \cdot (n-2)!}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the expression**

Expanding the factorial terms:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n!}{2 \cdot (n-2)!}$$

Since $n! = n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2)!$, we substitute:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2)!}{2 \cdot (n-2)!}$$

The $(n-2)!$ terms cancel out:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n \cdot (n-1)}{2}$$

**Step 5: Final result**

$$\boxed{\vert E \vert_{max} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}}$$

This is the **maximum number of edges** in any simple undirected graph with $n$ vertices. A graph achieving this maximum is called a **complete graph $K_n$**.

### 3.2 Analytical Verification: Handshaking Lemma

**Problem Statement:** A graph has 5 vertices with degrees 3, 4, 2, 5, and 2. Determine the number of edges and verify if this graph is possible.

**Step 1: Apply the Handshaking Lemma**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \deg(v_i) = 2 \cdot \vert E \vert$$

**Step 2: Sum the degrees**

$$\sum \deg(v) = 3 + 4 + 2 + 5 + 2 = 16$$

**Step 3: Solve for the number of edges**

$$\vert E \vert = \frac{\sum \deg(v)}{2} = \frac{16}{2} = 8$$

**Step 4: Verify the result using the maximum edges formula**

For $n = 5$ vertices, the maximum possible edges in a simple undirected graph is:

$$\vert E \vert_{max} = \frac{5 \cdot (5-1)}{2} = \frac{5 \cdot 4}{2} = 10$$

Since $\vert E \vert = 8 \le 10$, the graph is **feasible**.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Undirected vs Directed Graph Edge Counts

For a clear KTU-board-ready comparison, here is a detailed analytical breakdown:

| Property | Undirected Graph | Directed Graph (Digraph) |
|---|---|---|
| **Edge representation** | $\{u, v\}$ (unordered pair) | $\langle u, v \rangle$ (ordered pair) |
| **Edge symmetry** | $(u, v) \equiv (v, u)$ | $(u, v) \neq (v, u)$ generally |
| **Maximum edges (no self-loops)** | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $n(n-1)$ |
| **Degree type** | Single degree $\deg(v)$ | In-degree $\deg^-(v)$ and out-degree $\deg^+(v)$ |
| **Total degree sum** | $\sum \deg(v) = 2\vert E \vert$ | $\sum \deg^+(v) = \sum \deg^-(v) = \vert E \vert$ |
| **Traversal direction** | Bidirectional | Unidirectional |
| **Example** | Facebook friendships | Twitter follow relationships |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A directed graph can have **twice as many edges** as an undirected graph with the same number of vertices, because directionality adds an extra degree of freedom to each connection.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Identifying Graph Type from Edge Set

**Problem:** Given $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $E = \{(1,2), (2,3), (3,4), (4,1), (1,3)\}$, classify this graph.

**Step 1:** Check for self-loops: None of the edges are of the form $(v, v)$. ✓ No self-loops.

**Step 2:** Check for parallel edges: Each pair of vertices appears at most once. ✓ No parallel edges.

**Step 3:** Check edge direction: Edges are written as $(u, v)$, not $\langle u, v \rangle$, and the problem does not specify direction. ✓ Undirected.

**Step 4:** Calculate $|E| = 5$, $n = 4$, so $|E|_{max} = \frac{4 \cdot 3}{2} = 6$. Since $5 < 6$, the graph is not complete.

**Conclusion:** This is a **simple, undirected, non-complete graph** on 4 vertices with 5 edges.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Graph Data Structure

The following diagram illustrates the high-level functional architecture of a graph and its core components, mapped to a typical KTU Data Structures exam question on graph representation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GRAPH G = V, E] --> B[Vertex Set V]
    A --> C[Edge Set E]
    B --> B1[Vertex V1]
    B --> B2[Vertex V2]
    B --> B3[Vertex V3]
    B --> B4[Vertex Vn]
    C --> C1[Edge connects V1 to V2]
    C --> C2[Edge connects V2 to V3]
    C --> C3[Edge connects V3 to V4]
    C --> C4[Edge connects V1 to Vn]
