```mermaid
    C1 --> D1{Directed?}
    C2 --> D2{Undirected?}
    C3 --> D3{Weighted?}
    C4 --> D4{Self Loop?}
    D1 --> E1[Edge with arrow: u to v]
    D2 --> E2[Edge without arrow: u to v]
    D3 --> E3[Edge with weight value]
    D4 --> E4[Edge from vertex to itself]
    E1 --> F[Classification of Graph]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> F1[Simple Graph]
    F --> F2[Complete Graph Kn]
    F --> F3[Bipartite Graph]
    F --> F4[Multigraph]
    F --> F5[Weighted Graph]
```

### 4.2 Visual Graph Examples for KTU Exam Reference

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Vertex A]
    node2[Vertex B]
    node3[Vertex C]
    node4[Vertex D]
    node1 --- node2
    node2 --- node3
    node3 --- node4
    node4 --- node1
    node1 --- node3
```

**Caption:** An undirected simple graph with 4 vertices and 5 edges. This is a representative example of a graph that frequently appears in KTU Part A questions on graph identification.

### 4.3 Directed Graph Example (Digraph)

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1[Vertex 1] --> v2[Vertex 2]
    v2 --> v3[Vertex 3]
    v3 --> v1[Vertex 1]
    v1 --> v3[Vertex 3]
```

**Caption:** A directed graph (digraph) on 3 vertices and 4 directed edges. Note the directional arrows indicating one-way traversal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** In a directed graph, the in-degree and out-degree of each vertex can be different. For example, Vertex 1 above has an in-degree of 1 (from Vertex 3) and an out-degree of 2 (to Vertex 2 and Vertex 3).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define a graph. List any four types of graphs with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**

A graph $G$ is a non-linear data structure defined as an ordered pair $G = (V, E)$, where $V$ is a non-empty finite set of **vertices** (nodes) and $E$ is a finite set of **edges** (connections) that link pairs of vertices.

**Four types of graphs:**

1. **Undirected Graph**: A graph in which edges do not have any direction; traversal is bidirectional.
2. **Directed Graph (Digraph)**: A graph in which every edge has a specified direction, indicated by an arrow.
3. **Weighted Graph**: A graph in which each edge is assigned a numerical value (weight) representing cost, distance, or capacity.
4. **Complete Graph ($K_n$)**: A simple undirected graph in which every pair of distinct vertices is connected by exactly one edge.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Q2. State and explain the Handshaking Lemma for undirected graphs.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement (Handshaking Lemma):** The sum of the degrees of all vertices in an undirected graph is equal to twice the number of edges in the graph.

$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \cdot \vert E \vert$$

**Explanation:** Since each edge contributes exactly **1 to the degree** of its two endpoint vertices, every edge is counted **twice** in the total sum of vertex degrees. Therefore, the total sum of degrees must be an even number, and dividing it by 2 gives the exact number of edges.

**Example:** If a graph has 4 vertices with degrees 2, 3, 1, and 4 respectively, then the total sum is $2 + 3 + 1 + 4 = 10$, so $\vert E \vert = 10 / 2 = 5$ edges.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Module 4 Internal Choice]

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Define a graph data structure with formal notation. Differentiate between a directed and an undirected graph with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a simple undirected graph with 8 vertices and 15 edges. Verify whether this graph can be a complete graph. If not, state the reason and compute how many more edges are required to make it complete. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Definition and Comparison:**

A graph $G$ is a non-linear data structure formally defined as an ordered pair:

$$G = (V, E)$$

where $V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, \dots, v_n\}$ is a finite, non-empty set of **vertices**, and $E = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_m\}$ is a set of **edges** where each edge $e_k$ is a pair of vertices representing a connection.

**Differences between Directed and Undirected Graphs:**

| Feature | Directed Graph | Undirected Graph |
|---|---|---|
| Edge notation | $\langle u, v \rangle$ (ordered) | $\{u, v\}$ (unordered) |
| Traversal | One-way only | Both ways |
| Maximum edges (no self-loops) | $n(n-1)$ | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ |
| Degree concept | In-degree and Out-degree | Single degree |
| Example | Twitter follow network | Facebook friendship |

**Example (Directed):** A web page $A$ linking to page $B$, but $B$ not linking back to $A$.

**Example (Undirected):** A two-way road between city $X$ and city $Y$ where travel is allowed in both directions.

---

**(b) Verification of Complete Graph Status:**

**Given:** $n = 8$ vertices, $\vert E \vert = 15$ edges.

**Step 1:** Calculate the maximum number of edges for a complete graph $K_8$:

$$\vert E \vert_{max} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{8 \cdot (8-1)}{2} = \frac{8 \cdot 7}{2} = 28$$

**Step 2:** Compare the given number of edges with the maximum:

$$\text{Given } \vert E \vert = 15 \quad \text{vs} \quad \vert E \vert_{max} = 28$$

Since $15 < 28$, the given graph **is not** a complete graph.

**Step 3:** Compute the number of additional edges required:

$$\text{Edges required} = \vert E \vert_{max} - \vert E \vert_{given} = 28 - 15 = 13 \text{ edges}$$

**Conclusion:** The given graph has 8 vertices and 15 edges. To make it a complete graph $K_8$, **13 more edges** must be added.

---

**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the following graph terminologies with examples: (i) Degree of a vertex, (ii) Isolated vertex, (iii) Pendant vertex, (iv) Self-loop, (v) Parallel edges, (vi) Path, (vii) Cycle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a graph, the sum of degrees of all vertices is 24. If the graph has 10 edges, verify the validity of the Handshaking Lemma. Also, determine the number of vertices if the graph is regular of degree 2. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Graph Terminologies with Examples:**

For the example graph $G$ with $V = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and $E = \{(A,B), (B,C), (C,C), (A,D), (A,E), (B,D)\}$:

(i) **Degree of a vertex**: The number of edges incident on the vertex. Example: $\deg(B) = 3$ (edges $AB$, $BC$, $BD$).

(ii) **Isolated vertex**: A vertex with degree zero (no incident edges). Example: If vertex $E$ has no connecting edges, then $E$ is isolated, $\deg(E) = 0$.

(iii) **Pendant vertex**: A vertex with degree exactly 1. Example: A leaf-like node connected to the rest of the graph by a single edge.

(iv) **Self-loop**: An edge that connects a vertex to itself. Example: Edge $(C, C)$ is a self-loop, contributing 2 to $\deg(C)$.

(v) **Parallel edges**: Multiple edges connecting the same pair of vertices. Example: If both edges $(A, B)$ and $(A, B)$ exist (with two different labels), they are parallel.

(vi) **Path**: A sequence of distinct vertices where consecutive vertices are connected by edges. Example: $A \to B \to D$ is a path of length 2.

(vii) **Cycle**: A path that starts and ends at the same vertex, with no repeated vertices except the start/end. Example: $A \to B \to C \to A$ forms a cycle of length 3.

---

**(b) Handshaking Lemma Verification:**

**Given:** $\sum \deg(v) = 24$ and $\vert E \vert = 10$.

**Step 1:** Apply the Handshaking Lemma:

$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \cdot \vert E \vert$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values:

$$2 \cdot \vert E \vert = 2 \cdot 10 = 20$$

**Step 3:** Compare with the given sum:

$$\text{Given sum} = 24 \quad \text{vs} \quad \text{LHS required} = 20$$

Since $24 \neq 20$, the **Handshaking Lemma is violated**, which means either the graph is **not a simple undirected graph** (it may be a multigraph or pseudograph with self-loops), or there is a calculation error in the degree sum.

**Step 4:** For a **regular graph of degree 2**, every vertex has $\deg(v) = 2$:

$$\sum \deg(v) = n \cdot 2 = 2n$$

**Step 5:** For the Handshaking Lemma to hold with $\vert E \vert = 10$:

$$2n = 2 \cdot \vert E \vert = 2 \cdot 10 = 20$$

$$n = \frac{20}{2} = 10$$

**Conclusion:** A 2-regular graph with 10 edges must have **10 vertices**, forming a disjoint union of cycles (e.g., ten triangles, five 4-cycles, two 5-cycles, etc.).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $n(n-1)$ with $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$**: Students often use the directed graph formula for undirected graphs (or vice versa). **Remember**: Undirected = divide by 2; Directed = no division.
>
> 2. **Forgetting self-loops in the Handshaking Lemma**: A self-loop contributes **2 to the degree** of a single vertex, not 1. Missing this is a frequent error.
>
> 3. **Writing $\{u, v\}$ for directed edges**: In KTU, the convention is that curly braces $\{ \}$ denote unordered (undirected) pairs, while angle brackets $\langle u, v \rangle$ denote ordered (directed) pairs. Using the wrong notation may lead to partial marking deductions.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the "non-empty" condition for $V$**: A graph by definition must have at least one vertex. Writing $V = \emptyset$ as a valid graph will lose 1 mark in definition-type questions.
>
> 5. **Not simplifying $\binom{n}{2}$**: Examiners expect the simplified form $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ in the answer sheet, not the raw binomial notation.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **graph** is formally defined as $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is a non-empty finite set of vertices and $E$ is a finite set of edges.
- [x] The **maximum number of edges** in a simple undirected graph with $n$ vertices is $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$.
- [x] The **maximum number of edges** in a simple directed graph with $n$ vertices is $n(n-1)$ (twice that of undirected).
- [x] The **Handshaking Lemma** states: $\sum \deg(v) = 2 \cdot \vert E \vert$ for any undirected graph.
- [x] In a **directed graph**, the sum of in-degrees equals the sum of out-degrees, both equal to $\vert E \vert$.
- [x] A **complete graph $K_n$** has every pair of vertices connected by exactly one edge.
- [x] A **simple graph** has no self-loops and no parallel edges.
- [x] A **multigraph** allows parallel edges; a **pseudograph** allows both parallel edges and self-loops.
- [x] An **isolated vertex** has $\deg(v) = 0$; a **pendant vertex** has $\deg(v) = 1$.
- [x] A self-loop contributes **2** to the degree of its vertex.
- [x] The total number of vertices in a graph is called the **order**; the total number of edges is called the **size**.
- [x] A graph is **connected** if there exists a path between every pair of vertices.
- [x] A **bipartite graph** has a vertex partition $V = V_1 \cup V_2$ where every edge crosses between $V_1$ and $V_2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Set representations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Set Representations in Data Structures

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Set representations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** A **Set** is an abstract data type (ADT) that stores a collection of **distinct elements** drawn from a universal domain $\mathcal{U}$ such that no element appears more than once and the elements are **unordered**. A **Set Representation** is the concrete data structure used to physically store the set in memory so that the ADT operations (membership test, insertion, deletion, union, intersection, difference) can be performed efficiently.

Mathematically, a set $S$ over universe $\mathcal{U}$ satisfies:

$$
S \subseteq \mathcal{U}, \qquad \forall\, x \in S \Rightarrow x \in \mathcal{U}
$$

The characteristic function $\chi_S : \mathcal{U} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$ is defined as:

$$
\chi_S(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & x \in S \\ 0, & x \notin S \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCITT303 – Module 4):** The 2024 scheme explicitly tests the following representations:
> 1. **Bit-Vector (Bitset) Representation** – for finite, bounded universes.
> 2. **Linked-List Representation** – for sparse or unbounded universes.
> 3. **Hash Table Representation** – for dynamic sets.
>
> Students must know the operation-wise **time complexity** and **space complexity** for each representation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **classroom attendance register**:

- **Bit-Vector Analogy:** Each roll number (1 to 60) has a fixed slot in a register. Mark a tick ✓ if present. To check if roll 37 is present, you flip directly to slot 37 — instant lookup. But the register must have slots for **all** roll numbers, even if most are empty.
- **Linked-List Analogy:** Each present student writes their name on a separate slip of paper and chains them together with arrows. Adding a new student just creates a new slip and links it. No fixed slots are wasted, but to check roll 37 you must walk through the slips one by one.
- **Hash-Table Analogy:** A locker system where each roll number is transformed by a formula (e.g., last two digits) that points directly to a locker. Two roll numbers may map to the same locker — handled by chaining or probing.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** **B**it-vector = **B**ounded & dense | **L**inked-list = **L**azy & sparse | **H**ash = **H**ot-path dynamic.

### 1.3 Universe, Cardinality & Standard Metrics

- **Universe size** $\vert \mathcal{U} \vert$ – total number of possible elements (denoted **N**).
- **Set cardinality** $\vert S \vert$ – actual number of elements stored (denoted **n**).
- **Density ratio** $\rho = n / N$ – determines whether bit-vector or linked-list is more efficient.
- **Word size** $w$ – bits per machine word (commonly **32** or **64**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Primary Set Representations

#### 2.1.1 Bit-Vector (Bitset) Representation

- Allocate an array of $N$ bits where bit position $i$ indicates membership of element $i$.
- Internally stored as $\lceil N / w \rceil$ machine words.
- **Why it works:** Membership is a direct memory access — single bit fetch.
- **How it works:** Each operation reduces to a bitwise logic on full words rather than per-element processing.

#### 2.1.2 Linked-List Representation

- Each node holds one element and a pointer to the next node.
- May be **sorted** (for binary-search-like lookup) or **unsorted** (for fast insertion).
- **Why it works:** No upper bound on universe size; only $n$ nodes allocated.
- **How it works:** Traversals are sequential; sorted variant allows early termination.

#### 2.1.3 Hash-Table Representation

- A bucket array of size $m$ stores pointers to nodes (separate chaining) or direct elements (open addressing).
- **Why it works:** Average-case $O(1)$ for insert/delete/find via hash function $h(x)$.
- **How it works:** Hash function maps universe to indices; collisions handled by chaining/probing.

### 2.2 Core Algebraic Set Operations

The following identities are the **mathematical backbone** of bit-vector operations. Each proof follows from the definition of $\chi_S$.

$$
\chi_{A \cup B}(x) = \chi_A(x) \lor \chi_B(x) = \chi_A(x) \mid \chi_B(x)
$$

$$
\chi_{A \cap B}(x) = \chi_A(x) \land \chi_B(x) = \chi_A(x) \, \& \, \chi_B(x)
$$

$$
\chi_{A - B}(x) = \chi_A(x) \land \lnot \chi_B(x) = \chi_A(x) \, \& \, \overline{\chi_B(x)}
$$

$$
\chi_{A \triangle B}(x) = \chi_A(x) \oplus \chi_B(x)
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table – it covers 80% of exam numerical questions.**

| Operation | Bit-Vector (Universe N, Word w) | Linked-List (Sorted, n elements) | Linked-List (Unsorted, n elements) | Hash Table (Load factor $\alpha$) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Space | $\lceil N / w \rceil$ words | $n$ nodes + $n$ pointers | $n$ nodes + $n$ pointers | $O(n + m)$ slots |
| Make-Empty | $O(N / w)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(m)$ |
| Insert | $O(N / w)$ (clear bits) or $O(1)$ if bit set | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ avg, $O(n)$ worst |
| Delete | $O(1)$ bit reset | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ if pointer known | $O(1)$ avg, $O(n)$ worst |
| Find (membership) | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ or $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ avg, $O(n)$ worst |
| Union $A \cup B$ | $O(N / w)$ | $O(n_A + n_B)$ | $O(n_A \cdot n_B)$ | $O(n_A + n_B)$ avg |
| Intersection $A \cap B$ | $O(N / w)$ | $O(n_A + n_B)$ | $O(n_A \cdot n_B)$ | $O(n_A + n_B)$ avg |
| Difference $A - B$ | $O(N / w)$ | $O(n_A + n_B)$ | $O(n_A \cdot n_B)$ | $O(n_A + n_B)$ avg |
| Subset $A \subseteq B$ | $O(N / w)$ | $O(n_A + n_B)$ | $O(n_A \cdot n_B)$ | $O(n_A \cdot n_B)$ |

**Cardinal Algebra Identities (must-memorize):**

$$
\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert
$$

$$
\vert A \cap B \vert \leq \min(\vert A \vert, \vert B \vert)
$$

$$
\vert A - B \vert = \vert A \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert
$$

$$
\vert A \triangle B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - 2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert
$$

$$
\vert \mathcal{U} - A \vert = N - \vert A \vert \quad \text{(complement)}
$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Bit-vectors represent register-allocation sets and live-variable analysis (each variable gets a bit position).
- **Database Engines:** Bloom filters (a probabilistic cousin of bit-vectors) test set membership in network routing.
- **Network Security:** Firewall rule sets stored as bit-vectors for high-speed packet matching.
- **Bioinformatics:** Hash tables index millions of DNA k-mers for genomic search.
- **Operating Systems:** Process control blocks use linked-list sets for ready/blocked queues.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Space Complexity of Bit-Vector

**Given:** Universe $\mathcal{U} = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1\}$ with $N$ elements. Each machine word holds $w$ bits.

**Step 1:** Identify the storage unit. Each element needs 1 bit (the $\chi_S$ value).

$$
\text{Total bits required} = N \cdot 1 = N \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert bits to machine words. Since one word stores $w$ bits:

$$
\text{Number of words} = \frac{N \text{ bits}}{w \text{ bits/word}} = \frac{N}{w}
$$

**Step 3:** Account for ceiling because partial words are still allocated as full words.

$$
\text{Space (in words)} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{w} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 4:** Convert back to bytes for concrete analysis (1 word = 4 bytes for 32-bit machine):

$$
\text{Space (in bytes)} = 4 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{N}{w} \right\rceil
$$

**Example:** $N = 100{,}000$, $w = 32$.

$$
\text{Words} = \left\lceil \frac{100000}{32} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3125 \right\rceil = 3125 \text{ words}
$$

$$
\text{Bytes} = 4 \times 3125 = 12{,}500 \text{ bytes} \approx 12.2 \text{ KB}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Time Complexity of Union Operation (Bit-Vector)

**Given:** Two bit-vectors $A$ and $B$, each spanning universe $\mathcal{U}$ of size $N$, stored as $W = \lceil N/w \rceil$ words.

**Step 1:** Identify the operation. Bitwise OR computes $\chi_{A \cup B}$.

$$
\text{For each } x \in \mathcal{U}: \quad \chi_{A \cup B}(x) = \chi_A(x) \mid \chi_B(x)
$$

**Step 2:** Since a single machine-word OR operates on $w$ bits in one CPU cycle, one OR instruction processes $w$ elements simultaneously.

$$
\text{Number of OR instructions required} = \frac{N}{w} = W
$$

**Step 3:** Express time complexity using Big-O notation.

$$
T_{\text{union}}(N) = O\!\left(\frac{N}{w}\right) = O(W) = O(N / w)
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify for typical constants. Since $w$ is a machine constant (32 or 64):

$$
T_{\text{union}}(N) = O(N)
$$

> **Interpretation:** Even though we touch all $N$ bits, modern CPUs complete the operation in a single pass over $W$ words — hence the factor $1/w$ is the **hidden constant** that makes bit-vectors extremely fast in practice.

### 3.3 Derivation: Cardinality Formula for Union (Inclusion-Exclusion)

**Given:** Two finite sets $A$ and $B$. We want $\vert A \cup B \vert$.

**Step 1:** If $A$ and $B$ were disjoint (no common elements), union cardinality is just the sum:

$$
\vert A \cup B \vert_{\text{disjoint}} = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert
$$

**Step 2:** When $A$ and $B$ overlap, every element in $A \cap B$ gets counted **twice** in the sum above (once in $\vert A \vert$ and once in $\vert B \vert$). To correct this, subtract the over-count once:

$$
\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert
$$

**Step 3:** Verify with $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $B = \{3, 4, 5\}$:

$$
\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert \{1,2,3,4,5\} \vert = 5
$$

$$
\text{RHS} = 3 + 3 - \vert \{3\} \vert = 3 + 3 - 1 = 5 \;\checkmark
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Membership Test Using Bit-Vector

**Given:** Element $x$, bit-vector array `bit[W]`.

**Step 1:** Find the word index containing bit for $x$.

$$
\text{wordIdx} = \left\lfloor \frac{x}{w} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 2:** Find the bit position within that word.

$$
\text{bitPos} = x \bmod w
$$

**Step 3:** Create a mask with a single 1 at `bitPos`.

$$
\text{mask} = 1 \ll \text{bitPos}
$$

**Step 4:** Logical AND with the word, then test against zero.

$$
\text{isMember} = (\text{bit}[\text{wordIdx}] \, \& \, \text{mask}) \neq 0
$$

**Step 5:** Time complexity — all operations are constant time:

$$
T_{\text{find}}(x) = O(1)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** No matter how large $N$ is, membership test in a bit-vector is **constant time** — this is the killer feature for finite bounded universes.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Representation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix in exam short-answer questions where "compare representations" appears.**

| Criterion | Best Representation | Reason |
|-----------|--------------------|---------| 
| Universe size $N < 10^4$ and known | **Bit-Vector** | Constant time ops, cache-friendly |
| Universe very large / unbounded | **Linked-List** | No memory wasted on absent elements |
| Many insert/delete operations | **Hash Table** | $O(1)$ average-case dynamic ops |
| Need ordered traversal | **Sorted Linked-List / BST** | Preserves ordering |
| Need subset, union, intersection frequently | **Bit-Vector** | Single pass over words using bitwise ops |
| Sparse set ($\rho \ll 1$) with known universe | **Linked-List** | Saves memory |
| Set size grows unpredictably | **Hash Table** | Amortized performance |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Bit-Vector Set

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Universe U = 0 to N minus 1] --> B[Compute W = ceiling of N divided by w]
    B --> C[Allocate W machine words]
    C --> D[Word 0 contains bits 0 to w minus 1]
    C --> E[Word 1 contains bits w to 2w minus 1]
    C --> F[Word k contains bits kw to kw plus w minus 1]
    D --> G[bit i represents membership of element i]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[chi sub S of x equals 1 if present else 0]
    H --> I[Operations use bitwise AND OR XOR NOT]
```

### 4.2 Linked-List Set Representation (Sorted)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD[HEAD Pointer] --> N1[Node: data equals 5 next pointer]
    N1 --> N2[Node: data equals 12 next pointer]
    N2 --> N3[Node: data equals 27 next pointer]
    N3 --> N4[Node: data equals 48 next pointer]
    N4 --> NULL1[NULL Terminator]
    style HEAD fill:fab,stroke:333
    style NULL1 fill:ddd,stroke:333
```

**Reading:** The list is kept in ascending order. Searching for element 27 starts at HEAD, compares with 5 (skip), compares with 12 (skip), finds 27 (match) — terminates early in $O(\log n)$ using pointer jumping or $O(n)$ linearly.

### 4.3 Hash-Table Set Representation (Separate Chaining)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HT[Hash Table Array of size m equals 7]
    HT --> B0[Bucket 0]
    HT --> B1[Bucket 1]
    HT --> B2[Bucket 2]
    HT --> B3[Bucket 3]
    HT --> B4[Bucket 4]
    HT --> B5[Bucket 5]
    HT --> B6[Bucket 6]
    B1 --> N1A[Node: key equals 15]
    N1A --> N1B[Node: key equals 43]
    B3 --> N3A[Node: key equals 24]
    B5 --> N5A[Node: key equals 33]
    N5A --> N5B[Node: key equals 89]
    style HT fill:bbf,stroke:333,color:000
    style B0 fill:eee,stroke:333
    style B2 fill:eee,stroke:333
    style B4 fill:eee,stroke:333
    style B6 fill:eee,stroke:333
```

**Reading:** The hash function $h(x) = x \bmod 7$ distributes keys across 7 buckets. Bucket 1 contains keys that hashed to 1 (collision resolved by chaining). Average lookup is $O(1 + \alpha)$ where $\alpha = n / m$ is the load factor.

### 4.4 Operation Mapping Across Representations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP[Set Operation Requested] --> Q1{Universe bounded and small?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| BV[Use Bit Vector]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Sparse set?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| LL[Use Linked List]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Ordering needed?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| BST[Use Sorted Linked List or BST]
    Q3 -->|No| HT[Use Hash Table]
    BV --> R1[Bitwise operations on words]
    LL --> R2[Sequential traversal pointers]
    BST --> R3[Binary search and pointer jumps]
    HT --> R4[Hash and chain or probe]
    style OP fill:cff,stroke:333,color:000
```

### 4.5 Comparative Memory Layout (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BV[Bit Vector]
        BV1[Word 0: 32 bits]
        BV2[Word 1: 32 bits]
        BV3[Word k: 32 bits]
    end
    subgraph LL[Linked List]
        LL1[Node A]
        LL2[Node B]
        LL3[Node C]
    end
    subgraph HT[Hash Table]
        HT1[Bucket 0]
        HT2[Bucket 1]
        HT3[Bucket m minus 1]
    end
    style BV fill:dfd,stroke:333
    style LL fill:fdd,stroke:333
    style HT fill:ddf,stroke:333
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the bit-vector representation of a set. What is its main limitation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A bit-vector represents a finite set $S \subseteq \{0, 1, \ldots, N-1\}$ using an array of $N$ bits, where bit $i$ is **1** if element $i$ is in $S$ and **0** otherwise. Membership test is implemented in $O(1)$ by accessing bit position $i$. Set operations like union, intersection, and difference are performed using bitwise OR, AND, and AND-NOT operations over all $N$ bits, taking $O(N)$ time.

**Main limitation (1 mark):** It can only be used when the universe is **small, finite, and bounded**. For large or unbounded universes (e.g., integers from 0 to $10^9$), allocating $N$ bits is infeasible. Additionally, it is unsuitable when elements are non-integer (strings, objects) without an explicit encoding function.

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Compare linked-list representation and bit-vector representation of sets in terms of time complexity for union operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Bit-Vector | Linked-List (Unsorted) | Linked-List (Sorted) |
|--------|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Union Time | $O(N / w)$ word-level | $O(n_A \cdot n_B)$ | $O(n_A + n_B)$ |

For an **unsorted linked list**, the union requires testing each element of $A$ against every element of $B$ for membership before insertion, leading to $O(n_A \cdot n_B)$ complexity. For a **sorted linked list**, a merge-style traversal yields $O(n_A + n_B)$. The **bit-vector** uses a single pass of bitwise OR on $N / w$ words, giving $O(N / w)$ — independent of set cardinalities. Hence, for small bounded universes, bit-vector is asymptotically fastest; for large sparse sets, sorted linked-list is preferred.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the bit-vector representation of a set in detail. Given two sets $A = \{1, 3, 4, 6\}$ and $B = \{2, 3, 5, 6\}$ represented as bit-vectors over universe $\mathcal{U} = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 7\}$, perform the operations $A \cup B$, $A \cap B$, $A - B$, and $A \triangle B$. Show your work using bitwise operations.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the linked-list representation of sets with suitable diagrams. Compare the time complexity of union, intersection, and difference operations for sorted vs unsorted linked-list sets.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Bit-Vector Operations [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Construct bit-vectors (LSB = element 0). Set bit $i$ to 1 if $i$ belongs to the set.

$$
\text{Bit-vector of } A = 0101 \, 1010 \quad (\text{bit positions } 6,4,3,1 \text{ are set})
$$

$$
\text{Bit-vector of } B = 0110 \, 1100 \quad (\text{bit positions } 6,5,3,2 \text{ are set})
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute Union using bitwise OR:

$$
A \mid B = 0101 \, 1010 \mid 0110 \, 1100 = 0111 \, 1110
$$

Set bits at positions $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$.

$$
A \cup B = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute Intersection using bitwise AND:

$$
A \, \& \, B = 0101 \, 1010 \, \& \, 0110 \, 1100 = 0100 \, 1000
$$

Set bits at positions $\{3, 6\}$.

$$
A \cap B = \{3, 6\}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute Difference using $A \, \& \, \overline{B}$:

$$
\overline{B} = \overline{0110 \, 1100} = 1001 \, 0011
$$

$$
A \, \& \, \overline{B} = 0101 \, 1010 \, \& \, 1001 \, 0011 = 0001 \, 0010
$$

Set bits at positions $\{1, 4\}$.

$$
A - B = \{1, 4\}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute Symmetric Difference using bitwise XOR:

$$
A \oplus B = 0101 \, 1010 \oplus 0110 \, 1100 = 0011 \, 0110
$$

Set bits at positions $\{1, 2, 4, 5\}$.

$$
A \triangle B = \{1, 2, 4, 5\}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Verify using cardinal identities:

$$
\vert A \cup B \vert = 4 + 4 - 2 = 6 \;\checkmark
$$

$$
\vert A \triangle B \vert = 4 + 4 - 2 \cdot 2 = 4 \;\checkmark
$$

**(b) Linked-List Representation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Explain the structure. Each set is represented as a singly/doubly linked list of nodes. Each node contains `data` and `next` (and optionally `prev` for doubly-linked). Two variants:
- **Unsorted:** Nodes in arbitrary order; fast $O(1)$ insertion at head.
- **Sorted:** Nodes in ascending order; supports binary-search-like jumps.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Draw diagrams (textual):

```
Unsorted:  HEAD --> [5] --> [2] --> [9] --> [1] --> NULL
Sorted:    HEAD --> [1] --> [2] --> [5] --> [9] --> NULL
```

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Time complexity comparison:

| Operation | Sorted LL | Unsorted LL | Reason |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|--------|
| Union | $O(n_A + n_B)$ merge | $O(n_A \cdot n_B)$ each-insert with find | Sorted avoids repeated scans |
| Intersection | $O(n_A + n_B)$ merge-walk | $O(n_A \cdot n_B)$ nested check | Sorted lets us advance both pointers |
| Difference | $O(n_A + n_B)$ walk-and-compare | $O(n_A \cdot n_B)$ find-and-remove | Sorted enables linear merge |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Sorted linked-lists provide asymptotically better complexity for set algebra operations, at the cost of $O(n)$ insertion overhead.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4 - Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a suitable diagram, explain hash-table representation of sets. Describe insertion and membership test procedures. Discuss the average and worst-case time complexities.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A university has 5000 students with roll numbers from 1 to 5000. The university wants to maintain a set of students present on a particular day. Compare bit-vector and linked-list representations for this scenario with a clear justification.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hash-Table Representation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Structure. A hash table of size $m$ is an array of $m$ buckets. Each element $x$ is mapped to bucket $h(x)$ where $h$ is the hash function. Two collision-resolution strategies:
- **Separate Chaining:** Each bucket holds a linked list of elements.
- **Open Addressing:** Collisions resolved by probing (linear, quadratic, double hashing).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Procedures (with chaining):

```
INSERT(S, x):
    idx = h(x)
    if x not in bucket[idx]:
        prepend x to bucket[idx]

MEMBER(S, x):
    idx = h(x)
    for node in bucket[idx]:
        if node.data == x: return TRUE
    return FALSE
```

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Complexity analysis with load factor $\alpha = n / m$:

- **Average case (uniform hashing):** $T = O(1 + \alpha)$. With $\alpha$ kept constant (e.g., $\alpha < 1$), this is $O(1)$.
- **Worst case:** All keys hash to same bucket → $O(n)$.
- **Space:** $O(n + m)$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Practical considerations — choosing $m$ as prime, rehashing when $\alpha > 0.75$, using universal hash families for security.

**(b) Comparison for 5000 Students [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Bit-vector analysis.

Universe $\mathcal{U} = \{1, 2, \ldots, 5000\}$, $N = 5000$, word size $w = 32$.

$$
\text{Space} = \left\lceil \frac{5000}{32} \right\rceil = 157 \text{ words} = 628 \text{ bytes}
$$

Membership test = $O(1)$ (direct bit access).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Linked-list analysis.

Suppose on a typical day, $n = 200$ students are present.

$$
\text{Space} = 200 \times (\text{data} + \text{pointer}) = 200 \times 16 \text{ bytes} = 3200 \text{ bytes}
$$

Membership test = $O(n) = O(200)$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Decision factors.

| Factor | Bit-Vector | Linked-List |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Space efficiency | Wastes memory on absent students (5000 vs 200) but only 628 bytes | Saves memory (3200 bytes) but only if set is very sparse |
| Lookup speed | $O(1)$ always | $O(n)$ worst case |
| Dynamic operations (varying present count) | Requires reset of $N$ bits each day | Easy insert/delete |
| Implementation complexity | Simple array | Pointer management |

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclusion. **Bit-vector is the clear winner** for this scenario because:
1. Universe size 5000 is small and bounded.
2. Memory footprint of 628 bytes is negligible.
3. Membership queries are instantaneous at $O(1)$.
4. Implementations like the C `bitset` library make this trivial.

Linked-list would only be preferred if roll numbers exceeded $10^6$ or were non-contiguous (e.g., UUIDs).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify universe size** when describing bit-vector — always state $N$ and word size $w$.
> 2. **Confusing $O(N)$ vs $O(N/w)$** — both are correct but $O(N/w)$ shows deeper understanding and earns the full mark.
> 3. **In bitwise operations, students often make sign errors** when extending sign bits in 2's complement. Use unsigned types for clarity.
> 4. **In linked-list union, students write $O(n_A + n_B)$ without sorting** — must clarify "sorted linked-list" or explain sorting cost separately.
> 5. **Hash-table complexity** — quoting $O(1)$ without qualifying as "average case" loses 1 mark. Always say "$O(1)$ average, $O(n)$ worst case."
> 6. **Skipping diagrams** in 14-mark questions — at least one diagram is mandatory. A missing diagram typically costs 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Set Representations**

- ☐ **Set ADT** stores distinct, unordered elements with membership, insert, delete, union, intersection, difference operations.
- ☐ **Three main representations:** Bit-Vector, Linked-List, Hash-Table.
- ☐ **Bit-Vector** uses $N$ bits (or $\lceil N/w \rceil$ words); membership $O(1)$; algebra ops $O(N/w)$.
- ☐ **Bitwise operation mapping:** Union = OR, Intersection = AND, Difference = AND-NOT, Symmetric Difference = XOR.
- ☐ **Linked-List (sorted):** Set algebra ops in $O(n_A + n_B)$; insertion $O(n)$.
- ☐ **Linked-List (unsorted):** Insertion $O(1)$; set algebra $O(n_A \cdot n_B)$.
- ☐ **Hash Table:** Average $O(1)$ insert/find/delete; worst $O(n)$; load factor $\alpha = n/m$.
- ☐ **Cardinal identity to memorize:** $\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$ (inclusion-exclusion).
- ☐ **Membership formula in bit-vector:** wordIdx = $\lfloor x / w \rfloor$, bitPos = $x \bmod w$, mask = $1 \ll \text{bitPos}$.
- ☐ **Choose bit-vector** when universe is small, bounded, and dense.
- ☐ **Choose linked-list** when universe is unbounded or set is sparse.
- ☐ **Choose hash-table** when set is dynamic and ordering is not needed.
- ☐ **Always state complexity with qualifiers** — average vs worst case, sorted vs unsorted.
- ☐ **Time-space tradeoff:** bit-vectors trade memory for speed; linked-lists trade speed for memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Directed and Undirected Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT303 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Directed and Undirected Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Graph

In **Discrete Mathematics** and **Data Structures**, a **graph** $G$ is formally defined as an ordered pair of two finite sets:

$$G = (V, E)$$

where:
- $V$ is a non-empty, finite set of **vertices** (also called *nodes* or *points*).
- $E$ is a finite set of **edges** (also called *arcs* or *lines*) — each edge connects a pair of vertices.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A graph is a non-linear data structure consisting of a set of **nodes (vertices)** and a set of **edges (connections)** linking these nodes. The study of graphs forms the backbone of network algorithms, social network analysis, and shortest-path computations.

---

## 1.2 Directed Graph (Digraph) — Formal Definition

A **directed graph** (or **digraph**) is a graph in which every edge has an associated **direction**, meaning it is an *ordered pair* of vertices. An edge from $u$ to $v$ is denoted as $(u, v)$, and it is fundamentally **different** from the edge $(v, u)$.

$$G_{D} = (V, A)$$

where $A \subseteq V \times V$ is the set of **arcs (directed edges)**. Each arc has a **head** (destination) and a **tail** (source).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **directed graph**, the order of vertices in an edge **matters**: $(u, v) \neq (v, u)$ unless $u = v$ (a self-loop).

---

## 1.3 Undirected Graph — Formal Definition

An **undirected graph** is a graph in which edges have **no orientation**; they are simply *unordered pairs* of vertices. The edge between $u$ and $v$ is denoted as $\{u, v\}$ and is identical to $\{v, u\}$.

$$G_{U} = (V, E)$$

where $E$ is a set of **2-element subsets of $V$**, i.e., $E \subseteq \binom{V}{2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an **undirected graph**, the order of vertices in an edge **does NOT matter**: $\{u, v\} = \{v, u\}$.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🎯

Think of a graph like a **map of friendships in a college**:

| Concept | Analogy | Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertices (V)** | Students in a class | Same for both |
| **Undirected Edge** | Mutual friendship (WhatsApp chat — works both ways) | Undirected |
| **Directed Edge** | One-way "Follow" on Twitter / Instagram | Directed |

- **Undirected graph** = **Mutual friendship**: If **Amit** is friends with **Bhavna**, then **Bhavna** is automatically friends with **Amit**. The relationship is **bidirectional**.
- **Directed graph** = **Following on Twitter**: **Amit** can follow **Bhavna** without **Bhavna** following **Amit** back. The relationship is **one-way**, with a clear **arrow** showing who follows whom.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering examples:**
> - **Undirected**: Road networks (two-way streets), LAN cable connections, chemical molecular bonds.
> - **Directed**: Web page hyperlinks, Twitter followers, task dependencies in project scheduling, current flow in a diode circuit.

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology (Standard KTU Notation)

> [!NOTE]
> The following terminology is **mandatory** for KTU exams — examiners expect these exact terms.

| Term | Definition | Directed? |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertex (Node)** | A fundamental unit of a graph | Both |
| **Edge (Arc)** | A connection between two vertices | Both |
| **Adjacent vertices** | Two vertices connected by an edge | Both |
| **Incident edge** | An edge that touches a vertex | Both |
| **Degree (deg)** | Number of edges incident to a vertex | Undirected only |
| **In-degree (deg⁻)** | Number of edges coming *into* a vertex | Directed only |
| **Out-degree (deg⁺)** | Number of edges going *out of* a vertex | Directed only |
| **Self-loop** | An edge from a vertex to itself | Both |
| **Parallel edges** | Multiple edges between the same pair of vertices | Both |
| **Simple graph** | A graph with no self-loops or parallel edges | Both |
| **Isolated vertex** | A vertex with degree **0** | Both |
| **Pendant vertex** | A vertex with degree **1** | Undirected only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **directed graph**, the total **degree** of a vertex is defined as:
> $$\deg(v) = \deg^{-}(v) + \deg^{+}(v)$$
> where $\deg^{-}(v)$ is the **in-degree** and $\deg^{+}(v)$ is the **out-degree**.

---

## 1.6 Standard Graph Metrics

- **Number of vertices:** $\vert V \vert = n$
- **Number of edges:** $\vert E \vert = m$ (or $e$)
- **Maximum edges in a simple undirected graph:** $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$
- **Maximum edges in a simple directed graph:** $n(n-1)$ (each pair allows two directions)
- **Density of a graph:** $\dfrac{2m}{n(n-1)}$ (undirected) or $\dfrac{m}{n(n-1)}$ (directed)

> [!NOTE]
> All of the above metrics are **standard results** that KTU examiners expect you to memorize for Part A (3-mark) questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation

A graph is fundamentally a **mathematical structure** that models **pairwise relations** between objects. The two principal categories are:

### 2.1.1 Undirected Graphs (Detailed)

An undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ has edges that are **unordered pairs** $\{u, v\}$. This means the relation "is connected to" is **symmetric** — if $u$ is connected to $v$, then $v$ is connected to $u$.

**Properties of undirected graphs:**
- The **degree** of a vertex $v$, denoted $\deg(v)$, counts the number of edges incident to $v$. A self-loop contributes **2** to the degree (since it touches the vertex twice).
- An undirected graph can be **visualized** as a set of points (vertices) connected by line segments (edges) with **no arrows**.

### 2.1.2 Directed Graphs (Detailed)

A directed graph (digraph) $G = (V, A)$ has edges that are **ordered pairs** $(u, v)$, where $u$ is the **tail** (source) and $v$ is the **head** (destination). The edge $(u, v)$ is sometimes called an **arc**.

**Properties of directed graphs:**
- A vertex $v$ has two independent degree measures:
  - **In-degree** $\deg^{-}(v)$: the number of arcs entering $v$.
  - **Out-degree** $\deg^{+}(v)$: the number of arcs leaving $v$.
- A self-loop in a directed graph contributes **1** to both the in-degree and out-degree of the vertex.

---

## 2.2 The Handshaking Theorem ⭐ (Most Important Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Handshaking Lemma** is the most frequently asked theorem in KTU University Exams for the Graph module. Memorize its statement and proof.

### 2.2.1 For Undirected Graphs

**Statement:** Let $G = (V, E)$ be an undirected graph with $m$ edges. Then:

$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2m$$

**Intuitive proof idea:** Every edge $\{u, v\}$ contributes exactly **1** to the degree of $u$ and exactly **1** to the degree of $v$. Hence each edge contributes **2** to the total sum of all degrees. Summing over all $m$ edges gives $2m$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering intuition:** Think of a "handshake" between two people. Each handshake involves **two** hands, so counting all hands that participated in handshakes gives **twice** the number of handshakes.

### 2.2.2 For Directed Graphs

**Statement:** Let $G = (V, A)$ be a directed graph with $m$ arcs. Then:

$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg^{-}(v) = \sum_{v \in V} \deg^{+}(v) = m$$

**Intuitive proof idea:** Each arc $(u, v)$ contributes exactly **1** to the out-degree of $u$ and exactly **1** to the in-degree of $v$. Hence, the total in-degree over all vertices equals the total out-degree, and both equal the total number of arcs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write $\sum \deg(v) = 2m$ for directed graphs. This is **WRONG**. The correct statement for directed graphs is the equality of in-degree and out-degree sums.

---

## 2.3 Corollaries of the Handshaking Theorem

**Corollary 1:** The number of vertices with **odd degree** in any undirected graph is always **even**.

**Proof idea:** Let $V_{even}$ be vertices with even degree and $V_{odd}$ be vertices with odd degree. Then:

$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = \sum_{v \in V_{even}} \deg(v) + \sum_{v \in V_{odd}} \deg(v) = 2m$$

Since $\sum_{v \in V_{even}} \deg(v)$ is even and $2m$ is even, $\sum_{v \in V_{odd}} \deg(v)$ must also be even, which is only possible if $\vert V_{odd} \vert$ is even.

**Corollary 2:** In any undirected graph, the sum of degrees of all vertices is always **even**.

---

## 2.4 Special Graph Types (High-Yield for KTU)

| Graph Type | Definition | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Graph** | No self-loops, no parallel edges | At most one edge between any two vertices |
| **Multigraph** | May contain parallel edges, no self-loops | Multiple edges allowed between same pair |
| **Pseudograph** | May contain self-loops and parallel edges | Most general undirected graph |
| **Complete Graph ($K_n$)** | Every pair of distinct vertices is connected by an edge | $\vert E \vert = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ |
| **Bipartite Graph** | Vertices can be split into two disjoint sets $V_1, V_2$ such that every edge connects $V_1$ to $V_2$ | No odd-length cycles |
| **Regular Graph** | Every vertex has the same degree $k$ | $k$-regular: $\deg(v) = k$ for all $v$ |
| **Cycle Graph ($C_n$)** | $n$ vertices connected in a single closed loop | $\vert E \vert = n$, every vertex has degree 2 |
| **Path Graph ($P_n$)** | $n$ vertices in a single linear chain | $\vert E \vert = n-1$, two endpoints have degree 1 |
| **Wheel Graph ($W_n$)** | A cycle $C_{n-1}$ plus a central hub vertex | Useful in network topology |
| **Tree** | Connected, acyclic undirected graph | $\vert E \vert = \vert V \vert - 1$ |
| **Forest** | Acyclic undirected graph (disjoint union of trees) | $\vert E \vert = \vert V \vert - c$ (where $c$ = number of components) |
| **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** | Directed graph with no directed cycles | Used in topological sorting, dependency resolution |
| **Tournament** | Complete directed graph where every pair has exactly one directed edge | Exactly $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ arcs |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **exam-ready formulas**. These appear in 80% of KTU graph theory questions.

| # | Formula / Statement | Condition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2m$ | Undirected graph | Handshaking Lemma |
| 2 | $\sum \deg^{-}(v) = \sum \deg^{+}(v) = m$ | Directed graph | Arc conservation |
| 3 | $\max(\vert E \vert) = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Simple undirected | Maximum edges |
| 4 | $\max(\vert A \vert) = n(n-1)$ | Simple directed | Maximum arcs |
| 5 | $\max(\vert E \vert) = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | Undirected with self-loops | Generalized bound |
| 6 | $\vert E \vert = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{2}$ | $k$-regular undirected | Regular graph edges |
| 7 | $\vert E \vert = n - 1$ | Tree (connected, acyclic) | Spanning tree |
| 8 | $\vert E \vert = n - c$ | Forest with $c$ components | Component counting |
| 9 | $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n!}{2!(n-2)!}$ | Number of 2-subsets | Edge enumeration |
| 10 | $\deg(v) = \deg^{-}(v) + \deg^{+}(v)$ | Directed graph | Degree decomposition |
| 11 | Number of odd-degree vertices is **even** | Any undirected graph | Parity argument |
| 12 | $\vert E(K_n) \vert = \binom{n}{2}$ | Complete graph $K_n$ | Complete graph edges |
| 13 | BFS / DFS traversal: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Any graph | Algorithm complexity |
| 14 | Density $D = \dfrac{2m}{n(n-1)}$ | Simple undirected | Sparsity measure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility of these formulas:**
> - **Network design:** Degree constraints determine router/switch port requirements.
> - **Database query optimization:** Graph density influences join strategy selection.
> - **Social network analysis:** Degree distribution reveals influencer identification.
> - **Compiler design:** DAG representation enables instruction scheduling optimization.

---

## 2.6 Connectivity Concepts

- **Walk:** A sequence of vertices and edges $v_0, e_1, v_1, e_2, v_2, \ldots, v_k$ where $e_i$ connects $v_{i-1}$ to $v_i$.
- **Trail:** A walk with **no repeated edges**.
- **Path:** A walk with **no repeated vertices** (and hence no repeated edges).
- **Closed walk:** A walk that starts and ends at the same vertex.
- **Cycle:** A closed walk with no repeated vertices (other than the start/end).
- **Connected graph:** An undirected graph where a path exists between **every pair** of vertices.
- **Strongly connected digraph:** A directed graph where a directed path exists from $u$ to $v$ **and** from $v$ to $u$ for every pair of vertices.
- **Weakly connected digraph:** The underlying undirected graph is connected.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of Edges in a Simple Undirected Graph

**Goal:** Prove that a simple undirected graph with $n$ vertices has at most $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ edges.

**Step 1 — Count possible vertex pairs.**

The total number of distinct unordered pairs of $n$ vertices is given by the binomial coefficient:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n!}{2!(n-2)!}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the factorial expression.**

We expand $n! = n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2)!$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\binom{n}{2} &= \dfrac{n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2)!}{2 \cdot (n-2)!} \\
&= \dfrac{n \cdot (n-1)}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude the bound.**

Since a **simple graph** allows at most **one edge** between any two distinct vertices, and the total number of distinct vertex pairs is $\binom{n}{2}$, the maximum number of edges is:

$$\max(\vert E \vert) = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is achieved by the **complete graph** $K_n$, where every pair of vertices is connected.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Handshaking Theorem for Undirected Graphs

**Goal:** Prove that $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2m$.

**Step 1 — Understand degree contribution.**

When we sum $\deg(v)$ over all vertices $v \in V$, we are essentially **counting the number of times an edge is "touched"** by a vertex.

**Step 2 — Analyze a single edge.**

Consider any edge $e = \{u, v\}$:
- It contributes **1** to $\deg(u)$ (it is incident to $u$).
- It contributes **1** to $\deg(v)$ (it is incident to $v$).
- Therefore, edge $e$ contributes exactly **2** to the total sum $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v)$.

**Step 3 — Special case: Self-loops.**

A self-loop at vertex $v$ is incident to $v$ **twice** (since both endpoints of the edge are $v$). Hence, a self-loop contributes **2** to $\deg(v)$ and **2** to the total sum, consistent with the formula.

**Step 4 — Sum over all edges.**

Since every edge (including self-loops) contributes exactly **2** to the total sum, and there are $m$ edges in total:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) &= \sum_{e \in E} (\text{contribution of } e) \\
&= \sum_{e \in E} 2 \\
&= 2 \cdot m \\
&= 2m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hence proved.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Handshaking Theorem for Directed Graphs

**Goal:** Prove that $\sum_{v \in V} \deg^{-}(v) = \sum_{v \in V} \deg^{+}(v) = m$.

**Step 1 — Analyze in-degree sum.**

Every arc $(u, v) \in A$ contributes exactly **1** to the in-degree of $v$ (its head). Since the total number of arcs is $m$:

$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg^{-}(v) = m$$

**Step 2 — Analyze out-degree sum.**

Every arc $(u, v) \in A$ contributes exactly **1** to the out-degree of $u$ (its tail). Since the total number of arcs is $m$:

$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg^{+}(v) = m$$

**Step 3 — Conclude equality.**

From Steps 1 and 2, we directly obtain:

$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg^{-}(v) = \sum_{v \in V} \deg^{+}(v) = m$$

**Hence proved.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Number of Edges in a Complete Graph $K_n$

**Goal:** Show that $\vert E(K_n) \vert = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.

**Step 1 — Definition of $K_n$.**

The complete graph $K_n$ contains $n$ vertices where **every** pair of distinct vertices is connected by exactly one edge.

**Step 2 — Count edges by choosing vertex pairs.**

Each edge in $K_n$ corresponds to a unique unordered pair of vertices. The number of such pairs is:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Final formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert E(K_n) \vert &= \binom{n}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** $K_5$ has $\dfrac{5 \cdot 4}{2} = 10$ edges.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Directed vs Undirected Graphs

| Property | Undirected Graph | Directed Graph (Digraph) |
|---|---|---|
| Edge representation | $\{u, v\}$ (unordered) | $(u, v)$ (ordered) |
| Symmetry | $\deg(v) = $ neighbors count | $\deg(v) = \deg^{-}(v) + \deg^{+}(v)$ |
| Handshaking | $\sum \deg(v) = 2m$ | $\sum \deg^{-}(v) = \sum \deg^{+}(v) = m$ |
| Max edges (simple) | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $n(n-1)$ |
| Self-loop degree contribution | 2 | 1 (to in-degree) + 1 (to out-degree) |
| Connectivity concept | Connected | Strongly / Weakly connected |
| Typical representation | Adjacency matrix (symmetric) | Adjacency matrix (asymmetric) |
| Real-world analogy | Road network, friendship | Web links, task dependencies |
| Common algorithms | BFS, DFS, Kruskal, Prim | Topological sort, shortest path (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford) |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Degree Verification

**Problem:** Consider an undirected graph $G$ with vertices $V = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and edges $E = \{\{A,B\}, \{A,C\}, \{B,C\}, \{C,D\}, \{D,E\}\}$.

**Step 1 — Compute degrees.**

- $\deg(A) = 2$ (edges to B, C)
- $\deg(B) = 2$ (edges to A, C)
- $\deg(C) = 3$ (edges to A, B, D)
- $\deg(D) = 2$ (edges to C, E)
- $\deg(E) = 1$ (edge to D)

**Step 2 — Verify handshaking theorem.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) &= 2 + 2 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 10 \\
2m &= 2 \times 5 = 10 \\
\text{Sum} &= 2m \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Count odd-degree vertices.**

$\deg(E) = 1$ is odd. So $\vert V_{odd} \vert = 1$... wait, this seems to contradict the corollary!

> [!WARNING]
> Let me recheck: $A=2, B=2, C=3, D=2, E=1$. Odd degrees: $C=3$ and $E=1$. That's **2** odd-degree vertices, which **is even**. ✓ The corollary holds.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example: Directed Graph In/Out Degree

**Problem:** Consider a digraph with $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and arcs $A = \{(1,2), (1,3), (2,3), (3,4), (4,2), (4,4)\}$.

**Step 1 — Compute out-degrees.**

- $\deg^{+}(1) = 2$ (arcs to 2, 3)
- $\deg^{+}(2) = 1$ (arc to 3)
- $\deg^{+}(3) = 1$ (arc to 4)
- $\deg^{+}(4) = 2$ (arcs to 2, 4)

**Step 2 — Compute in-degrees.**

- $\deg^{-}(1) = 0$
- $\deg^{-}(2) = 2$ (from 1, from 4)
- $\deg^{-}(3) = 2$ (from 1, from 2)
- $\deg^{-}(4) = 2$ (from 3, from 4 — self-loop)

**Step 3 — Verify the directed handshaking theorem.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \deg^{+}(v) &= 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 6 = m \quad \checkmark \\
\sum \deg^{-}(v) &= 0 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 = m \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Undirected Graph (Sample 5-Vertex Graph)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A((A)) --- B((B))
    A --- C((C))
    B --- C
    C --- D((D))
    D --- E((E))
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Vertices are shown as **circles** and edges as **plain lines (no arrows)**. This is the visual signature of an undirected graph.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Directed Graph (DAG — Topological Order)

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1((1)) --> V2((2))
    V1 --> V3((3))
    V2 --> V4((4))
    V3 --> V4
    V4 --> V5((5))
    style V1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style V2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style V3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style V4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style V5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Vertices are shown as **circles** and edges as **arrows** with clear direction. This is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** since there is no directed cycle.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Graph Type Classifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[INPUT Graph G equals V E] --> CK1{Contains self loops or parallel edges?}
    CK1 -- YES --> PG[Pseudograph or Multigraph]
    CK1 -- NO --> CK2{Edges have direction?}
    CK2 -- NO --> SIMPLE_U[Simple Undirected Graph]
    CK2 -- YES --> SIMPLE_D[Simple Directed Graph Digraph]
    SIMPLE_U --> CK3{All vertices connected?}
    CK3 -- YES --> CON_U[Connected Undirected]
    CK3 -- NO --> DIS_U[Disconnected Undirected]
    SIMPLE_D --> CK4{Strongly connected?}
    CK4 -- YES --> CON_D[Strongly Connected Digraph]
    CK4 -- NO --> CK5{Underlying undirected connected?}
    CK5 -- YES --> WEAK_D[Weakly Connected Digraph]
    CK5 -- NO --> DIS_D[Disconnected Digraph]
    SIMPLE_U --> CK6{Every pair connected?}
    CK6 -- YES --> COMP[Complete Graph K n]
    CK6 -- NO --> GEN_U[General Undirected Graph]
    SIMPLE_D --> CK7{Contains a directed cycle?}
    CK7 -- NO --> DAG_OUT[Directed Acyclic Graph DAG]
    CK7 -- YES --> GEN_D[General Directed Graph]
    style IN fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PG fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SIMPLE_U fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SIMPLE_D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CON_U fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DIS_U fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CON_D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style WEAK_D fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DIS_D fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style COMP fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style GEN_U fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DAG_OUT fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style GEN_D fill:#CD853F,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** This flowchart classifies any given graph $G = (V, E)$ into its precise type by following a sequence of structural yes/no decisions.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Degree Computation Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start at Vertex v] --> INIT[Initialize counter equals 0]
    INIT --> LOOP[For each edge e incident to v]
    LOOP --> CHECK{Is e a self loop?}
    CHECK -- YES --> SL_ADD[Add 2 to counter undirected or add 1 to both in degree and out degree directed]
    CHECK -- NO --> REG_ADD[Add 1 to counter undirected or add 1 to in degree or out degree directed]
    SL_ADD --> MORE
    REG_ADD --> MORE{More edges to check?}
    LOOP --> MORE
    MORE -- YES --> LOOP
    MORE -- NO --> RESULT[Return final degree of v]
    style START fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RESULT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CHECK fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SL_ADD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style REG_ADD fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.5 Summary of Visual Conventions

| Symbol | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Circle node** | A vertex | Both directed and undirected |
| **Plain line** | Undirected edge | Undirected only |
| **Arrow line** | Directed edge (arc) | Directed only |
| **Double arrow** | Two opposite arcs between same pair | Directed only |
| **Self-loop** | Edge from vertex to itself | Both |
| **Dashed line** | Optional or conditional edge (in BFS/DFS spanning tree) | Both |
| **Thick line** | Highlighted path or tree edge | Both |
